| Relation between $T_d$ and $\theta$ | **Linear** ($\theta \propto T_d$) | **Non-linear** ($\sin\theta \propto T_d$) |
| Scale type | Uniform (linear) | Non-uniform (cramped) |
| Sensitivity | **Higher** | Lower |
| Position dependence | **Independent** of orientation | **Must be vertical** |
| Effect of temperature | Small (springs are compensated) | Negligible |
| Construction cost | **Higher** (precision springs) | Lower |
| Robustness to overload | Spring may deform | Weight simply shifts |
| Used in | Portable instruments (ammeters, voltmeters) | Panel-mount switchboard instruments |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Types of Damping

| **Damping Type** | **Principle** | **Where Used** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Air Friction** | Piston moving in an enclosed air chamber | Moving Iron instruments | Simple, cheap | Less effective, depends on air density |
| **Fluid Friction** | Vane immersed in oil/damping fluid | Electrostatic voltmeters, precision meters | Strong damping | Oil can leak, temperature dependent |
| **Eddy Current (EM)** | Disc moving in a magnetic field induces opposing currents | PMMC instruments | Frictionless, efficient, reliable | Only works with magnetic fields |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Operating Principle of Indicating Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MEASURAND INPUT Voltage or Current"] --> nodeB["DEFLECTING SYSTEM Produces Deflecting Torque Td"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MOVING SYSTEM Pointer and Spindle"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CONTROL SYSTEM Spring or Gravity Produces Tc"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["DAMPING SYSTEM Air Fluid or Eddy Current Produces Tv"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["POINTER DEFLECTION Final Steady State Position"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Spring Control Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Pointer"] --> nodeB["Spindle"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Pivot Point"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Upper Hair Spring"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Lower Hair Spring"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Frame"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG["Moving Coil"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Permanent Magnet Field"]
    nodeH -.-> nodeI["Deflecting Torque Td"]
    nodeD -.-> nodeJ["Controlling Torque Tc"]
    nodeE -.-> nodeJ
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Gravity Control Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Pointer Axis"] --> nodeB["Pointer"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Control Weight W"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Pivot Point P"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Distance l from pivot"]
    nodeE -.-> nodeF["Torque W x l x sin theta"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Deflecting Torque Td"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Equilibrium Position"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Three-Torque Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input Measurand"] --> nodeB["Td Generation"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Summing Junction"]
    nodeD["Tv from Velocity"] --> nodeC
    nodeE["Tc from Deflection"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF["Moving System"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Pointer Output"]
    nodeF -.Velocity Feedback.-> nodeD
    nodeF -.Position Feedback.-> nodeE
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Damping Methods Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["TYPES OF DAMPING"] --> nodeB["Air Friction"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Fluid Friction"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Eddy Current Electromagnetic"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Piston in Air Chamber"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["Used in Moving Iron"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Vane in Oil"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2["Used in Electrostatic Voltmeters"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Conductive Disc in Magnetic Field"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2["Used in PMMC Ammeters"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Deflecting Torque, Controlling Torque, and Damping Torque. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$):** The torque produced due to the measurand (input quantity) that causes the pointer of the indicating instrument to deflect from its zero position. It is the **active driving torque**. For example, in a PMMC ammeter, $T_d$ is produced by the interaction of the current-carrying coil with the permanent magnet field.

- **Controlling Torque ($T_c$):** The torque that acts in **opposition** to the deflecting torque and grows with the angular deflection. It brings the pointer to a stable equilibrium position and ensures the pointer returns to zero when the input is removed. Generated either by a **spring** ($T_c = K_s\theta$) or by **gravity** ($T_c = Wl\sin\theta$).

- **Damping Torque ($T_v$):** The torque that **opposes the motion** of the moving system. It is proportional to the angular velocity ($T_v = D \cdot d\theta/dt$) and exists only when the pointer is moving. Its purpose is to bring the pointer to its final position quickly without oscillation.

At steady state: $T_d = T_c$ and $T_v = 0$.

---

### Question 2: State the difference between Spring Control and Gravity Control. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Spring Control** | **Gravity Control** |
|---|---|
| Uses a spiral/hair spring for restoring torque | Uses a small weight attached to the pointer |
| $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$ (linear relation) | $T_c = W \cdot l \cdot \sin\theta$ (non-linear relation) |
| Scale is **uniform** (linear scale) | Scale is **non-uniform** (cramped near zero) |
| Instrument may be used in **any position** | Instrument must be used in **vertical position** |
| Used in portable instruments | Used in panel-mount switchboard instruments |
| More expensive due to precision springs | Cheaper and simpler construction |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the construction and working of a spring-controlled PMMC indicating instrument. Derive the expression for steady-state deflection. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the three types of damping used in indicating instruments with suitable diagrams. **[7 Marks]**

#### (a) Model Solution — Spring-Controlled PMMC Instrument

**Construction:**
A Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument consists of:
1. **Permanent Magnet:** Provides a strong, uniform magnetic field in the air gap.
2. **Moving Coil:** A rectangular coil of fine copper wire wound on a non-magnetic (aluminium) former, pivoted between jewel bearings.
3. **Pointer:** Attached to the spindle, moves over a graduated scale.
4. **Hair Springs:** Two spiral springs (top and bottom) provide the controlling torque and also serve as current leads.
5. **Damping System:** Eddy current damping provided by the aluminium former moving in the magnetic field.
6. **Scale:** Uniform, calibrated linearly.

**Working Principle:**
When current $I$ flows through the moving coil placed in the magnetic field $B$, a force $F$ acts on each side of the coil:

$$
F = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot N
$$

Where $B$ is flux density, $l$ is the effective length of the coil side, and $N$ is the number of turns. The deflecting torque produced is:

$$
T_d = F \cdot d = B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot N \cdot d
$$

Where $d$ is the breadth (width) of the coil. Since $B$, $l$, $N$, and $d$ are all constants for a given instrument, we can write:

$$
T_d = G \cdot I
$$

Where $G = B \cdot l \cdot N \cdot d$ is the magnetic constant of the instrument.

**At steady-state equilibrium:**

$$
T_d = T_c
$$

$$
G \cdot I = K_s \cdot \theta
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = \frac{G \cdot I}{K_s}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Deflection $\theta$ is directly proportional to current $I$, hence the scale is **uniform and linear**.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Three Types of Damping

**1. Air Friction Damping:**
- A light **piston** attached to the spindle moves inside a curved air chamber.
- As the pointer moves, air is forced through a narrow gap, providing damping.
- **Application:** Moving Iron instruments, where eddy current damping cannot be used (iron is not a good conductor).
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to temperature and air pressure variations.

**2. Fluid Friction Damping:**
- A **vane** attached to the spindle is immersed in a damping oil.
- As the vane moves, the viscous drag of the oil provides damping.
- **Application:** Electrostatic voltmeters, precision laboratory instruments.
- **Disadvantage:** Oil may leak, viscosity changes with temperature.

**3. Eddy Current (Electromagnetic) Damping:**
- A thin **aluminium disc** attached to the spindle rotates in the field of a permanent magnet.
- As the disc moves, eddy currents are induced in it, which oppose the motion (per Lenz's law).
- **Application:** PMMC ammeters and voltmeters.
- **Advantage:** Frictionless, efficient, no moving parts to wear out.

---

### Question B: Choice 2 (Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the equation of motion of the moving system of an indicating instrument. Hence obtain the condition for critical damping. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compare the merits and demerits of spring control and gravity control. Why is spring control preferred in portable instruments? **[7 Marks]**

#### (a) Model Solution — Equation of Motion & Critical Damping

**Derivation:**

Consider the moving system of an indicating instrument with moment of inertia $J$, damping coefficient $D$, and spring constant $K_s$. Let $\theta$ be the angular deflection.

Applying Newton's second law for rotational motion, the net torque equals the moment of inertia times angular acceleration:

$$
J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = T_d - T_c - T_v
$$

Substituting the expressions:
- $T_c = K_s \cdot \theta$
- $T_v = D \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$

We obtain:

$$
J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = T_d - K_s \cdot \theta - D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K_s \cdot \theta = T_d
$$

This is the **equation of motion** of the moving system — a second-order linear differential equation.

**Condition for Critical Damping:**

The standard second-order form is:

$$
\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \omega_n^2 \cdot \theta = \frac{T_d}{J}
$$

Comparing coefficients:

$$
2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{D}{J} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{K_s}{J}
$$

The natural undamped frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{J}}
$$

For critical damping, $\zeta = 1$, hence:

$$
2\omega_n = \frac{D_c}{J}
$$

Substituting $\omega_n$:

$$
D_c = 2J \cdot \omega_n = 2J \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{J}}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_c = 2\sqrt{J \cdot K_s}}
$$

When $D = D_c$, the system is **critically damped** and the pointer reaches its final position in minimum time without oscillation.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Comparison of Spring vs Gravity Control

**Merits of Spring Control:**
- Provides a **linear** relationship between $T_d$ and $\theta$ → uniform scale.
- Instrument can be used in **any orientation** (portable use possible).
- Higher **sensitivity** because deflection is large for small inputs.
- **Self-compensating** for minor position variations.
- Robust and reliable for transportation shocks.

**Demerits of Spring Control:**
- Springs may **deform permanently** under large overload currents.
- **Temperature changes** can affect spring stiffness (requires careful material selection — phosphor bronze).
- **More expensive** due to precision manufacturing of springs.
- Springs may **age** over time, changing calibration.

**Merits of Gravity Control:**
- **Cheaper** and simpler construction.
- No spring fatigue or aging issues.
- Cannot be permanently damaged by overload.

**Demerits of Gravity Control:**
- Gives a **non-linear scale** (sinusoidal response) — difficult to read accurately.
- Must always be used in **vertical position** → cannot be portable.
- **Lower sensitivity** compared to spring control.
- Requires **leveling** during installation.

**Why Spring Control is Preferred in Portable Instruments:**
Portable instruments (like multimeters, handheld ammeters) are carried and used in various positions and orientations. Since gravity control requires the instrument to be perfectly vertical, it is unsuitable for portable use. Spring control is **position-independent**, making it the universal choice for portable analog meters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit of angle:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if you write deflection in **degrees** instead of **radians** in derivations. Always use $\theta$ in **radians** in $T_c = K_s\theta$.
> 2. **Forgetting to state "at steady state":** The equation $T_d = T_c$ is **only** valid at equilibrium. If the pointer is moving, the full differential equation must be used.
> 3. **Damping torque confusion:** Many students write $T_v = D\theta$ instead of $T_v = D \cdot d\theta/dt$. Damping torque is proportional to **velocity**, not position.
> 4. **Mixing up critical damping condition:** $D_c = 2\sqrt{JK}$ is the critical damping **coefficient**, not the value of damping torque.
> 5. **Forgetting Lenz's law reference:** When explaining eddy current damping, you must explicitly state that the induced currents **oppose the cause** (Lenz's law). Examiners specifically look for this phrase.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The three essential torques in any indicating instrument are **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)**, **Controlling Torque ($T_c$)**, and **Damping Torque ($T_v$)**.
- ✅ At **steady state (equilibrium)**: $T_d = T_c$ and $T_v = 0$.
- ✅ **Spring control** gives a **linear** scale and is **position-independent** → used in portable instruments.
- ✅ **Gravity control** gives a **non-linear** (sinusoidal) scale and is **position-dependent** → used in panel instruments.
- ✅ The **equation of motion** is $J\ddot{\theta} + D\dot{\theta} + K_s\theta = T_d$ (second-order linear ODE).
- ✅ **Critical damping** condition: $D_c = 2\sqrt{J \cdot K_s}$ — gives fastest non-oscillatory response (dead-beat).
- ✅ **Air friction damping** → used in Moving Iron instruments.
- ✅ **Fluid friction damping** → used in Electrostatic voltmeters.
- ✅ **Eddy current (electromagnetic) damping** → used in PMMC instruments (most efficient, frictionless).
- ✅ For PMMC: $T_d \propto I$ (linear) → uniform scale.
- ✅ For Moving Iron: $T_d \propto I^2$ (square law) → non-uniform scale (cramped at low values).
- ✅ Sensitivity of spring-controlled instrument: $S = d\theta/dI = G/K_s$ — increases with softer spring or stronger magnetic field.
- ✅ For damped oscillations, the system is **underdamped** when $\zeta < 1$, **critically damped** when $\zeta = 1$, and **overdamped** when $\zeta > 1$.
- ✅ Remember the practical design rule: instruments are designed to be **slightly underdamped** so the pointer crosses the final value once before settling, confirming that the system is not sluggish.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SAD":**
> **S**pring control → A (Air) Damping for MI
> **A**ir damping → for Moving Iron
> **E**ddy current → for PMMC
> **F**luid → for Electrostatic
> Use this mapping to remember which damping technique goes with which instrument type!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Coil Instruments: Types (Permanent Magnet, Dynamometer Type Meters)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moving Coil Instruments: Types (Permanent Magnet, Dynamometer Type Meters)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Moving Coil Instruments** are electromechanical analog indicating devices in which a coil carrying the current (or a current proportional to the quantity being measured) is suspended or pivoted within a magnetic field, and the resulting electromagnetic force causes a pointer deflection proportional to the measurand. They form the cornerstone of DC measurement in laboratory and industrial instrumentation.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) classifies these instruments into two principal families:

1. **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instruments** — also universally known as the **D'Arsonval Galvanometer** when used in null-detection mode.
2. **Dynamometer Type Moving Coil Instruments** — in which the magnetic field is produced by a current-carrying fixed coil (electromagnet) instead of a permanent magnet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT722 – Module 2):** Students must be able to *describe the construction, derive the torque equation, draw the equivalent circuit, list the errors, and justify the application* of both PMMC and Dynamometer-type meters. Numerical problems on deflection, full-scale current, and shunt/multiplier design are **frequently asked (high-weightage)** in Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**PMMC Analogy — "The Compass in a Magnetic Cage":**
Imagine a small rectangular wire loop carrying current, placed inside the gap of a strong horseshoe magnet. The current-carrying loop behaves exactly like a tiny bar magnet (right-hand rule), and since it is forced to sit inside an external magnetic field, it experiences a **twisting force (torque)** — just like a compass needle trying to align with the Earth's magnetic field. The stronger the current, the more violently the loop tries to rotate. A fine spiral spring (analogous to a torsion balance) opposes this rotation, and the pointer settles where the magnetic torque equals the spring restoring torque. **The deflection angle is therefore a direct mechanical image of the current** — like a weighing scale where the pointer's position reveals the weight.

**Dynamometer Analogy — "Two Magnets Talking to Each Other":**
Now imagine removing the permanent magnet and replacing it with **two big fixed coils** that carry the *same* current. The moving coil, suspended between them, is now immersed in a magnetic field whose strength depends on the current itself. Both coils act like electromagnets trying to "grip" each other. If you reverse one current, they repel instead of attract — and the pointer moves the opposite way. This **mutual coupling** between two current-carrying coils is the heart of every wattmeter, since power depends on the *product* of two quantities (voltage and current).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** PMMC meters are the gold standard for DC measurements (multimeters, panel meters, galvanometers), while Dynamometer instruments are the only meters that can accurately measure **AC power (watts)** and **power factor** — making them indispensable in power-system laboratories and energy-meter calibration.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Flux density of modern Alnico / Rare-earth magnets used in PMMC:** **B = 0.1 to 1.0 Tesla (T)**
- **Current sensitivity of a D'Arsonval galvanometer:** typically **0.1 µA/division to 1 mA/full-scale**
- **Coil resistance (moving coil):** typically **50 Ω to 2000 Ω** depending on the application
- **Standard full-scale deflection angle:** **θ_FSD = 90°** (for rectilinear scale) or 60°–120° (for circular scale)
- **Operating temperature range (standard):** **0 °C to 40 °C** unless otherwise specified
- **Accuracy class (PMMC panel meters):** **±1 % to ±5 %** of full scale
- **Mutual inductance variation (Dynamometer):** **dM/dθ** — the core design parameter

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument

#### 2.1.1 Construction (Logical Breakdown)

The PMMC instrument consists of the following functionally distinct sub-systems:

- **Magnet Assembly (Field System):**
  - A high-grade **permanent magnet** (historically Alnico, modernly **Alnico-V / Samarium-Cobalt / Neodymium-Boron-Iron**) produces a **uniform, radial magnetic field** in a narrow cylindrical air gap.
  - Soft-iron pole pieces and a central soft-iron core shape the field into **radial lines** so that the coil conductors always move *perpendicular* to **B**, regardless of pointer position. This **linearizes the scale** because the force on a current-carrying wire in a radial field is independent of the angular position of the coil.

- **Moving Coil (Deflecting System):**
  - A lightweight rectangular coil of **fine insulated copper wire** (typically 40–200 turns depending on current rating) wound on a non-magnetic metallic former (aluminum).
  - The aluminum former also acts as a **damper (eddy-current damper)** because as it rotates in the magnetic field, eddy currents are induced which oppose the motion, providing critical damping.

- **Controlling System:**
  - Two helical **control springs** (phosphor-bronze or beryllium-copper) — one above and one below the spindle — provide the restoring (controlling) torque.
  - These springs also serve as **current leads** to the moving coil, eliminating the need for slip rings or brushes (a major advantage of PMMC over MI instruments).

- **Damping System:**
  - Provided automatically by the **aluminum former** (eddy-current damping). Some sensitive galvanometers use a separate **damping resistor** across the coil for critical damping.

- **Indicating System:**
  - A lightweight pointer attached to the spindle moves over a calibrated scale.

- **Balancing System:**
  - **Counterweights** on the spindle ensure the moving system is dynamically balanced to minimize torque error due to vibration or tilt.

#### 2.1.2 Operating Principle — The Physics of Deflection

When a current **I** flows through the moving coil (N turns), each conductor of the coil experiences a **Lorentz force** given by **F = BIl** where *l* is the active length of the conductor in the field.

Since the field is radial, this force is always **tangential** to the motion of the coil sides, producing a pure torque without any zero-position error. The deflecting torque is:

$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$

where:
- **N** = number of turns of the moving coil
- **B** = flux density in the air gap (Tesla)
- **I** = current through the moving coil (Amperes)
- **A** = effective area of the coil (m²)

The control spring provides a restoring torque proportional to the angular deflection:

$$T_c = K \cdot \theta$$

At steady-state equilibrium, **T_d = T_c**:

$$N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K \cdot \theta$$

Solving for the deflection:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K} \cdot I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Since N, B, A, and K are constants for a given instrument, **θ is directly proportional to I**. This is the mathematical reason PMMC instruments have a **uniform (linear) scale**.

#### 2.1.3 Characteristics, Advantages, and Disadvantages of PMMC

**Advantages:**
- **High sensitivity and accuracy** (±0.1 % to ±2 % of full scale achievable in precision instruments).
- **Linear (uniform) scale** — easy to read.
- **Low power consumption** (very important for measuring small currents).
- **Effective eddy-current damping** — quick response, no external damping needed.
- **No hysteresis error** in the moving system (the permanent magnet's hysteresis is part of the field and is constant).
- **No stray magnetic field error** because external fields are negligible compared to the strong PM field.
- **No spring carry-leakage** — phosphor-bronze springs are high-resistance, minimizing errors.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Operates only on DC** — on AC, the pointer cannot follow rapid reversals and reads zero (rectifier or thermal converter needed for AC use).
- **More expensive** than Moving Iron instruments.
- **Aging of the permanent magnet** causes a slow decrease in flux density (drift), requiring periodic recalibration.
- **Temperature error** — temperature changes affect (i) magnet strength (≈ −0.02 %/°C for Alnico) and (ii) spring stiffness and coil resistance.

#### 2.1.4 Errors in PMMC Instruments

| Error Type | Cause | Compensation Method |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature error | Heating changes coil R, spring K, and B | Swamping resistor (manganin) in series; low-temperature-coefficient magnet material |
| Error due to weak permanent magnet | Aging of Alnico | Calibration |
| Error due to spring | Non-linear spring at extreme deflection | Use within linear range |
| Error due to stray magnetic field | External fields superimposed on B | Magnetic shielding (mu-metal case) |
| Error due to friction | Pivot and jewel wear | Use taut-band suspension (no pivots) for high-end meters |

---

### 2.2 Dynamometer Type Moving Coil Instrument

#### 2.2.1 Construction

A Dynamometer instrument has two coils:

- **Fixed Coils (Field Coils):**
  - Two coils wound with heavy-gauge wire (for ammeter/wattmeter-current-coil use) or fine wire (for voltmeter use), connected in series and placed coaxially on either side of the moving coil.
  - They produce the magnetic field in which the moving coil operates. Because the field depends on the current, it is generally **weaker and less uniform** than a permanent-magnet field.

- **Moving Coil (Deflecting Coil):**
  - A small, lightweight coil suspended (or pivoted) on a spindle between the fixed coils. The pointer is attached to this spindle.

- **Control System:**
  - Spiral springs (one or two) provide controlling torque. The springs are connected such that their torsion effect is in opposite directions for opposite currents — a vital feature for **AC operation**.

- **Damping:**
  - **Air damping** (piston in a chamber) is preferred over eddy-current damping because there is no permanent-magnet field to induce damping currents in a metal former. (Eddy damping would interfere with the operating magnetic field.)

- **Shielding:**
  - A **soft-iron shielding case** is essential to protect the instrument from external magnetic fields (which, unlike PMMC, the Dynamometer cannot inherently reject).

#### 2.2.2 Operating Principle

When currents **I₁** (in fixed coils) and **I₂** (in moving coil) flow, energy stored in the magnetic field of the system is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 + M I_1 I_2$$

where **M** is the **mutual inductance** between the fixed and moving coils, which **varies with the angular position θ of the moving coil**.

The deflecting torque is obtained by the energy method (generalized force = ∂W/∂θ at constant current):

$$\boxed{T_d = I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}}$$

For **DC operation**: T_d = I₁ · I₂ · (dM/dθ)
For **AC operation** (instantaneous): t_d = i₁ · i₂ · (dM/dθ)
For **sinusoidal AC** (with phase angle φ between i₁ and i₂):
i₁ = I_{m1} sin(ωt), i₂ = I_{m2} sin(ωt + φ)

The average torque over one cycle is:

$$\boxed{T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}}$$

where I₁, I₂ are RMS values. This **cos φ dependence** is what makes the Dynamometer the natural **wattmeter**, because:

$$P = V I \cos\phi$$

If I₁ ∝ V and I₂ ∝ I, then T_d ∝ V · I · cos φ = P.

#### 2.2.3 Applications of Dynamometer

| Configuration | Fixed Coil | Moving Coil | Measures | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ammeter (in series with load, heavy wire) | Current I | Current I (or shunt) | I² (square-law scale) | Non-uniform |
| Voltmeter (in series with multiplier) | Current ∝ V | Same | V² (square-law scale) | Non-uniform |
| Wattmeter | Current I (thick wire) | Current ∝ V (with multiplier) | V·I·cos φ | **Uniform** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** When used as an ammeter or voltmeter, the Dynamometer gives a **square-law (non-linear) scale** because T_d ∝ I² or V². When used as a wattmeter, with one coil carrying the load current and the other carrying a current proportional to voltage, the scale becomes **uniform (linear)** because P varies linearly.

#### 2.2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Dynamometer

**Advantages:**
- Can be used for both **AC and DC** measurements.
- High accuracy (used as **transfer instrument** for AC calibration).
- When used as wattmeter, gives **true RMS × cos φ** — directly indicates power.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Low torque-to-weight ratio** — the field is weaker than a permanent magnet, so the instrument is more delicate.
- **Higher power consumption** (current must energize the field coils).
- **Scale is non-uniform** for ammeter/voltmeter use (square-law).
- **Affected by stray magnetic fields** — requires magnetic shielding.
- **More expensive** than PMMC or MI.
- **Lower sensitivity** than PMMC.

#### 2.2.5 Errors in Dynamometer

- **Error due to inductance of operating coil** — frequency-dependent; corrected by a non-inductive shunt in the ammeter.
- **Error due to capacitance** between fixed and moving coils at high frequencies.
- **Error due to eddy currents** in the metal parts of the instrument — minimized by lamination or using non-metallic formers.
- **Stray magnetic field error** — corrected by magnetic shielding.
- **Temperature error** — affects coil resistances and spring stiffness.
- **Error due to connection (in wattmeter)** — the moving-coil current is slightly out of phase with the actual voltage due to multiplier inductance; this is the famous **"wattmeter error"** and is corrected using a **compensating coil**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PMMC Deflecting Torque | $T_d = N B I A$ | N = turns, B = T, I = A, A = m² | Valid for radial field |
| 2 | PMMC Control Torque | $T_c = K\theta$ | K = N·m/rad, θ = rad | Hooke's law for spring |
| 3 | PMMC Deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{N B A}{K}\cdot I$ | θ = rad | Linear scale with I |
| 4 | PMMC Current Sensitivity | $S_I = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{NBA}{K}$ | rad/A | KTU classical formula |
| 5 | PMMC Voltage Sensitivity | $S_V = \dfrac{\theta}{V} = \dfrac{NBA}{K(R_c + R_s)}$ | rad/V | R_c = coil R, R_s = multiplier R |
| 6 | PMMC Multiplier Resistor | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_{FSD}} - R_c$ | Ohms (Ω) | For voltmeter conversion |
| 7 | PMMC Shunt Resistor | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{I_g R_c}{I - I_g}$ | Ohms (Ω) | For ammeter conversion (Ayrton) |
| 8 | Dynamometer Deflecting Torque (DC) | $T_d = I_1 I_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m | M is mutual inductance (H) |
| 9 | Dynamometer Deflecting Torque (AC avg) | $T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m | I₁, I₂ = RMS |
| 10 | Dynamometer Power (Wattmeter) | $T_d \propto V I \cos\phi$ | N·m | Fixed coil ∝ I, moving coil ∝ V |
| 11 | Equilibrium (any instrument) | $T_d = T_c$ | — | At final pointer position |
| 12 | Damping Coefficient (PMMC) | $\zeta = \dfrac{(NBA)^2}{2\sqrt{KJ\,R}}$ | dimensionless | Critical damping at ζ = 1 |
| 13 | Torque-to-Weight Ratio (PMMC) | $T/W \approx 0.1$ to $1.0$ | — | Higher = better sensitivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The *only* difference between the DC and AC torque equations in a Dynamometer is the **cos φ** term for AC. This is the single most-tested differentiator in 14-mark questions.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PMMC instruments** are embedded in:
  - Digital multimeters (after ADC).
  - Analog panel meters in power-supply units.
  - D'Arsonval movement-based strip-chart recorders.
  - Automotive dashboards (voltage, temperature via bridge transducer).
  - Galvanometers in Wheatstone / Kelvin bridges.

- **Dynamometer instruments** are the **workhorse of AC power measurement**:
  - Standard laboratory wattmeters (0.1 % class).
  - Reactive-power (VAR) meters with phase-shifting networks.
  - **Transfer standards** — because the same physical instrument can be calibrated on DC and then used on AC, making them ideal for AC calibration chains.
  - Power-factor meters (with crossed-coil dynamometer construction).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PMMC Deflection Equation

**Statement to derive:** Show that the steady-state deflection of a PMMC instrument is linearly proportional to the current.

**Given:**
- Coil with N turns, each of effective area A
- Radial flux density B in the air gap
- Current I flowing through the coil
- Spring constant K
- Side length (depth in field) of coil = d (some texts use 'l')

**Step 1 — Force on a single current-carrying conductor.**
The current-carrying wire placed in a magnetic field **B** experiences a force:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$
where *l* is the length of the conductor within the field. This is the Lorentz force on a current element in a uniform field.

**Step 2 — Force per side of the rectangular coil.**
The rectangular coil has two vertical sides of length *l* in the active magnetic-field region (the horizontal sides lie along the spindle axis and contribute zero torque). Each vertical side carries the full current I and experiences:
$$F_{\text{side}} = B I l$$

**Step 3 — Torque produced by one side.**
The two vertical sides are at a perpendicular distance equal to the radius of the coil. Let the width of the coil (breadth) be **b**. The torque produced by *one* vertical side is:
$$T_{\text{one side}} = F_{\text{side}} \cdot b = B I l b = B I A$$
where A = l·b is the area enclosed by one turn.

**Step 4 — Total deflecting torque with N turns.**
Since there are **N turns**, the total force is multiplied by N, and there are **two sides** contributing in the *same* direction (one pushes up, the other pushes down, both creating rotation in the same sense):
$$T_d = 2 \cdot N \cdot B I l b / 2 = N B I A$$

> *Note on factor 2:* Many textbooks directly write T_d = N·B·I·A without the factor of 2 because the "A" is defined as the **total effective area** of the coil (which already includes the contribution of both sides). To avoid confusion, we adopt the standard convention:
$$\boxed{T_d = N B I A}$$

**Step 5 — Restoring (controlling) torque of the spiral spring.**
By Hooke's law for a torsion spring:
$$T_c = K \theta$$
where **K** is the spring constant (N·m/rad) and **θ** is the angular deflection in radians.

**Step 6 — Equilibrium condition.**
At steady state, the moving system is at rest, so the net torque is zero:
$$T_d = T_c$$
$$N B I A = K \theta$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the deflection.**
$$\theta = \frac{N B A}{K} \cdot I$$

**Step 8 — Define current sensitivity.**
$$S_I = \frac{\theta}{I} = \frac{N B A}{K} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{rad}}{\text{A}} \right]$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion.**
Since **NBA/K** is a constant for a given instrument, the deflection θ is **directly proportional to I**. This is why the PMMC scale is **linear (uniform)** — every equal increment in current produces an equal increment in pointer movement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The linearity depends critically on the **radial field** design. If the field were not radial (e.g., uniform in space), the force would have a cos θ factor, and the scale would be **non-linear** like a tangential-coil galvanometer (a historical design that is no longer used). This is the single most important reason for the soft-iron cylindrical core and curved pole pieces in modern PMMC meters.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Dynamometer Deflection (DC Case)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the deflection of a Dynamometer-type moving coil instrument under DC excitation.

**Given:**
- Fixed coils carrying current **I₁**
- Moving coil carrying current **I₂**
- Mutual inductance M(θ) between the two coil systems, where θ is the deflection angle of the moving coil from its zero position.

**Step 1 — Total stored energy in the magnetic field.**
The total magnetic energy in a system of two circuits carrying currents I₁ and I₂ is:
$$W = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^2 + M I_1 I_2$$
where:
- L₁ = self-inductance of fixed coils
- L₂ = self-inductance of moving coil
- M = mutual inductance between the two sets of coils

**Step 2 — Generalized force (torque) from energy principle.**
For an electromechanical system at constant current, the generalized force (torque) tending to increase a displacement θ is:
$$T_d = \left( \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta} \right)_{I = \text{const}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate W with respect to θ.**
The self-inductances L₁ and L₂ of the fixed and moving coils do **not** depend on θ (the geometry of each coil relative to itself does not change as the moving coil rotates). However, the **mutual inductance M** does depend on θ:
$$T_d = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta} = I_1 I_2 \cdot \frac{\partial M}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 4 — Hence, the deflecting torque is:**
$$\boxed{T_d = I_1 I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply equilibrium with the control spring.**
At steady state:
$$I_1 I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} = K \theta$$

**Step 6 — Solve for deflection.**
$$\theta = \frac{I_1 I_2}{K} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Dynamometer Deflection (AC Case)

**Statement:** Show that the average deflecting torque of a Dynamometer on AC is proportional to V · I · cos φ.

**Step 1 — Write the instantaneous currents.**
$$i_1(t) = I_{m1} \sin(\omega t)$$
$$i_2(t) = I_{m2} \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Write the instantaneous torque.**
$$t_d(t) = i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions.**
$$t_d(t) = I_{m1} \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_{m2} \sin(\omega t + \phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the trigonometric identity sin A · sin B = ½[cos(A − B) − cos(A + B)].**
$$t_d(t) = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \left[ \cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t + \phi) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Average over one complete cycle.**
The integral of cos(2ωt + φ) over one full period of 2π/ω is zero:
$$T_{d,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Convert peak currents to RMS.**
Since $I_{m1} = \sqrt{2} I_1$ and $I_{m2} = \sqrt{2} I_2$ (RMS):
$$T_{d,\text{avg}} = \frac{(\sqrt{2} I_1)(\sqrt{2} I_2)}{2} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**
$$T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 8 — Apply equilibrium with the control spring.**
$$\theta = \frac{I_1 I_2 \cos\phi}{K} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 9 — Wattmeter interpretation.**
If the fixed coil carries the **load current I** and the moving coil is connected across the load with a multiplier such that the moving-coil current is **proportional to V** (i.e., I₂ ∝ V/R_mult), then:
$$\theta \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P_{\text{real}}$$

**Conclusion:** The Dynamometer directly indicates the **real (true) AC power**, which is the reason it forms the basis of all precision wattmeters.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem Worked Out (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A PMMC voltmeter has a coil of 100 turns, area 2 cm², suspended in a uniform radial flux density of 0.1 T. The spring constant is 5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m/rad. The coil resistance is 50 Ω. Calculate:
(a) The current required for full-scale deflection of 90°.
(b) The multiplier resistance to convert it into a 0–10 V voltmeter.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units.**
- A = 2 cm² = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- θ_FSD = 90° = π/2 rad ≈ 1.5708 rad

**Step 2 — Apply the deflection formula.**
$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = \frac{K \cdot \theta}{N \cdot B \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**
$$I = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1.5708)}{100 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator.**
$$\text{Num} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5708 = 7.854 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator.**
$$\text{Den} = 100 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6 — Divide.**
$$I = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \approx 3.93 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 7 — Multiplier resistance.**
$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_{FSD}} - R_c = \frac{10}{3.93 \times 10^{-3}} - 50$$

**Step 8 — Compute.**
$$R_s = 2544.5 - 50 \approx 2494.5 \, \Omega \approx 2.49 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:**
> - Full-scale current: **≈ 3.93 mA**
> - Multiplier resistance: **≈ 2.49 kΩ**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PMMC vs. Dynamometer

| Parameter | PMMC | Dynamometer |
|---|---|---|
| Field source | Permanent magnet | Current-carrying fixed coils |
| Torque magnitude | High (strong B) | Moderate (depends on I) |
| Frequency response | DC only | AC + DC |
| Scale nature | Linear | Square-law (ammeter/voltmeter); Linear (wattmeter) |
| Sensitivity | High | Low–Medium |
| Power consumption | Very low | High |
| Damping type | Eddy-current (aluminum former) | Air damping |
| Shielding needed? | No (strong B dominates) | Yes (soft-iron shield) |
| Aging error | Yes (magnet aging) | None (no permanent magnet) |
| Accuracy class | 0.1–2 % | 0.1–0.5 % (as transfer standard) |
| Cost | Medium | High |
| Used as wattmeter? | No (DC only, square-law) | Yes (precision standard) |
| T_d expression | T_d = N B I A | T_d = I₁ I₂ (dM/dθ) |
| T_d AC form | N/A (DC only) | I₁ I₂ cos φ (dM/dθ) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PMMC Instrument

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Input Current I] --> B[Multiplier Resistor Rs]
    B --> C[Moving Coil Rc with N turns]
    C --> D[Permanent Magnet Field B in Radial Air Gap]
    D --> E[Deflecting Torque Td = NBAI]
    E --> F[Spindle / Pointer Shaft]
    F --> G[Pointer over Calibrated Scale]
    F --> H[Control Spring Ktheta]
    H --> I[Restoring Torque Tc]
    I --> J{Equilibrium Td = Tc}
    J -->|Yes| K[Pointer Settles at theta = NBAI/K]
    F --> L[Aluminum Former]
    L --> M[Eddy Current Damping]
    M --> N[Critical Damping Achieved]
    K --> O[Linear Scale Indication]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dynamometer Wattmeter

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Load Current IL] --> B[Fixed Current Coil Thick Wire]
    B --> C[Produces Magnetic Field Bprop IL]
    C --> D[Magnetic Flux Links Moving Coil]
    E[Load Voltage V] --> F[Multiplier Resistor Rmult]
    F --> G[Moving Coil Carries Current Iv = V/Rmult]
    G --> H[Mutual Inductance Mtheta Varies with Deflection]
    H --> I[Instantaneous Deflecting Torque t = i1 i2 dMdtheta]
    I --> J[Average Torque over AC Cycle]
    J --> K[Tavg = I1 I2 cos phi dMdtheta]
    K --> L[Control Spring Restoring Torque Ktheta]
    L --> M{Equilibrium Tavg = Ktheta}
    M -->|Yes| N[Pointer Deflection prop V I cos phi = P]
    N --> O[Linear Scale Reads Power in Watts]
    P[External Magnetic Field] --> Q[Soft-Iron Magnetic Shield]
    Q --> D
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic — PMMC (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    PM[Permanent Magnet N-S] --> PP1[Soft Iron Pole Piece Upper]
    PM --> PP2[Soft Iron Pole Piece Lower]
    PP1 --> GAP[Radial Air Gap]
    PP2 --> GAP
    GAP --> COIL[Moving Coil with N turns, former]
    COIL --> SP[Spindle and Pointer]
    SP --> SPG1[Control Spring Top]
    SP --> SPG2[Control Spring Bottom]
    SPG1 --> PT[Pointer Tip on Calibrated Scale]
    SPG2 --> CW[Counterweight for Balance]
    COIL --> DAMPR[Aluminum Former Eddy Damper]
```

### 4.4 Signal-Flow Diagram — Dynamometer Ammeter vs. Wattmeter

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Ammeter_Configuration
        A1[Current I] --> A2[Fixed Coil and Moving Coil in Series]
        A2 --> A3[Td proportional to I squared]
        A3 --> A4[Square Law Non Uniform Scale]
    end
    subgraph Wattmeter_Configuration
        W1[Current I] --> W2[Fixed Coil in Series with Load]
        W3[Voltage V] --> W4[Multiplier R and Moving Coil in Parallel]
        W2 --> W5[Deflecting Torque prop V I cos phi]
        W4 --> W5
        W5 --> W6[Linear Scale Reads Real Power in W]
    end
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Error Compensation in PMMC

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ambient Temperature Rise] --> B1[Increase in Coil Resistance Rc]
    A --> B2[Decrease in Spring Constant K]
    A --> B3[Decrease in Flux Density B]
    B1 --> C1[Use Swamping Resistor Manganin in Series]
    B2 --> C2[Use Beryllium Copper Spring]
    B3 --> C3[Use Alnico V or Rare Earth Magnet]
    A --> D[Net Effect Reduced to 0.02 percent per degC]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a D'Arsonval movement? Why is it not suitable for direct AC measurement?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks' worth):**

The **D'Arsonval movement** is the classical construction of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument, named after the French biophysicist Jacques-Arsène d'Arsonval (1882), who invented the moving-coil galvanometer for physiological measurements.

It consists of a lightweight rectangular coil of fine insulated copper wire suspended (or pivoted) in the **radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet**, with current entering and leaving through two spiral control springs. A pointer attached to the spindle indicates the deflection on a calibrated scale, and eddy currents induced in the aluminum former provide damping.

**Why unsuitable for direct AC measurement:**
- In AC, the current reverses direction every half-cycle (50 times per second for 50 Hz).
- The deflecting torque **T_d = N B I A** therefore alternates in sign at twice the supply frequency.
- The pointer cannot mechanically follow such rapid reversals because of its inertia, and therefore **hovers near zero** showing a small vibration.
- The heating effect (I²R) does occur, but the meter cannot display a meaningful average of zero.
- To use a D'Arsonval for AC, the AC must first be **rectified** (using a bridge rectifier and shunting resistor network) or converted to a DC equivalent (using a thermocouple).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of PMMC instruments over Moving Iron (MI) instruments.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks' worth):**

1. **Linear (uniform) scale:** The deflection θ is directly proportional to the current because of the radial magnetic field, whereas MI instruments have a **non-linear (square-law) scale** unless shaped pole pieces are used.

2. **Higher accuracy and sensitivity:** PMMC instruments achieve ±0.1 % accuracy in precision-grade versions, while MI instruments are typically limited to ±2 % to ±5 %.

3. **Lower power consumption:** PMMC operates on small currents (microamperes) due to strong permanent-magnet field, while MI requires enough magnetizing current to saturate the iron vanes.

4. **Effective eddy-current damping** is built into the aluminum former; MI requires external air damping.

5. **No hysteresis error** in the moving system; MI suffers from **iron hysteresis** which causes different up-scale and down-scale readings (reversal error).

6. **No stray-field error** because the strong PM field dominates external fields; MI is highly susceptible to stray fields.

7. **Single-range operation easily extended to multi-range** by simple shunt/multiplier resistor switching (Ayrton shunt for current).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In torque derivations, students often forget to mention that the **field is radial**, which is the reason for the linear scale. Marks are specifically awarded for this statement. Also, do not write "F = BIl sin θ" — for a radial field, sin θ = 1 by construction. The factor of 2 in the torque (two sides of the coil) is the second most common mistake; either include it explicitly or define *A* as the effective total area.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument**. Derive the expression for its deflecting torque and hence obtain the deflection equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMMC voltmeter uses a coil of **80 turns** wound on a former of area **1.5 cm²**. The coil resistance is **75 Ω** and the spring constant is **4 × 10⁻⁶ N·m/rad**. The flux density in the air gap is **0.15 T**. The instrument is to be used as a **0–50 V voltmeter**.
   (i) Find the current for full-scale deflection. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the multiplier resistance required. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) If the instrument is now connected across an unknown voltage and reads **42 V**, find the current through the coil. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**[Refer to Section 2.1.1 of these notes for full construction details.]**

The PMMC instrument comprises:
1. **Magnet system:** Alnico-V or rare-earth permanent magnet with curved soft-iron pole pieces and a soft-iron cylindrical core to create a uniform radial magnetic field in the narrow air gap.
2. **Moving coil:** A rectangular coil of N turns of fine insulated copper wire wound on a lightweight aluminum former, pivoted on jewelled bearings.
3. **Control springs:** Two phosphor-bronze spiral springs (above and below the spindle) provide the controlling torque and act as current leads.
4. **Damping:** Eddy-current damping from the aluminum former.
5. **Pointer and scale:** Lightweight pointer reads deflection on a uniformly divided scale.
6. **Counterweights:** Balance the moving system.

**Working:** When a current I flows through the moving coil, each vertical side (length l) in the radial field experiences a force F = BIl. These two forces, separated by the coil width b, produce a deflecting torque:
$$T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$
where A = l·b. The control spring produces a restoring torque T_c = Kθ. At equilibrium:
$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I \quad \text{(Linear scale)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- N = 80 turns
- A = 1.5 cm² = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- R_c = 75 Ω
- K = 4 × 10⁻⁶ N·m/rad
- B = 0.15 T
- Full-scale voltage V = 50 V
- Full-scale angle θ_FSD = π/2 rad (assumed; otherwise use given value)

**(i) Current for FSD:**
$$I = \frac{K \cdot \theta_{FSD}}{N \cdot B \cdot A} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-6})(\pi/2)}{80 \times 0.15 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Numerator = 4 × 10⁻⁶ × 1.5708 = 6.2832 × 10⁻⁶
Denominator = 80 × 0.15 × 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ = 1.8 × 10⁻³

$$I = \frac{6.2832 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.49 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{A} \approx 3.49 \, \text{mA}$$

**(ii) Multiplier resistance:**
$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_{FSD}} - R_c = \frac{50}{3.49 \times 10^{-3}} - 75 = 14326.6 - 75 \approx 14{,}251.6 \, \Omega \approx 14.25 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Current through coil at 42 V reading:**
$$I = \frac{V}{R_s + R_c} = \frac{42}{14251.6 + 75} = \frac{42}{14326.6} \approx 2.93 \, \text{mA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** (i) I_FSD ≈ **3.49 mA**; (ii) R_s ≈ **14.25 kΩ**; (iii) I ≈ **2.93 mA**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working principle of a Dynamometer-type wattmeter**. Derive the average deflecting torque expression for AC operation and show that it is proportional to the **real power**. **(8 marks)**

**(b)** Compare PMMC and Dynamometer-type instruments on the basis of (i) scale nature, (ii) frequency range, (iii) power consumption, (iv) damping type, (v) accuracy, (vi) typical application. **(6 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (8 marks):**

A **Dynamometer wattmeter** has two coil systems:
1. **Fixed (current) coils:** Two heavy-wire coils connected in series with the load, producing a magnetic field proportional to the load current.
2. **Moving (potential) coil:** A lightweight coil suspended (or pivoted) on a spindle between the fixed coils, with its current proportional to the load voltage via a series multiplier resistor.
3. **Control springs:** Two spiral springs providing restoring torque, connected such that opposite currents produce opposite torques (essential for AC).
4. **Air damping:** Piston-type air damper — eddy damping is unsuitable here because the field is not constant and any metal former would disturb the operating field.
5. **Pointer and scale** with a uniform (linear) scale for power indication.
6. **Soft-iron magnetic shield** to exclude external stray fields.

**Working principle (DC):** With currents I₁ (fixed) and I₂ (moving), the deflecting torque derived from magnetic co-energy is:
$$T_d = I_1 I_2 \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**AC operation:** If i₁ = I_{m1} sin(ωt) and i₂ = I_{m2} sin(ωt + φ), then:
$$T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Wattmeter interpretation:** If the fixed coil is connected in series with the load (so I₁ = I) and the moving coil is in parallel with the load through a multiplier (so I₂ = V/R_mult), then:
$$T_{d,\text{avg}} \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = P_{\text{real}}$$

The deflection is therefore proportional to the **real (true) power** consumed by the load, making the Dynamometer the standard wattmeter.

**Part (b) — Comparison (6 marks):**

| Parameter | PMMC | Dynamometer |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Scale nature | Linear (uniform) | Uniform for wattmeter; Square-law for ammeter/voltmeter |
| (ii) Frequency range | DC only (or rectified AC) | DC and AC (up to a few hundred Hz) |
| (iii) Power consumption | Very low (µW to mW) | High (current heats the field coils) |
| (iv) Damping type | Eddy-current (Al former) | Air damping (piston) |
| (v) Accuracy | 0.1–2 % | 0.1–0.5 % (transfer standard) |
| (vi) Typical application | DC voltmeters, ammeters, panel meters, DMMs | Precision AC wattmeters, power-factor meters, AC calibration standards |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PMMC** = D'Arsonval movement; **Dynamometer** = electrodynamometer.
- [x] PMMC field is **radial** → linear scale (θ ∝ I).
- [x] PMMC torque: **T_d = N B I A**, control: **T_c = Kθ**, equilibrium → **θ = NBA/K · I**.
- [x] PMMC can measure **DC only**; AC requires **rectifier** (or **thermocouple** for true RMS).
- [x] PMMC damping: **eddy-current** (aluminum former); PMMC springs: **phosphor-bronze** (also current leads).
- [x] PMMC errors: magnet aging, temperature, friction at pivots (use **taut-band** for high accuracy).
- [x] PMMC voltmeter: **R_s = V/I_FSD − R_c**; ammeter: **R_sh = I_g R_c / (I − I_g)**.
- [x] Dynamometer torque (DC): **T_d = I₁ I₂ (dM/dθ)**.
- [x] Dynamometer torque (AC, avg): **T_d = I₁ I₂ cos φ (dM/dθ)** — the **cos φ** factor is the heart of wattmeter operation.
- [x] Dynamometer wattmeter: fixed coil ∝ load current, moving coil ∝ load voltage → **T_d ∝ V I cos φ = P**.
- [x] Dynamometer damping: **air damping** (NOT eddy, because field is current-dependent).
- [x] Dynamometer requires **soft-iron magnetic shielding** (susceptible to stray fields).
- [x] Dynamometer is a **transfer instrument** — same calibration valid for AC and DC.
- [x] When Dynamometer is used as ammeter/voltmeter, scale is **square-law** (∝ I² or V²).
- [x] Errors in Dynamometer: inductance, capacitance, eddy currents in metal parts, temperature, stray fields.
- [x] In wattmeters, the **compensating coil** corrects the inductance-induced phase error of the potential coil.
- [x] **Always remember:** N, B, A, K are constants → PMMC has **uniform scale**. dM/dθ is a function of θ → Dynamometer has **non-uniform scale** (except as wattmeter).
- [x] **Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Why is the scale of a PMMC uniform while that of a Dynamometer is non-uniform when used as an ammeter or voltmeter?" — Answer: Because in PMMC, θ ∝ I, while in Dynamometer (ammeter/voltmeter), θ ∝ I² (since I₁ = I₂ = I) and dM/dθ varies with θ, both contributing to non-linearity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** Memorize the **two master equations** — **θ = (NBA/K)·I** for PMMC and **θ = (I₁I₂cos φ/K)·(dM/dθ)** for Dynamometer. Every problem in this module reduces to one of these two equations (often with simple extension to ammeter/voltmeter/wattmeter conversion). Master these, and you master Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Iron Instruments: Attraction and Repulsion Type, Principles and Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moving Iron Instruments: Attraction and Repulsion Type, Principles and Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Moving Iron (MI) Instruments?

**Moving Iron (MI) instruments** are a class of electro-mechanical analog measuring instruments used for the measurement of electrical quantities such as **AC voltage, AC current, and DC current/voltage**. The operating principle is based on the force of attraction or repulsion between a fixed piece of soft iron and a movable piece of soft iron, both of which are magnetized by the current flowing through a nearby coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Moving Iron instruments are the most commonly used instruments for AC measurements in the laboratory and switchboard. They operate on the principle that a piece of soft iron placed in a magnetic field experiences a force of attraction toward the region of higher magnetic flux density.*

They are inherently **RMS responding instruments** because their deflection is proportional to the square of the current (which integrates over time to give the RMS value for AC). They can be used on both **AC and DC** without any change in construction, making them highly versatile.

## 1.2 Classification of Moving Iron Instruments

Based on the nature of the magnetic action, MI instruments are classified into two fundamental categories:

| S.No | Type | Core Mechanism | Typical Application |
|:----:|:-----|:--------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Attraction Type (Single Iron)** | A soft-iron vane is attracted toward the operating coil carrying current | Small-range ammeters and voltmeters |
| 2 | **Repulsion Type (Double Iron)** | Two parallel soft-iron vanes magnetized with the same polarity repel each other | Switchboard panels, high-range meters |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two simple scenarios:

- **Analogy 1 (Attraction Type):** Think of a small piece of iron filings held near the tip of a bar magnet. The instant the magnet is energized, the iron filing is yanked toward the magnet with a noticeable "pull." If the magnet is made stronger (more current), the pull becomes stronger, and the iron moves further. In MI instruments, the "magnet" is an energized coil and the "iron filing" is a precisely mounted soft-iron vane attached to the pointer spindle.

- **Analogy 2 (Repulsion Type):** Place two bar magnets with their North poles facing each other on a table — they violently push apart. Now imagine two soft-iron pieces inside a coil. The current magnetizes *both* in the same direction (say, North on top of both). They now behave like two bar magnets with similar poles facing — they repel. The moving vane is pushed away from the fixed vane, rotating the pointer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The force in both types depends on the *square* of the current. This means the **deflection direction remains the same** whether current flows in one direction or the other. That is why these instruments can read **both AC and DC** without a rectifier!

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Permeability of free space (vacuum):** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$
- **Relative permeability of soft iron:** $\mu_r \approx 200 \text{ to } 5000$ (high for low-carbon steel)
- **Operating temperature range:** Typically $\mathbf{0^{\circ}C \ to \ 50^{\circ}C}$ for standard industrial grade
- **Frequency range:** Up to about **125 Hz** for ordinary instruments; up to **1 kHz** with specially designed coils

## 1.5 Advantages and Limitations (KTU Quick-Recall)

**Advantages:**
- Simple and robust construction
- Cheap and inexpensive compared to PMMC and dynamometer instruments
- Can be used for both AC and DC measurements
- Reasonably accurate for general industrial work

**Limitations:**
- Non-uniform scale (compressed at the lower end and expanded at the upper end)
- Affected by hysteresis and eddy-current losses
- Errors due to frequency and waveform variations
- Lower accuracy compared to PMMC instruments

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics Behind It

The underlying physics rests on two fundamental electromagnetic laws:

1. **Magnetization of soft iron:** When a current-carrying coil produces a magnetic field, any ferromagnetic material (like soft iron) placed inside it gets magnetized by *induction*. The polarity of the induced magnetism depends only on the *direction* of the magnetic field, not its sign in terms of magnitude.
2. **Force on magnetized iron:** A magnetized iron piece in a non-uniform magnetic field experiences a force directed from the *low-flux region* toward the *high-flux region* (force of attraction). Two pieces magnetized with the *same polarity* repel each other.

The current $i(t)$ through the coil produces a magnetic field. The iron vanes get magnetized. The mechanical force $F$ between the field and the vane is always directed to *increase* the flux linkage (Lenz's law in mechanical form), and this force translates into a **deflecting torque** $T_d$.

## 2.2 Constructional Details — Attraction Type

The attraction-type moving iron instrument consists of:

- **A stationary coil (flat or oval-shaped)** wound on a former; the current to be measured passes through this coil.
- **A soft-iron vane (movable)** attached eccentrically to the spindle carrying the pointer.
- **A control spring** providing restoring (controlling) torque.
- **A damping chamber** using an air piston or vane in an enclosed box.
- **Pointer and scale** graduated for direct readout.

When current flows, the coil becomes an electromagnet. The eccentric iron vane is pulled *toward the center* of the coil (where flux density is maximum). The spindle rotates, moving the pointer across the scale.

## 2.3 Constructional Details — Repulsion Type

The repulsion-type instrument is more common in practice. Its components include:

- **A cylindrical operating coil** wound on a bobbin.
- **Two soft-iron vanes** placed inside the coil:
  - *Fixed vane* — attached to the coil frame (does not move).
  - *Movable vane* — attached to the spindle carrying the pointer.
- Both vanes lie along the axis of the coil initially.
- **Control spring, damping vane, pointer, and scale** complete the assembly.

When current $i$ flows through the coil:
- Both vanes are magnetized in the *same direction* (e.g., both become North on the left side, South on the right side).
- Since like poles face each other, the vanes **repel** each other.
- The movable vane is pushed away from the fixed vane, rotating the spindle and pointer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the scale is non-uniform:** The deflection is proportional to $i^2$. When $i$ is small, $i^2$ is even smaller, so the lower scale is compressed. When $i$ is large, $i^2$ grows much faster, expanding the upper scale. The scale shape is approximately *square-law* (parabolic).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------------------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| 1 | Deflecting torque (general) | $T_d \propto i^2$ | N·m | Squared relationship |
| 2 | Deflecting torque expression | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Energy balance method |
| 3 | Operating energy stored | $W = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2$ | Joules | Energy in magnetic field |
| 4 | Control torque (spring) | $T_c = K \theta$ | N·m | $K$ = spring constant (N·m/rad) |
| 5 | Equilibrium condition | $T_d = T_c$ | N·m | At steady-state deflection |
| 6 | Final deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | rad | Squared, non-uniform scale |
| 7 | RMS reading for AC | $\theta \propto i_{rms}^{\,2}$ | rad | DC calibration holds for AC RMS |
| 8 | Force on iron vane | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | N | Attraction force formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constraint:** All absolute-value notation must be rendered as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. In markdown tables, use `\vert` for safety, e.g., $i_{rms} \approx \vert i(t) \vert_{rms}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Moving Iron instruments find extensive use in:

- **Switchboards and distribution panels** (repulsion type for industrial-grade accuracy).
- **AC voltmeters and ammeters** in laboratory experiments.
- **Multimeters** (the AC measurement function of analog multimeters uses MI mechanism).
- **Panel meters** for monitoring motor current, transformer load, and generator output.
- **Educational laboratories** where students learn AC measurement techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deflecting Torque Equation

We now rigorously derive the famous torque equation for a moving iron instrument using the **principle of conservation of energy** (energy balance method).

### 3.1.1 Assumptions

- The coil is rigid and does not deform.
- The iron vane magnetization is instantaneous (no lag).
- Hysteresis and eddy-current losses are neglected initially.
- The inductance of the coil varies with the angular position $\theta$ of the moving iron vane.

### 3.1.2 Energy Stored in the Magnetic Field

When a current $i$ flows through the operating coil, the energy stored in the magnetic field of the coil is given by:

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2
$$

where:
- $L(\theta)$ is the inductance of the coil, which depends on the angular position $\theta$ of the moving iron vane.
- $i$ is the instantaneous current through the coil.

### 3.1.3 Application of the Conservation of Energy

Suppose the moving vane rotates through a small angle $d\theta$ under the influence of the deflecting torque $T_d$. The work done by the deflecting torque is:

$$
dW_{mech} = T_d \, d\theta
$$

Simultaneously, the source supplies electrical energy to maintain the current $i$. By the **principle of virtual work** applied to a conservative magnetic system:

$$
\text{Energy supplied by source} = \text{Increase in field energy} + \text{Mechanical work done}
$$

At constant current (a fair assumption for the instantaneous analysis), the source supplies additional energy equal to the mechanical work done *plus* the change in stored magnetic energy. Therefore:

$$
T_d \, d\theta = dW_{field} = d\left[\dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2\right] \bigg|_{i=\text{const}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Differentiation Step

Differentiating $W$ with respect to $\theta$ at constant $i$:

$$
T_d = \dfrac{\partial W}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i=\text{const}} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
$$

This is the **fundamental deflecting torque equation** for any moving iron instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Comprehension Note:** This single equation explains *everything* about MI instruments. The torque depends on the square of the current, the rate of change of inductance with deflection angle, and that is why the scale is non-uniform.

### 3.1.5 Equilibrium Condition

At steady state, the deflecting torque is balanced by the control torque provided by the spring:

$$
T_d = T_c
$$

The control torque from a spiral spring is linear in deflection:

$$
T_c = K \, \theta
$$

where $K$ is the spring constant in N·m/rad.

### 3.1.6 Final Deflection Angle

Equating both torques:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = K \, \theta
$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} \, i^2 \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-variable dependence:** Note that $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ itself is a function of $\theta$, which is why the scale is not strictly parabolic — it deviates from a pure square-law response.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Operating Quantity | Expression | Direction | Magnitude Dependence | Scale Character |
|:-------------------|:-----------|:----------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| Deflecting torque $T_d$ | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2}i^2\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Always positive (non-directional) | $\propto i^2$ | Non-uniform (square law) |
| Control torque $T_c$ | $T_c = K\theta$ | Opposes $T_d$ | $\propto \theta$ | Linear |
| Pointer deflection $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{i^2}{2K}\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | One-way | $\propto i^2$ | Squared, non-uniform |
| Force on vane (attraction) | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Toward higher flux | $\propto i^2$ | Squared |
| Repulsion force (radial) | $F_r = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}\left(\mu_r - 1\right)$ | Radial outward | $\propto i^2$ | Squared |

## 3.3 Numerical Sanity Check — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU typical):**
> An attraction-type MI voltmeter has a coil of $\mathbf{200}$ turns and a coil resistance of $\mathbf{200 \ \Omega}$. A series multiplier resistance of $\mathbf{49{,}800 \ \Omega}$ is connected to extend the range. If the meter reads full-scale at $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ and the spring constant is $K = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m/rad, find the rate of change of inductance with deflection.

**Solution:**

Full-scale current through the meter movement:

$$
I_{fs} = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{250}{200 + 49800} = \dfrac{250}{50000} = 0.005 \text{ A} = 5 \text{ mA}
$$

Using the equilibrium equation:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \implies \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = \dfrac{2K \theta}{i^2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = \dfrac{2 \times (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times (\pi/2)}{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = \dfrac{8\pi \times 10^{-6}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{8\pi}{25} \ \text{H/rad}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \approx \boxed{1.005 \ \text{H/rad}}
$$

This value is consistent with practical MI instrument design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an MI Instrument

Below is a high-level block diagram showing how the moving iron instrument functions as a complete system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal Current or Voltage] --> B[Operating Coil]
    B --> C[Magnetic Flux Generation phi]
    C --> D[Soft Iron Vane Magnetization]
    D --> E[Mechanical Force Attraction or Repulsion]
    E --> F[Deflecting Torque Td]
    F --> G[Pointer Rotation theta]
    G --> H[Scale Reading Display]
    I[Control Spring] --> J[Control Torque Tc]
    J --> H
    K[Damping Chamber] --> G
    G --> K
    F --> L{Td equals Tc}
    J --> L
    L -->|Yes| H
    L -->|No| G
    M[Energy Balance Equation] --> F
    C --> M
    N[Inductance L of theta] --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Attraction vs Repulsion Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Moving Iron Instrument] --> Q[Attraction Type]
    P --> R[Repulsion Type]
    Q --> Q1[Single Soft Iron Vane]
    Q --> Q2[Eccentric Mounting on Spindle]
    Q --> Q3[Pulled Toward Coil Center]
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q3
    R --> R1[Two Iron Vanes Fixed and Movable]
    R --> R2[Both Magnetized Same Polarity]
    R --> R3[Vanes Repel Each Other]
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Torque Generation and Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Current i flows in coil] --> mag[Flux phi produced in coil]
    mag --> induc[Inductance L varies with theta]
    induc --> energy[Magnetic Energy W equals half L times i squared]
    energy --> diff[Differentiate W wrt theta]
    diff --> td[Deflecting Torque Td equals half i squared dL by d theta]
    td --> force[Magnetic Force acts on iron vane]
    force --> motion[Pointer deflects by angle theta]
    motion --> spring[Spiral spring gets twisted]
    spring --> tc[Control Torque Tc equals K times theta]
    tc --> eq{Equilibrium Td equals Tc}
    eq -->|Steady State| final[Pointer stops at theta proportional to i squared]
    eq -->|Transient| motion
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Reading Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC or DC Input] --> B[Heating and RMS Effect]
    B --> C[i squared proportional to I rms squared]
    C --> D[Torque proportional to i squared]
    D --> E[Pointer Deflection theta proportional to I rms squared]
    E --> F[Scale Calibrated to Read RMS Value Directly]
    F --> G[Final Reading in Volts or Amps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All the above Mermaid diagrams are designed to be *block-level functional architecture flows* rather than physical drawings. This adaptation follows the fallback rule specified in the system protocol for topics requiring complex physical layout representation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define a moving iron instrument. Explain why it can be used for both AC and DC measurements.

**Model Answer:**

A Moving Iron (MI) instrument is an electrical measuring instrument whose operating principle is based on the magnetic effect of current — specifically, the force of attraction or repulsion between a piece of soft iron and a current-carrying coil.

It can be used for both AC and DC because the deflecting torque is proportional to the **square of the current** ($T_d \propto i^2$). Since $i^2$ is always positive irrespective of the direction of current flow, the deflection direction is independent of polarity. For AC, the instrument responds to the **RMS value** because the mechanical inertia averages out the instantaneous squared current over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention the squaring effect and RMS averaging. That alone fetches 2 out of 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** Compare attraction-type and repulsion-type moving iron instruments.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Attraction Type | Repulsion Type |
|:--------|:----------------|:---------------|
| Number of vanes | One (movable) | Two (one fixed, one movable) |
| Force mechanism | Iron pulled toward coil | Two vanes repel each other |
| Scale uniformity | More non-uniform | Relatively better uniformity |
| Range | Smaller | Larger (suitable for panels) |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Common use | Small portable meters | Industrial switchboards |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Q(a)** Derive the deflecting torque equation for a moving iron instrument. Explain how the deflecting torque depends on the operating current. (7 Marks)

**Q(b)** A moving iron voltmeter is designed to read up to 250 V when connected across a supply. The coil has a resistance of 500 Ω and an inductance of 0.5 H. A series multiplier of 49,500 Ω is used. Calculate: (i) the current at full-scale deflection, and (ii) the operating torque if the spring constant is $K = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m/rad and $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = 1.2$ H/rad. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Step 1 — Statement of the principle:**
The deflecting torque is obtained using the principle of conservation of energy applied to the magnetic system of the instrument.

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the magnetic field:**

$$
W = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical work done for small rotation $d\theta$:**

$$
dW_{mech} = T_d \, d\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Equating energy terms at constant current:**

$$
T_d \, d\theta = d\left[\dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2\right] = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL
$$

**Step 5 — Final deflecting torque:**

$$
\boxed{T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}}
$$

**Step 6 — Dependence on current:**
Since $T_d \propto i^2$, the torque increases with the square of the current. The deflection direction does not depend on the current direction — this is why the instrument is suitable for both AC and DC.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Step 1 — Total circuit resistance:**

$$
R_{total} = 500 + 49500 = 50000 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Full-scale current:**

$$
I_{fs} = \dfrac{V}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{250}{50000} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 5 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Operating torque calculation:**

$$
T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 1.2
$$

$$
T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2 = 15 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_d = 15 \ \mu\text{N·m}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a repulsion-type moving iron instrument. Why is it preferred for switchboard applications? (7 Marks)

**Q(b)** A moving iron ammeter has a full-scale deflection of 100 mA. The spring constant is $5 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m/rad. If $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = 0.8$ H/rad at the operating point, calculate the deflection angle at 50 mA and at 100 mA. Comment on the scale uniformity. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Construction:**
A repulsion-type MI instrument consists of:
- A cylindrical coil wound on a non-magnetic former.
- A *fixed* soft-iron vane mounted on the coil's inner wall.
- A *movable* soft-iron vane attached to the spindle.
- A spiral control spring, air-damping chamber, and pointer with scale.

**Working:**
When current $i$ passes through the coil, both vanes get magnetized in the same direction (similar to the field direction). Since like poles face each other, a repulsive force pushes the movable vane away from the fixed vane. The spindle rotates, producing pointer deflection proportional to $i^2$.

**Why preferred for switchboards:**
- More uniform scale than attraction type.
- Higher sensitivity and longer scale length.
- Robust construction suitable for industrial environments.
- Better magnetic shielding and stable zero.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Formula:**

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1}{2K} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}
$$

**At $i = 50$ mA $= 0.05$ A:**

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} \times (0.05)^2 \times 0.8
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{0.08 \times 0.05^2}{10^{-5}} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 25 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-5}} = 20 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(reduced to practical scale)}
$$

Working in proportional terms (relative to full scale):

$$
\theta_{50} = \left(\dfrac{50}{100}\right)^2 \times \theta_{100} = 0.25 \times \theta_{100}
$$

**At $i = 100$ mA:**

$$
\theta_{100} = \left(\dfrac{100}{100}\right)^2 \times \theta_{100} = 1 \times \theta_{100} = \theta_{100} \text{ (full scale)}
$$

**Scale Uniformity Comment:**
Since deflection is proportional to $i^2$, at half current the deflection is only **one-fourth** of full scale. This causes the lower portion of the scale to be **cramped (compressed)** and the upper portion to be **expanded**, making the scale non-uniform. This is a fundamental disadvantage of all MI instruments.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\dfrac{1}{2}$ factor in the torque equation.** Many students write $T_d = i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$, losing 1 to 2 marks immediately. The correct form is $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
>
