> 2. **Confusing AC and DC behavior.** Students often claim MI instruments cannot measure DC. This is wrong — they measure both. What they *cannot* measure is the polarity of DC, but they will give a positive deflection regardless.
>
> 3. **Not stating units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units like "N·m", "A", or "rad".
>
> 4. **Using $i$ instead of $I_{rms}$ when discussing AC measurements.** Always clarify that for sinusoidal AC, $T_d \propto I_{rms}^2$ (the time-averaged effect).
>
> 5. **Skipping the assumption section in derivations.** Examiners expect a one-line statement of "constant current" and "negligible losses" before the derivation begins. Missing this costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Writing "non-linear" when "non-uniform scale" is meant.** These are different concepts. Be precise in vocabulary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Moving Iron Instruments:**
>
> - MI instruments work on the **force of attraction or repulsion** between magnetized soft iron and a magnetic field.
> - There are **two main types**: *Attraction (single vane)* and *Repulsion (double vane)*.
> - The **deflecting torque equation** is: $T_d = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
> - The **control torque equation** is: $T_c = K \theta$.
> - At equilibrium: $T_d = T_c \implies \theta = \dfrac{i^2}{2K} \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
> - The **scale is non-uniform** (square-law) — compressed at the low end and expanded at the high end.
> - MI instruments can measure **both AC and DC** because deflection $\propto i^2$ is independent of current direction.
> - For AC, the deflection is proportional to the **RMS value** of current ($I_{rms}^2$).
> - Major **errors** come from: hysteresis, eddy currents, frequency variations, and temperature changes.
> - **Repulsion type** is preferred for industrial switchboards due to better scale uniformity and robust construction.
> - **Frequency range:** Generally up to 125 Hz for standard models; up to 1 kHz with special design.
> - **Advantages:** Cheap, robust, AC/DC dual use, simple construction.
> - **Disadvantages:** Non-uniform scale, hysteresis errors, frequency errors, lower accuracy than PMMC.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Moving Iron Instruments**
> **Coverage:** Definition, classification, attraction & repulsion types, principles, and torque equation derivation.
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (OEICT722) Examination Standards.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wattmeters: Dynamometer Type Wattmeter, Principles and Torque Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wattmeters: Dynamometer Type Wattmeter, Principles and Torque Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Dynamometer Type Wattmeter** (also called an **Electrodynamometer Wattmeter**) is a direct-reading, power-measuring instrument that operates on the **electrodynamic principle** — the same principle used in dynamometer-type ammeters and voltmeters. It measures the **average active power** dissipated in an electrical load, in both **DC and AC circuits**, by exploiting the electromagnetic force produced between a pair of **current-carrying coils**.

In its standard form, it contains:
- A pair of **Fixed Coils (Current Coils / CC)** connected in series with the load.
- A **Moving Coil (Potential Coil / PC or Pressure Coil)** connected across the load terminals.
- **Spiral Control Springs** providing the restoring (controlling) torque.
- An **Air Friction Damping Chamber**.
- A **Pointer-Scale Assembly** for deflection indication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A dynamometer wattmeter is a *transfer-type* (square-law) instrument whose **deflecting torque is proportional to the instantaneous product of the currents in the two coils**, and whose **average deflection is proportional to the true RMS active power** in the load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two bar magnets that you can switch ON and OFF by passing electricity through them.

- The **first magnet (Fixed Coil)** represents the *load current* — how much current the device under test is pulling.
- The **second magnet (Moving Coil)** represents the *voltage* across the load — how hard the supply is "pushing" the current.
- The **twisting force (torque)** between these two magnets exists only when **BOTH are switched on simultaneously**.

If the current fluctuates, the force fluctuates with it. But the meter spring stores the *time-averaged* twist. So the needle stops at a position that depends on:
- **How much current flowed** (I),
- **How big the voltage was** (V),
- **How well-timed they were** (the phase angle φ — measured separately by power factor).

This *time-averaged twist* is **Power** (P = VI cos φ). The spring is calibrated so the needle lands directly on the wattage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two coils and not one?**
> A single coil can only sense current (Ammeter) or voltage (Voltmeter). Power is a *product* of V and I, so we need **two coils interacting** to physically multiply the two quantities. The electrodynamic force between them is intrinsically a *multiplicative* quantity — perfect for power.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standards Highlighted

- **Standard commercial supply frequencies:** **50 Hz** (India) and **60 Hz** (USA/Japan).
- **Magnetic constant (permeability of free space):** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$.
- **Boltzmann's constant** (for noise floor in precision wattmeters): $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$.
- **Reference temperature for accuracy class:** **27 °C** (as per IS 1248 / IEC 60051).
- **Standard power frequencies for laboratory wattmeters:** **50 Hz – 1 kHz** (for high-precision grade instruments, bandwidth extends to a few kHz).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "state the principle," always write both the **instrument principle** (electrodynamic) and the **measurement principle** (force between two coils is proportional to product of currents → average proportional to power). This guarantees full marks in 2-mark short questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction — Detailed Breakdown

| Component | Function | Construction Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Coils (CC)** | Carry the load current | Two identical sections wound on a non-magnetic former (to avoid hysteresis); placed parallel and close to each other to create a uniform field in the central region |
| **Moving Coil (PC)** | Carries a current proportional to load voltage | Wound on a non-magnetic former, mounted on a spindle; positioned in the air gap between the fixed coils |
| **Spiral Springs** | Provide controlling torque & lead-in wires for moving coil | Phosphor-bronze; two springs wound in opposite directions to neutralize temperature effects |
| **Damping System** | Provides quick, oscillation-free settling | Air-friction type (piston in a chamber) — eddy current damping is avoided because it would distort the operating field |
| **Pointer and Scale** | Indication | Knife-edge pointer; scale calibrated directly in watts |
| **Shielding** | Protection from stray fields | Aluminium or soft-iron enclosure (sometimes double-shielded in precision instruments) |

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Current Sensing:** Load current $I_L$ flows through the fixed coils (CC), producing a magnetic field $B_f$ proportional to $I_L$ in the air gap.
2. **Voltage Sensing:** A current $i_p = v_L / Z_p$ flows through the moving coil (PC), where $Z_p$ is the impedance of the potential coil circuit.
3. **Electrodynamic Force:** The moving coil, carrying $i_p$ and immersed in $B_f$, experiences a force $F \propto B_f \cdot i_p \propto i_L \cdot i_p$.
4. **Torque Generation:** The force produces a **deflecting torque** $T_d$ on the spindle, given by:

   $$T_d = i_L \cdot i_p \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

   where $M$ is the mutual inductance between the fixed and moving coils, and $\theta$ is the angular displacement.
5. **Spring Opposition:** The spiral springs produce a **controlling torque** $T_c = K \theta$, where $K$ is the spring constant.
6. **Damping:** Air friction dissipates kinetic energy; the pointer settles at the equilibrium angle.
7. **Equilibrium:** At steady state, $T_d = T_c$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board tested** — they cover ~95% of numerical and derivation questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deflecting torque (instantaneous) | $T_d(t) = i_1(t) \, i_2(t) \, \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m | $i_1$ = current in CC, $i_2$ = current in PC |
| 2 | Average deflecting torque (DC) | $T_d = \dfrac{I_1 \, I_2}{R_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m | $I_2 = V/R_p$ for DC; $R_p$ = PC resistance |
| 3 | Average deflecting torque (AC) | $T_d = \dfrac{V \, I \cos\phi}{Z_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m | $Z_p$ = PC impedance (resistance + inductance) |
| 4 | Controlling torque | $T_c = K \theta$ | N·m | $K$ = spring constant (N·m/rad) |
| 5 | Steady-state deflection (AC) | $\theta = \dfrac{V I \cos\phi}{K Z_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | rad | **Master Power Equation** |
| 6 | Steady-state deflection (DC) | $\theta = \dfrac{V I}{K R_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | rad | DC form |
| 7 | Power reading | $P = \dfrac{K Z_p}{\,dM/d\theta\,} \cdot \theta$ | W | Final calibrated result |
| 8 | Condition for uniform scale | $\dfrac{dM}{d\theta} = \text{constant}$ | H/rad | Achieved by proper coil geometry |
| 9 | Multiplier resistor (for V-range extension) | $R_s = R_m \left( \dfrac{V}{V_m} - 1 \right)$ | Ω | $R_m$ = PC resistance, $V_m$ = basic voltage range |
| 10 | Shunt resistor (for I-range extension) | $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m \cdot I_m}{I - I_m}$ | Ω | For higher current ranges |
| 11 | Phase error due to PC inductance | $\tan \epsilon = \dfrac{\omega L_p}{R_p}$ | rad | Small angle approximation: $\epsilon \approx \omega L_p / R_p$ |
| 12 | True power vs indicated power | $P_{true} = P_{ind} \cos \epsilon \approx P_{ind} (1 - \epsilon^2/2)$ | W | For non-inductive PC, $\epsilon \to 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the dynamometer wattmeter called a "true RMS" power meter?**
> Because its deflection depends on $\cos\phi$ (the true phase relationship) and the RMS values of V and I, **not** on waveform shape. Unlike average-responding meters, it correctly measures power from distorted, non-sinusoidal waveforms — provided the bandwidth is sufficient.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-station switchboards:** Class 0.2 / 0.5 dynamometer wattmeters are used as secondary standards to calibrate electronic power analyzers.
- **Transformer & motor testing labs:** Measure copper loss ($I^2R$), iron loss, and total input power under loaded conditions.
- **Transmission-line monitoring:** With phase-shifting transformers, they double as **standard power comparators** for calibrating energy meters (kWh).
- **Audio engineering (historical):** Electrodynamic movement is the heart of the **moving-coil loudspeaker** — the same force law (F ∝ BI × l) is at work.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deflecting Torque in a Dynamometer Wattmeter

> **Goal:** Derive the expression for the average deflecting torque in a dynamometer type wattmeter when connected in an AC circuit with phase angle $\phi$ between voltage and current.

**Given / Assumptions:**

- $i_1(t)$ = instantaneous current in the fixed (current) coil = $I_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ (since CC carries the full load current, $i_1 = i_L$).
- $i_2(t)$ = instantaneous current in the moving (potential) coil = $I_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ (since PC current lags/supplies voltage by phase angle $\phi$).
- $M$ = mutual inductance between the fixed and moving coils at angular position $\theta$.
- $\theta$ = angular position of the moving coil (measured from the reference position).
- $Z_p$ = total impedance of the potential coil branch (resistance $R_p$ + inductive reactance $\omega L_p$).
- $V$ = RMS voltage across the load (and hence across the potential coil).
- $I$ = RMS load current.

**Step 1 — General expression for electrodynamic torque**

The instantaneous electromagnetic force between two current-carrying coils is governed by the change of co-energy $W'$ with respect to displacement. The co-energy is $W' = M(\theta) \cdot i_1 \cdot i_2$. The torque (about the spindle axis) is:

$$T_d(t) = \frac{\partial W'}{\partial \theta} = i_1(t) \, i_2(t) \, \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

> This is the **fundamental force equation of all electrodynamic instruments**. It is *not* an assumption; it follows directly from the co-energy method of energy conversion.

**Step 2 — Substitute sinusoidal expressions**

$$T_d(t) = \left[ I_{1m} \sin(\omega t) \right] \cdot \left[ I_{2m} \sin(\omega t - \phi) \right] \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

$$T_d(t) = I_{1m} \, I_{2m} \, \sin(\omega t) \, \sin(\omega t - \phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity**

Using $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[ \cos(A - B) - \cos(A + B) \right]$:

$$T_d(t) = \frac{I_{1m} I_{2m}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \left[ \cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time-averaged torque over one full cycle**

The pointer cannot follow the high-frequency ripple ($\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ term), so we integrate over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T_d(t) \, dt$$

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{I_{1m} I_{2m}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \left[ \cos(\phi) - \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\, dt \right]$$

The integral of a cosine over a complete number of cycles is **zero**:

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\, dt = 0$$

Therefore:

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{I_{1m} I_{2m}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Convert peak values to RMS values**

Using $I_{1m} = I_1 \sqrt{2}$ and $I_{2m} = I_2 \sqrt{2}$:

$$I_{1m} I_{2m} = I_1 \sqrt{2} \cdot I_2 \sqrt{2} = 2 I_1 I_2$$

Substituting:

$$T_{d,avg} = \frac{(2 I_1 I_2)}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{\,T_d = I_1 I_2 \cos(\phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Replace $I_1$ and $I_2$ in terms of V, I, and $Z_p$**

- $I_1$ = RMS current in CC = load current $I$ (since CC is in series with the load).
- $I_2$ = RMS current in PC = $V / Z_p$ (Ohm's law for the potential coil branch).

Therefore:

$$T_d = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot \cos(\phi)}{Z_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 7 — Apply equilibrium with controlling torque**

For steady deflection, $T_d = T_c$:

$$\frac{V I \cos(\phi)}{Z_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} = K \theta$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\boxed{\,\theta = \frac{V I \cos(\phi)}{K \, Z_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the power expression**

Since $P = V I \cos(\phi)$ is the **average active power** dissipated in the load:

$$\boxed{\,\theta = \frac{P}{K \, Z_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** The angular deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional to the true average power $P$**, provided that $\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ is constant. This is achieved by carefully shaping the fixed coils so that the radial field is uniform across the working angular range.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Deflection Characteristics at a Glance

| Quantity | DC | Single-Phase AC (lagging p.f. $\cos\phi$) | Pure Capacitive Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| $i_1(t)$ | $I$ | $I_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ | $I_{1m} \sin(\omega t)$ |
| $i_2(t)$ | $V/R_p$ | $(V/Z_p) \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ | $(V/Z_p) \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ |
| Deflection $\theta$ | $\dfrac{V I}{K R_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $\dfrac{V I \cos\phi}{K Z_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $\dfrac{V I \cos\phi}{K Z_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ |
| Scale behavior | Uniform (if $dM/d\theta$ = const) | Uniform + p.f. dependence | Same as lagging case |
| Sign of deflection | Positive | Positive (consumes power) | Positive (still consumes power) |
| Reverse load | Negative (reverse terminals) | **Still positive** (p.f. cosφ is positive) | **Still positive** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** In a DC wattmeter, reversing either the CC or the PC connections reverses the deflection. In an AC wattmeter, **reversing the load terminals does NOT reverse the deflection** because $\cos\phi$ remains positive for both lagging and leading loads. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Compensation for Pressure-Coil Inductance (Lagging-Power-Factor Error)

**Problem statement:** In a real wattmeter, the potential coil has inductance $L_p$ in series with $R_p$. Thus, the current $I_2$ in the PC lags the voltage $V$ by a small angle $\epsilon$ (the *coil angle*):

$$\epsilon = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L_p}{R_p}\right)$$

The actual measured deflection becomes:

$$\theta \propto V I \cos(\phi + \epsilon)$$

The true value should be $VI\cos\phi$, so the wattmeter reads low for lagging loads (and high for leading loads).

**Compensation (Lag Adjustment):** Add a capacitor $C$ in parallel with a portion $r$ of the multiplier resistance $R_s$, so that the **PC branch becomes purely resistive** at the operating frequency. The condition is:

$$\omega L_p = \frac{r \cdot (R_s - r)}{R_s} \cdot \omega C \cdot \text{(network factor)}$$

In the simplest form, a capacitor $C$ across a part of $R_s$ cancels the inductive phase shift, making the effective PC branch a **pure resistance** at the calibration frequency.
