## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the principle of operation of a dynamometer type wattmeter. Why is it suitable for both AC and DC power measurement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The dynamometer type wattmeter works on the **electrodynamic principle**: when two coils carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ are placed such that one is fixed and the other is movable, an electromagnetic deflecting torque is produced which is proportional to the **product of the two currents**.

$$T_d \propto I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

For DC: $T_d \propto V \cdot I / R_p$ (which is power, scaled by a constant).
For AC: $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cos\phi / Z_p$ (true average power, scaled by a constant).

Hence, the **average deflection is proportional to true power** in both DC and AC circuits, including non-sinusoidal waveforms. This makes it a *true RMS power meter* and a universal wattmeter.

(3 marks — full: principle statement, formula, and universal applicability)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is air friction damping used in a dynamometer wattmeter and not eddy current damping?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a dynamometer wattmeter, the operating field itself is very weak (since there is no iron core to amplify the flux). If eddy current damping were used, the conducting disc required for damping would distort the operating field of the instrument and introduce serious reading errors.

Therefore, **air friction damping** is used because it is **independent of the operating magnetic field** and does not interfere with the measurement. The piston-in-chamber arrangement provides a damping torque proportional to the angular velocity of the pointer, allowing smooth and oscillation-free settling.

(3 marks — full: 1 mark for weak field, 1 mark for eddy current distortion, 1 mark for air-friction independence)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **deflecting torque** of a dynamometer type wattmeter when used in an AC circuit. State all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **construction and working** of a dynamometer type wattmeter with a neat diagram. Discuss the **sources of error** and their **compensation methods**. (7 Marks)

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Deflecting Torque (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The two coils are concentric and lie in mutually perpendicular planes.
2. The mutual inductance $M$ varies sinusoidally with $\theta$, so $dM/d\theta$ is a constant over the working range (uniform scale condition).
3. The pointer inertia is large enough that it cannot follow the 100 Hz ripple of $T_d(t)$; only the average is indicated.

**Derivation (6 marks):** Follow the **Step-by-Step Derivation in Section 3.1** above. The final result is:

$$T_d = I_1 \, I_2 \cos(\phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} = \frac{V I \cos\phi}{Z_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}$$

The student must show:
- $T_d(t) = i_1 i_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ (1 mark)
- Substitution of sinusoids (1 mark)
- Product-to-sum identity and integration (1 mark)
- Conversion to RMS values (1 mark)
- Replacement by $V$, $I$, $Z_p$ (1 mark)
- Final boxed expression and identification with $P = VI\cos\phi$ (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — Construction, Working, Errors, Compensation (7 Marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):** Present the same table as in Section 2.1. Stress on:
- **Fixed coils** in two sections, wound on non-magnetic former.
- **Moving coil** on spindle, suspended in the air gap.
- **Air-friction damping** (justify why not eddy current — refer Q2 above).
- **Spiral springs** for control and lead-in.
- **Aluminium shielding** to protect from stray fields.

**Working (1 mark):** Brief paragraph describing how the load current and voltage-scaled current interact to produce torque proportional to power.

**Sources of Error and Compensation (4 marks):**

| Error | Cause | Compensation |
|---|---|---|
| **PC inductance error** | $L_p$ causes PC current to lag voltage by $\epsilon = \tan^{-1}(\omega L_p / R_p)$ | Connect capacitor $C$ across a portion of multiplier $R_s$ to make PC branch purely resistive |
| **Mutual inductance error** | Magnetic coupling between CC and PC varies with load | Use fixed coils in two identical sections with PC mounted symmetrically between them |
| **Eddy current error** | Conducting metal parts near the coil | Use non-magnetic (wooden or aluminium) formers |
| **Stray field error** | External fields interfere with weak operating field | Soft-iron or double-shielded enclosure |
| **Temperature error** | Spring constant and coil resistance change with temperature | Phosphor-bronze springs with low temperature coefficient; compensating resistors |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** derive the torque equation in both DC and AC forms separately — that wastes time. First derive the **general form** $T_d = i_1 i_2 dM/d\theta$ and then state the AC average and DC forms as **sub-cases**. This is the standard KTU 2024 marking scheme.
> 2. **Do NOT** write "the wattmeter measures VI cos φ" without explaining that the meter responds to the **average** of the instantaneous product — the cos φ appears from time-averaging the trigonometric identity, not from the meter itself.
> 3. **Forgetting the air-friction damping justification** costs 1 mark. Examiners specifically look for the contrast with PMMC (which uses eddy current damping) — dynamometer does NOT, because it has no permanent magnet.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a **phasor diagram**, explain the **error introduced by pressure-coil inductance** in a dynamometer type wattmeter at low power factors. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A dynamometer wattmeter has a **current coil resistance of 0.5 Ω**, **pressure coil resistance of 5000 Ω**, and **pressure coil inductance of 5 mH**. It is connected in a circuit with a load of **10 A** at **200 V, 50 Hz** at a **power factor of 0.2 lagging**.
Calculate:
- (i) The percentage error due to pressure-coil inductance.
- (ii) The **true power**, if the meter reads **400 W**.
- (iii) The **compensating capacitance** required at 50 Hz. (7 Marks)

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Phasor Analysis of PC Inductance Error (7 Marks):**

Draw the phasor diagram showing:
- $V$ = supply voltage (reference phasor along the horizontal).
- $I_L$ = load current, lagging $V$ by $\phi$ (so true p.f. = $\cos\phi$).
- $I_p$ = pressure-coil current, ideally in phase with $V$, but in practice **lagging** $V$ by small angle $\epsilon$ due to $L_p$.
- $V_{Rp}$ = voltage drop across $R_p$ (in phase with $I_p$).
- $V_{L_p} = I_p \omega L_p$ (leading $I_p$ by 90°).
- $V = V_{Rp} + jV_{L_p}$.

The wattmeter indicates the **in-phase component** of $I_p$ with respect to $I_L$ (i.e., the projection of $I_p$ on $I_L$). The indicated power is:

$$P_{ind} = V I_L \cos(\phi + \epsilon)$$

The true power is $P_{true} = V I_L \cos\phi$. The error is most significant at **low power factors** (when $\phi$ is large), because the term $\cos(\phi + \epsilon)$ deviates from $\cos\phi$ more substantially. For leading power factor, the error reverses sign.

**Diagrammatic representation (3 marks):** Proper phasor diagram with $V$, $I_L$, $I_p$, angle $\phi$, and angle $\epsilon$ clearly marked.

**Analytical discussion (4 marks):** Explain the deviation and the compensation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_{cc} = 0.5\ \Omega$ (current coil resistance)
- $R_p = 5000\ \Omega$ (pressure coil resistance)
- $L_p = 5\ \text{mH} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$
- $I_L = 10\ \text{A}$
- $V = 200\ \text{V}$
- $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$
- $\cos\phi = 0.2$ (lagging) → $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.2) = 78.463°$
- $P_{ind} = 400\ \text{W}$

**Step 1 — Compute pressure-coil impedance $Z_p$:**

$$X_{L_p} = 2 \pi f L_p = 2 \pi (50)(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.5708\ \Omega$$

$$Z_p = \sqrt{R_p^2 + X_{L_p}^2} = \sqrt{(5000)^2 + (1.5708)^2}$$

$$Z_p \approx \sqrt{25{,}000{,}000 + 2.467} \approx \sqrt{25{,}000{,}002.47}$$

$$Z_p \approx 5000.000247\ \Omega$$

Since $X_{L_p} \ll R_p$, we can use the approximation $Z_p \approx R_p$ for power calculations, but the **phase shift** $\epsilon$ is significant for error analysis.

**Step 2 — Compute the phase error $\epsilon$:**

$$\tan \epsilon = \frac{X_{L_p}}{R_p} = \frac{1.5708}{5000} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\epsilon = \tan^{-1}(3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad} \approx 0.018°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage error:**

The true power is $P_{true} = V I_L \cos\phi = 200 \times 10 \times 0.2 = 400\ \text{W}$.

The indicated power is $P_{ind} = V I_L \cos(\phi + \epsilon)$:

$$\cos(\phi + \epsilon) = \cos\phi \cos\epsilon - \sin\phi \sin\epsilon$$

$$\cos(78.463° + 0.018°) \approx \cos(78.481°)$$

Using small-angle approximation $\cos(\phi + \epsilon) \approx \cos\phi - \epsilon \sin\phi$ (for small $\epsilon$ in radians):

$$P_{ind} \approx V I_L (\cos\phi - \epsilon \sin\phi)$$

$$P_{ind} \approx 400 - 200 \times 10 \times (3.1416 \times 10^{-4}) \times \sin(78.463°)$$

$$P_{ind} \approx 400 - 2000 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.9798$$

$$P_{ind} \approx 400 - 2000 \times 3.0784 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P_{ind} \approx 400 - 0.6157 \approx 399.38\ \text{W}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{P_{ind} - P_{true}}{P_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{399.38 - 400}{400} \times 100 = -0.154\%$$

(The negative sign indicates the meter reads *low* for lagging power factor — consistent with theory.)

**Step 4 — True power if meter reads 400 W:**

If $P_{ind} = 400\ \text{W}$, then using the inverse correction:

$$P_{true} = \frac{P_{ind}}{\cos(\phi + \epsilon)} \cdot \cos\phi = \frac{400}{0.99923} \times 0.2$$

$$P_{true} \approx 400.308\ \text{W}$$

(The true power is slightly higher than the indicated value at lagging p.f.)

**Step 5 — Compensating capacitance:**

For perfect compensation, the PC branch must be **purely resistive** at 50 Hz. The compensation is achieved by placing a capacitor $C$ across a portion $r$ of the multiplier $R_s$. In a simplified scenario, the compensating capacitor in parallel with the entire PC branch must satisfy:

$$\omega C = \frac{\omega L_p}{R_p^2 + (\omega L_p)^2} \cdot \text{(network factor)}$$

If the capacitor $C$ is placed **in parallel with the pressure coil branch** (the most common configuration in textbook problems), the resonance condition is:

$$\omega^2 L_p C \approx 1 \quad \text{(approximate, since } R_p \gg X_{L_p}\text{)}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{\omega^2 L_p} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 50)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{(314.159)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{98696 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{493.48}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 2.026\ \text{mF} = 2026\ \mu\text{F}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (KTU Board Examiner's Note):**
> 1. **Sign of the error:** The meter reads *low* for **lagging** p.f. and *high* for **leading** p.f. — students often get this backwards.
> 2. **Approximation:** $Z_p \neq R_p$ when computing $\epsilon$. Use $R_p$ for magnitude, but the phase shift is $\epsilon = \tan^{-1}(\omega L_p / R_p)$ — both are needed.
> 3. **Compensation capacitor placement:** If the question says "across a portion of the multiplier," the formula is different. The simple $C = 1/(\omega^2 L_p)$ formula applies only when the capacitor is **in parallel with the entire potential coil branch**, not across a partial resistance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Principle:** Electrodynamic — torque proportional to product of currents in two coils.
- [x] **Master torque equation:** $T_d = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$
- [x] **Master power relation:** $\theta = \dfrac{P}{K Z_p} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ (proves linear scale if $dM/d\theta$ is constant).
- [x] **Coil types:** Fixed (CC) in series with load; Moving (PC) across load with multiplier $R_s$.
- [x] **Damping:** Air-friction (NOT eddy current) — because the operating field is weak and easily disturbed.
- [x] **Universal usage:** Works for DC and AC, including non-sinusoidal waveforms (true RMS).
- [x] **Reverse-load test:** DC meter reverses; AC meter does NOT (because $\cos\phi$ is positive for both lagging and leading).
- [x] **Sources of error:** PC inductance, mutual inductance, eddy currents, stray fields, temperature, lead resistance.
- [x] **PC inductance compensation:** Capacitor $C$ across portion of $R_s$ to make the PC branch purely resistive.
- [x] **Phase error formula:** $\epsilon = \tan^{-1}(\omega L_p / R_p)$; small-angle: $\epsilon \approx \omega L_p / R_p$.
- [x] **Error direction:** Lagging p.f. → meter reads **low**; leading p.f. → meter reads **high**.
- [x] **Uniform scale condition:** $dM/d\theta = \text{constant}$ (achieved by careful fixed-coil shaping).
- [x] **Standard accuracy classes:** 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0 (per IS 1248 / IEC 60051).
- [x] **Multiplier resistor formula:** $R_s = R_m \left( \dfrac{V}{V_m} - 1 \right)$
- [x] **Shunt resistor formula:** $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_m I_m}{I - I_m}$
- [x] **Real-world use:** Switchboards, transformer & motor testing labs, energy-meter calibration.

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In every Part B (14-mark) question on dynamometer wattmeters, always present:
> 1. Construction (with diagram) — 3 marks
> 2. Torque derivation (general, then DC, then AC) — 5 marks
> 3. Working explanation — 2 marks
> 4. Errors and compensation — 4 marks
>
> This is the **canonical KTU marking pattern**. Stick to it for guaranteed full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Single Phase and Three Phase Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Single Phase and Three Phase Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Measurement of Single Phase and Three Phase Power

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power (P)** in an electrical circuit is defined as the rate at which electrical energy is transferred or dissipated per unit time. In an AC system, since voltage and current are sinusoidal and may be out of phase, the **instantaneous power** varies with time, and the **average (real) power** delivered over one complete cycle is what is physically meaningful for billing and engineering analysis.

Mathematically, for a single-phase AC circuit with voltage $v(t)$ and current $i(t)$:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

The **average (real) power** consumed by a load over one full cycle $T$ is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

For sinusoidal steady-state operation, this reduces to the well-known KTU fundamental:

$$P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$$

where $\phi$ is the **phase angle** between voltage and current, and $\cos\phi$ is the **power factor (pf)** of the load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Water Pipe System**
> Imagine water flowing through a hose:
> - **Voltage (V)** → Water pressure pushing water through the pipe
> - **Current (I)** → Actual flow rate of water (volume per second)
> - **Power (P)** → Useful work done (water turning a turbine wheel)
> - **Phase angle ($\phi$)** → The tilt of the water wheel — if perfectly aligned, all water energy converts to work. If misaligned (slanted), some water energy just splashes off and does no useful work.
> - **Power factor ($\cos\phi$)** → The *effectiveness* with which electrical energy is being converted to useful work. A pf of 1.0 means 100% efficient; a pf of 0.5 means half the energy is "wasted" reactive oscillation.

In a purely resistive load (heater, incandescent bulb), voltage and current are perfectly in step ($\phi = 0°$), and **all** the energy is converted to heat. In inductive loads (motors, transformers) or capacitive loads, voltage and current drift apart, and only the *in-phase component* of current contributes to real power.

## 1.3 Types of Power in AC Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Types of Power (The "Power Triangle")**
>
> | Type | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
> |------|--------|---------|------------------|
> | **Real (Active) Power** | **P** | $VI\cos\phi$ | Actual work done (kW) |
> | **Reactive Power** | **Q** | $VI\sin\phi$ | Energy oscillating between source and load (kVAR) |
> | **Apparent Power** | **S** | $VI$ | Total power supplied (kVA) |
>
> The relationship: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$

- **Real Power (P)** — measured in **watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**, this is the useful power that does actual work (heating, mechanical motion).
- **Reactive Power (Q)** — measured in **VAR (Volt-Ampere Reactive)** or **kVAR**, this power circulates between source and load and does no net work but is essential to maintain magnetic/electric fields.
- **Apparent Power (S)** — measured in **VA** or **kVA**, this is the product of RMS voltage and current (what the utility must supply in terms of current capacity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Measure Power?**
> - For **energy billing** (kWh consumed)
> - For **power quality monitoring**
> - For **load management** and **demand-side analysis**
> - For **determining power factor** for penalty/reward schemes

## 1.4 The Measurement Instrument: Electrodynamometer Wattmeter

For AC power measurement at commercial frequencies (50/60 Hz), the standard instrument is the **Electrodynamometer Wattmeter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An electrodynamometer wattmeter is a moving-coil instrument in which the deflecting torque is produced by the interaction of two magnetic fields: one set up by a **current-carrying fixed coil** (current coil) carrying the load current, and another set up by a **moving coil** (potential/voltage coil) carrying a current proportional to the load voltage.

This instrument can directly measure real power in AC because its deflection is proportional to the **instantaneous product** of the two currents, which, when averaged, gives the average power.

- **Operating Principle:** $T_{deflection} \propto i_1 \cdot i_2 \cos\alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is the angle between coil planes)
- **For sinusoidal AC:** $\alpha$ is the phase angle $\phi$ between V and I, so deflection $\propto VI\cos\phi = P$
- **Power factor of the wattmeter itself:** unity (unlike induction-type meters), making it suitable for accurate laboratory measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrodynamometer Wattmeter — Operating Theory

The electrodynamometer wattmeter consists of two coils:

1. **Fixed (Current) Coil (CC):** Connected in **series** with the load, carries the full load current $I_L$. It has very low impedance (few turns of thick wire).

2. **Moving (Potential/Pressure) Coil (PC):** Connected in **parallel** with the load, carries a current $I_P$ proportional to the load voltage $V_L$. It has high resistance (many turns of fine wire) in series with a multiplier resistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Construction Works for Power Measurement**
> - The magnetic field in the fixed coil is proportional to $i_1(t) = I_{max}\sin(\omega t)$.
> - The current in the moving coil is proportional to the load voltage: $i_2(t) = \frac{V_{max}}{R_{PC}}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$.
> - The instantaneous torque on the moving coil is proportional to $i_1 \cdot i_2 \propto v(t) \cdot i(t) = p(t)$.
> - The mechanical inertia of the moving system averages this torque over one cycle, giving a deflection proportional to the **average power**.

### Deflection Equation:

$$\theta = K \cdot V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi = K \cdot P_{real}$$

where $K$ is the meter constant (depends on spring constant, coil dimensions, and damping).

## 2.2 Errors in Electrodynamometer Wattmeters

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Sources of Error (High-Yield for KTU)**
>
> 1. **Error due to Connection (Pressure Coil Inductance):**
>    - If the pressure coil has residual inductance, the current $I_P$ lags the actual voltage $V$ by a small angle. This causes a systematic error, especially at low power factors.
>    - **Two Compensating Connections:**
>      - **(a) Voltage coil connected on the LOAD side:** Power measured = $P_{true} + I_P^2 R_L$ (over-reading)
>      - **(b) Voltage coil connected on the SUPPLY side:** Power measured = $P_{true} - V_P^2 / R_P$ (under-reading, used for high-current low-pf loads)
>
> 2. **Error due to Eddy Currents:** Induced in nearby metal parts, introduces phase shift in coil fields.
>
> 3. **Error due to Stray Magnetic Fields:** Since the operating field is weak, external fields can cause significant error (mitigated by an electrostatic shield).
>
> 4. **Temperature Error:** Affects spring stiffness and coil resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Single-Phase Power Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Formulas for Single-Phase AC Power**

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol/Unit |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Instantaneous Power | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | watts (W) |
| Average (Real) Power | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | **W (watts)** |
| Reactive Power | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ | **VAR** |
| Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ | **VA** |
| Power Factor | $pf = \cos\phi = P/S$ | dimensionless |
| Power Triangle | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | — |
| Wattmeter Reading | $\theta = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | divisions |

## 2.4 Three-Phase Power Measurement — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Standard Methods (Direct KTU Syllabus Topic)**
>
> 1. **Three-Wattmeter Method** (for 4-wire, unbalanced loads)
> 2. **Two-Wattmeter Method** (for 3-wire systems, balanced or unbalanced) — *MOST IMPORTANT*
> 3. **One-Wattmeter Method** (for balanced 3-phase loads only)

### Why Multiple Methods?

In a three-phase system, the **total instantaneous power is constant** (equal to average power) only when the load is balanced. For unbalanced loads, the instantaneous power varies with time, and multiple wattmeters are needed to capture the total correctly. **Blondel's Theorem** governs this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Blondel's Theorem (KTU Highlight)**
> *"The total power in an n-wire system can be measured using (n-1) wattmeters, provided the pressure (voltage) coils of all wattmeters are connected to a common point (the neutral, or an artificial neutral created for the purpose)."*
>
> - 2-wire system → 1 wattmeter
> - 3-wire system → 2 wattmeters
> - 4-wire system → 3 wattmeters (for unbalanced loads) — though 2 wattmeters may suffice for **balanced** 4-wire loads

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Three-Phase Power Measurement

| Method | Total Power Equation | Applicability |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| Three Wattmeter | $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3$ | 4-wire, any load (balanced/unbalanced) |
| Two Wattmeter | $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$ | 3-wire, any load |
| One Wattmeter (balanced) | $P_{total} = 3 \cdot W_1$ | 3-wire, balanced load only |
| Two-Wattmeter Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot (W_1 - W_2)$ | 3-wire, balanced load |
| Two-Wattmeter pf (balanced) | $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}$ | 3-wire, balanced load |
| Reactive Power (3 wattmeters) | $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot (W_1 + W_2 - 2W_3)$ | 4-wire, balanced load (with neutral current) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where This Knowledge Is Applied**
> - **Electricity billing** at industrial consumers (kWh meters based on these principles)
> - **Power quality audits** in factories
> - **Transformer testing** (open-circuit and short-circuit tests use wattmeters to measure iron and copper losses)
> - **Motor testing** and efficiency calculations
> - **Generator output monitoring** in power stations
> - **Substation metering** (CTs and PTs extend wattmeter concept to high-voltage/high-current systems)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Power in a Single-Phase AC Circuit

**Given:** 
- $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ (current lags voltage by phase angle $\phi$)

**Find:** Average power $P_{avg}$ over one complete cycle $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$

**Step 1:** Write the instantaneous power as the product:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 2:** Use the trigonometric identity $\sin A \cdot \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= V_m I_m \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(\omega t - (\omega t - \phi)) - \cos(\omega t + (\omega t - \phi))\right] \\
p(t) &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\left[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the average over one full period. The first term is a constant; the second term is a pure sinusoid whose average over a complete period is zero:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} p(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi \, dt - \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \phi) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each integral separately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi \cdot \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} dt - 0 \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi \cdot \frac{1}{T} \cdot T \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert peak values to RMS values using $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} I_{rms}}{2}\cos\phi \\
P_{avg} &= \frac{2 V_{rms} I_{rms}}{2}\cos\phi \\
\boxed{P_{avg} &= V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental AC power equation** — the cornerstone of all KTU power measurement problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Two-Wattmeter Method (3-Phase, 3-Wire)

**Setup:** Consider a 3-phase, 3-wire system with line voltages $V_{AB}$, $V_{BC}$, $V_{CA}$ and line currents $I_A$, $I_B$, $I_C$. Two wattmeters $W_1$ and $W_2$ are connected such that:
- $W_1$: current coil in line A, voltage coil between A and B
- $W_2$: current coil in line C, voltage coil between C and B

**Step 1:** Write the instantaneous readings of the two wattmeters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1(t) &= v_{AB}(t) \cdot i_A(t) \\
w_2(t) &= v_{CB}(t) \cdot i_C(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the instantaneous wattmeter readings:

$$w_1 + w_2 = v_{AB} i_A + v_{CB} i_C$$

**Step 3:** Express line voltages in terms of phase voltages (with $v_N$ as neutral potential, which is the artificial reference):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{AB} &= v_{AN} - v_{BN} \\
v_{CB} &= v_{CN} - v_{BN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 + w_2 &= (v_{AN} - v_{BN}) i_A + (v_{CN} - v_{BN}) i_C \\
&= v_{AN} i_A + v_{CN} i_C - v_{BN}(i_A + i_C)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply KCL at the load node. For a 3-wire system (no neutral wire), $i_A + i_B + i_C = 0$, so $i_A + i_C = -i_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 + w_2 &= v_{AN} i_A + v_{CN} i_C - v_{BN}(-i_B) \\
&= v_{AN} i_A + v_{BN} i_B + v_{CN} i_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the right-hand side as the total instantaneous 3-phase power:

$$\boxed{p_{total}(t) = v_{AN} i_A + v_{BN} i_B + v_{CN} i_C = w_1 + w_2}$$

**Step 7:** Taking the time-average, the **average (real) total power** is:

$$P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$$

This derivation shows that the two-wattmeter method works for **any load condition** (balanced or unbalanced) in a 3-wire system — this is the power of Blondel's theorem.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Factor from Two-Wattmeter Readings (Balanced Load)

**Given:** Balanced 3-phase load with phase angle $\phi$ between phase voltage and phase current.

**Step 1:** For a balanced load, let the phase sequence be ABC, with:
- $V_{AN} = V_p \angle 0°$
- $V_{BN} = V_p \angle -120°$
- $V_{CN} = V_p \angle +120°$
- $I_A = I_L \angle -\phi$
- $I_C = I_L \angle +120° - \phi$

**Step 2:** Wattmeter $W_1$ measures the product of $V_{AB}$ and $I_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{AB} &= V_{AN} - V_{BN} = \sqrt{3} V_p \angle 30° \\
I_A &= I_L \angle -\phi \\
W_1 &= \text{Re}\{V_{AB} \cdot I_A^*\} = \sqrt{3} V_p I_L \cos(30° + \phi) \\
W_1 &= V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

(using $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_p$ for a star-connected system)

**Step 3:** Wattmeter $W_2$ measures the product of $V_{CB}$ and $I_C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CB} &= V_{CN} - V_{BN} = \sqrt{3} V_p \angle 90° \\
I_C &= I_L \angle (120° - \phi) \\
W_2 &= \text{Re}\{V_{CB} \cdot I_C^*\} = \sqrt{3} V_p I_L \cos(90° - (120° - \phi)) \\
W_2 &= V_L I_L \cos(\phi - 30°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the wattmeter readings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 + W_2 &= V_L I_L [\cos(30° + \phi) + \cos(\phi - 30°)] \\
&= V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cos\phi \cos(30°) \\
&= V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\
\boxed{W_1 + W_2 &= \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi = P_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Difference the wattmeter readings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 - W_2 &= V_L I_L [\cos(30° + \phi) - \cos(\phi - 30°)] \\
&= V_L I_L \cdot (-2)\sin\phi \sin(30°) \\
&= -V_L I_L \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

In magnitude, $|W_1 - W_2| = V_L I_L \sin\phi = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{3}}$

**Step 6:** Take the ratio to obtain the power factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2} \\
\cos\phi &= \cos\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Important Reading Sign Rules (Two-Wattmeter Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Sign Convention**
>
> | Power Factor | $\phi$ | $W_1$ | $W_2$ | Total $P$ |
> |--------------|--------|-------|-------|-----------|
> | Unity (1.0) | 0° | Equal | Equal | $2W$ |
> | 0.5 Lagging | 60° | Positive | **Zero** | $W_1$ |
> | < 0.5 Lagging | > 60° | Positive | **Negative** | $W_1 - \mid W_2 \mid$ |
> | Unity Leading | 0° | Equal | Equal | $2W$ |
> | 0.5 Leading | -60° | **Zero** | Positive | $W_2$ |
> | < 0.5 Leading | < -60° | **Negative** | Positive | $W_2 - \mid W_1 \mid$ |
>
> **Key Tip:** One wattmeter may read **zero** at pf = 0.5, and read **negative** for pf < 0.5. This is the most commonly asked KTU concept!

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electrodynamometer Wattmeter — Internal Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FIXED["FIXED CURRENT COIL (CC)"]
        CC1["Coil 1: Carries Load Current I_L"]
        CC2["Coil 2: Carries Load Current I_L"]
    end
    subgraph MOVING["MOVING POTENTIAL COIL (PC)"]
        PC["Moving Coil: Carries Current I_P = V_L / R_P"]
        SP["Spiral Control Spring + Damping"]
    end
    subgraph POINTER["INDICATING SYSTEM"]
        PT["Pointer"]
        SC["Calibrated Scale (Watts)"]
    end
    FIXED -- Magnetic Field B1 --> MOVING
    MOVING -- Deflection Torque --> POINTER
    POINTER --> SC
```

**Explanation:** The fixed current coil produces a magnetic field proportional to the load current. The moving potential coil, carrying a current proportional to the load voltage, experiences a torque proportional to the product of the two fields. The pointer indicates real power in watts on a calibrated scale.

## 4.2 Single-Phase Wattmeter Connection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUP["AC Supply (V_L, f)"] -->|Line| T1["Terminal 1 of Wattmeter"]
    T1 --> CC["Current Coil (Fixed)"]
    CC --> T2["Terminal 2 of Wattmeter"]
    T2 --> LOAD["Load Z_L (R, L, C)"]
    LOAD --> NEUT["Neutral/Return"]
    T1 -.->|Tap| PC["Potential Coil (Moving)"]
    PC --> MP["Multiplier Resistor R_M"]
    MP --> NEUT
    PC --> MOV["Pointer + Spring Mechanism"]
```

**Explanation:** The current coil is in **series** with the load. The potential coil is connected **across** (in parallel with) the supply, with a high-value multiplier resistor in series to limit current.

## 4.3 Two-Wattmeter Method for 3-Phase Power Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R-Phase Supply"] --> WR1["W1 Current Coil"]
    WR1 --> LR["Load R"]
    LR --> RT["Return from R"]
    Y["Y-Phase Supply"] --> WY["(Reference / Common Point)"]
    B["B-Phase Supply"] --> WR2["W2 Current Coil"]
    WR2 --> LB["Load B"]
    LB --> BT["Return from B"]
    LR -.->|Phase R| WY
    LB -.->|Phase B| WY
    WR1 -. Voltage Coil .-> V1["V_RB (V_RY)"]
    WR2 -. Voltage Coil .-> V2["V_BY"]
    V1 --> WY
    V2 --> WY
    style WR1 fill:#f9e79f
    style WR2 fill:#aed6f1
```

**Explanation:** Two wattmeters are connected with their current coils in two of the three lines (here R and B). Both voltage coils are referenced to the third line (Y) — the common point. Per Blondel's theorem, the sum of the two wattmeter readings equals the total power.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Modern Digital Power Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V["Voltage Input (PT Step-Down)"] --> ISO_V["Isolation Amplifier"]
    I["Current Input (CT Step-Down)"] --> ISO_I["Isolation Amplifier"]
    ISO_V --> ADC1["ADC Channel 1"]
    ISO_I --> ADC2["ADC Channel 2"]
    ADC1 --> DSP["DSP/Microcontroller"]
    ADC2 --> DSP
    DSP --> MULT["Multiply V_sample x I_sample"]
    MULT --> AVG["Average over N cycles"]
    AVG --> DISP["Display: P, Q, S, pf"]
    DSP --> COMM["Communication: Modbus/RS485"]
```

**Explanation:** Modern digital wattmeters sample voltage and current at high frequency, compute the instantaneous product digitally, average it to obtain real power, and additionally compute reactive and apparent power using discrete Fourier transforms (DFT) or zero-crossing detection.

## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram — Methods Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD3P["3-Phase Load"] --> Q1{"Number of Wires?"}
    Q1 -->|4-Wire| M1["3-Wattmeter Method (Unbalanced)"]
    Q1 -->|4-Wire, Balanced| M1B["1 Wattmeter x3 OR 3-Wattmeter"]
    Q1 -->|3-Wire| Q2{"Load Balanced?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M2["2-Wattmeter Method (or 1-Wattmeter x 3)"]
    Q2 -->|No| M2
    M1 --> SUM1["P_total = W1 + W2 + W3"]
    M2 --> SUM2["P_total = W1 + W2"]
    M2B["1-Wattmeter x 3 Method"] --> SUM3["P_total = W1 + W2 + W3 (or 3 x W1 if balanced)"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]** `[3 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand]`
> 
> **Q:** *What is the principle of an electrodynamometer wattmeter? Why is it suitable for AC power measurement?*
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An electrodynamometer wattmeter operates on the principle that the **deflecting torque is produced by the interaction of two magnetic fields**: one created by a fixed current coil (carrying the load current) and the other by a moving potential coil (carrying a current proportional to the load voltage). The instantaneous torque is proportional to the product $i_1 \cdot i_2$, which is the instantaneous power $v(t) \cdot i(t)$. Due to the mechanical inertia of the moving system, the deflection indicates the **average value**, which is the real (active) power. It is suitable for AC measurement because its **scale is uniform** at the power frequency (no frequency-dependent error like in moving-iron instruments), and it has an inherently high accuracy class. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]** `[3 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply]`
> 
> **Q:** *In the two-wattmeter method applied to a balanced 3-phase load, one wattmeter reads zero. What is the power factor of the load? Justify.*
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> When $W_1 = 0$ in a balanced 3-phase system, applying the formula $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$:
> $$\cos(30° + \phi) = 0 \implies 30° + \phi = 90° \implies \phi = 60°$$
> Therefore, the power factor is:
> $$\cos\phi = \cos(60°) = 0.5$$
> The load has a **power factor of 0.5 (lagging)**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model ESE Question, Module 2]** `[14 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]`
> 
> **Question A:**
> 
> **(a)** With the help of a neat connection diagram, explain the **two-wattmeter method** for measurement of power in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Derive the expression for **total power**, **reactive power**, and **power factor** in terms of the two wattmeter readings for a balanced load. **[7 marks]**
> 
> **(b)** A balanced 3-phase, 3-wire load draws a line current of **20 A** at a line voltage of **400 V, 50 Hz**. Two wattmeters connected to measure total power give readings of **$W_1$ = 5 kW** and **$W_2$ = 2.5 kW**. Calculate:
> - (i) Total active power
> - (ii) Total reactive power
> - (iii) Power factor of the load
> - (iv) Phase impedance (assuming star connection)
> **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Connection Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   R ●─────[W1 CC]─────● Load R
                      │
                      │
   Y ●────────────────● (Common reference for V-coils)
                      │
                      │
   B ●─────[W2 CC]─────● Load B
   
   W1 Voltage Coil: between R and Y
   W2 Voltage Coil: between B and Y
```

**Derivation of Total Power (3 marks):**

The total instantaneous power is:
$$p(t) = v_{RN} i_R + v_{YN} i_Y + v_{BN} i_B$$

Using KCL: $i_R + i_Y + i_B = 0$, so $i_Y = -(i_R + i_B)$.

Substituting and simplifying (as derived in Section 3.2), the wattmeter readings give:

$$W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi) \quad \text{(or } 30° + \phi \text{ depending on phase sequence)}$$

$$W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$$

**Total Power (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi}$$

**Reactive Power (0.5 mark):**
$$\boxed{Q = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)}$$

**Power Factor (0.5 mark):**
$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 20$ A, $W_1 = 5000$ W, $W_2 = 2500$ W

**(i) Total Active Power (1.5 marks):**
$$P = W_1 + W_2 = 5000 + 2500 = 7500 \text{ W} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Total Reactive Power (1.5 marks):**
$$Q = \sqrt{3} (W_1 - W_2) = \sqrt{3} \times (5000 - 2500) = \sqrt{3} \times 2500 = 4330.13 \text{ VAR} \approx 4.33 \text{ kVAR}$$

**(iii) Power Factor (2 marks):**
$$\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{2500}{7500} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = 30°$$

$$\cos\phi = \cos(30°) = 0.866 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**(iv) Phase Impedance (Star Connection) (2 marks):**

Phase voltage:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

Impedance magnitude (line current = phase current in star):
$$Z_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{230.94}{20} = 11.547 \text{ }\Omega$$

Since $\phi = 30°$ (lagging, inductive), the impedance is:
$$Z_{ph} = 11.547 \angle 30° = R + jX_L$$

$$\boxed{R = 11.547 \cos(30°) = 10.0 \text{ }\Omega, \quad X_L = 11.547 \sin(30°) = 5.774 \text{ }\Omega}$$

$$L = \frac{X_L}{2\pi f} = \frac{5.774}{2\pi \times 50} = 18.38 \text{ mH}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model ESE Question, Module 2 — Internal Choice]** `[14 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply]`
> 
> **Question B:**
> 
> **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of an **electrodynamometer wattmeter**. Discuss the sources of error in this instrument. **[7 marks]**
> 
> **(b)** A single-phase load takes a current of **10 A** at a power factor of **0.6 lagging** when connected to a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. Calculate:
> - (i) Power consumed by the load
> - (ii) Reactive power
> - (iii) Apparent power
> - (iv) The readings of two wattmeters if the same load is connected in a 3-phase system using the two-wattmeter method (assume balanced load and star connection).
> **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. Key points:
- **Fixed (Current) Coil:** Two coils in series, wound with thick wire, low resistance, few turns. Connected in **series** with the load.
- **Moving (Potential) Coil:** Wound on a non-metallic former (to avoid eddy currents), pivoted between the fixed coils. High resistance, many turns. Connected in **parallel** with the load via a multiplier resistor.
- **Control System:** Spiral springs (also serve as current leads for the moving coil).
- **Damping:** Pneumatic (air-foil) damping, since eddy-current damping would interfere with the weak operating field.
- **Pointer and Scale:** Uniform scale calibrated directly in watts.

**Working (2 marks):** The instantaneous torque on the moving coil is:
$$T_i = K_1 \cdot i_1 i_2 \cos\alpha$$
where $i_1$ = current in fixed coil (load current), $i_2$ = current in moving coil (proportional to load voltage), $\alpha$ = angle between coil planes. For a properly designed wattmeter, $\alpha$ equals the phase angle between V and I, so the average torque becomes:
$$T_{avg} = K_2 \cdot VI \cos\phi = K_2 \cdot P$$

The spring provides a restoring torque $T_s = K_3 \cdot \theta$. At equilibrium: $\theta \propto P$.

**Sources of Error (2 marks):**
1. Pressure coil inductance (cause of phase angle error in low pf measurements)
2. Eddy currents in metal parts
3. Stray magnetic fields (instrument field is weak)
4. Temperature variation (changes spring constant and coil resistance)
5. Friction in pivots (especially for moving-coil at low torque)

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V = 230$ V, $I = 10$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.6$ lagging, $f = 50$ Hz

**(i) Power Consumed (1.5 marks):**
$$P = VI \cos\phi = 230 \times 10 \times 0.6 = 1380 \text{ W} = 1.38 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Reactive Power (1.5 marks):**
$$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.6^2} = 0.8$$
$$Q = VI \sin\phi = 230 \times 10 \times 0.8 = 1840 \text{ VAR} = 1.84 \text{ kVAR}$$

**(iii) Apparent Power (1 mark):**
$$S = VI = 230 \times 10 = 2300 \text{ VA} = 2.3 \text{ kVA}$$

**(iv) Two-Wattmeter Readings in Balanced 3-Phase (3 marks):**

For a balanced 3-phase star load with same per-phase values:
- Line voltage: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \times 230 = 398.37$ V (we use the same per-phase load, so $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$)
- Line current: $I_L = 10$ A (star connection)
- Total power = $3 \times 1380 = 4140$ W

Using the two-wattmeter formulas with $\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.6) = 53.13°$:

$$W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 398.37 \times 10 \times 0.6 = 4140 \text{ W}$$

$$W_1 - W_2 = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3 \times 1840}{\sqrt{3}} = \sqrt{3} \times 1840 = 3187 \text{ W}$$

Solving simultaneously:
$$W_1 = \frac{4140 + 3187}{2} = 3663.5 \text{ W}$$
$$W_2 = \frac{4140 - 3187}{2} = 476.5 \text{ W}$$

**Verification:** $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{3187}{4140} = 1.333 = \tan(53.13°)$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** In the two-wattmeter method at low power factors (pf < 0.5), one wattmeter may read **negative**. Students often forget to include the negative sign in $W_1 - W_2$ formula, costing 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Phase Sequence Assumption:** The formula $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}(W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$ gives the **magnitude** of $\phi$. Whether it is lagging or leading depends on which wattmeter is larger. Students often lose the "lagging/leading" identification.
>
> 3. **Connection Errors:** Students often incorrectly write $P = W_1 + W_2$ without specifying the wattmeter connection (R-Y, B-Y) — KTU expects the specific reference line to be identified.
>
> 4. **Blondel's Theorem Misapplication:** For a 4-wire unbalanced system, the two-wattmeter method gives **erroneous** results because $i_A + i_B + i_C \neq 0$ (KCL doesn't hold without the neutral current). Students often blindly apply 2-wattmeter formula to 4-wire systems.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kW and W, or using degrees vs radians in $\cos\phi$ calculation, costs 1-2 marks in numerical problems.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Derivation:** For 7-mark derivation questions, merely writing the final formula without showing the algebraic steps results in only 2-3 marks maximum. Always derive step-by-step using the aligned block format.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Single-Phase Power Fundamentals**
> - $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ (watts) — the most important equation
> - $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ (VAR) — reactive power
> - $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ (VA) — apparent power
> - Power triangle: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$
> - pf = $\cos\phi$, with $\phi = 0°$ (resistive) to $90°$ (purely reactive)
>
> ✅ **Electrodynamometer Wattmeter**
> - Two-coil system: Current Coil (CC) in series, Potential Coil (PC) in parallel
> - Deflection $\propto VI\cos\phi$ (true average power)
> - Best for AC, accurate at 50/60 Hz, uniform scale
> - Errors: pressure coil inductance, eddy currents, stray fields
> - Compensating connections: load-side vs supply-side for pressure coil
>
> ✅ **Two-Wattmeter Method (Most Important!)**
> - Used for 3-phase, 3-wire systems (balanced or unbalanced)
> - Both voltage coils reference a common line
> - $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2$
> - $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot (W_1 - W_2)$ (balanced)
> - $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{W_1 + W_2}$ (balanced)
> - At pf = 0.5, one wattmeter reads zero
> - At pf < 0.5, one wattmeter reads negative
>
> ✅ **Three-Wattmeter Method**
> - Used for 4-wire systems (or 3-wire unbalanced with artificial neutral)
> - $P_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3$
> - Each wattmeter measures power in one phase
>
> ✅ **One-Wattmeter Method**
> - Only for **balanced** 3-phase loads
> - Total power = 3 × wattmeter reading
> - pf = $\cos\phi$ obtained by reading V, I, W
>
> ✅ **Blondel's Theorem**
> - (n-1) wattmeters needed for n-wire system
> - All voltage coils must share a common reference point
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Substitutions to Memorize**
> - $\cos(30°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$
> - $\sin(30°) = 0.5$
> - $\cos(60°) = 0.5$
> - $\sin(60°) = \sqrt{3}/2$
> - $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True RMS Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** True RMS Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **True RMS (Root Mean Square) Meter** is an electronic measuring instrument designed to accurately determine the **effective (heating) value of an alternating current (AC) voltage or current**, irrespective of the waveform shape. Mathematically, it computes the square root of the time-averaged squared instantaneous value of the input signal.

The fundamental governing relation that every True RMS Meter implements is:

$$v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

where:
- $v(t)$ is the instantaneous input signal
- $T$ is the period of the waveform (or integration window for non-periodic signals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A True RMS Meter is fundamentally different from an *Average-Reading* or *Peak-Reading* meter. A True RMS meter gives correct readings for **sinusoidal, square, triangular, sawtooth, distorted, and harmonic-rich waveforms**, whereas ordinary meters assume a pure sine wave and apply a fixed form factor (1.11) — leading to large errors on non-sinusoidal signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Build-Up

Imagine you have a **kettle connected to an unknown AC source**. Suppose you want to know "how much effective heat" is being delivered to the water. The kettle doesn't care whether the voltage waveform is a smooth sine wave or a jagged, distorted wave — it only cares about the **total thermal energy** delivered per cycle.

A True RMS Meter does the exact same thing — it converts the AC signal into an equivalent **DC value that would produce the same heating effect in a resistor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "RMS" name itself is the recipe:**
> - **R** → **Root** (take the square root at the end)
> - **M** → **Mean** (average over time)
> - **S** → **Square** (square the instantaneous value first)

### Plain-English Intuitive Steps:
1. **Square** every tiny instant of the waveform — this makes all values positive and emphasizes peaks.
2. **Average** them over one complete cycle — this gives a single "mean power" number.
3. Take the **Square Root** — this brings the result back to a voltage/current magnitude.

The output is the *true* equivalent DC value that would dissipate identical power in a resistive load.

## 1.3 Why It Matters (Standard Metrics)

| Property | Standard Value (Bold Constants) |
|----------|--------------------------------|
| Form Factor of Pure Sine | **1.11** (= π/(2√2)) |
| Crest Factor of Pure Sine | **1.414** (= √2) |
| True RMS Formula | $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ |
| Power Equivalence | $P_{DC} = P_{AC} \Rightarrow \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{v^2(t)}{R}\,dt$ |
| Crest Factor Range | **1:1 to 10:1** (typical True RMS meter capability) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard **IEC 61010** and **IEEE C57.13** norms define RMS measurement accuracy for instrumentation. KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **crest factor** of common waveforms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A True RMS Meter can be implemented in **three major architectural families**. Let us dissect each one with KTU-style rigor.

### 🔹 Architecture 1: Thermal (Heating-Effect) True RMS Meter

This is the **oldest, most accurate, and physically most direct** method.

1. The input voltage $v(t)$ is applied across a **low-value heating resistor** $R_h$.
2. A **thermocouple (or thermopile)** is physically attached to $R_h$, sensing its temperature rise.
3. Simultaneously, an **adjustable DC reference voltage** $V_{DC}$ is applied to an *identical* heating resistor equipped with a *matched* thermocouple.
4. The two thermocouples are connected in **opposition** to a sensitive galvanometer.
5. $V_{DC}$ is manually (or automatically via servo) adjusted until the galvanometer reads **zero**, i.e., the two heating effects are equal.

When balance is achieved:

$$\frac{V_{DC}^{2}}{R_h} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T}\frac{v^{2}(t)}{R_h}\,dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{DC} = v_{rms}$$

6. The DC voltage $V_{DC}$ is then displayed on a calibrated DPM (Digital Panel Meter).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The thermal method is *implicitly* a True RMS converter because the heating effect is **physically** governed by $I^2R$ losses — square-law behavior is built into the physics of heat dissipation.

### 🔹 Architecture 2: Electronic (Analog Computational) True RMS Meter

Uses active analog ICs to explicitly compute the **square → mean → square-root** chain.

**Block-Level Operational Logic:**

1. **Input Buffer / Attenuator:** Scales the input signal to a level suitable for the squarer.
2. **Squaring Circuit:** Implemented using one of:
   - **Multiplier IC** (e.g., AD534, AD633) — multiplies signal by itself
   - **Log-AntiLog technique** — uses transistor $V_{BE}$-current exponential law: $V_{out} = V_T \ln^{-1}\big(\ln V_{in} + \ln V_{in}\big)$
3. **Averaging Circuit:** A **low-pass filter (LPF)** with cut-off frequency well below the lowest signal frequency.
   - Time constant $\tau = RC$ must be $\gg T$ (signal period)
4. **Square-Root Circuit:** Either another log-antilog stage, or a dedicated **square-root extractor IC**.
5. **Output Display / Buffer:** Drives a moving-coil meter or ADC.

### 🔹 Architecture 3: Digital (Sampling) True RMS Meter

The modern dominant method.

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** (analog LPF) limits input bandwidth.
2. **ADC** samples the signal at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion).
3. **Microcontroller / DSP** computes:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v^{2}(k\Delta t)}$$

4. **Display** on LCD/7-segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Architecture 2 (Electronic) and Architecture 3 (Digital) are most likely to appear in board questions. The log-antilog computational chain is a **favourite KTU question**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | **Master RMS definition** (continuous-time) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$ | **Discrete-time RMS** (used in digital meters) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | **Pure sine only** — true for $v(t)=V_m\sin\omega t$ | V |
| 4 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_{peak}}{\pi}$ | Average of full-wave rectified sine | V |
| 5 | $K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = 1.11$ | **Form Factor** of pure sine (constant!) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_c = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt{2}$ | **Crest Factor** of pure sine | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{DC,\text{equiv}} = V_{rms}$ | DC voltage producing same heating | V |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R}$ | RMS-based power dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $e(t) = v^2(t)$ | Squaring step output | $V^2$ |
| 10 | $\tau_{LPF} = RC \gg T$ | Averaging time-constant requirement | seconds (s) |
| 11 | $V_{rms}^{2} = V_{1}^{2} + V_{2}^{2} + V_{3}^{2} + \cdots$ | **Orthogonality of harmonics** (key result) | $V^2$ |
| 12 | $\%\text{ Error} = \dfrac{V_{measured} - V_{true}}{V_{true}} \times 100$ | Error metric for waveform shape error | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Real-World Utility:** True RMS meters are **mandatory** in power-quality analyzers, UPS testing, variable-frequency drives (VFDs), switched-mode power supplies, and audio engineering — anywhere the waveform is not a clean sine.

## 2.3 Why Ordinary Meters Fail — The Waveform Shape Error

An *Average-Reading* meter rectifies, filters, and then **assumes** the form factor is 1.11 (sine). For a square wave (form factor = 1.0), this assumption causes:

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{1.11 - 1.00}{1.00} \times 100 = 11\%\ \text{(over-reading)}$$

For a triangular wave (form factor = $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$):

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{1.11 - 1.155}{1.155} \times 100 \approx -3.9\%\ \text{(under-reading)}$$

For pulse trains with high crest factors, errors can exceed **30–50%**. **True RMS meters eliminate this entire class of error.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS of a Pure Sinusoidal Waveform

**Given:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $V_m$ is the peak amplitude and $\omega = 2\pi/T$.

**Step 1:** Apply the master RMS definition.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the signal.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \big[V_m \sin(\omega t)\big]^{2}\, dt}$$

**Step 3:** Pull the constant $V_m^2$ out (since $V_m$ does not depend on $t$).

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, dt}$$

**Step 4:** Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, dt}$$

**Step 5:** Split the integral.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{2T}\left[\int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt - \int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt\right]}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the first integral.

$$\int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt = T$$

**Step 7:** Evaluate the second integral. Since $2\omega T = 2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{T} \cdot T = 4\pi$, which represents an integer number of full cycles of cosine.

$$\int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\right]_{0}^{T} = \frac{\sin(4\pi) - \sin(0)}{2\omega} = 0$$

**Step 8:** Substitute the evaluated integrals.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \cdot [T - 0]}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{2}}{2}}$$

**Step 10:** Final result.

$$\boxed{\,V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\, V_m\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS of a Distorted (Non-Sinusoidal) Periodic Waveform

**Given:** A periodic signal containing a fundamental and harmonics:

$$v(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where $V_0$ is the DC offset, $V_n$ is the amplitude of the $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic, and $\phi_n$ is its phase.

**Step 1:** Write the square of the signal.

$$v^{2}(t) = \left[V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)\right]^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square. This produces three classes of terms:
- **Squared individual terms:** $V_0^2$ and $V_n^2 \sin^{2}(n\omega t + \phi_n)$
- **Cross-products:** $2 V_0 V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$
- **Harmonic-mixing products:** $2 V_m V_n \sin(m\omega t + \phi_m)\sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$

**Step 3:** Apply the averaging operator $\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T (\cdot)\, dt$.

For the **DC squared term**:

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_0^{2}\, dt = V_0^{2}$$

For the **sine-squared harmonic terms**, using the result of Derivation 1:

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_n^{2}\sin^{2}(n\omega t + \phi_n)\, dt = \frac{V_n^{2}}{2}$$

For the **cross-product DC-sine term** (mean of sine over a full period is zero):

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 2V_0 V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)\, dt = 0$$

For the **harmonic-mixing product**, use the identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$, and the fact that the mean of a cosine of integer-multiple of $\omega t$ over a full period is zero (when $m \neq n$):

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 2V_m V_n \sin(m\omega t + \phi_m)\sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)\, dt = 0 \quad (m \neq n)$$

**Step 4:** Combine all surviving terms.

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt = V_0^{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{V_n^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_0^{2} + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + \frac{V_3^{2}}{2} + \cdots}$$

**Step 6:** Express in the elegant compact form.

$$\boxed{\,V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_{DC}^{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{V_n}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}} = \sqrt{V_{DC}^{2} + V_{1,rms}^{2} + V_{2,rms}^{2} + V_{3,rms}^{2} + \cdots}\,}$$

This is the **key result for power-quality analysis** — different frequency components add in quadrature (RMS-squares), not linearly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electronic Log-AntiLog True RMS Computation

**Step 1:** Express squaring using logarithm properties. We want $v^2(t)$. Use the identity:

$$\ln\big(v^{2}(t)\big) = 2\ln\big(v(t)\big)$$

This means: if we apply a **log amplifier** to the input, multiply the output by **2**, and then apply an **anti-log amplifier**, we obtain $v^2(t)$.

**Step 2:** Log amplifier output. Using a BJT's exponential $I_C = I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$ characteristic:

$$V_{log} = -V_T \ln\left(\frac{v(t)}{R \cdot I_S}\right)$$

(where $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at room temperature, $I_S$ is the saturation current).

**Step 3:** Multiply by 2 (a simple gain-of-2 op-amp stage):

$$V_{mid} = 2 V_{log} = -2V_T \ln\left(\frac{v(t)}{R \cdot I_S}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Anti-log amplifier restores the square:

$$v^{2}(t) \propto e^{-V_{mid}/V_T} = e^{2\ln(v/(R I_S))} = \left(\frac{v(t)}{R I_S}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Low-pass filter (averaging) over one period:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt$$

**Step 6:** Apply another log-antilog pair (with gain $\frac{1}{2}$) to extract the square root:

$$V_{out} = \sqrt{V_{avg}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt} = V_{rms}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — True RMS vs. Other Meter Types

| Feature | Average-Reading | Peak-Reading | **True RMS** |
|---------|----------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Internal Assumption** | $K_f = 1.11$ (sine) | $K_c = 1.414$ (sine) | **None** — direct heating-equivalent |
| **Accuracy on Sine** | ±0.5% | ±0.5% | **±0.05% to ±0.1%** |
| **Accuracy on Square** | ~11% high | 0% (correct) | **±0.1%** |
| **Accuracy on Triangle** | ~4% low | ~30% high | **±0.1%** |
| **Accuracy on Harmonic-Rich** | Up to 50% error | Up to 50% error | **±0.1%** |
| **Crest-Factor Tolerance** | Limited (~3:1) | Limited (~3:1) | **Wide (1:1 to 10:1)** |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | **Moderate to High** |
| **Complexity** | Simple rectifier | Peak detector + scaling | **Multiplier / DSP / Thermal** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Electronic True RMS Meter (Log-AntiLog)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Signal v t"] --> ATT["Attenuator and Buffer"]
    ATT --> LOG1["Log Amplifier 1"]
    LOG1 --> MUL["Multiplier Gain equals 2"]
    MUL --> ANTILOG1["AntiLog Amplifier 1"]
    ANTILOG1 --> SQR["Squared Signal v squared t"]
    SQR --> LPF["Low Pass Filter Averager"]
    LPF --> LOG2["Log Amplifier 2"]
    LOG2 --> DIV["Divider Gain equals 0.5"]
    DIV --> ANTILOG2["AntiLog Amplifier 2"]
    ANTILOG2 --> OUT["True RMS Output Vrms"]
    OUT --> DSP["Display DPM or LCD"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Thermal True RMS Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ACIN["AC Input v t"] --> RESAC["Heating Resistor RH1"]
    DCIN["Adjustable DC Vref"] --> RESDC["Matched Resistor RH2"]
    RESAC --> TC1["Thermocouple 1"]
    RESDC --> TC2["Matched Thermocouple 2"]
    TC1 --> OPP["Opposing Junction"]
    TC2 --> OPP
    OPP --> GAL["Sensitive Galvanometer"]
    GAL --> FB["Servo Feedback Loop"]
    FB --> DCADJ["DC Reference Adjust"]
    DCADJ --> DCIN
    OPP --> DPM["Calibrated DPM reads Vrms"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Digital (DSP-Based) True RMS Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Input v t"] --> B["Anti-Aliasing LPF"]
    B --> C["Sample and Hold"]
    C --> D["ADC 12 or 16 bit"]
    D --> E["Microcontroller or DSP"]
    E --> F["Compute v squared k"]
    F --> G["Accumulate Sum"]
    G --> H["Divide by N and Square Root"]
    H --> I["LCD Display Vrms"]
    E --> CLK["Clock Generator"]
    CLK --> C
```

## 4.4 Signal-Waveform Transformation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["v t Sine"] --> SQ["Square v squared t"]
    SQ --> AV["Average Mean of v squared t"]
    AV --> SR["Square Root Vrms"]
    SR1["Square Wave input"] --> SQ1["Squarer"]
    SQ1 --> AV1["Averager"]
    AV1 --> SR1A["Square Root Vrms True"]
    TR["Triangular input"] --> SQ2["Squarer"]
    SQ2 --> AV2["Averager"]
    AV2 --> SR2A["Square Root Vrms True"]
```

## 4.5 Functional Comparison: Average Meter vs True RMS Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AM["Average Reading Meter"]
        AM1["Rectifier"] --> AM2["RC Filter Average"]
        AM2 --> AM3["Scale by 1.11 Assumes Sine"]
        AM3 --> AM4["Display Reading"]
    end
    subgraph TM["True RMS Meter"]
        TM1["Squarer v squared t"] --> TM2["True Average no assumption"]
        TM2 --> TM3["Square Root"]
        TM3 --> TM4["Display True Vrms"]
    end
    W1["Distorted Waveform Input"] --> AM1
    W1 --> TM1
    AM4 --> ER["ERROR up to 50 percent"]
    TM4 --> CR["CORRECT any waveform"]
```

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define True RMS value of an AC signal. Why is it preferred over average and peak values for measurement purposes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **True RMS value** of an AC signal $v(t)$ is the steady-state DC equivalent that would produce the same heating effect (power dissipation) in a resistive load. It is mathematically defined as:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

**Why preferred (1 mark each):**

1. **Power equivalence:** Electrical power dissipated in a resistor depends on $V^2/R$, hence the RMS value directly gives real power: $P = V_{rms}^2 / R$. Peak and average values do **not** directly yield power.

2. **Waveform independence:** True RMS correctly measures sine, square, triangle, sawtooth, and distorted waveforms alike. Peak and average meters assume a sine wave and apply fixed form/crest factors, producing large errors on non-sinusoidal signals.

3. **Physical meaning:** RMS represents the **effective** working value of the signal — the value that actually drives current, torque, heat, and energy in loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (1 mark):** The examiner will specifically look for the *heating effect / power dissipation* justification — mentioning "effective value" alone is incomplete.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between Form Factor and Crest Factor? Compute both for a pure sinusoidal waveform.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Form Factor** ($K_f$): The ratio of RMS value to the average value of the rectified signal. (1 mark)

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$$

- **Crest Factor** ($K_c$): The ratio of peak value to the RMS value. (1 mark)

$$K_c = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$$

**For a pure sine wave** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: (1 mark)

- $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$
- $V_{avg,\text{rectified}} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$
- $V_{peak} = V_m$

Therefore:

$$K_f = \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.11$$

$$K_c = \frac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} = 1.414$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of an **electronic True RMS meter** using the squaring-averaging-square-root method. Derive the governing mathematical relation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A non-sinusoidal periodic voltage waveform is given by $v(t) = 100\sin(314t) + 30\sin(942t) + 10\sin(1570t)$ volts. Calculate its **True RMS value**, **average value of rectified waveform**, and the resulting **form factor**. Comment on whether an average-reading meter calibrated for sine will over-read or under-read this signal.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A True RMS meter directly computes $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$ using an explicit **square → mean → square-root** electronic chain.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Input → Attenuator → Squarer → Averager (LPF) → Square-Root Extractor → Output Display
```

**Working (3 marks):**

1. **Input Attenuator/Buffer:** Scales the unknown $v(t)$ to a range suitable for the squarer stage.
2. **Squaring Circuit:** Built using either a **log-antilog pair** (using BJT $V_{BE}$-current exponential law) or a **multiplier IC** (e.g., AD534) wired to multiply signal by itself: $e(t) = v(t) \times v(t) = v^2(t)$.
3. **Averaging Circuit:** A **low-pass RC filter** with time constant $\tau = RC$ chosen such that $\tau \gg T$ (e.g., $\tau = 10T$ to $100T$). It computes the mean:

$$\bar{e} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt$$

4. **Square-Root Extractor:** A second log-antilog pair (with halving gain) computes $\sqrt{\bar{e}}$.
5. **Output:** Drives a calibrated DPM or analog meter.

**Governing Relation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}}$$

The meter output is a **DC voltage proportional to $V_{rms}$**, valid for any periodic waveform.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $v(t) = 100\sin(314t) + 30\sin(942t) + 10\sin(1570t)\ \text{V}$

**Identify harmonic amplitudes (1 mark):**
- Fundamental ($n=1$): $V_1 = 100$ V, RMS = $\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}} = 70.71$ V
- 3rd harmonic ($n=3$): $V_3 = 30$ V, RMS = $\frac{30}{\sqrt{2}} = 21.21$ V
- 5th harmonic ($n=5$): $V_5 = 10$ V, RMS = $\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 7.07$ V

**Step 1 — True RMS Calculation (3 marks):**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_{1,rms}^{2} + V_{3,rms}^{2} + V_{5,rms}^{2}}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{(70.71)^{2} + (21.21)^{2} + (7.07)^{2}}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{5000 + 450 + 50} = \sqrt{5500}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 74.16\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 2 — Average of Rectified Waveform (2 marks):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T |v(t)|\, dt \approx \frac{2}{\pi}\left[V_1 + V_3 + V_5\right] = \frac{2}{\pi}(100 + 30 + 10)$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 \times 140}{\pi} = \frac{280}{\pi} \approx 89.13\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Form Factor (1 mark):**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{74.16}{89.13} \approx 0.832$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Average-Reading Meter (0.5 + 0.5 = 1 mark):**

The **true form factor** of this signal is **0.832**, but the average-reading meter *assumes* a form factor of **1.11** for a pure sine. Since the meter scales the measured average by 1.11, while the actual $K_f$ is 0.832, the **meter will over-read**:

$$\text{Reading}_{\text{meter}} = 1.11 \times V_{avg,\text{true}} = 1.11 \times 89.13 = 98.93\ \text{V}$$

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{98.93 - 74.16}{74.16} \times 100 \approx +33.4\%\ \text{(over-reading)}$$

A **True RMS meter** gives the correct value of **74.16 V**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **(b) Step 1 trap:** Students often compute RMS of *each harmonic* incorrectly as $V_n/\sqrt{2}$ but then *linearly add* amplitudes. **You must square, sum, and root** — this is a 2-mark penalty area.
> 2. **(b) Step 2 trap:** The average is taken over the *full-wave rectified* signal, not the raw signal. The raw signal's mean over a full period is **zero**.
> 3. **(a) Working trap:** Don't forget to mention the **time-constant condition** $\tau \gg T$ for the LPF — students often skip this and lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a block diagram the **thermal-type True RMS meter**. Explain the principle of operation and derive the condition for balance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **log-antilog method** of computing True RMS using analog circuits. Show mathematically how the squaring and square-root operations are achieved using logarithmic amplifiers.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle (2 marks):** The thermal True RMS meter operates on the principle that the **heating effect of current** (and hence power) in a resistor is proportional to the *square* of the instantaneous current. A DC voltage that produces the *same heating* in an *identical resistor* is, by definition, equal to the RMS of the AC signal.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
AC Input ──► Resistor R₁ (heated by AC) ──► Thermocouple T₁
                                                    │
DC Reference (Vdc, adjustable) ──► Resistor R₂ (identical, heated by DC) ──► Thermocouple T₂
                                                    │
                                              Junction (T₁ and T₂ in opposition)
                                                    │
                                            Sensitive Galvanometer
                                                    │
                                            Null Detector (zero = balance)
```

**Working (2 marks):**

1. The unknown AC voltage $v(t)$ heats resistor $R_1$. The temperature rise is sensed by thermocouple $T_1$, producing an EMF $E_1 \propto \overline{v^2(t)}$.
2. An adjustable DC reference $V_{DC}$ heats resistor $R_2$. Thermocouple $T_2$ produces EMF $E_2 \propto V_{DC}^2$.
3. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are connected in **series opposition** to a sensitive galvanometer.
4. $V_{DC}$ is varied (manually or by a servo loop) until the galvanometer reads **zero** — meaning $E_1 = E_2$, hence the two heating effects are equal.

**Balance Condition / Derivation (1 mark):**

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{v^2(t)}{R_1}\,dt = \frac{V_{DC}^{2}}{R_2}$$

With $R_1 = R_2$ (matched resistors):

$$\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt = V_{DC}^{2}$$

$$V_{DC} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt} = V_{rms}$$

The **DC reference voltage** $V_{DC}$ at balance is **numerically equal to the True RMS** of the AC input. A calibrated DPM reads it directly.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Concept (1 mark):** The log-antilog method exploits the **exponential $I$-$V$ characteristic of a BJT** to perform multiplication/division by addition/subtraction of logarithms, which is mathematically elegant for analog computation.

**BJT exponential law:**

$$I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

where $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at room temperature, $I_S$ is reverse saturation current.

**Log Amplifier Output (2 marks):**

A log amplifier using an op-amp with a BJT in the feedback path produces:

$$V_{out} = -V_T \ln\left(\frac{v_{in}}{R \cdot I_S}\right)$$

**Squaring Operation (2 marks):**

Apply two identical log amplifiers to the *same* input $v(t)$ and sum their outputs (since $2 \ln x = \ln x^2$):

$$V_{log1} + V_{log2} = -2V_T \ln\left(\frac{v(t)}{R I_S}\right)$$

Then pass through an **anti-log amplifier** (BJT at input, resistor in feedback):

$$V_{sq} = R_f \cdot I_S \cdot e^{(V_{log1}+V_{log2})/V_T} = R_f \cdot I_S \cdot \left(\frac{v(t)}{R I_S}\right)^{2} = k \cdot v^{2}(t)$$

where $k = \frac{R_f}{R^2 I_S}$ is a constant gain.

**Averaging (1 mark):**

A low-pass RC filter with $\tau \gg T$ computes:

$$\bar{V}_{sq} = k \cdot \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt$$

**Square-Root via Log-AntiLog (1 mark):**

Apply a log amplifier to $\bar{V}_{sq}$, halve the output, then apply an anti-log amplifier:

$$V_{out} = R_{f2} \cdot I_S \cdot e^{\frac{1}{2}\ln(\bar{V}_{sq}/(R_3 I_S))} = K\sqrt{\bar{V}_{sq}}$$

This $V_{out}$ is proportional to:

$$V_{out} \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt} = V_{rms}$$

**Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}}$$

where $K$ is a calibrated scale factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Question B:**
> 1. **(a) Pitfall:** Students often forget to state that the **two resistors must be matched** ($R_1 = R_2$) — without this, the balance equation is wrong. **1-mark penalty.**
> 2. **(a) Pitfall:** Don't confuse the *thermo-EMF* (output of thermocouple) with the *heating power* — the relationship $E \propto$ *average power* is what gives the RMS equivalence.
> 3. **(b) Pitfall:** The factor of **2 in $2\ln v$** is the *squaring step* — many students miss this and write $\ln v$ instead, leading to a wrong answer. **2-mark penalty zone.**
> 4. **(b) Pitfall:** Always include the **time-constant condition** $\tau \gg T$ for the LPF — its omission costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **True RMS Meter — High-Density Revision Points**

- ✅ **Master Formula:** $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ — memorize and be ready to derive.

- ✅ **Sine-specific shortcut:** $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ — applies **only** to pure sinusoids.

- ✅ **Square-wave RMS:** $V_{rms} = V_m$ (since the signal is constant at $\pm V_m$).

- ✅ **Triangular-wave RMS:** $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{3}$.

- ✅ **Form Factor of Sine:** $K_f = 1.11$ (use this **only** for sine!).

- ✅ **Crest Factor of Sine:** $K_c = 1.414 = \sqrt{2}$.

- ✅ **Three architectures to remember:**
  1. **Thermal** (heating effect + thermocouples + null balance) — most accurate, slow.
  2. **Electronic** (squarer + averager + square-root) — common, fast.
  3. **Digital** (ADC + DSP/microcontroller) — modern, flexible.

- ✅ **Log-AntiLog squaring:** $2\ln v(t)$ followed by anti-log = $v^2(t)$.

- ✅ **Log-AntiLog square root:** $\frac{1}{2}\ln v(t)$ followed by anti-log = $\sqrt{v(t)}$.

- ✅ **Harmonic orthogonality result:** $V_{rms}^2 = V_{1,rms}^2 + V_{2,rms}^2 + V_{3,rms}^2 + \cdots$ — components add in **quadrature**, not linearly.

- ✅ **LPF requirement:** Time constant $\tau = RC \gg T$ for accurate averaging.

- ✅ **Why True RMS is needed:** Average and peak meters assume a fixed form/crest factor of a pure sine. Distorted, harmonic-rich, and pulse waveforms cause large errors (often > 30%) in such meters.

- ✅ **Typical applications:** Power-quality analyzers, VFD drives, UPS testing, audio engineering, switch-mode power supplies, harmonic analysis.

- ✅ **Crest factor tolerance of a good True RMS meter:** Typically **3:1 to 10:1** — wider is better for pulsed/distorted signals.

- ✅ **KTU examiner keywords to use in answers:** *heating effect, effective value, power equivalence, waveform independence, form factor, crest factor, quadrature summation, time-averaged square*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Exam Warning:** If a question asks "why use True RMS meter?", **always** anchor your answer in (1) heating effect equivalence and (2) waveform independence. Avoid vague phrases like "it's more accurate" — the examiner wants *physical reasoning*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
