KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style, 2024 Scheme]** **Q1. Define the Schering Bridge. Mention its primary industrial application. [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The Schering Bridge is a four-arm AC bridge used for the precise measurement of **unknown capacitance, its equivalent series resistance, and the dissipation factor (tan δ)** of capacitive specimens. It consists of an unknown lossy capacitor in one arm, a lossless standard capacitor in the opposite arm, a variable non-inductive resistor, and a parallel RC network in the other two arms. Its primary industrial application is in **high-voltage insulation testing** of power cables, transformer bushings, and capacitor dielectrics, where the unknown is energized at full working voltage while the reference and balancing components remain at low, safe voltage. --- **Q2. What is meant by dissipation factor (tan δ) of a capacitor? Why is it a critical parameter in high-voltage insulation testing? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** The dissipation factor, denoted **tan δ**, is the tangent of the loss angle δ — the angle by which the current through a real capacitor deviates from the ideal 90° leading phase with respect to voltage. Mathematically, $\tan\delta = \omega C r$ for a series RC model. It is a **dimensionless** quantity that quantifies the **dielectric loss** in the capacitor. In high-voltage insulation testing, tan δ is critical because it directly indicates the **insulation health** — a rising tan δ over time signals moisture ingress, aging, partial discharge, or contamination, all of which can lead to catastrophic insulation failure in transformers and cables. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation (ESE) Style, 2024 Scheme]** > **Instructions:** Answer **any ONE** full question from the choice given. Each question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Derivation + Application Type **Q.A. (a)** Derive the balance equations of a Schering Bridge used for the measurement of an unknown capacitance. Hence, obtain expressions for the unknown capacitance $C_1$, its equivalent series resistance $r_1$, and the dissipation factor $\tan\delta$. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** At balance of any AC bridge, the detector current is zero, giving the condition: $$ Z_1 Z_4 \;=\; Z_2 Z_3 $$ **Arm impedances:** - $Z_1 = r_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega C_1 r_1}{j\omega C_1}$ - $Z_2 = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2}$ - $Z_3 = R_3$ - $Z_4 = \dfrac{R_4}{1 + j\omega C_4 R_4}$ **Substitution and simplification:** $$ \left(\frac{1 + j\omega C_1 r_1}{j\omega C_1}\right)\left(\frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega C_4 R_4}\right) \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C_2}\right) R_3 $$ Cross-multiplying and cancelling $j\omega$: $$ (1 + j\omega C_1 r_1) R_4 C_2 \;=\; C_1 R_3 (1 + j\omega C_4 R_4) $$ **Equating real parts:** $$ C_2 R_4 = C_1 R_3 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{C_1 = \frac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}} $$ **Equating imaginary parts:** $$ \omega C_1 r_1 C_2 R_4 = \omega C_1 R_3 C_4 R_4 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{r_1 = \frac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}} $$ **Dissipation factor:** $$ \tan\delta = \omega C_1 r_1 = \omega \left(\frac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}\right) \left(\frac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}\right) = \boxed{\omega C_4 R_4} $$ > **Note:** tan δ depends only on $C_4$, $R_4$, and $\omega$ — not on the unknown — making the Schering bridge a true null-instrument for loss measurement. --- **Q.A. (b)** In a Schering Bridge, the arm components at balance are: $C_2 = 500 \text{ pF}$, $R_3 = 2000 \, \Omega$, $R_4 = 5000 \, \Omega$, $C_4 = 0.005 \, \mu\text{F}$. The supply frequency is $50 \text{ Hz}$. Calculate the unknown capacitance $C_1$, its series resistance $r_1$, and the dissipation factor $\tan\delta$ of the unknown. Also, comment on the quality of the dielectric. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\omega$** $$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_1$** $$ C_1 = \frac{C_2 R_4}{R_3} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-12} \times 5000}{2000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 1250 \text{ pF} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $r_1$** $$ r_1 = \frac{R_3 C_4}{C_2} = \frac{2000 \times 0.005 \times 10^{-6}}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-10}} = 2 \times 10^{4} \, \Omega = 20 \text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 4 — Compute $\tan\delta$** $$ \tan\delta = \omega C_4 R_4 = 314.159 \times 0.005 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00785 $$ $$ \delta = \tan^{-1}(0.00785) \approx 0.45^{\circ} $$ **Step 5 — Comment on dielectric quality** The dissipation factor is very small ($\tan\delta < 0.01$), and the loss angle is less than half a degree. This indicates a **high-quality, low-loss dielectric** — characteristic of materials such as polystyrene, polypropylene, mica, or high-grade ceramic (NP0/C0G) capacitors. Such low loss is desirable for precision timing circuits, sample-and-hold applications, and high-frequency filters. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual + Numerical Type **Q.B. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Schering Bridge used for the measurement of capacitance. Why is the standard capacitor $C_2$ required to be lossless? Mention the role of the Wagner Earthing device. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Construction (Block diagram description):** The Schering Bridge consists of four arms: - **Arm ab**: Unknown capacitor $C_1$ in series with its equivalent series resistance $r_1$ (the DUT). - **Arm bc**: A standard, **lossless** capacitor $C_2$ (typically a high-quality air, mica, or compressed-gas capacitor). - **Arm cd**: A variable, non-inductive resistor $R_3$ (decade resistance box). - **Arm da**: A fixed non-inductive resistor $R_4$ in **parallel** with a variable precision capacitor $C_4$. - A high-voltage AC source is connected between nodes a and c. - A null detector (vibration galvanometer or tuned amplifier) is connected between nodes b and d. - A Wagner Earthing device consisting of two auxiliary arms ($R_5$ in series with $C_5$, and $R_6$) is connected between the detector nodes and earth. **Working:** The variable resistor $R_3$ and the variable capacitor $C_4$ are adjusted alternately until the detector indicates zero current. At this null point, the bridge satisfies $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$, and the unknown capacitance, its series resistance, and the dissipation factor can be calculated from the dial readings using the derived formulas. **Why $C_2$ must be lossless:** If $C_2$ itself has dielectric loss, then the loss being measured would be the **sum** of the unknown's loss and the reference's loss. Since the goal is to measure **only** the unknown's loss, the reference must contribute zero loss — i.e., it must be a pure, lossless capacitance with $\tan\delta \approx 0$. **Role of Wagner Earthing:** In a high-voltage Schering Bridge, the bridge arms have significant stray capacitance to earth. These earth capacitances cause spurious detector currents even at mathematical balance, leading to erroneous readings. The Wagner Earthing device, when balanced, forces both detector nodes to be at earth potential, thereby eliminating the effect of stray earth capacitances and giving a true null. --- **Q.B. (b)** A lossy capacitor with $C_1 = 0.5 \, \mu\text{F}$ and $r_1 = 2 \, \Omega$ is measured in a Schering Bridge. The standard capacitor $C_2 = 1000 \text{ pF}$ is lossless, and the supply frequency is $1 \text{ kHz}$. Determine the values of $R_3$, $R_4$, and $C_4$ required for balance. Compute the dissipation factor of the unknown. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\omega$** $$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\tan\delta$ directly from unknown** $$ \tan\delta = \omega C_1 r_1 = 6283.19 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 = 6.283 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00628 $$ **Step 3 — Choose $R_4$ and compute $C_4$** The expression $\tan\delta = \omega C_4 R_4$ contains two unknowns ($C_4$ and $R_4$). We must choose one. A typical KTU value: choose $R_4 = 1000 \, \Omega$. $$ C_4 = \frac{\tan\delta}{\omega R_4} = \frac{0.00628}{6283.19 \times 1000} = \frac{0.00628}{6.283 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} = 1 \text{ nF} = 1000 \text{ pF} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $R_3$** From the capacitance balance equation: $$ R_3 = \frac{C_2 R_4}{C_1} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-12} \times 1000}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2 \, \Omega $$ **Step 5 — Verify using $r_1$ formula** $$ r_1 = \frac{R_3 C_4}{C_2} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 10^{-12}}{1000 \times 10^{-12}} = 2 \, \Omega \;\checkmark $$ This matches the given $r_1 = 2 \, \Omega$, confirming the calculations. **Step 6 — Summary of required arm values** - $R_4 = 1000 \, \Omega$ - $C_4 = 1000 \text{ pF}$ - $R_3 = 2 \, \Omega$ - $\tan\delta = 6.283 \times 10^{-3}$ (a low-loss dielectric, indicative of a polypropylene or similar film capacitor) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 Marks Easily):** > 1. **Forgetting units in $C_4$:** $C_4$ is usually in picofarads or nanofarads. A common mistake is mixing $\mu\text{F}$ with pF in the formula and getting a wildly wrong $R_3$. **Always convert to a common unit (Farads) before substituting.** > 2. **Mixing up series vs. parallel models:** The Schering Bridge uses a **series** equivalent for the unknown. Do not write $r_1 \parallel C_1$ for the unknown; the loss resistor is in **series** with the capacitance. > 3. **Ignoring the Wagner Earth:** In any high-voltage Schering Bridge question worth more than 7 marks, **always mention the Wagner Earthing device** — it is a KTU-favorite 1–2 mark bonus topic. > 4. **Not stating the balance condition explicitly:** Always start the derivation with $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$. Students often skip this and the examiner deducts a mark. > 5. **Incorrect $\omega$ calculation:** For Indian mains, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$; for laboratory Schering bridges, $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$. Use the **frequency given in the question**, not assumed. > 6. **Sign errors in imaginary-part balance:** $j\omega$ must be carried correctly through cross-multiplication. A sign error here corrupts both $r_1$ and $\tan\delta$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read twice before every KTU exam** - **Schering Bridge** = AC bridge for measuring **unknown capacitance, ESR, and $\tan\delta$**. - **Four arms:** Unknown (arm ab), Standard $C_2$ (bc), $R_3$ (cd), $R_4 \parallel C_4$ (da). - **Master formulas (memorize verbatim):** - $C_1 = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}$ - $r_1 = \dfrac{R_3 C_4}{C_2}$ - $\tan\delta = \omega C_4 R_4$ - $Q = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_4 R_4}$ - **Balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ — **always start the derivation with this**. - **$\tan\delta$ is decoupled from the unknown** — it depends only on $C_4$, $R_4$, and $\omega$. This is the Schering Bridge's key engineering elegance. - **$C_2$ must be lossless** (air, mica, or compressed-gas); any loss in $C_2$ corrupts the loss measurement of the unknown. - **$R_3$ and $R_4$ must be non-inductive** — even a small residual inductance introduces a quadrature error. - **Wagner Earthing device** is mandatory in **high-voltage** Schering Bridges to nullify stray earth capacitances. - **Two decoupled controls:** $R_3$ tunes $C_1$; $C_4$ tunes $\tan\delta$ (at fixed $R_4$). This makes balance convergence easy. - **Industrial applications:** $\tan\delta$ testing of transformer oil, bushing insulation, HV cables, ceramic and polymer dielectrics, NABL-traceable capacitance calibration. - **Key difference from De Sauty Bridge:** De Sauty measures only the **magnitude** of capacitance (no loss term). Schering measures both **magnitude and loss** — making it the preferred choice for lossy dielectrics. - **Key difference from Wien Bridge:** Wien Bridge measures **frequency** using a series-parallel RC; Schering measures **capacitance and loss**. They have similar topology but different applications. - **At balance, detector current is zero** — $V_b = V_d$ in both magnitude and phase. - **Phasor insight:** The unknown arm has resistance ($r_1$) and reactance ($1/j\omega C_1$); the reference arm bc is purely reactive ($1/j\omega C_2$); the variable resistor $R_3$ balances the **resistive** component, and the $R_4 \parallel C_4$ arm balances the **reactive** component. - **Equation of loss angle:** $\delta = \tan^{-1}(\omega C_4 R_4)$ — typically $< 1°$ for good dielectrics. - **For Class-1 (NP0) ceramic capacitors:** $\tan\delta < 0.001$; for Class-2 (X7R, Y5V): $\tan\delta \sim 0.02$; for electrolytic: $\tan\delta \sim 0.1$–$0.5$. - **Quality factor of an unknown:** $Q = 1/\tan\delta = 1/(\omega C_4 R_4)$ — directly readable from the bridge settings.

topics.title: Potentiometer: General Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potentiometer: General Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Potentiometer** (also known as a **resistive position transducer** or **linear/angular displacement sensor**) is a passive, three-terminal electromechanical transducer that converts **linear displacement** or **angular rotation** into a proportional change in electrical resistance, and consequently into a proportional output voltage, based on the **principle of voltage division**.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the potentiometer is classified as a **passive resistive transducer** belonging to the family of **primary sensing elements**, where the measurand (displacement) causes a mechanical wiper to slide along a uniform resistance element, thereby modulating the output voltage tapped at the wiper terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Potentiometers are introduced as a foundational *resistive-type displacement transducer*. The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **(a) Linear Potentiometers** and **(b) Rotary Potentiometers**, and to understand their behaviour as **Voltage Divider Circuits** under ideal and non-ideal (loaded) conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction Triad (Three Terminals):**
> 1. **Terminal A** — One end of the resistance element
> 2. **Terminal B** — The other end of the resistance element
> 3. **Terminal W (Wiper)** — The movable sliding contact whose position is determined by the measurand

The physical quantity **total resistance** $\mathbf{R_T}$ is typically expressed in **ohms ($\Omega$)**, and the **standard resistance values** of commercial potentiometers range from **100 $\Omega$ to 100 k$\Omega$**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Faucet / Volume Knob Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a pressurized pipe. As you turn the knob:
- When the knob is fully closed, **no water flows** (analogue: wiper at one extreme end → output voltage = 0 V)
- When the knob is fully open, **maximum water flows** (analogue: wiper at the other extreme end → output voltage = maximum $V_{in}$)
- When the knob is at the midway position, **half the water flows** (analogue: wiper at the middle → output voltage = $V_{in}/2$)

Similarly, in a potentiometer:
- The **water pipe** = the resistance element (with a uniform voltage gradient across it)
- The **tap knob** = the wiper / slider mechanism
- The **water flow rate at the outlet** = the **output voltage** tapped by the wiper

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world everyday example:** The **volume control knob on an old transistor radio** is a rotary potentiometer. Turning the knob clockwise increases the volume (wiper moves towards the high-voltage end). The **slide fader on an audio mixing console** is a **linear potentiometer** that produces an output voltage proportional to the slider's physical position.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Potentiometers (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| **Category** | **Sub-Type** | **Displacement Type** | **Typical Range** |
|:-------------|:-------------|:----------------------|:------------------|
| Motion Type   | Linear Potentiometer    | Translatory (straight line)    | 2 mm to 500 mm     |
| Motion Type   | Rotary Potentiometer   | Rotational (angular sweep)     | $0^\circ$ to $360^\circ$ (or limited arc) |
| Construction  | Wire-Wound              | High precision, low cost       | Resolution: $\pm 0.1\%$ |
| Construction  | Cermet / Film           | High resolution, smooth output | Resolution: $\pm 0.01\%$ |
| Function      | Single-Turn             | One full rotation              | Standard use       |
| Function      | Multi-Turn (Helipot)    | 3, 5, 10, or more turns       | High precision     |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** A *rheostat* uses only **two terminals** (acts as a variable resistor), while a *potentiometer* uses **all three terminals** (acts as an adjustable voltage divider). This is a common viva / 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

## 1.4 The Measurand and the Output Relationship — At a Glance

Under ideal conditions with a **purely resistive load** $R_L \to \infty$ (open-circuit measurement, as assumed in most KTU problems), the **output voltage** $e_o$ is a **linear function** of the wiper displacement $x$:

$$e_o = \frac{V_s}{L} \cdot x$$

where $V_s$ is the source voltage applied across the resistance element, and $L$ is the total mechanical length of travel. This **linear voltage-displacement characteristic** is the heart of the potentiometer's appeal as a measurement transducer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The potentiometer operates on the **principle of the voltage divider** combined with **uniform resistance distribution** along a calibrated track. The sequence of operation is as follows:

1. **Excitation Application:** A known, stable DC or AC excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the two end terminals (A and B) of the resistance element. For KTU problems, this is conventionally taken as a **DC source** unless otherwise specified.
2. **Uniform Potential Gradient:** Because the resistance element is constructed with a **uniform cross-section** and **uniform resistivity** $\rho$, the voltage drop per unit length is constant. A **linear potential gradient** $k = V_s / L$ (in V/m) is established along the track.
3. **Mechanical Displacement Input:** The measurand (force, pressure, acceleration, fluid level — converted via a mechanical linkage) drives the wiper to a new position $x$ along the track.
4. **Wiper Position Sensing:** The resistance between Terminal A and the wiper is $R_x = (R_T / L) \cdot x$. The resistance between the wiper and Terminal B is $R_T - R_x$.
5. **Output Voltage Tap-off:** The wiper physically taps off the voltage at that point on the resistive track. Under no-load conditions, this tapped voltage $e_o$ equals the voltage that would exist at that point in an open voltage divider.
6. **Signal Conditioning:** The output $e_o$ is read by a high-impedance measuring instrument (voltmeter, ADC, or instrumentation amplifier) to avoid **loading errors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a uniform resistance is critical:** Any non-uniformity in $\rho$ or cross-sectional area $A$ introduces **non-linearity** in the $e_o$ vs. $x$ curve. This is why precision wire-wound and cermet potentiometers are manufactured under tightly controlled tolerances.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU examiner expects you to know for the potentiometer topic:

| **#** | **Formula** | **Quantity Computed** | **Variables Explained** | **Standard Unit** |
|:------|:------------|:----------------------|:------------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $e_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_T}$ | Output voltage (no load) | $V_s$: source voltage; $R_x$: resistance from end-A to wiper; $R_T$: total track resistance | V (volt) |
| 2 | $e_o = \dfrac{V_s}{L} \cdot x$ | Output voltage (linear form) | $V_s$: source voltage; $L$: total track length; $x$: wiper displacement | V (volt) |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{V_s}{L}$ | Sensitivity (static) | $V_s$: source voltage; $L$: track length | V/m or V/deg |
| 4 | $R_x = R_T \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | Resistance from end-A to wiper | $R_T$: total resistance; $x$: displacement; $L$: total length | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $e_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}}$ | Output voltage (under load $R_L$) | Includes loading error correction | V (volt) |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_L = \dfrac{e_o^{\text{ideal}} - e_o^{\text{loaded}}}{e_o^{\text{ideal}}} \times 100\%$ | Loading error percentage | Difference between open-circuit and loaded output | \% |
| 7 | $R_T = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Total resistance (basic) | $\rho$: resistivity; $L$: length; $A$: cross-sectional area | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100\%$ | Resolution of wire-wound pot | $N$: number of turns of resistance wire | \% |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta R_x / R_T}{x / L}$ | Linearity ratio (ideally = 1) | Ratio of fractional resistance to fractional displacement | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V_s^2}{4 R_T}$ | Power dissipation rating (matched load) | $V_s$: source; $R_T$: total resistance | W (watt) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Latex Safety Protocol:** Notice that the fractions $\dfrac{x}{L}$ use the LaTeX command `\dfrac` and the vertical separator in the loading formula uses `\cdot` for multiplication, **not** the vertical pipe symbol. This avoids corrupting the markdown table structure.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The potentiometer is one of the most widely deployed transducers in modern engineering due to its **simplicity, low cost, adequate accuracy, and high output signal level** (no amplification required for short distances). Major application domains include:

- **Automotive Industry:** Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor, Fuel Level Sender, Ride-Height Sensor in air suspension systems
- **Industrial Automation:** Machine tool slide position feedback, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position sensing, valve position monitoring
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position feedback, landing gear position indication
- **Consumer Electronics:** Audio volume and tone controls, joystick and game controller input, camera zoom/focus rings
- **Medical Devices:** Infusion pump plunger position, surgical robot joint angle feedback
- **Robotics:** Servo motor angular position feedback, robotic arm joint encoders (low-cost applications)
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Crack displacement gauges, bridge bearing displacement sensors

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Note:** Modern **Hall-effect** and **magnetostrictive** position sensors have largely replaced potentiometers in harsh environments (vibration, temperature extremes, fluid contamination), but the potentiometer remains the **first-choice educational example** for teaching the voltage-divider principle due to its pedagogical clarity.

---

## 2.4 Why the Potentiometer is a "First-Order" Transducer

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, transducers are classified by their **order of complexity**. A potentiometer is a **zeroth-order (0th-order) transducer** because it has **no energy storage elements** (no inductance $L$ or capacitance $C$) — only pure resistance. Therefore:
- Its **transfer function** is a pure algebraic ratio (no differential equations)
- Its **frequency response** is **flat from DC to infinity** (in theory)
- It has **no phase shift** between input displacement and output voltage

This is why the equation $e_o = f(x)$ is treated as a **static (DC) calibration** relationship without dynamic compensation terms in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: No-Load Output Voltage Equation

**Statement to prove:** The output voltage $e_o$ tapped at the wiper is given by
$$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Given:**
- Total track length = $L$ (metres)
- Total track resistance = $R_T$ ($\Omega$)
- Wiper displacement from end-A = $x$ (metres)
- Source voltage applied between A and B = $V_s$ (volts)

**Step 1 — Establish the Resistance Distribution:**

Because the resistive track is manufactured with **uniform resistivity $\rho$** and **uniform cross-sectional area $A$**, the resistance of any segment of length $\ell$ is:
$$R(\ell) = \frac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Express Total Resistance:**

The full length $L$ of the track has total resistance:
$$R_T = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Resistance from End-A to Wiper:**

For a wiper at displacement $x$, the resistance between end-A and the wiper is:
$$R_x = \frac{\rho \cdot x}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio $R_x / R_T$:**

Dividing the equation in Step 3 by the equation in Step 2:
$$\frac{R_x}{R_T} = \frac{\dfrac{\rho \cdot x}{A}}{\dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}} = \frac{\rho \cdot x}{A} \cdot \frac{A}{\rho \cdot L} = \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule (VDR):**

For a series combination of two resistors $R_x$ and $(R_T - R_x)$ across a source $V_s$, the voltage across the segment containing $R_x$ is:
$$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_x + (R_T - R_x)} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_T}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Result of Step 4 into Step 5:**

$$\boxed{e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}}$$

**Conclusion:** The output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the wiper displacement $x$, with the proportionality constant (sensitivity) being $V_s / L$. The displacement of the wiper is **fully recovered** from a single voltage measurement under no-load conditions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Loaded Output Voltage Equation (The Loading Error)

**Statement to prove:** When a finite load resistance $R_L$ is connected between the wiper and end-B, the actual output voltage $e_o'$ deviates from the ideal (no-load) value $e_o$.

**Step 1 — Define the Loaded Network:**

The potentiometer with load becomes a **three-resistor network**:
- $R_x$ between end-A and wiper
- $(R_T - R_x)$ between wiper and end-B
- $R_L$ between wiper and end-B (in parallel with the segment $R_T - R_x$)

**Step 2 — Compute the Equivalent Resistance from Wiper to End-B:**

$$R_{eq} = (R_T - R_x) \parallel R_L = \frac{(R_T - R_x) \cdot R_L}{(R_T - R_x) + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule on the Loaded Divider:**

The total resistance from end-A to end-B is $R_x + R_{eq}$. The voltage at the wiper is:
$$e_o' = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_x + R_{eq}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_{eq}$ from Step 2:**

$$e_o' = V_s \cdot \frac{\dfrac{(R_T - R_x) R_L}{R_T - R_x + R_L}}{R_x + \dfrac{(R_T - R_x) R_L}{R_T - R_x + R_L}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $(R_T - R_x + R_L)$:**

$$e_o' = V_s \cdot \frac{(R_T - R_x) R_L}{R_x (R_T - R_x + R_L) + (R_T - R_x) R_L}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the Denominator:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= R_x R_T - R_x^2 + R_x R_L + R_T R_L - R_x R_L \\
&= R_x R_T - R_x^2 + R_T R_L
\end{aligned}

**Step 7 — Recognize the Symmetric Form:**

Because the wiper can be defined from either end, the **symmetric** loaded output equation is conventionally written as:

$$e_o' = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}}$$

**Step 8 — Define the Loading Error:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{e_o - e_o'}{e_o} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum Loading Error Analysis:**

The product $\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ achieves its **maximum value of $\dfrac{1}{4}$** when $\dfrac{x}{L} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (wiper at the centre). Therefore, the **maximum loading error** is:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{L,\max} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{4 R_L}{R_T}} \approx \frac{R_T}{4 R_L} \quad \text{(for } R_L \gg R_T\text{)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** The loading error is **maximum at the mid-stroke** and **zero at both ends**. This is a counter-intuitive but frequently-tested fact. Designers overcome this by ensuring $R_L \geq 10 R_T$ (keeping error below ~2.5%).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution of a Wire-Wound Potentiometer

**Statement to prove:** The resolution of a wire-wound potentiometer is determined by the **number of turns** of resistance wire.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**

In a wire-wound potentiometer, the resistance element is a **helical coil of fine resistance wire**. The wiper can only contact **one turn at a time**, jumping from turn to turn as it moves.

**Step 2 — Define the Number of Turns:**

Let the total number of turns wound on the former = $N$. The resistance of one turn is:
$$R_{\text{turn}} = \frac{R_T}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Define Resolution:**

**Resolution** is the **smallest fractional change in output** that the transducer can resolve, expressed as a percentage of full-scale:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{\Delta x_{\min}}{L} \times 100\% = \frac{\Delta R_{\min}}{R_T} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Resolution:**

The smallest change in resistance is one turn, so $\Delta R_{\min} = R_{\text{turn}} = R_T / N$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{N} \times 100\%}$$

**Example:** A wire-wound pot with $N = 200$ turns has a resolution of $0.5\%$. A precision multi-turn **helipot** with $N = 5000$ turns achieves $0.02\%$ resolution.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Performance Parameter** | **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | **Cermet / Film Potentiometer** | **KTU Comment** |
|:--------------------------|:-----------------------------|:--------------------------------|:----------------|
| Resolution                | Limited by turns ($\sim 0.1\%$) | Very high (in theory infinite) | Cermet preferred for high precision |
| Output Smoothness         | Stepwise (jumpy)              | Continuous, smooth              | Cermet preferred for servo feedback |
| Power Rating              | High (5 W to 50 W)            | Low (0.5 W to 2 W)              | Wire-wound for high-power rheostat use |
| Temperature Stability     | Good                          | Excellent                       | Cermet preferred for harsh environments |
| Cost                      | Moderate                      | Higher                          | Wire-wound for cost-sensitive applications |
| Lifespan (cycles)         | $10^5$ to $10^6$             | $10^7$ to $10^8$                | Cermet far superior in wear resistance |
| Linearity                 | $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 1\%$       | $\pm 0.05\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$     | Both acceptable for KTU problems |
| Noise Level               | Higher (contact resistance)   | Very low                        | Cermet preferred for low-noise instrumentation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Potentiometer Transducer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of a complete potentiometric measurement system, from the physical measurand to the displayed/recorded output. This is the **block-level functional architecture** mapping signal flow through the transducer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Physical Measurand: Displacement x"]:::input
    P2["Mechanical Coupling: Linkage Lever Bellows Bellows_to_Pot"]:::coupling
    P3["Wiper Slider Mechanism: Translates x into Contact Position"]:::mech
    P4["Resistive Track: Uniform R_T between End A and End B"]:::resist
    P5["Excitation Source: Stable DC Voltage V_s Applied across A and B"]:::src
    P6["Voltage Divider Network: V_s Distributed Linearly along Track"]:::divider
    P7["Output Voltage Tap: e_o Sampled at Wiper Terminal"]:::tap
    P8["Loading Stage: Load Resistor R_L or Measuring Instrument"]:::load
    P9["Signal Conditioner: Amplifier Filter Buffer"]:::cond
    P10["Display or Recorder: Voltage Reading Mapped to Displacement"]:::out

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P5 --> P4
    P4 --> P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coupling fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resist fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef divider fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tap fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Linear Potentiometer — Internal Construction Schematic

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **internal electrical topology** of a linear potentiometer with the wiper at a generic displacement $x$, including the loaded output condition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["End Terminal A: Voltage + V_s"]:::endA
    P2["Resistance Segment R_x: from A to Wiper Position x"]:::segA
    P3["Wiper Contact: Sliding Tap at Position x"]:::wip
    P4["Resistance Segment R_T minus R_x: from Wiper to End B"]:::segB
    P5["End Terminal B: Ground or Zero Reference"]:::endB
    P6["Load Resistor R_L: Connected between Wiper and End B"]:::load
    P7["Output Voltage e_o: Measured between Wiper and End B"]:::vout
    P8["Excitation Source V_s: Connected between End A and End B"]:::src

    P8 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P8 --> P5
    P3 --> P6 --> P5
    P3 --> P7

    classDef endA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef segA fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wip fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef segB fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vout fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef src fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Rotary Potentiometer — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Rotary Input: Angular Displacement theta in degrees"]:::in
    P2["Shaft Coupling: Connects Input Shaft to Wiper Hub"]:::coup
    P3["Wiper Hub: Rotates with Shaft across Arc Track"]:::hub
    P4["Arc Resistive Track: Partial or Full 360 degree Arc"]:::arc
    P5["End A Terminal: Start of Track at 0 degrees"]:::tA
    P6["End B Terminal: End of Track at theta_max degrees"]:::tB
    P7["Excitation V_s: Applied across A and B"]:::exc
    P8["Output e_o theta: Proportional to theta"]:::out

    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P5 --> P4 --> P6
    P7 --> P5
    P7 --> P6
    P3 --> P8

    classDef in fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coup fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hub fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef arc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tA fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tB fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef exc fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In all three diagrams, the arrows represent the **flow of signal or mechanical motion**, not necessarily electrical current paths. The **wiper node** (highlighted in red-orange) is the central feature that converts mechanical position into an electrical voltage tap.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**"Define a potentiometer and state its general principle of operation as a displacement transducer."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **potentiometer** is a **passive, three-terminal resistive transducer** used to convert **linear or angular displacement** into a proportional **electrical voltage** signal. **[1 mark]**
>
> **General Principle of Operation:** A stable excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the two end terminals of a uniformly wound resistance element of total resistance $R_T$. **[1 mark]** This establishes a **uniform potential gradient** along the track. A movable wiper, mechanically coupled to the measurand, slides along the track and taps off a voltage $e_o$ that is directly proportional to the wiper displacement $x$ according to the relation:
> $$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_T}$$
> **[1 mark]**
> This voltage divider behaviour is the underlying principle of the potentiometer as a displacement transducer.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between a rheostat and a potentiometer in terms of terminals and function."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis of Comparison** | **Rheostat** | **Potentiometer** |
|:------------------------|:-------------|:------------------|
| **Number of terminals used** | Two terminals (one end + wiper) | Three terminals (both ends + wiper) |
| **Circuit function** | Acts as a **variable resistor** | Acts as an **adjustable voltage divider** |
| **Output quantity** | Variable resistance, not voltage | Variable output voltage proportional to wiper position |
| **Connection** | Connected in **series** in the circuit | Connected in **parallel** across the voltage source |
| **KTU application** | Used to control current (e.g., lamp dimmer) | Used to sense / control position (e.g., volume control) |

**(1 mark each for the first three rows; well-tabulated answer is awarded full 3 marks as per KTU valuation key.)**

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set A]

**A linear potentiometer has a total resistance of $R_T = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a total mechanical stroke of $L = 100 \text{ mm}$. It is excited by a DC source of $V_s = 10 \text{ V}$.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compute the output voltage $e_o$ when the wiper is displaced by $x = 40 \text{ mm}$. Also determine the sensitivity of the transducer.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A load resistance $R_L = 40 \text{ k}\Omega$ is now connected across the output. Calculate the loaded output voltage and the percentage loading error at $x = 40 \text{ mm}$. Comment on whether this loading error is acceptable for instrumentation purposes.

---

**Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
- $R_T = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10{,}000 \text{ }\Omega$
- $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $V_s = 10 \text{ V}$
- $x = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-load output voltage formula:**

$$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10 \cdot \frac{40}{100} = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{V_s}{L} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{0.1 \text{ m}} = 100 \text{ V/m}$$

This means that every 1 mm of wiper displacement produces a 0.1 V change in the output voltage.

**Step 4 — Verify using the resistance-ratio formula:**

$$R_x = R_T \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.4 = 4{,}000 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_T} = 10 \cdot \frac{4000}{10000} = 4 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (a) Answer:** $e_o = 4 \text{ V}$, Sensitivity $S = 100 \text{ V/m}$ — **[7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for answer, 1 for units]**

---

**Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — State the loaded output formula:**

$$e_o' = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

\begin{aligned}
e_o' &= 10 \cdot 0.4 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{10000}{40000} \cdot (1 - 0.4) \cdot 0.4} \\
&= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 0.25 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.4} \\
&= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 0.06} \\
&= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{1.06} \\
&= 4 \cdot 0.9434 \\
&\approx 3.7736 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Compute the loading error:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{e_o - e_o'}{e_o} \times 100\% = \frac{4 - 3.7736}{4} \times 100\% = \frac{0.2264}{4} \times 100\% \approx 5.66\%$$

**Step 4 — Check using the approximation formula (alternative verification):**

$$\varepsilon_L \approx \frac{R_T}{4 R_L} \cdot \text{(correction at non-midpoint)} = \frac{10}{4 \cdot 40} \cdot 0.96 = 0.06 = 6\% \quad \text{(close agreement)} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Comment on acceptability:**

A loading error of **5.66%** is **NOT acceptable for high-precision instrumentation** (typical tolerance is $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 1\%$). The design rule of thumb is to use $R_L \geq 10 R_T$ to keep the error below 2.5%. Here $R_L / R_T = 4$, which is insufficient.

**Part (b) Answer:** $e_o' \approx 3.77 \text{ V}$, Loading error $\approx 5.66\%$ — Not acceptable for precision use — **[7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for error calculation, 1 for engineering comment]**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set C]

**A wire-wound rotary potentiometer is used to measure the angular position of a control valve. The potentiometer has $N = 500$ turns of resistance wire, total resistance $R_T = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and is excited by $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$. The full angular range is $\theta_{\max} = 270^\circ$.**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Determine the resolution of the potentiometer, the resistance of a single turn, and the output voltage at an angular displacement of $\theta = 135^\circ$.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A buffer amplifier with input impedance $R_L = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ is connected. Calculate the new output voltage at $\theta = 135^\circ$ and the corresponding percentage loading error. Is the use of the buffer amplifier justified?

---

**Solution for Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Resolution:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{500} \times 100\% = 0.2\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the resistance of a single turn:**

$$R_{\text{turn}} = \frac{R_T}{N} = \frac{5000}{500} = 10 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Convert the angular displacement to a fractional displacement:**

$$\frac{x}{L} = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{\max}} = \frac{135^\circ}{270^\circ} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute the no-load output voltage at $\theta = 135^\circ$:**

$$e_o = V_s \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{\max}} = 24 \cdot 0.5 = 12 \text{ V}$$

**Part (a) Answer:** Resolution = 0.2%, $R_{\text{turn}} = 10 \text{ }\Omega$, $e_o = 12 \text{ V}$ — **[7 marks: 2 for resolution, 2 for turn resistance, 3 for output voltage with full working]**

---

**Solution for Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the loaded output formula:**

Given $R_L = 100 \text{ k}\Omega = 100{,}000 \text{ }\Omega$ and the wiper is at the midpoint ($x/L = 0.5$, so the product $(1 - x/L)(x/L) = 0.25$):

\begin{aligned}
e_o' &= V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot 0.25} \\
&= 24 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{5000}{100000} \cdot 0.25} \\
&= 12 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 0.0125} \\
&= 12 \cdot \frac{1}{1.0125} \\
&= 12 \cdot 0.98765 \\
&\approx 11.852 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Compute the loading error:**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{12 - 11.852}{12} \times 100\% \approx 1.234\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the rule-of-thumb:**

The ratio $R_L / R_T = 100{,}000 / 5000 = 20$, which exceeds the recommended minimum of 10. The error is small (just over 1%) but not negligible for high-precision work.

**Step 4 — Justify the use of the buffer amplifier:**

A buffer amplifier (voltage follower with unity gain and very high input impedance, typically $> 10^{10} \text{ }\Omega$) presents an effective $R_L \to \infty$ to the potentiometer, eliminating the loading error entirely. Therefore, **the use of the buffer amplifier is fully justified** — it prevents the loading effect, preserves linearity, and allows the potentiometer to drive low-impedance loads (cables, ADCs, etc.) without distortion.

**Part (b) Answer:** $e_o' \approx 11.852 \text{ V}$, $\varepsilon_L \approx 1.23\%$, Buffer use is **justified** — **[7 marks: 3 for loaded calculation, 2 for error, 2 for engineering justification]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Potentiometer Problems (Read Carefully Before the Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_x$ with $(R_T - R_x)$:** Students often define $R_x$ as "resistance from the wiper to ground" instead of "resistance from the **end-A** to the wiper." This reverses the formula direction. **Always state your definition explicitly at the start of your solution.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the loading error entirely:** When a load $R_L$ is given in the problem, the no-load formula $e_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ is **wrong**. KTU examiners will deduct 3-4 marks for using the no-load formula in a loaded scenario.
>
> 3. **Mixing units:** The total length $L$ may be given in **mm** while the displacement $x$ may be in **m** (or vice versa). **Always convert both to the same unit before substituting into the formula.** A 1-mark penalty is common.
>
> 4. **Ignoring resolution in wire-wound pots:** A common viva question is "Why is the output of a wire-wound pot *stepwise* and not smooth?" The correct answer is the **finite resolution = 1/N**, not "it has poor quality."
>
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on the loading error's acceptability:** KTU valuation keys explicitly require an **engineering interpretation sentence** (e.g., "This error is acceptable / not acceptable for..."). Skipping this loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 6. **Wrong formula for sensitivity:** Sensitivity is $\Delta e_o / \Delta x = V_s / L$, **not** $R_T / L$ and **not** $V_s / R_T$. Memorize the correct form: $S = V_s / L$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A potentiometer is a **3-terminal, passive, resistive transducer** that converts **mechanical displacement** (linear or angular) into a **proportional output voltage** using the **voltage divider principle**. **[Critical]**
- **Core Output Formula (No Load):** $e_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{L} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_T}$ — Memorize in **both forms**. **[Critical]**
- **Sensitivity:** $S = V_s / L$ — Units are **V/m** (linear) or **V/deg** (rotary). **[Critical]**
- **Loaded Output Formula:** $e_o' = V_s \cdot \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{R_T}{R_L} \cdot (1 - \dfrac{x}{L}) \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}}$ — Use whenever $R_L$ is given. **[Critical]**
- **Maximum Loading Error:** Occurs at **mid-stroke** ($x/L = 0.5$) and equals approximately $R_T / (4 R_L)$ for $R_L \gg R_T$. **[Important]**
- **Resolution of Wire-Wound Pot:** $\dfrac{1}{N} \times 100\%$ where $N$ is the number of turns. **[Important]**
- **Three Terminals:** End-A, End-B, and **Wiper** (sliding contact). Rheostat uses only **two** terminals. **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Construction Types:** Wire-wound (high power, stepwise) vs. Cermet/Film (high resolution, smooth). **[Frequently Asked]**
- **Frequency Response:** Flat from **DC to infinity** (no reactive elements). **[Sometimes Asked]**
- **Order:** It is a **0th-order (zeroth-order)** transducer with a **static (algebraic) transfer function**. **[Sometimes Asked]**
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** Choose $R_L \geq 10 R_T$ to keep loading error below 2.5%. **[Important for Design Problems]**
- **Buffer Amplifier Use:** Always use a **unity-gain buffer (voltage follower)** when the load impedance is not at least 10× the potentiometer resistance. **[Critical for Practical Design]**
- **Common Applications:** Throttle Position Sensor, Joystick, Audio Fader, Slide Position Sensor, Fuel Level Gauge, Valve Position Indicator. **[General Awareness]**
- **Loading Error Sign:** Loading **always reduces** the output magnitude compared to the ideal no-load value (since the parallel $R_L$ provides an additional current path). **[Important]**
- **KTU-2024 Specific:** Be ready for a **3-mark question** asking for the difference between rheostat and potentiometer, and a **7-mark sub-question** asking for a numerical on loaded output voltage with loading error computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Form of DC Potentiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modern Form of DC Potentiometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Modern DC Potentiometer** is an advanced, refined, and highly precise version of the classical (simple) Poggendorff DC potentiometer. It incorporates auxiliary features such as **Vernier slide-wires**, **multi-range shunting networks**, **calibrated standard-cell compensation circuits**, and **mechanical/electrical null-detection systems** to enhance the accuracy, range, and ease of measurement of unknown DC EMFs, voltages, and currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEICT722 Module 3):**
> "Modern DC potentiometers are precision instruments employing Vernier, multi-range, and standardized circuit arrangements to measure DC voltages with accuracies better than 0.01% and provide direct reading of unknown EMFs without loading the source."

The four principal modern forms conventionally covered under the KTU 2024 Scheme are:
1. **Vernier DC Potentiometer** (used for very high precision)
2. **Multi-Range DC Potentiometer** (used for different voltage ranges)
3. **Crompton DC Potentiometer** (used for DC voltmeters/ammeter calibration)
4. **Drysdale (Polar) DC Potentiometer** (used for both DC and AC measurements)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Digital Scale vs. the Jeweler's Balance"**
>
> Imagine weighing a sack of rice. A simple spring scale is fast but only accurate to ±100 g. A jeweler's balance, however, is so sensitive that it uses a tiny extra weight (a "vernier" rider) to fine-tune the reading — making it accurate to 0.01 g. The **classical potentiometer** is like the spring scale (good for general use), while the **modern potentiometer** is the jeweler's balance — it uses additional precision techniques (Vernier slides, range shunts, calibrated reference cells) to push accuracy from ±0.1 % down to ±0.001 %.

## 1.3 Why "Modern" Is Needed — Limitations of Simple Potentiometer

The basic (simple) Poggendorff potentiometer suffers from three major practical limitations:

| Limitation | Description | Modern Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Accuracy** | Readings limited to main slide-wire resolution | **Vernier slide-wire** adds fractional precision |
| **Single Range** | Cannot measure voltages beyond the slide-wire voltage drop | **Multi-range shunts** allow range switching |
| **No Direct Reading** | Requires manual calculation of unknown EMF | **Calibrated dials and direct-reading scales** |
| **No AC Capability** | Works only for DC | **Drysdale polar type** with phase-shifting circuits handles AC |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Standard Cell EMF:** **1.0186 V** (Weston Standard Cell at 20 °C)
- **Working Current (typical):** **0.1 mA** to **10 mA** (range dependent)
- **Accuracy of Modern Potentiometers:** **±0.01 % to ±0.001 %** of full scale
- **Resolution of Vernier slide-wire:** **0.0001 V** (100 µV)
- **Operating Temperature:** **20 °C ± 1 °C** (controlled environment)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Vernier DC Potentiometer

### 2.1.1 Construction and Working Principle

A **Vernier potentiometer** uses **two slide-wires in series** — a **main slide-wire** and an **auxiliary (vernier) slide-wire**. The unknown EMF is first balanced on the main slide-wire (coarse reading). The small residual unbalance is then read with extreme precision on the vernier slide-wire.

### 2.1.2 Circuit Operation Steps

- The main slide-wire is energized by a stable working current $I$.
- The unknown EMF $E_x$ is balanced at a contact point on the main slide-wire, giving a coarse reading $V_1 = I \cdot R_1$.
- The **residual voltage** $\Delta V = E_x - V_1$ is transferred to the vernier slide-wire.
- The vernier slide-wire carries a slightly different current (achieved via a parallel resistor) so its scale is expanded.
- The vernier reading $V_2$ adds the fine-resolution value.
- The total unknown EMF becomes:

$$E_x = V_1 + V_2 = I \cdot R_1 + I' \cdot R_2$$

where $I$ is the main slide-wire current and $I'$ is the vernier slide-wire current (typically $I' < I$ to spread the scale).

### 2.1.3 Why Vernier Works — The 'How'

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** If the main slide-wire is 1 m long and 1.0186 V is dropped across it, each millimetre represents **1.0186 mV**. By adding a vernier slide-wire with a *smaller* current, the same physical length drops a *smaller* voltage — but the divisions now read **microvolts** instead of millivolts, multiplying the resolution by 1000×.

### 2.1.4 High-Yield Vernier Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Total Unknown EMF | $E_x = I \cdot R_1 + I' \cdot R_2$ | Coarse + fine reading |
| Vernier resolution | $\Delta V_{vernier} = I' \cdot \Delta r$ | Smallest readable voltage |
| Main slide-wire current | $I = \dfrac{E_{std}}{R_{std}}$ | Standardized via Weston cell |
| Vernier-to-Main current ratio | $k = \dfrac{I'}{I} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage-divider relationship |

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Multi-Range DC Potentiometer

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

A **multi-range potentiometer** uses a single calibrated slide-wire with **range-changing shunts** (parallel resistors) connected across the galvanometer. By selecting different shunt values, the working current through the slide-wire is changed, allowing the same instrument to read different voltage ranges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common multi-range units:** Leeds & Northrup **K-3**, **K-5**, **Type 7650** — all use rotary range switches with calibrated $1\times$, $0.5\times$, $0.1\times$, $0.01\times$ multipliers.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

- The slide-wire is calibrated for the **highest range** (say, 0 to 1.6 V).
- To measure smaller voltages (e.g., 0 to 0.16 V), the working current is reduced by **10×** using a range shunt.
- The dial readings are then **multiplied by the range factor** to obtain the true voltage.
- Standard cell standardization is performed **before** every range change to maintain accuracy.

### 2.2.3 Multi-Range Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Slide-wire full-scale voltage | $V_{fs} = I \cdot R_{sw}$ | For the highest range |
| Working current for range $n$ | $I_n = \dfrac{I_1}{n}$ | Reduced by range factor $n$ |
| True unknown EMF | $E_x = n \cdot V_{dial}$ | Dial × range multiplier |
| Standardization equation | $I_n \cdot R_{std} = E_{std}$ | Required to fix $I_n$ |

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Crompton DC Potentiometer

### 2.3.1 Design Philosophy

The **Crompton potentiometer** is a **deflectional** type using two dials — a **coarse dial** and a **fine dial** — that are connected through a chain of resistors. It uses a **standard cell** connected in opposition to a portion of the slide-wire for direct calibration.

### 2.3.2 Key Features

- **Two-dial construction** (coarse + fine)
- **Standard-cell compensating circuit** inbuilt
- **No auxiliary slide-wire** (unlike Vernier)
- **Higher accuracy** through finer dials (each dial has 200 steps)

### 2.3.3 Crompton Working Equations

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Standardizing condition | $I \cdot R_{s} = E_{std}$ | Set working current |
| Coarse dial reading | $V_c = I \cdot R_c$ | Coarse voltage balance |
| Fine dial reading | $V_f = I \cdot R_f$ | Fine voltage balance |
| Total unknown EMF | $E_x = V_c + V_f$ | Sum of dial readings |

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Drysdale (Polar) DC Potentiometer

### 2.4.1 Unique Construction

The **Drysdale potentiometer** uses a **calibrated circular slide-wire** and a **phase-shifting transformer (polar)** arrangement. While primarily used for AC measurements, the **DC version** employs a calibrated standard cell and a specially designed galvanometer.

### 2.4.2 Polar DC Potentiometer Formulas

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Slide-wire EMF | $V_{sw} = I \cdot R_{sw}$ | Calibrated range |
| Working current standardization | $I = \dfrac{E_{std}}{R_{std}}$ | Before each measurement |
| Unknown EMF | $E_x = V_{sw} \cdot k$ | With scale multiplier $k$ |
| Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{fs}}{N_{div}}$ | Per division step |

---

## 2.5 Comparative Summary Table — KTU Exam Favourite

| Feature | Vernier | Multi-Range | Crompton | Drysdale (Polar) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No. of Slide-wires** | 2 (main + vernier) | 1 | 1 (two dials) | 1 (circular) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.001 % | ±0.01 % | ±0.01 % | ±0.05 % |
| **Voltage Range** | 0–2 V | 0.001 V to 1.6 V (multi) | 0–1.5 V | 0–1.5 V |
| **AC Measurement** | No | No | No | Yes (with rectifier) |
| **Standard Cell** | External | Inbuilt | Inbuilt | Inbuilt |
| **Common Use** | Laboratory | Calibration labs | Industry | AC/DC calibration |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Modern DC Potentiometers Are Used Today:**
> - **Calibration of digital voltmeters** in ISO 17025 NABL-accredited labs.
> - **Temperature measurement** using calibrated thermocouples and RTDs.
> - **Calibration of precision shunts** for current transformer (CT) testing.
> - **Strain-gauge and load-cell calibration** in aerospace and structural testing.
> - **Reference-standard for energy meters** in power-quality test benches.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vernier Potentiometer Reading Equation

### Statement
Show that the total unknown EMF measured by a Vernier potentiometer is the sum of the main slide-wire voltage drop and the vernier slide-wire voltage drop.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the working current in the main slide-wire.**

The main slide-wire is energized by a stable DC source through a fixed resistor $R_{std}$ set so that:

$$I_{main} = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the coarse balance voltage on the main slide-wire.**

When the slider taps a resistance $R_1$ from the start of the main slide-wire, the voltage at that point is:

$$V_1 = I_{main} \cdot R_1$$

**Step 3 — Define the vernier slide-wire current.**

The vernier slide-wire is connected in series with a parallel resistor arrangement, such that the current through the vernier wire is:

$$I_{vernier} = I_{main} \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_p + R_{vernier}}$$

where $R_p$ is the parallel range resistor and $R_{vernier}$ is the resistance of the vernier slide-wire.

**Step 4 — Express the fine balance voltage on the vernier slide-wire.**

The vernier slider taps a small resistance $\Delta R_2$ from the vernier slide-wire. The fine voltage reading is:

$$V_2 = I_{vernier} \cdot \Delta R_2$$

**Step 5 — Combine coarse and fine readings.**

The total voltage balanced against the unknown EMF is the sum of both readings. By the null-balance condition (no galvanometer deflection), the unknown EMF equals this sum:

$$E_x = V_1 + V_2 = I_{main} \cdot R_1 + I_{vernier} \cdot \Delta R_2$$

**Step 6 — Substituting the current expressions in a unified form.**

Replacing $I_{main} = \dfrac{E_{std}}{R_{std}}$:

$$E_x = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot \left[ R_1 + \frac{R_p}{R_p + R_{vernier}} \cdot \Delta R_2 \right]$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E_x = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot R_1 + \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_p + R_{vernier}} \cdot \Delta R_2}$$

**Interpretation:** The first term is the coarse balance (in volts), and the second term is the fine balance (in millivolts or microvolts), together yielding high-precision reading.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Range Potentiometer — Working Current for a Given Range

### Statement
Derive the expression for the working current of a multi-range potentiometer when the range multiplier is changed.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the basic working current at full range.**

At the highest range (multiplier $n = 1$), the slide-wire carries a current $I_1$ such that the full-scale voltage is:

$$V_{fs} = I_1 \cdot R_{sw}$$

where $R_{sw}$ is the total slide-wire resistance.

**Step 2 — Define the range-multiplier action.**

When the range switch is set to a multiplier $n > 1$, a shunt resistor $R_{sh}$ is placed in parallel with the slide-wire. The total current drawn from the supply $I_{total}$ splits into two components:

$$I_{total} = I_{sw} + I_{sh}$$

By current-divider rule, the slide-wire current becomes:

$$I_{sw} = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}}$$

**Step 3 — Set the new working current proportional to $1/n$.**

For the range multiplier to be $n$, the slide-wire current must be:

$$I_{sw} = \frac{I_1}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for the required shunt.**

$$\frac{I_1}{n} = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}}$$

Solving for $R_{sh}$:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1}}$$

**Engineering meaning:** For a range multiplier of $n = 10$ (i.e., the potentiometer now reads 0–0.16 V instead of 0–1.6 V), the shunt resistance must be $R_{sw} / 9$ — i.e., approximately **11.1 % of the slide-wire resistance**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crompton Potentiometer — Two-Dial Balance Equation

### Statement
Show that in a Crompton DC potentiometer, the unknown EMF is the algebraic sum of the coarse-dial and fine-dial voltages.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Standardize the working current.**

The Crompton circuit uses a Weston standard cell in opposition to a fixed resistor $R_{std}$ on the coarse dial. The working current is fixed such that:

$$I \cdot R_{std} = E_{std}$$

This gives:

$$I = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}}$$

**Step 2 — Coarse-dial balance.**

The coarse dial has 15 (or 20) steps of resistance $\Delta R_c$ each. When the coarse dial is set at step $m_c$, the resistance tapped is $m_c \cdot \Delta R_c$. The coarse voltage is:

$$V_c = I \cdot m_c \cdot \Delta R_c$$

**Step 3 — Fine-dial balance.**

The fine dial is connected in series with the coarse dial and has 200 steps (or 100 steps) of much smaller resistance $\Delta R_f$ each. When the fine dial is set at step $m_f$, the fine voltage is:

$$V_f = I \cdot m_f \cdot \Delta R_f$$

**Step 4 — Null-balance condition.**

At balance, the galvanometer reads zero, meaning the unknown EMF equals the sum:

$$E_x = V_c + V_f$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the working current.**

$$E_x = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot \left[ m_c \cdot \Delta R_c + m_f \cdot \Delta R_f \right]$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E_x = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot (m_c \cdot \Delta R_c + m_f \cdot \Delta R_f)}$$

**Resolution check:** If $\Delta R_f$ is 100× smaller than $\Delta R_c$, the fine dial provides 100× finer resolution than the coarse dial — the heart of the Crompton's precision.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU 2024 Style

### Problem
A Crompton DC potentiometer has a coarse dial with **15 steps of 0.1 Ω each** and a fine dial with **200 steps of 0.0005 Ω each**. The standardization is done with a **Weston cell of 1.0186 V** across a **101.86 Ω** resistor. If the galvanometer shows null at coarse dial step **8** and fine dial step **120**, calculate the unknown EMF.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the working current.**

$$I = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} = \frac{1.0186}{101.86} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the coarse-dial resistance tapped.**

$$R_c = m_c \cdot \Delta R_c = 8 \times 0.1 = 0.8 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the coarse-dial voltage.**

$$V_c = I \cdot R_c = 0.01 \times 0.8 = 0.008 \text{ V} = 8 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the fine-dial resistance tapped.**

$$R_f = m_f \cdot \Delta R_f = 120 \times 0.0005 = 0.06 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the fine-dial voltage.**

$$V_f = I \cdot R_f = 0.01 \times 0.06 = 0.0006 \text{ V} = 0.6 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total unknown EMF.**

$$E_x = V_c + V_f = 8 \text{ mV} + 0.6 \text{ mV} = 8.6 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E_x = 0.0086 \text{ V} = 8.6 \text{ mV}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Multi-Range Shunt Calculation

### Problem
A multi-range DC potentiometer has a slide-wire resistance of **1800 Ω** calibrated for **1.8 V full-scale** (working current = **1 mA**). Calculate the shunt resistance required to convert it to a **0.18 V** full-scale range.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the range multiplier.**

$$n = \frac{1.8 \text{ V}}{0.18 \text{ V}} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Apply the shunt-resistance formula.**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1} = \frac{1800}{10 - 1} = \frac{1800}{9} = 200 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the new slide-wire current.**

$$I_{sw} = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}} = 1 \text{ mA} \cdot \frac{200}{200 + 1800} = 0.1 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the new full-scale voltage.**

$$V_{fs,new} = I_{sw} \cdot R_{sw} = 0.1 \text{ mA} \times 1800 \text{ Ω} = 0.18 \text{ V} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{R_{sh} = 200 \text{ Ω}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
