# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain with a neat sketch the working of a Vernier DC potentiometer. Why is the Vernier slide-wire carrying a smaller current than the main slide-wire?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Vernier DC potentiometer consists of a main slide-wire and an auxiliary (vernier) slide-wire connected in series. The unknown EMF is first balanced on the main slide-wire for a coarse reading $V_1 = I \cdot R_1$. The residual small voltage is then balanced on the vernier slide-wire, giving a fine reading $V_2 = I' \cdot \Delta R_2$, where $I' < I$. The total EMF is:

$$E_x = V_1 + V_2 = I \cdot R_1 + I' \cdot \Delta R_2$$

The vernier slide-wire carries a smaller current so that the same physical length drops a smaller voltage — this *spreads* the divisions and increases the resolution to the order of **0.0001 V** (100 µV), making the instrument suitable for laboratory-grade measurements of small EMFs.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a multi-range DC potentiometer? How is the range changed by using a shunt resistor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-range DC potentiometer is a single calibrated slide-wire instrument capable of measuring voltages over several ranges (e.g., 0–1.6 V, 0–0.16 V, 0–0.016 V) by using a range-selector switch that places different shunt resistors in parallel with the slide-wire.

When the range is changed to a multiplier $n$, the shunt resistance required is:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1}$$

This reduces the slide-wire current to $I_{sw} = I_{total}/n$, effectively reducing the full-scale voltage by the same factor. The dial reading is then multiplied by the range factor $n$ to obtain the true unknown EMF.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Crompton DC potentiometer**. Derive the equation for the unknown EMF in terms of the standard cell EMF and the dial readings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Crompton DC potentiometer is standardized using a Weston cell of EMF **1.0186 V**. The coarse dial has **15 steps of 0.1 Ω** each, and the fine dial has **200 steps of 0.001 Ω** each. The standard-cell balancing resistor is **101.86 Ω**. When an unknown EMF is connected, the galvanometer shows null at coarse dial step **10** and fine dial step **75**. Calculate the unknown EMF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The Crompton DC potentiometer is a **deflectional-type** instrument using **two dials in series** — a coarse dial (15 steps) and a fine dial (200 steps). It uses an **inbuilt standard-cell compensation circuit**.

**Working principle:** A stable working current $I$ is first established by adjusting the rheostat until the standard cell balances a fixed reference resistor $R_{std}$ (i.e., $I \cdot R_{std} = E_{std}$). The unknown EMF is then connected in opposition to the dial network. At null balance:

$$E_x = I \cdot R_c + I \cdot R_f$$

where $R_c$ and $R_f$ are the resistances tapped on the coarse and fine dials respectively. Substituting $I = E_{std}/R_{std}$:

$$\boxed{E_x = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} \cdot (R_c + R_f)}$$

The two-dial construction gives a coarse reading to 0.1 V and a fine reading to 0.001 V, yielding a combined accuracy of ±0.01 %.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the working current.

$$I = \frac{E_{std}}{R_{std}} = \frac{1.0186}{101.86} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the coarse-dial resistance.

$$R_c = 10 \times 0.1 = 1.0 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the coarse-dial voltage.

$$V_c = 0.01 \times 1.0 = 0.01 \text{ V} = 10 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the fine-dial resistance.

$$R_f = 75 \times 0.001 = 0.075 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the fine-dial voltage.

$$V_f = 0.01 \times 0.075 = 0.00075 \text{ V} = 0.75 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the unknown EMF.

$$E_x = V_c + V_f = 10 \text{ mV} + 0.75 \text{ mV} = 10.75 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{E_x = 0.01075 \text{ V} = 10.75 \text{ mV}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **multi-range DC potentiometer**. Derive the expression for the shunt resistance required to change the range by a factor of $n$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-range DC potentiometer has a slide-wire of resistance **2000 Ω** and is calibrated for a full-scale voltage of **2.0 V**. It is required to convert it into a **0.2 V full-scale range**. Calculate: (i) the range multiplier, (ii) the shunt resistance required, and (iii) verify the new working current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Multi-Range DC Potentiometer (7 marks):**

A multi-range DC potentiometer uses a **single calibrated slide-wire** with a **range-selector switch** that places different shunt resistors in parallel with the slide-wire. The instrument is standardized with a Weston cell before every range change.

**Working principle:** When the range multiplier is set to $n$, the working current through the slide-wire is reduced to $I/n$, so the full-scale voltage reduces from $V_{fs}$ to $V_{fs}/n$. The shunt resistor required is derived from the current-divider rule.

**Derivation of $R_{sh}$:**

Total current $I_{total}$ splits as $I_{sw}$ (slide-wire) and $I_{sh}$ (shunt). By current-divider rule:

$$I_{sw} = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}}$$

Setting $I_{sw} = I_{total}/n$:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1}}$$

This allows the same instrument to read multiple voltage ranges by simply switching shunts.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_{sw} = 2000 \text{ Ω}$, $V_{fs} = 2.0 \text{ V}$, new range $V_{fs,new} = 0.2 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1:** Original working current.

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{fs}}{R_{sw}} = \frac{2.0}{2000} = 1 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 (i):** Range multiplier.

$$n = \frac{V_{fs}}{V_{fs,new}} = \frac{2.0}{0.2} = 10$$

**Step 3 (ii):** Required shunt resistance.

$$R_{sh} = \frac{R_{sw}}{n - 1} = \frac{2000}{10 - 1} = \frac{2000}{9} = 222.22 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 4 (iii):** Verify the new working current.

$$I_{sw,new} = I_1 \cdot \frac{R_{sh}}{R_{sh} + R_{sw}} = 1 \text{ mA} \cdot \frac{222.22}{222.22 + 2000} = 1 \text{ mA} \cdot \frac{222.22}{2222.22} = 0.1 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 5:** Verify the new full-scale voltage.

$$V_{fs,new} = I_{sw,new} \cdot R_{sw} = 0.1 \text{ mA} \times 2000 \text{ Ω} = 0.2 \text{ V} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{n = 10, \quad R_{sh} = 222.22 \text{ Ω}, \quad I_{sw,new} = 0.1 \text{ mA}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardize** before range change — every multi-range potentiometer *must* be restandardized with the Weston cell after switching ranges. Marks lost: **2**.
> 2. **Confusing $I$ and $I'$** in Vernier potentiometer problems. Always state: $I$ = main slide-wire current, $I'$ = vernier slide-wire current, with $I' < I$.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — $1.0186 \text{ V} = 1018.6 \text{ mV}$; missing this multiplication costs **1–2 marks** in numericals.
> 4. **Writing the wrong formula for shunt resistance** — the formula is $R_{sh} = R_{sw}/(n-1)$, **not** $R_{sw}/n$. This is one of the most common errors in KTU answer sheets.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram** — KTU examiners expect a neat labelled block/circuit diagram for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 6. **In Crompton problems, missing the "sum of coarse + fine" step** — always write $E_x = V_c + V_f$ explicitly.
> 7. **Writing "vernier potentiometer" instead of "Vernier DC potentiometer"** — full name is required in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Modern DC Potentiometers:**
>
> - **Vernier Potentiometer** → Two slide-wires (main + auxiliary). Used for highest precision (±0.001 %).
> - **Multi-Range Potentiometer** → One slide-wire + multiple shunts. Used for varied voltage ranges.
> - **Crompton Potentiometer** → Two dials (coarse + fine) in series. Standard cell inbuilt. Most widely used in industry.
> - **Drysdale Polar Potentiometer** → Circular slide-wire. Can measure AC with phase-shifting transformer.
> - **Working Current Formula (Universal):** $I = E_{std}/R_{std}$ — must be set first.
> - **Vernier Reading Equation:** $E_x = I \cdot R_1 + I' \cdot \Delta R_2$.
> - **Multi-Range Shunt Formula:** $R_{sh} = R_{sw}/(n-1)$ — memorize carefully (NOT $R_{sw}/n$).
> - **Crompton Reading Equation:** $E_x = (E_{std}/R_{std}) \cdot (m_c \cdot \Delta R_c + m_f \cdot \Delta R_f)$.
> - **Standard Cell EMF:** $E_{std} = 1.0186 \text{ V}$ (Weston, at 20 °C).
> - **Accuracy Hierarchy:** Vernier > Crompton ≈ Multi-Range > Drysdale.
> - **AC Capability:** Only Drysdale (Polar) type, with phase-shifting transformer and rectifier.
> - **Galvanometer Always in Null Condition** during measurement — no current is drawn from the source under test.
> - **Range multiplier formula:** $n = V_{fs,old}/V_{fs,new}$.
> - **Dial × Range Factor = True Voltage** (for multi-range).
> - **All modern forms use the same null-deflection principle** as the basic Poggendorff potentiometer.
> - **Common KTU exam question types:** (1) Explain + diagram (7 marks), (2) Numerical with given dial readings (7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vernier Dial Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURING INSTRUMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vernier Dial Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Vernier Dial Principle** is an advanced extension of the classical **Vernier linear measurement principle** in which the auxiliary (Vernier) scale is replaced by a **circular graduated dial** mechanically coupled to the measuring spindle through a **rack-and-pinion gear train**. Small linear displacements of the measuring jaw are converted into amplified angular displacements of a pointer across a fixed circular dial, thereby producing a faster, parallax-free, and higher-resolution readout than a sliding Vernier scale.

In formal KTU terminology, the Vernier Dial instrument is a **mechanical amplification-type linear measuring device** in which:

$$\text{Linear Displacement (Spindle)} \xrightarrow{\text{Rack-Pinion}} \text{Angular Displacement (Pointer)}$$

The **least count** is determined jointly by the **pitch of the rack**, the **number of divisions on the dial**, and the **gear reduction ratio** of the pinion assembly.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Speedometer of a Car**
>
> Imagine a car's speedometer. The wheel rotates only a few times for every kilometre travelled, but the needle moves across a large dial. A small linear movement (the car moving forward) is converted via gears into a large visible movement (the needle swinging). 
>
> **A dial caliper works exactly the same way.** When you slide the jaw a tiny amount — say, 0.01 mm — the rack (a toothed strip) pushes the pinion (a small gear) by one tooth, and the pointer attached to the pinion rotates across a tiny fraction of the dial. Multiply that tiny rotation by the gear ratio, and what was invisibly small becomes a clearly readable needle deflection.

### Why It Was Invented
The plain Vernier scale (à la Vernier Caliper) suffers from:
- **Parallax error** — the eye must align the sliding scale marks.
- **Slow reading** — counting coincident lines takes time.
- **Limited resolution** — human eye struggles below ~0.02 mm.

The **dial** solves all three problems by converting the small motion into a sweeping needle movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the official syllabus, the Vernier Dial Principle belongs to the family of *Mechanical Amplification Methods* and is the precursor to modern electronic/digital calipers. Examiners frequently ask for the *least count derivation* and the *role of the gear train*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Key Quantities

The following physical/metrical quantities govern the Vernier Dial system:

- **Pitch of the rack (p):** typically **0.5 mm** or **1 mm** (linear distance between adjacent rack teeth).
- **Number of dial divisions (n):** typically **100** or **200** divisions per full revolution.
- **Gear reduction ratio (G):** integer ratio between the drive pinion and the indicator pinion.
- **Standard least count range:** **0.01 mm** or **0.02 mm** for typical dial calipers.
- **Measurement range:** typically **0–150 mm** or **0–300 mm**.

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the trio **(p, n, G)** — every KTU question on the dial principle boils down to these three parameters.

---

## 1.4 System Components (Block-Level Preview)

A Vernier dial instrument is composed of five interacting sub-systems:

1. **Fixed Jaw / Anvil** — reference face against which the workpiece rests.
2. **Movable Jaw / Spindle** — carries the rack on its underside.
3. **Rack** — precision-toothed linear scale machined into the spindle.
4. **Pinion + Bevel Gear Train** — converts linear motion to amplified rotary motion.
5. **Dial + Pointer Assembly** — graduated circular face (typically 0–100) for direct readout.

> [!NOTE]
> Some premium dial calipers add a **revolution counter** (small secondary dial) to extend the range beyond one full pointer revolution. This uses the classical Vernier overlay on top of the dial.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Vernier dial mechanism executes measurement through a **five-stage amplification chain**:

### Stage 1 — Linear Engagement
The workpiece is gripped between the fixed anvil and the movable spindle. As the spindle slides linearly by a small distance $x$, the rack (fixed to the spindle) translates identically.

### Stage 2 — Rack-to-Pinion Conversion
The rack teeth engage the **drive pinion**. Linear motion of the rack causes the drive pinion to rotate by an angle $\theta_1$ given by:

$$\theta_1 = \frac{x}{r_1}$$

where $r_1$ is the **pitch radius** of the drive pinion. Equivalently, in terms of pitch:

$$\theta_1 = \frac{2\pi \cdot x}{p}$$

because one full pinion revolution advances the rack by exactly one pitch length $p$.

### Stage 3 — Gear-Train Amplification
The drive pinion meshes with a **bevel/idler gear pair** that steps up the rotation. If the drive pinion has $N_1$ teeth and the indicator pinion has $N_2$ teeth, the angular amplification (gear ratio) is:

$$G = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

The angular displacement transmitted to the dial shaft becomes:

$$\theta_2 = G \cdot \theta_1$$

### Stage 4 — Pointer Indication
A pointer rigidly mounted on the indicator shaft sweeps across the graduated dial. The dial is typically divided into $n$ equal divisions per revolution.

### Stage 5 — Least Count Generation
Each smallest division on the dial corresponds to a linear displacement of:

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = \frac{p}{n \cdot G}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this a true *amplification* and not just a 1:1 conversion?**
> Because $n \cdot G \gg 1$, the denominator is large, so the LC is **very small** — the same small linear motion now produces a *large, visible, and countable* pointer deflection.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Implication |
|---|---|
| $G = 1$, $n = 1$ | Reduces to direct pinion indicator — coarse |
| $G \uparrow$, $n \uparrow$ | LC $\downarrow$ — finer resolution |
| Backlash in gear train | Causes **hysteresis error** (KTU favourite) |
| Wear on rack teeth | Causes **progressive error** along length |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Print and Memorise**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Pitch of rack | $p$ | Machined constant | **0.5** or **1.0** | mm |
| 2 | No. of dial divisions | $n$ | Machined constant | **100** or **200** | divisions |
| 3 | Gear ratio | $G$ | $N_1 / N_2$ | **2, 4, 5** | — |
| 4 | Pointer angular travel | $\theta_2$ | $G \cdot (2\pi x / p)$ | variable | rad |
| 5 | **Least Count** | $\text{LC}$ | $p / (n \cdot G)$ | **0.01** or **0.02** | mm |
| 6 | Full-range travel per revolution | $x_{rev}$ | $p / G$ | **0.25** or **0.5** | mm |
| 7 | Total measurement range | $R$ | $p \cdot N_{teeth} / G$ | **150** or **300** | mm |
| 8 | Magnification (M) | $M$ | $D / (p/(n \cdot G))$ | depends on dial diameter $D$ | — |
| 9 | Hysteresis error | $\epsilon_h$ | $\Delta x_{fwd} - \Delta x_{rev}$ | usually **< 1 LC** | mm |
| 10 | Cumulative pitch error | $\epsilon_p$ | Sum of tooth-pitch deviations | spec sheet | mm |

> **Tip for KTU students:** The single most-asked derivation is **LC = p / (n·G)**. Learn the derivation, not just the formula.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Vernier Dial Principle is the workhorse of:

- **Mechanical metrology labs** — direct reading of small dimensions without parallax.
- **Machine shops** — quick GO/NO-GO checks and bore measurements.
- **Quality control** — inspection stations on production lines.
- **Field engineering** — portable, battery-free, robust.

It is also the **direct mechanical ancestor** of the modern **digital caliper**, where the rack-and-pinion is replaced by a **linear encoder (capacitive or optical)** but the geometry remains identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Least Count (LC)

### Derivation 1 — From First Principles (Single Revolution of Pointer)

**Given:**
- Pitch of rack: $p$ mm/tooth
- Number of teeth on drive pinion: $N_1$
- Number of teeth on indicator (driven) pinion: $N_2$
- Number of equally-spaced graduations on the dial: $n$
- The drive pinion and indicator pinion are rigidly connected by an idler/bevel pair giving total gear ratio $G = N_1/N_2$.

**Step 1 — Linear travel of spindle per full revolution of drive pinion.**
When the drive pinion completes **one full revolution**, every tooth has engaged the rack once, advancing the rack by exactly **one tooth-pitch**:

$$x_{rev,1} = p \quad [\text{mm}]$$

*Text:* Because the drive pinion and rack are in mesh, one revolution moves the rack by exactly one pitch length.

**Step 2 — Angular motion transmitted to the indicator shaft.**
The idler/bevel gear couples the drive pinion to the indicator pinion. For a gear pair of teeth $N_1$ and $N_2$, the angular velocity ratio is:

$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

Therefore, when the drive pinion rotates **once** ($2\pi$ rad), the indicator shaft rotates:

$$\theta_{rev,2} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2} = 2\pi \cdot G \quad [\text{rad}]$$

*Text:* Each driver revolution produces $G$ revolutions of the indicator (since $N_1 > N_2$ for amplification).

**Step 3 — Angular displacement per dial division.**
The dial face is divided into $n$ equal angular sectors. Therefore the angular width of one smallest division is:

$$\delta\theta = \frac{2\pi \cdot G}{n} \quad [\text{rad}]$$

*Text:* The total angular sweep ($2\pi G$) divided by the number of divisions.

**Step 4 — Linear spindle movement per dial division.**
This is the *Least Count* by definition — the smallest linear displacement that causes a readable pointer shift of one division:

$$\text{LC} = x_{rev,1} \cdot \frac{1}{G \cdot n} = \frac{p}{G \cdot n}$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LC} = \frac{p}{n \cdot G} \quad [\text{mm}]}
$$

*Text:* Divide the pitch (advance per driver revolution) by the total angular multiplication ($G$ driver-to-indicator ratio times $n$ dial divisions).

---

### Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A dial caliper has rack pitch $p = 0.5$ mm, dial with $n = 100$ divisions, and a gear train with $N_1 = 50$ teeth on the drive pinion and $N_2 = 10$ teeth on the indicator pinion. Find the least count.

**Step 1 — Compute the gear ratio.**

$$G = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$

*Text:* The drive pinion has five times as many teeth as the indicator pinion.

**Step 2 — Apply the LC formula.**

$$\text{LC} = \frac{p}{n \cdot G} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mm}}{100 \times 5} = \frac{0.5}{500}$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.001 \text{ mm} = 1\ \mu\text{m}}
$$

*Text:* The instrument can resolve 1 micrometre — sub-micron-class mechanical resolution.

---

### Derivation 3 — Spindle Travel per Pointer Revolution

When the pointer sweeps **one full revolution** ($n$ divisions), the spindle has moved:

$$x_{rev} = n \cdot \text{LC} = n \cdot \frac{p}{n \cdot G} = \frac{p}{G}$$

**Numerical check** (using above values):

$$x_{rev} = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.1 \text{ mm per revolution}$$

So for a **0–150 mm** range, the pointer makes:

$$N_{revolutions} = \frac{150}{0.1} = 1500 \text{ full revolutions}$$

This is why high-end dial calipers include a **revolution counter dial** — to keep track of the integer number of full pointer revolutions.

---

### Analytical Matrix — LC Versus Design Parameters

| $p$ (mm) | $n$ | $G$ | LC (mm) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 100 | 1 | 0.010 | Basic direct pinion |
| 0.5 | 100 | 1 | 0.005 | Finer pitch rack |
| 0.5 | 100 | 2 | 0.0025 | Add gear step-up |
| 0.5 | 200 | 5 | 0.0005 | High-resolution lab unit |
| 1.0 | 200 | 4 | 0.00125 | Standard workshop |

> [!NOTE]
> Trend observation: as **either $n$ or $G$ doubles**, LC halves — i.e., resolution doubles. This is the engineering lever used by designers.

---

### Derivation 4 — Error Analysis (Hysteresis)

When the spindle is moved forward by $\Delta x_f$ and then reversed back to the same nominal position, the pointer may not return to the exact original reading due to **gear-train backlash** $b$. The hysteresis error is:

$$\epsilon_h = b \cdot \frac{n \cdot G}{p} \quad [\text{dial divisions}]$$

Or in linear units:

$$\epsilon_h = b \quad [\text{mm, equivalent to the linear backlash itself}]$$

*Text:* Each tooth-space of backlash in the gear train translates directly into a linear reading error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\epsilon_h = b \cdot nG/p$ thinking it amplifies — but backlash is already in *linear* units at the rack. Don't multiply by the dial gain. The gain only converts it into *divisions*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete functional flow** of the Vernier dial principle — from mechanical input (jaw movement) through the amplification chain to the human-readable output (dial face).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WORKPIECE"] --> B["MOVABLE JAW / SPINDLE"]
    B --> C["RACK LINEAR SCALE PITCH P"]
    C --> D["DRIVE PINION N1 TEETH"]
    D --> E["IDLER BEVEL GEAR STAGE"]
    E --> F["INDICATOR PINION N2 TEETH"]
    F --> G["POINTER SHAFT ANGLE THETA2"]
    G --> H["DIAL FACE N DIVISIONS"]
    H --> I["HUMAN READER OBSERVES LC"]
    J["FIXED JAW ANVIL"] -.reference.-> A
    K["REVOLUTION COUNTER DIAL"] -.secondary readout.-> H
    L["SPRING LOADED RACK KEEPER"] -.eliminates backlash.-> C

    classDef input fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000

    class A,B,J input
    class C,D,E,F,G,L mech
    class H,I,K out
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **orange nodes** are the input/measurement boundary (jaw-workpiece interface).
- The **green nodes** are the mechanical amplification chain — the heart of the dial principle.
- The **blue nodes** are the readout subsystem — where the amplified signal becomes human-visible.
- The **dashed lines** represent auxiliary/subsidiary elements (revolution counter, spring-loaded rack keeper that pushes the rack against the pinion flank to eliminate backlash).

---

## 4.2 Gear-Train Amplification Chain (Detailed View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["RACK P 0.5 MM"] -- "1 tooth = 0.5 mm linear" --> P1["DRIVE PINION N1 50 TEETH"]
    P1 -- "mesh contact" --> B["BEVEL IDLER GEAR"]
    B -- "90 degree redirect" --> P2["INDICATOR PINION N2 10 TEETH"]
    P2 -- "rigid shaft" --> S["POINTER SHAFT"]
    S -- "5x amplified rotation" --> D["DIAL FACE 100 DIV"]
    D -- "1 div = 0.001 mm" --> OUT["READOUT 1 MICROMETRE"]

    classDef gear fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    class R,P1,B,P2,S gear
    class D,OUT output
```

---

## 4.3 Least-Count Decision Tree (How to Compute LC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: KTU PROBLEM GIVEN"] --> B{"WHICH DATA PROVIDED?"}
    B -- "p, n, G all given" --> C["USE LC = p / n times G"]
    B -- "Only p and n given" --> D["ASSUME G = 1 FOR SIMPLE PINION"]
    B -- "Reading given as pointer + main scale" --> E["READING = MAIN + N POINTER DIV x LC"]
    B -- "Pointer divisions and total range given" --> F["LC = RANGE / N_total_divisions"]
    C --> G["COMPUTE NUMERICAL LC IN MM"]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["CONVERT TO MICROMETRES IF ASKED 1 MM = 1000 MICROMETRES"]
    H --> I["FINAL ANSWER WITH UNITS"]

    classDef start fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#311B92,color:#FFF
    class A start
    class B,C,D,E,F branch
    class G,H,I final
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic of Dial Caliper Internal Layout (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSTRUMENT["DIAL CALIPER BODY"]
        direction LR
        FIX["FIXED JAW LEFT"] --- ANV["ANVIL FACE"]
        ANV --- MAIN["MAIN SCALE 0 TO 150 MM"]
        MAIN --- REV["REVOLUTION COUNTER SMALL DIAL"]
        REV --- BIG["MAIN DIAL 0 TO 100"]
        BIG --- MOV["MOVABLE JAW RIGHT"]
        MOV --- SPR["RACK SPRING KEEPER"]
        SPR --- GEAR["PINION BEVEL GEAR BOX"]
        GEAR --- POINTER["POINTER NEEDLE"]
    end
    WP["WORKPIECE BETWEEN JAWS"] -.held.-> ANV
    WP -.clamped.-> MOV

    classDef jaw fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef scale fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef readout fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    class FIX,ANV,MOV jaw
    class MAIN,REV,BIG scale
    class POINTER readout
    class SPR,GEAR mech
```

---

## 4.5 Reading Interpretation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["READ DIAL CALIPER"] --> B["STEP 1: MAIN SCALE MILLIMETRES"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: REVOLUTION COUNTER INTEGER MM"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: POINTER DIVISIONS 0 TO N MINUS 1"]
    D --> E["MULTIPLY POINTER DIV BY LC"]
    E --> F["TOTAL = MAIN + REVOLUTIONS + POINTER CONTRIBUTION"]
    F --> G["APPLY SIGN CONVENTION ZERO ERROR IF ANY"]
    G --> H["FINAL DIMENSION IN MM"]

    classDef step fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E step
    class F,G,H final
```
