# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Vernier Dial Principle. State the formula for least count.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The Vernier Dial Principle is a method of linear measurement in which the small linear displacement of a measuring spindle is converted into an amplified angular displacement of a pointer across a graduated circular dial, using a rack-and-pinion gear mechanism. This replaces the traditional sliding Vernier scale with a directly readable needle indicator, eliminating parallax error.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\text{LC} = \dfrac{p}{n \cdot G}$, where $p$ = pitch of rack, $n$ = number of dial divisions, $G$ = gear ratio.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four advantages of a dial caliper over a plain Vernier caliper.**

**Model Answer (any 4 × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**
1. No parallax error — direct needle readout.
2. Faster reading — no need to align coincident lines.
3. Finer resolution achievable via gear amplification (up to 0.01 mm or better).
4. Reduced operator skill requirement.
5. Easier reading in poorly lit conditions.
6. Less eye fatigue in repetitive inspection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B from Module 3 offers an internal choice. **Question A** and **Question B** below are the two alternative selections; the student answers **either** one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the least count of a dial caliper in terms of the rack pitch, dial divisions, and gear ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dial caliper has the following specifications: rack pitch $p = 1$ mm, dial with $n = 200$ divisions, and a gear train consisting of a 40-tooth drive pinion and a 10-tooth indicator pinion. Calculate: (i) least count, (ii) spindle travel per pointer revolution, (iii) total pointer revolutions over a 200 mm measurement range. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Define variables (1 mark):*
Let $p$ = pitch of rack (mm), $N_1$ = teeth on drive pinion, $N_2$ = teeth on indicator pinion, $n$ = dial divisions per revolution, $G = N_1/N_2$ = gear ratio.

*Step 2 — Rack engagement per driver revolution (2 marks):*
When the drive pinion completes one revolution, the rack advances by exactly one tooth-pitch:

$$x_{rev} = p \quad [\text{mm}]$$

*Step 3 — Angular gain via gear train (2 marks):*
The gear train amplifies rotation by factor $G$:

$$\theta_{indicator} = G \cdot 2\pi \quad [\text{rad per driver revolution}]$$

*Step 4 — LC derivation (2 marks):*
Linear displacement per dial division:

$$\text{LC} = \frac{p}{n \cdot G} \quad [\text{mm}]$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $p = 1$ mm, $n = 200$, $N_1 = 40$, $N_2 = 10$.

**(i) Least Count (3 marks):**

$$G = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{40}{10} = 4$$

$$\text{LC} = \frac{p}{n \cdot G} = \frac{1}{200 \times 4} = \frac{1}{800}$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.00125 \text{ mm} = 1.25\ \mu\text{m}}
$$

**(ii) Spindle travel per pointer revolution (2 marks):**

$$x_{rev} = n \cdot \text{LC} = 200 \times 0.00125 = 0.25 \text{ mm per revolution}$$

**(iii) Total revolutions over 200 mm (2 marks):**

$$N_{rev} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{0.25 \text{ mm/rev}} = 800 \text{ full revolutions}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a dial caliper with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component in the amplification chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various sources of error in a dial caliper. How is the gear-train backlash compensated in practice? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

*Construction (4 marks):* A dial caliper consists of:
1. **Fixed jaw (anvil):** provides reference face, calibrated to the zero of the main scale.
2. **Movable jaw with spindle:** slides on the main beam; carries a precision **rack** on its underside.
3. **Rack:** a linear toothed scale of pitch $p$, machined into the spindle.
4. **Drive pinion (N₁ teeth):** meshes with the rack; rotates as spindle moves.
5. **Bevel/idler gear pair:** redirects rotation by 90° and steps it up by factor $G = N_1/N_2$.
6. **Indicator pinion (N₂ teeth):** drives the pointer shaft.
7. **Dial face:** graduated into $n$ divisions, attached rigidly to the indicator shaft.
8. **Pointer:** needle fixed to indicator shaft, sweeps the dial.
9. **Revolution counter:** small secondary dial tracking integer pointer revolutions.
10. **Spring-loaded rack keeper:** holds rack against pinion flank to remove backlash.

*Working (3 marks):* When the spindle moves linearly by $x$, the rack rotates the drive pinion by $2\pi x/p$ rad. The bevel gear amplifies this by $G$. The pointer sweeps $G \cdot 2\pi x/p$ rad across the dial. Each division represents $p/(nG)$ mm linearly — the least count.

**Part (b) — Errors & Backlash Compensation (7 marks)**

*Sources of error (4 marks):*
1. **Backlash in gear train** — pointer lag on reversal.
2. **Pitch error of rack** — non-uniform tooth spacing.
3. **Eccentricity of pinion** — pointer wobble.
4. **Reading error** — parallax if dial face is tilted.
5. **Temperature error** — differential expansion of components.
6. **Wear error** — at rack-pinion contact surfaces.
7. **Zero error** — pointer not at zero when jaws are closed.

*Backlash compensation (3 marks):*
- **Spring-loaded rack keeper:** A leaf spring pushes the rack against one flank of the drive pinion, eliminating the tooth-to-tooth gap. This is the most common mechanical method.
- **Split pinion / split gear:** Two halves of the indicator pinion are spring-loaded against each other, taking up internal gear clearance.
- **Pre-loaded bevel pair:** Bevel gears are mounted with axial pre-load to remove radial play.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the gear ratio $G$:** Students often write $\text{LC} = p/n$, missing the amplification factor entirely. **Always check** if a gear train is present in the problem.
> 2. **Unit errors:** Mixing **mm** and **cm**. The pitch is *almost always* in **mm**. Final LC should match the question's requested unit.
> 3. **Sign of zero error:** If the pointer reads **+0.03** when jaws are closed, the *true* reading is **observed − 0.03**, not the other way around. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per such sign error.
> 4. **Gear ratio inversion:** Some students write $G = N_2/N_1$ instead of $N_1/N_2$. Remember: the *driver* is the rack-side pinion, and amplification occurs when the *driven* (indicator-side) pinion has **fewer** teeth.
> 5. **Ignoring the revolution counter:** For ranges > 0.25 mm/rev, students forget that an integer number of full revolutions must be added to the dial reading.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This**

- [ ] Vernier Dial Principle = **rack-pinion + gear amplification + circular dial readout**.
- [ ] Three governing parameters: **pitch $p$**, **divisions $n$**, **gear ratio $G$**.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $\text{LC} = p / (n \cdot G)$.
- [ ] **Spindle travel per pointer revolution:** $x_{rev} = p/G$.
- [ ] Gear ratio amplifies rotation; **higher $G$ ⇒ finer LC**.
- [ ] More dial divisions ⇒ finer LC.
- [ ] Spring-loaded rack keeper is the **standard backlash eliminator**.
- [ ] Total revolutions = range / $x_{rev}$.
- [ ] Zero error sign convention: **true = observed − zero error**.
- [ ] Typical dial caliper LC = **0.01 mm** or **0.02 mm**.
- [ ] Hysteresis error arises from gear-train backlash; do **not** multiply by $nG/p$ to get linear error — backlash itself is already in linear units.
- [ ] Modern digital calipers use the **same physical geometry** but replace the dial with an **electronic encoder**.
- [ ] Always state **units** in KTU numerical answers — marks are awarded for dimensional correctness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standardization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Standardization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standardization** is the process of establishing and implementing a set of *uniform criteria, reference values, and procedures* against which the output of a measuring instrument is compared, so that the measured quantity can be expressed in universally accepted units traceable to a recognized national or international standard. In metrology, **Standardization** operates on three hierarchical layers: 1. **Establishment** of a physical standard that physically embodies a unit (e.g., the International Prototype Kilogram). 2. **Dissemination** of that unit downward through a chain of progressively less-accurate working standards. 3. **Verification** of instruments by comparing them against these known references. > [!NOTE] > **Related Key Definitions (High-Yield for KTU)** > - **Calibration**: Comparison of an instrument's reading against a *known standard* under specified conditions. Result: a calibration curve or correction factor. > - **Traceability**: An unbroken chain of comparisons, each having stated uncertainties, that connects an instrument's reading to a national/international standard. > - **Accuracy**: Closeness of a measurement to the *true value* (defined by the standard). > - **Precision**: Closeness of repeated measurements to each other (independent of standardization). ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) Imagine you and your friend both bring a "one-foot ruler" to a cricket ground. Your ruler might be slightly shorter, your friend's slightly longer. If you measure a wicket-to-wicket pitch, you'll get two different answers. The match referee arrives with an **officially certified 1-foot bar** (the standard). He checks both your rulers, marks corrections, and stamps them *standardized*. Now every measurement using those rulers can be compared to the certified bar — and through it, to every other measurement in the world. In the same way: - The **certified bar** = the **Primary Standard** (kept under strict conditions at NPL/NIST). - **Your ruled ruler after correction** = a **Working Standard** (used on the shop floor/lab). - **The unbroken stamp-chain back to the certified bar** = **Traceability**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) The seven base units of the **SI (Système International)** that define all standardization are based on fixed physical constants: | Quantity | SI Base Unit | Defining Constant (Fixed Value) | |---|---|---| | Length | **metre (m)** | Speed of light *c* = 299 792 458 m/s | | Mass | **kilogram (kg)** | Planck constant *h* = 6.626 070 15 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s | | Time | **second (s)** | Caesium-133 hyperfine transition = 9 192 631 770 Hz | | Electric current | **ampere (A)** | Elementary charge *e* = 1.602 176 634 × 10⁻¹⁹ C | | Temperature | **kelvin (K)** | Boltzmann constant *k* = 1.380 649 × 10⁻²³ J/K | | Amount of substance | **mole (mol)** | Avogadro number *Nₐ* = 6.022 140 76 × 10²³ mol⁻¹ | | Luminous intensity | **candela (cd)** | Luminous efficacy of 540 THz radiation = 683 lm/W | > [!IMPORTANT] > Since the 2019 SI redefinition, **all seven base units are defined by fixed fundamental constants** rather than physical artifacts. This is the modern cornerstone of *primary standardization*. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Pyramid of Standards (Dissemination Chain) A measurement is only as good as the standard behind it. KTU expects the **chain of traceability** in order from most-accurate to least-accurate: - **Level 1 – International Standards** Maintained by the **Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM)** in Sèvres, France. Realizations of the SI units using fundamental constants. - **Level 2 – National Standards** Held by national laboratories such as: - **NPL – India** (National Physical Laboratory, New Delhi) — the custodian of Indian standards. - **NIST – USA** (National Institute of Standards and Technology). - **NPL – UK** (National Physical Laboratory, Teddington). - **Level 3 – Primary (Reference) Standards** Kept at accredited calibration labs. Used to calibrate secondary standards; very low uncertainty (e.g., ±0.001 %). - **Level 4 – Secondary (Transfer) Standards** Used in industrial calibration labs. Routinely checked against primary standards. - **Level 5 – Working Standards** Used on the shop floor, in test benches, and with measurement instruments for daily use. - **Level 6 – Measuring Instruments (the device under calibration).** > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU Problems:** Uncertainty roughly *multiplies by 10* (i.e., one full order of magnitude) at each step as you move *down* the pyramid. ### 2.2 Classification of Standards by Physical Quantity - **Length / Dimensional Standards:** line standards (end, line), gauge blocks (Johansson blocks, grade K – calibration, grade 0/1/2 – inspection/working). - **Mass Standards:** Class E₁ (primary), E₂, F₁, F₂, M₁, M₂ (working weights). - **Time & Frequency Standards:** Caesium atomic clock, rubidium standards, quartz oscillators. - **Temperature Standards:** Fixed-point cells (Ice point 0 °C, Steam point 100 °C, Zinc 419.527 °C, etc.), Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer (SPRT). - **Electrical Standards:** - DC voltage: **Zener-diode-based DC reference (Weston cell alternative)**. - Resistance: **Thomas-type 1 Ω standard** (low temperature coefficient). - Current: computed via Ohm's law and Josephson voltage standard. - **Pressure Standards:** Dead-weight tester (primary), mercury manometer. - **Force Standards:** Universal testing machine calibrated with load cells traceable to dead-weight machines. ### 2.3 Calibration vs Standardization — A Common Confusion | Aspect | Calibration | Standardization | |---|---|---| | **Goal** | Determine instrument error / drift | Bring readings onto a universally accepted reference | | **Output** | Correction factor / curve | Stamped, traceable reading | | **Frequency** | Periodic (scheduled) | Done *after* calibration; required for legal/contractual proof | | **Legal weight** | Internal QA only | Required by ISO 9001, ISO/IEC 17025, regulatory bodies | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2-mark questions:** Standardization = Calibration + Documentation + Traceability + Sign-off. Calibration alone is *not* standardization. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Table Format) | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Absolute Error | $e_a = x_m - x_t$ | where $x_m$ = measured, $x_t$ = true (standard) | | 2 | Relative (Fractional) Error | $e_r = \dfrac{x_m - x_t}{x_t}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Percentage Error | $\%e = e_r \times 100$ | Used in accuracy class | | 4 | Accuracy (as %) | $A = 100 - \vert e_r \vert \times 100$ | Higher = better | | 5 | Combined Uncertainty (root-sum-square) | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum_i u_i^2}$ | Independent random sources | | 6 | Expanded Uncertainty (k = 2 ≈ 95 % CL) | $U = k \, u_c$ | Reported on calibration certificate | | 7 | Sensitivity of instrument | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output per unit input | | 8 | Linearity deviation | $\delta_L = \dfrac{(y_{act} - y_{ideal})_{max}}{y_{FS}}$ | × 100 % gives % linearity | | 9 | Repeatability (Bland-Altman style) | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_n}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | $n \ge 10$ trials | | 10 | Standard cell EMF (Weston) | $E_t = E_{20} - 0.0000406(t-20) - 0.00000095(t-20)^2$ | Volts; $t$ in °C | | 11 | Gauge block length (thermal) | $L_t = L_{20}\,[1 + \alpha(t-20) + \beta(t-20)^2]$ | $\alpha$ ≈ 11.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C for steel | | 12 | Total expanded uncertainty for traceability chain | $U_{total} = \sqrt{U_1^2 + U_2^2 + U_3^2 + \dots}$ | Add in quadrature | > [!WARNING] > The pipe `|` symbol is **not** used inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value and conditional expressions. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing:** A CNC machine's probe is standardized against gauge blocks so that a 50.000 mm cut anywhere in the world is *traceably* 50.000 mm. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Balances in production must trace to E₁/E₂ mass standards for FDA/ISO compliance. - **Power Grid:** Voltage transformers at substations are standardized against Josephson voltage standards (±0.05 ppm) to ensure billing accuracy. - **Aerospace & Defense:** Pressure altimeters and inertial sensors trace to NPL/NIST — non-negotiable for flight safety. - **Calibration Industry:** NABL (India) and A2LA (US) accredit labs only if they maintain a documented traceability chain. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Propagation of Uncertainty Through a Two-Stage Calibration Chain Consider a working standard $W$ calibrated against a secondary standard $S$, which is itself calibrated against a primary standard $P$. Each comparison has its own standard uncertainty: $u_{W}$, $u_{S}$, and $u_{P}$ respectively. **Step 1 — Model the measured value of W as the sum of three independent contributions.** The working standard's calibrated value $y_W$ is mathematically modelled as: $$ y_W = y_P + \delta_{S} + \delta_{W} $$ where: - $y_P$ is the value assigned to the primary standard (a fixed constant of the system). - $\delta_{S}$ is the correction obtained when comparing $S$ to $P$. - $\delta_{W}$ is the correction obtained when comparing $W$ to $S$. **Step 2 — Assume the three errors are statistically independent (zero mean, Gaussian).** For independent random variables, variances add. $$ u_c^2(y_W) = u^2(\delta_S) + u^2(\delta_W) $$ Note: $y_P$ is a *defined* constant (zero uncertainty contribution in modern SI), so $u(y_P) = 0$. **Step 3 — Express the *standard* uncertainty of the secondary and working steps individually.** Let $u_{S \mid P}$ be the standard uncertainty of the secondary-vs-primary comparison, and $u_{W \mid S}$ the standard uncertainty of the working-vs-secondary comparison. $$ u_c^2(y_W) = u_{S \mid P}^{\,2} + u_{W \mid S}^{\,2} $$ **Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain the combined standard uncertainty.** $$ u_c(y_W) = \sqrt{u_{S \mid P}^{\,2} + u_{W \mid S}^{\,2}} $$ **Step 5 — Apply the coverage factor $k = 2$ for ~95 % confidence level to report the expanded uncertainty.** $$ U(y_W) = k \, u_c(y_W) = 2 \, \sqrt{u_{S \mid P}^{\,2} + u_{W \mid S}^{\,2}} $$ **Step 6 — Generalize to an $n$-step chain (full traceability pyramid).** $$ \boxed{\,U_{\text{chain}} \;=\; k\,\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i^{\,2}}\,} $$ This is the **root-sum-square (RSS)** rule used in ISO/IEC Guide 98-3 (GUM). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Expansion Correction of a Gauge Block A gauge block labelled as $L_{20} = 50.000\,\text{mm}$ at 20 °C is used at $t = 28$ °C. Its actual length is given by the linear-thermal expansion law. **Step 1 — State the governing equation.** $$ L_t = L_{20}\,\bigl[\,1 + \alpha\,(t - 20) + \beta\,(t - 20)^2\,\bigr] $$ For a typical steel block, the coefficients are: - $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}$ - $\beta = -0.05 \times 10^{-6}\,/^{\circ}\text{C}^2$ **Step 2 — Compute the temperature deviation.** $$ \Delta t = t - 20 = 28 - 20 = 8\,^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the first-order expansion term.** $$ \alpha\,\Delta t = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 = 92.0 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the second-order expansion term.** $$ \beta\,(\Delta t)^2 = -0.05 \times 10^{-6} \times 64 = -3.2 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 5 — Add the two terms inside the brackets.** $$ 92.0 \times 10^{-6} + (-3.2 \times 10^{-6}) = 88.8 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the actual length $L_{28}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{28} &= 50.000 \times \bigl(1 + 88.8 \times 10^{-6}\bigr) \\ &= 50.000 \times (1.000\,088\,8) \\ &= 50.000 + 50.000 \times 88.8 \times 10^{-6} \\ &= 50.000 + 0.00444 \\ &= 50.00444\;\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Express the deviation from nominal.** $$ \Delta L = L_{28} - L_{20} = +4.44\;\mu\text{m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For a Grade-K calibration block, this 4.44 µm drift is *the* quantity the calibration lab corrects via comparison with a *primary reference* standard. That correction is what makes the block **standardized** rather than merely *measured*. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Uncertainty of a Calibrated Thermometer A digital thermometer is calibrated against a Standard Platinum Resistance Thermometer (SPRT). The standard uncertainty of the SPRT is $u_{S} = 0.02\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The repeatability of the thermometer over 10 readings gives $u_{W} = 0.05\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. The resolution of its ADC contributes a rectangular-distribution uncertainty $u_{R} = 0.01/\sqrt{3} = 0.0058\,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Find the **expanded uncertainty** at $k = 2$. **Step 1 — Combine all three independent standard uncertainties in quadrature.** $$ \begin{aligned} u_c &= \sqrt{u_S^2 + u_W^2 + u_R^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(0.02)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.0058)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.0025 + 0.0000336} \\ &= \sqrt{0.0029336} \\ &= 0.0542\,^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply by coverage factor $k = 2$.** $$ U = 2 \times 0.0542 = 0.1084 \approx 0.11\,^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **Step 3 — State the final result on a calibration certificate.** The thermometer reads with an expanded uncertainty of **±0.11 °C at 95 % confidence level**, traceable to the national temperature standard. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hierarchical Traceability Pyramid The diagram below shows how a measurement made on the shop floor is linked, through an unbroken chain of comparisons, to the **BIPM / SI definition**. Each downward step introduces roughly an order-of-magnitude more uncertainty. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["BIPM Sèvres France
SI Realization via Fundamental Constants
Lowest Uncertainty Reference"] B["NPL India or NIST USA
National Primary Standards
Uncertainty 10x BIPM"] C["Accredited Lab Primary Reference
Class E1 Weights Grade K Gauge Blocks
Uncertainty 10x National"] D["Industrial Lab Secondary Standards
Transfer Standards Working Cells
Uncertainty 10x Reference"] E["Working Standards on Shop Floor
Used for Daily Calibration
Uncertainty 10x Secondary"] F["Field Measuring Instrument
The Device Under Test
Highest Uncertainty Level"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F A1["Fixed Constants c h e k NA"] -.-> A F2["Calibration Certificate Issued
with U at k 2"] -.-> F ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Solid arrows (top → bottom) represent the **dissemination of the unit** (top is most accurate, bottom is least accurate). - The dashed arrow into the top node shows that **modern SI units are fixed by fundamental physical constants**, not by artifacts. - The dashed arrow out of the bottom node shows that a **calibration certificate** is the tangible proof of standardization. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Standardization Cell The following block diagram represents a generalized **standardization station**, applicable whether the standard is electrical, dimensional, thermal, or pressure-based. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["PRIMARY STANDARD
Fixed by SI Constant
Stored in Controlled Environment"] P2["SECONDARY STANDARD
Used to Check Working Standards
Periodic Recalibration against P1"] P3["WORKING STANDARD
Used Daily in Calibration Lab
Checked against P2 Monthly"] P4["DEVICE UNDER TEST DUT
The Instrument Being Standardized"] P5["Calibration Certificate
with Correction Factor and U k=2"] P1 -- "Comparison with stated u1" --> P2 P2 -- "Comparison with stated u2" --> P3 P3 -- "Comparison with stated u3" --> P4 P4 -- "Documentation" --> P5 P5 -. "Traceability Claim" .-> P1 ``` **Functional interpretation of each block:** - **P1** is the immutable reference (e.g., Josephson voltage standard, Cs-133 clock, prototype kg realization via Kibble balance). - **P2** is the laboratory's "in-house" primary reference. - **P3** is what technicians actually handle. - **P4** is the customer's instrument. - **P5** is the document that legally binds P4 back to P1. --- ### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Standards Pyramid (Quick-Revision Visual) ``` ▲ / \ / SI \ ← Fundamental constants (2019) /______\ / \ / NATIONAL \ ← NPL / NIST /____________\ / \ / PRIMARY \ ← Reference laboratory / REFERENCE \ /____________________\ / \ / SECONDARY / \ ← Industrial calibration lab / TRANSFER \ /______________________________\ / \ / WORKING STANDARDS \ ← Shop floor /______________________________________\ / \ / MEASURING INSTRUMENTS \ ← DUT /________________________________________________\ | (Highest uncertainty — lowest in the chain) | ------------------------------------------------ ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** **Q1. Define the term *standardization* of a measuring instrument. How is it different from calibration?** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Standardization** is the process of evaluating and adjusting a measuring instrument's accuracy so that its readings conform to a known standard of measurement that is *traceable* to a national or international reference. | Feature | Calibration | Standardization | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Find the error | Bring readings to a *traceable* standard | | Output | Calibration curve / factor | Stamped, certified reading | | Documentation | Optional | Mandatory (certificate required) | > *Standardization is essentially a calibration that is supported by an unbroken chain of documented traceability.* --- **Q2. With a neat sketch, explain the *traceability chain* in measurements.** **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The traceability chain is a hierarchical pyramid that links a working instrument on the shop floor to the SI definition. From top (most accurate) to bottom (least accurate), the levels are: 1. **International Standard** (BIPM, Sèvres — based on fixed fundamental constants). 2. **National Standard** (NPL India / NIST USA). 3. **Primary / Reference Standard** (in accredited calibration labs). 4. **Secondary Standard** (industrial labs). 5. **Working Standard** (shop floor). 6. **Measuring Instrument / DUT.** Each downward step adds roughly an order of magnitude of uncertainty. The "unbroken chain" means every comparison is documented with its own uncertainty value, so a measurement made at the bottom can claim legal traceability to the SI unit at the top. *(For the diagram, refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU End-Semester Exam Pattern – Module 3 Internal Choice]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain in detail the various **categories of measurement standards** — international, primary, secondary, and working — with one example for each. State the role of the **National Physical Laboratory (NPL), New Delhi** in maintaining Indian national standards. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 50.000 mm nominal steel gauge block is used at 32 °C. The linear expansion coefficient is $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the quadratic coefficient is $\beta = -0.05 \times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}\text{C}^2$. Find the actual length and the deviation from nominal. If the calibration uncertainty of the working comparator is $u = 0.10\;\mu\text{m}$ and the standard reference's uncertainty is $u = 0.05\;\mu\text{m}$, find the **expanded uncertainty at k = 2**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** | Category | Custodian / Example | Typical Uncertainty | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **International** | BIPM, Sèvres (e.g., Cs-133 atomic clock) | < 1 ppb | Realizes SI units from constants | | **National** | NPL New Delhi (India) | ~1–10 ppb | Custodian of Indian standards | | **Primary** | NABL-accredited reference labs | ~10–100 ppb | Calibrates secondary standards | | **Secondary** | Industrial calibration labs | ~0.1–1 ppm | Calibrates working standards | | **Working** | Shop floor / production | ~1–10 ppm | Daily instrument checks | **Role of NPL, New Delhi** (2-Mark importance in answer): - Apex metrology institute of India, established 1947. - Custodian of *national standards of measurement* for all seven base quantities. - Provides calibration services to industry, defence, and research. - Represents India at the **General Conference on Weights and Measures (CGPM)**. - Conducts R&D in metrology and disseminates SI units. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Compute temperature deviation.** $$ \Delta t = 32 - 20 = 12\,^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the thermal expansion formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{32} &= 50.000 \times \bigl[\,1 + 11.5\times 10^{-6} \times 12 + (-0.05\times 10^{-6}) \times 144\,\bigr] \\ &= 50.000 \times \bigl[\,1 + 138.0\times 10^{-6} - 7.2\times 10^{-6}\,\bigr] \\ &= 50.000 \times (1 + 130.8\times 10^{-6}) \\ &= 50.000 + 50.000 \times 130.8 \times 10^{-6} \\ &= 50.000 + 0.00654 \\ &= 50.00654\;\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute deviation from nominal.** $$ \Delta L = +6.54\;\mu\text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Combine the two uncertainties in quadrature.** $$ u_c = \sqrt{(0.10)^2 + (0.05)^2} = \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0025} = \sqrt{0.0125} = 0.1118\;\mu\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Apply coverage factor k = 2.** $$ U = 2 \times 0.1118 = 0.2236 \approx 0.22\;\mu\text{m} $$ **Final Statement:** > *The gauge block's actual length at 32 °C is **50.00654 mm** with an expanded uncertainty of **±0.22 µm at k = 2** (≈95 % confidence level), traceable to the NPL India national standard of length.* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Differentiate between **calibration** and **standardization**. Discuss the procedure for standardizing a **moving-coil voltmeter** against a **DC voltage standard** (e.g., a zener-based reference). Mention the significance of a **calibration certificate** and the **chain of traceability**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A voltmeter is calibrated against a standard cell of EMF 1.01860 V. The voltmeter reads 1.020 V at its 1.5 V range. The standard cell has an expanded uncertainty $U = \pm 0.00005$ V (k = 2) and the voltmeter's own standard uncertainty due to resolution is $u_r = 0.0003$ V. Find: (i) the absolute and percentage error of the voltmeter, (ii) the combined standard uncertainty, and (iii) the expanded uncertainty at k = 2. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** **Calibration vs Standardization** (1.5 marks — provide a comparison table as in §2.3 above). **Procedure for Standardizing a Moving-Coil Voltmeter** (4 marks): 1. Connect the voltmeter in parallel with a stable DC voltage source (e.g., a zener reference driven by a constant current). 2. Allow 15–30 min warm-up for thermal stability. 3. Set source to 0 V; adjust **zero screw** until meter reads exactly zero. 4. Set source to full-scale value (here, 1.5 V) using a **potentiometric** or **Kelvin–Varley divider** referenced to the **Weston standard cell** (or modern zener reference). 5. Adjust the **series multiplier resistor** (the "swamping resistor") until the meter reads exactly 1.500 V. 6. Take readings at **five or more cardinal points** (0 %, 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, 100 % of FS). 7. Compute correction at each point; plot **calibration curve**. 8. Issue a **calibration certificate** stating: the value, the expanded uncertainty $U$ at $k=2$, the environmental conditions, the standards used, and the **traceability statement** linking the working reference back to the SI volt. **Significance of Calibration Certificate (0.5 mark):** It is the legal/documentary proof that the instrument is *standardized*; it carries the lab's accreditation logo (e.g., **NABL**) and a unique certificate number. **Chain of Traceability (1 mark):** Voltmeter → Working Zener Reference → Lab Secondary Standard → NPL Primary Voltage Standard (Josephson Array) → SI Volt (defined via $e$ and $h$). --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Compute absolute error.** $$ e_a = x_m - x_t = 1.020 - 1.01860 = +0.00140\;\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Compute percentage error.** $$ \%e = \frac{0.00140}{1.01860} \times 100 = 0.1374\,\% $$ **Step 3 — Convert expanded uncertainty of standard to standard uncertainty.** $$ u_S = \frac{U}{k} = \frac{0.00005}{2} = 0.000025\;\text{V} $$ **Step 4 — Combine in quadrature with voltmeter resolution uncertainty.** $$ \begin{aligned} u_c &= \sqrt{u_S^2 + u_r^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(0.000025)^2 + (0.0003)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{6.25\times 10^{-10} + 9.0 \times 10^{-8}} \\ &= \sqrt{9.0625 \times 10^{-8}} \\ &= 3.01 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{V} \\ &= 0.000301\;\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Apply coverage factor k = 2.** $$ U = 2 \times 0.000301 = 0.000602 \approx 0.00060\;\text{V} $$ **Step 6 — Final answer.** > The voltmeter reads with a percentage error of **+0.137 %** and the measured value is **1.020 V ± 0.00060 V** (expanded uncertainty, k = 2, ≈ 95 % confidence). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams** > > 1. **Writing "Calibration = Standardization"** — KTU examiners explicitly test the *distinction*. Always mention the role of **traceability** and the **certificate** in standardization. > 2. **Forgetting the coverage factor $k$** — If a question says "expanded uncertainty", you *must* multiply by $k$ (usually 2). Reporting only the standard uncertainty loses 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong uncertainty combination** — Independent random sources combine in **quadrature** (RSS), not by simple addition. Adding linearly is a common student error. > 4. **Unit inconsistency in thermal expansion** — Mixing °C and K is acceptable for $\Delta t$, but always state it. Mixing mm and µm in the *same* line of the final answer loses marks. > 5. **Not stating traceability path** — A standardization answer without a mention of *NPL → BIPM* (or equivalent) is considered incomplete. > 6. **Skipping the percentage sign in error** — Always write `%` after the number, not as superscript. > 7. **Confusing accuracy and precision** — Accuracy involves a *standard*; precision does not. KTU loves to test this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Standardization** = Calibration + Documentation + Traceability + Sign-off. - **Seven SI base units** (2019) are defined by **fixed fundamental constants** (c, h, e, k, Nₐ, Cs transition, Kcd). This is the foundation of primary standardization. - The **BIPM** in France is the apex; **NPL India** (New Delhi) is India's custodian. - The **traceability chain** has 6 levels: International → National → Primary → Secondary → Working → DUT. - **Uncertainty multiplies (roughly ×10) at each downward step** in the chain. - **Calibration curve** plots actual vs indicated values; the **correction** is the negative of the error. - **Combined uncertainty** uses **root-sum-square (RSS)**: $u_c = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$. - **Expanded uncertainty** at $k = 2$ corresponds to ~95 % confidence level. - **Gauge block thermal correction:** $L_t = L_{20}[1 + \alpha(t-20) + \beta(t-20)^2]$. - **Weston standard cell EMF** varies with temperature: $E_t = E_{20} - 0.0000406(t-20) - 0.00000095(t-20)^2$. - **Linearity deviation** = max deviation / full scale × 100 %. - **Calibration certificate** must include: value, $U$, $k$, environment, traceability statement, accreditation body. - **NABL** (India) and **A2LA** (US) are the key accreditation bodies whose logo on a certificate is the legal proof of standardization. - **Accuracy** ≠ **Precision**: a watch can be precise (consistent) but inaccurate (wrong by 5 minutes) — standardization fixes accuracy, not precision. - **Resolution uncertainty** is rectangular-distributed: divide the resolution by $\sqrt{3}$ to get standard uncertainty. - For KTU problems, *always* state the **traceability path** explicitly in the conclusion. ---

topics.title: Density, Surface Tension, Capillarity and Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Density, Surface Tension, Capillarity and Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Density (ρ)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Density is defined as the mass of a substance per unit volume. It is a fundamental intrinsic property of matter that quantifies how tightly the molecules of a material are packed within a given space.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $m$ = mass of the substance (kg)
- $V$ = volume occupied (m³)

**Specific Gravity (Relative Density, RD):**
It is the dimensionless ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a reference substance (usually water at 4 °C, where $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

$$RD = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific gravity is a pure number (dimensionless)** because it is a ratio of two similar quantities. It is temperature dependent and must always be reported with the reference temperature (e.g., RD at 20 °C / 4 °C).

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Suitcase Test":**
Imagine two suitcases of exactly the same size (volume). One is packed with feathers and the other with books. Both have the same volume, but the suitcase with books is *much heavier* — it has a higher density. Density essentially tells you, "How much stuff is squeezed into this space?" A small, heavy rock has high density; a large, light pillow has low density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Density matters in Measurements:** In industrial process control (KTU 2024 highlights), density measurement is critical for:
> - **Quality control** of milk, juices, fuels, and acids
> - **Concentration measurement** of solutions (e.g., sugar in juice, sulfuric acid in batteries)
> - **Detection of adulteration** in commercial liquids
> - **Level measurement** in sealed tanks (hydrostatic head depends on density)

---

### 1.2 Surface Tension (γ)

**Formal Definition:**
Surface tension is the property of a liquid surface that causes it to behave like a stretched elastic membrane. Quantitatively, it is defined as the tangential force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface.

$$\gamma = \frac{F}{L}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $F$ = force acting perpendicular to the line (N)
- $L$ = length of the imaginary line (m)

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Strider Effect":**
Have you ever seen a small insect (water strider) walking on water without sinking? That is surface tension in action! The molecules at the surface of water are "pulled" sideways and downward by neighboring molecules, but there is no molecule above them to pull them upward. This imbalance creates a "skin" or "film" on the water surface, strong enough to support tiny insects and small objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface tension decreases with temperature** because higher thermal energy reduces the cohesive forces between molecules. At the critical temperature, surface tension becomes zero (liquid and gas become indistinguishable).

---

### 1.3 Capillarity

**Formal Definition:**
Capillarity (or capillary action) is the phenomenon by which a liquid spontaneously rises (or falls) in a narrow tube (capillary tube) when the tube is dipped into the liquid. It is a direct consequence of the interplay between **cohesive forces** (liquid–liquid) and **adhesive forces** (liquid–solid).

- If **adhesion > cohesion** → liquid **rises** (e.g., water in glass tube)
- If **cohesion > adhesion** → liquid **falls** (e.g., mercury in glass tube)

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Paper Towel":**
When you dip the corner of a paper towel into water, the water climbs up the towel all by itself. This is capillarity! The tiny spaces between the paper fibers act like microscopic tubes, and adhesion between water and paper pulls the water upward against gravity.

---

### 1.4 Viscosity (μ)

**Formal Definition:**
Viscosity is the internal friction or resistance of a fluid to flow. It arises from the cohesive forces and momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. Quantitatively, it is defined by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

**Two Types of Viscosity:**
- **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** measured in Pa·s (SI) or Poise (CGS)
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$, measured in m²/s or Stokes (St)

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Honey vs. Water Test":**
Pour water and honey side by side. Water flows almost instantly, while honey crawls slowly. Honey is "thicker" because it has a **higher viscosity** — its internal friction is much greater, so its molecules resist sliding past each other. A more viscous fluid is "stickier" inside.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity classifies fluids:**
> - **Newtonian fluids** (water, air, light oils) → viscosity is **constant**, independent of shear rate
> - **Non-Newtonian fluids** (blood, paint, ketchup, mud) → viscosity changes with shear rate (pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, thixotropic)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Density — Theoretical Background

The mass of a body depends on the number and type of atoms/molecules, while volume depends on temperature, pressure, and phase. Hence, density is **temperature- and pressure-dependent**. For liquids, pressure effects are usually negligible, but temperature effects are significant. KTU examiners frequently ask about the variation of density with temperature:

$$\rho_T = \frac{\rho_0}{1 + \beta (T - T_0)}$$

where $\beta$ is the **coefficient of volumetric expansion**.

**Methods of Density Measurement (Transducer Perspective):**

| Method | Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrometer** | Buoyancy — depth of float indicates density | Liquids (battery acid, alcohol) |
| **Pycnometer** | Mass/volume of fixed-volume bottle | Laboratory precision work |
| **Hydrostatic weighing** | Apparent weight loss = buoyant force | Solids & liquids |
| **U-tube oscillating densitometer** | Natural frequency of vibrating U-tube | Online process control, fuels |
| **Radiation-based (gamma ray)** | Attenuation of γ-rays through fluid | Slurries, opaque liquids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **oscillating U-tube densitometer** is the most important *electronic transducer-based* method in modern industry. The period of oscillation $T$ relates to density as $T = k\sqrt{\rho + c}$ (where $k$, $c$ are constants of the tube). A higher density → slower oscillation → larger $T$.

---

### 2.2 Surface Tension — Theoretical Background

**Molecular Explanation:**
Within the bulk of a liquid, every molecule is pulled equally in all directions by neighboring molecules (net force = 0). However, a molecule at the surface experiences a net **inward pull** because there are fewer molecules above it. This pulls the surface molecules inward, minimizing the surface area — equivalent to a stretched membrane.

**Temperature Dependence (Eötvös Equation — KTU 2024 expects awareness):**

$$\gamma = k \left( T_c - T \right) \left( \frac{M}{\rho} \right)^{-2/3}$$

where $T_c$ is the critical temperature, $M$ is molar mass, $\rho$ is liquid density.

**Methods of Surface Tension Measurement:**

| Method | Principle | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Capillary Rise Method** | Liquid rises in a thin tube due to surface tension | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ |
| **Du Noüy Ring Method** | Force to pull a platinum ring off the surface | $\gamma = \dfrac{F}{2 \cdot 2\pi R} = \dfrac{F}{4\pi R}$ |
| **Wilhelmy Plate Method** | Force to pull a plate from the surface | $\gamma = \dfrac{F}{L \cos\theta}$ |
| **Drop Weight Method** | Weight of a detaching drop | $\gamma = \dfrac{mg}{2\pi r f}$ |
| **Maximum Bubble Pressure** | Pressure to release a bubble at the tip | $\gamma = \dfrac{r \Delta P}{2}$ |

---

### 2.3 Capillarity — Theoretical Background

**Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise Formula):**
For a liquid that wets the tube (contact angle $\theta < 90°$):

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

For a perfectly wetting liquid ($\theta = 0$, e.g., water in clean glass):

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma}{\rho g r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for KTU Exam:**
> - $\theta < 90°$ (wetting, e.g., water-glass) → $\cos\theta > 0$ → $h$ is **positive** → liquid **rises**
> - $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting, e.g., mercury-glass) → $\cos\theta < 0$ → $h$ is **negative** → liquid **falls**

**For annular (gap) capillaries** (tube inside another tube, inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$):

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g (r_2 - r_1)}$$

---

### 2.4 Viscosity — Theoretical Background

**Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Meaning:**
Consider a fluid flowing between two parallel plates, the top moving at velocity $U$ and the bottom stationary. The fluid layer in contact with the top plate moves with velocity $U$, and the layer in contact with the bottom plate is at rest. Velocity varies linearly (for steady laminar flow):

$$u(y) = \frac{U}{h} y$$

The shear stress required to maintain this motion is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

**Methods of Viscosity Measurement:**

| Method | Principle | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|
| **Ostwald Viscometer** | Time of flow through a capillary | Low-viscosity Newtonian fluids |
| **U-tube / Cannon-Fenske** | Efflux time under gravity | Transparent liquids |
| **Falling Ball / Falling Sphere** | Terminal velocity of ball under gravity | Moderate to high viscosity |
| **Rotational Viscometer (Brookfield, Cone-and-plate)** | Torque on a rotating spindle | Non-Newtonian fluids |
| **Saybolt / Redwood viscometer** | Efflux time for a fixed volume | Industrial oils (empirical) |
| **Capillary tube (Poiseuille)** | Pressure drop vs. flow rate | Laboratory precision |

**Reynolds Number — Flow Regime Indicator:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

- $Re < 2000$ → Laminar flow
- $2000 < Re < 4000$ → Transitional
- $Re > 4000$ → Turbulent

---

### KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume |
| 2 | Specific Gravity | $RD = \rho_{sub} / \rho_{water}$ | dimensionless | Reference: water at 4 °C |
| 3 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa | Pressure at depth h |
| 4 | Buoyant Force | $F_b = \rho_{fluid} g V_{displaced}$ | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 5 | Surface Tension (definition) | $\gamma = F / L$ | N/m | Force per unit length |
| 6 | Capillary Rise | $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ | m | Jurin's law |
| 7 | Du Noüy Ring | $\gamma = F / (4\pi R)$ | N/m | F = pull-off force |
| 8 | Wilhelmy Plate | $\gamma = F / (L\cos\theta)$ | N/m | F = pull-off force |
| 9 | Maximum Bubble Pressure | $\gamma = r \Delta P / 2$ | N/m | r = tube radius |
| 10 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | Pa | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |
| 11 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s | Ratio form |
| 12 | Poiseuille's Law (flow) | $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P / (8\mu L)$ | m³/s | Laminar flow in tube |
| 13 | Falling Sphere (Stokes) | $v = (2/9) \cdot r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) / \mu$ | m/s | Terminal velocity |
| 14 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | dimensionless | Flow regime |
| 15 | Stokes' Law (drag) | $F_D = 6\pi \mu r v$ | N | Drag on a sphere |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

**Setup:** A capillary tube of radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\gamma$. The liquid rises to a height $h$ above the free surface of the bulk liquid.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the liquid column inside the capillary tube:**

The liquid column of height $h$ experiences three vertical forces:
- **Upward force due to surface tension** ($F_\gamma$): Acts along the perimeter of the meniscus. The surface tension $\gamma$ acts tangentially along the contact line of length $2\pi r$. The vertical component is $F_\gamma = \gamma \cos\theta \cdot 2\pi r$, where $\theta$ is the contact angle.
- **Downward gravitational force (weight)** ($W$): $W = \rho g V_{column} = \rho g \cdot (\pi r^2 h)$.
- **Pressure at the bottom of the column**: Atmospheric pressure acts equally on both the bulk surface and the column bottom, so these cancel.

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition at the meniscus:**

At equilibrium, the net vertical force on the liquid column is zero.

$$F_{\gamma,\,up} = W_{down}$$

$$\gamma \cos\theta \cdot 2\pi r = \rho g \cdot \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 3 — Solve for h:**

Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma \cos\theta \cdot 2 &= \rho g \cdot r h \\
h &= \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (Jurin's Law):**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observations for KTU Exam:**
> 1. Capillary rise is **inversely proportional** to tube radius — narrower tube → higher rise.
> 2. The rise is **independent of tube length** (as long as the tube is longer than $h$).
> 3. For $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting), $h$ is maximum: $h_{max} = 2\gamma/(\rho g r)$.
> 4. For $\theta = 90°$ (no wetting), $h = 0$ — no capillary rise.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Pressure Difference Across a Curved Liquid Surface (Young–Laplace Equation)

**Setup:** Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ in equilibrium. The droplet has two principal radii of curvature, both equal to $R$ (for a sphere).

**Step 1 — Virtual work approach:**
Suppose the droplet radius decreases by an infinitesimal amount $dR$ due to surface tension. The change in surface area is:

$$dA = d(4\pi R^2) = 8\pi R \, dR$$

**Step 2 — Work done by surface tension:**
Surface tension acts to minimize area. The work done by surface tension when area decreases by $dA$ is:

$$dW = \gamma \cdot dA = \gamma \cdot 8\pi R \, dR$$

**Step 3 — Work done against pressure difference:**
The pressure inside the droplet exceeds the outside pressure by $\Delta P$. When the radius decreases by $dR$, the volume decreases by:

$$dV = d\left(\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3\right) = 4\pi R^2 \, dR$$

The work done against the pressure difference is:

$$dW = \Delta P \cdot dV = \Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two works (energy conservation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma \cdot 8\pi R \, dR &= \Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR \\
\Delta P &= \frac{2\gamma}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result — Young–Laplace Equation (Spherical):**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}}$$

For a soap bubble (with two surfaces, inner and outer):

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is fundamental to many measurement methods including the **Maximum Bubble Pressure** technique where $\gamma = r \Delta P / 2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Poiseuille's Equation for Laminar Flow

**Setup:** A Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu$ flows in steady laminar flow through a horizontal cylindrical pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, under a pressure difference $\Delta P$.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:**
- Pressure force pushing the element forward: $F_{push} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^2$
- Viscous drag force on the lateral surface: $F_{drag} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L$ (negative sign: drag opposes motion; velocity decreases as $r$ increases, so $du/dr < 0$ for $r < R$)

**Step 2 — Steady-state equilibrium:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 &= -\mu \frac{du}{dr} \cdot 2\pi r L \\
\frac{du}{dr} &= -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get velocity profile (Hagen–Poiseuille parabolic profile):**

Apply boundary condition: at $r = R$, $u = 0$ (no-slip at the wall).

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{u}^{0} du &= -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{r}^{R} r \, dr \\
-u &= -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^2 - r^2}{2} \\
u(r) &= \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)
\end{aligned}$$

This is a **parabolic** velocity profile — maximum at the center ($r=0$), zero at the wall.

**Step 4 — Compute volumetric flow rate $Q$:**

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr = \left[\frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Final result (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation):**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insights for KTU Exam:**
> 1. Flow rate is **proportional to $R^4$** — halving the radius reduces flow to 1/16th! This is why narrow tubes choke easily.
> 2. Flow rate is **inversely proportional to $\mu$** — higher viscosity → lower flow.
> 3. This equation is the **foundation of the capillary viscometer** measurement: $Q = V/t$, so $\mu = \pi R^4 \Delta P \cdot t / (8 L V)$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Terminal Velocity (Falling Sphere — Stokes' Law)

**Setup:** A sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ falls through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$, under gravity $g$. It reaches a constant (terminal) velocity $v_t$ when the net force = 0.

**Step 1 — Identify the three forces on the sphere:**
- **Weight** (down): $W = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g$
- **Buoyancy** (up): $F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g$
- **Viscous drag** (up, Stokes' law): $F_D = 6\pi \mu r v_t$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium at terminal velocity:**

$$W = F_b + F_D$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g + 6\pi \mu r v_t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_t$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
6\pi \mu r v_t &= \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) \\
v_t &= \frac{4\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{3 \cdot 6\pi \mu r} \\
v_t &= \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (Stokes' Terminal Velocity):**

$$\boxed{v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}}$$

Solving for viscosity (used in falling-ball viscometer):

$$\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Stokes' law is valid only for **laminar flow** past the sphere, i.e., $Re \ll 1$. For higher Reynolds numbers, a correction factor (e.g., the Oseen or Faxén correction) is needed.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Measurement Method

| Property to Measure | Best Method for Lab Precision | Best Method for Industrial Online Use | Typical Sensor Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (liquids)** | Pycnometer, hydrostatic balance | Oscillating U-tube densitometer | Frequency / period |
| **Surface tension** | Capillary rise, drop weight | Du Noüy ring tensiometer, Wilhelmy plate | Force (N) |
| **Viscosity (Newtonian)** | Capillary viscometer (Ostwald) | Vibrating reed viscometer, rotational | Pressure drop, torque |
| **Viscosity (Non-Newtonian)** | Cone-and-plate rotational | In-line rotational viscometer | Torque vs. RPM |
| **Capillarity height** | Direct scale reading | Optical/ultrasonic level sensor in capillary | Distance / time-of-flight |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Density Measurement Transducer Chain

The following Mermaid diagram shows the full signal chain for an industrial density measurement system, from the primary sensing element to the final display/output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Liquid] --> B[Primary Sensor: U-Tube Oscillator]
    B --> C[Oscillation Period T increases with density]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning: Charge Amplifier]
    D --> E[Microcontroller / DSP Unit]
    E --> F[Linearization: T = k sqrt rho + c]
    F --> G[Display Unit: Density in kg per cubic meter]
    F --> H[4-20 mA Current Loop Output]
    F --> I[HMI / SCADA Interface]
    I --> J[Alarm and Control Action]
    H --> K[PLC for Process Control]
    K --> L[Final Control Element: Valve or Pump]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The U-tube oscillator is the **primary sensing element**.
- The density affects the oscillation period, which is converted to an electrical signal.
- The signal is conditioned, linearized, and presented as a density reading.
- A 4–20 mA current loop output is sent to the PLC for process control.

---

### 4.2 Capillary Rise — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Capillary Tube radius r] --> B[Inserted in Liquid]
    B --> C{Contact Angle theta}
    C -->|theta less than 90| D[Adhesion dominant]
    C -->|theta greater than 90| E[Cohesion dominant]
    D --> F[Liquid RISES to height h]
    E --> G[Liquid FALLS to depth h]
    F --> H[Jurins Law: h = 2 gamma cos theta divided by rho g r]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Measure h to find gamma]
```

---

### 4.3 Viscosity Measurement — Comparative Method Selector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Measure Viscosity] --> B{Fluid Type}
    B -->|Newtonian, transparent| C[Capillary Viscometer Ostwald U-tube]
    B -->|Newtonian, opaque| D[Falling Ball Viscometer]
    B -->|Non-Newtonian| E[Rotational Viscometer Brookfield]
    B -->|Industrial, online| F[Vibrating Viscometer]
    C --> G[Apply Poiseuille Equation]
    D --> H[Apply Stokes Equation]
    E --> I[Measure Torque vs. RPM]
    F --> J[Measure Damping of Vibration]
    G --> K[Compute mu]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output: Viscosity in Pa.s]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Summary — Property → Method → Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Property of Fluid] --> P1[Density rho]
    P --> P2[Surface Tension gamma]
    P --> P3[Capillary Rise h]
    P --> P4[Viscosity mu]
    P1 --> M1[U-tube Oscillator]
    P1 --> M2[Hydrometer]
    P2 --> M3[Capillary Rise]
    P2 --> M4[Du Nouy Ring]
    P3 --> M5[Direct Observation]
    P4 --> M6[Ostwald Viscometer]
    P4 --> M7[Rotational Viscometer]
    M1 --> F1[rho = m per V]
    M2 --> F1
    M3 --> F2[h = 2 gamma cos theta per rho g r]
    M4 --> F3[gamma = F per 4 pi R]
    M5 --> F2
    M6 --> F4[Q = pi R4 delta P per 8 mu L]
    M7 --> F5[tau = mu du per dy]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following terms with their SI units:
(a) Dynamic viscosity
(b) Kinematic viscosity
(c) Specific gravity

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion of its layers. From Newton's law of viscosity, $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. **SI unit: Pa·s (N·s/m²).**

(b) **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid, $\nu = \mu/\rho$. **SI unit: m²/s.**

(c) **Specific Gravity (RD):** It is the ratio of the density of the substance to the density of water at 4 °C, $RD = \rho_{sub}/\rho_{water}$. **Unit: dimensionless.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State Jurin's law. What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer:**

**Jurin's Law:** The height of liquid rise in a capillary tube is given by $h = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$, where $\gamma$ is surface tension, $\theta$ is the contact angle, $\rho$ is the density, $g$ is acceleration due to gravity, and $r$ is the radius of the tube.

**Physical Significance:**
- It is the **basis of the capillary rise method** for measuring surface tension.
- It shows that **rise height is inversely proportional** to the tube radius — narrower tubes give higher rise.
- It is used in **oil recovery, soil moisture studies, and wicking action** in textiles.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **capillary rise** of a liquid in a narrow tube. Discuss the role of **contact angle** in determining whether the liquid rises or falls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **capillary rise method** for the measurement of surface tension. A glass capillary tube of diameter 0.8 mm is dipped in water at 20 °C. If the contact angle is $0°$, calculate the height of water rise. Take $\gamma_{water} = 0.0727 \text{ N/m}$ and $\rho_{water} = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Capillary Rise:**

[Refer to Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The student must show:]
- Free body diagram of the liquid column inside the tube.
- Force balance: $2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta = \pi r^2 \rho g h$.
- Final expression: $h = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$.
- Discussion: If $\theta < 90°$ (water-glass) → $\cos\theta > 0$ → liquid **rises**. If $\theta > 90°$ (mercury-glass) → $\cos\theta < 0$ → liquid **falls**. If $\theta = 90°$ → no rise or fall.

**(b) Capillary Rise Method & Numerical:**

**Diagram description (verbal):** A vertical glass capillary tube of radius $r$ is dipped into a beaker containing the test liquid. The liquid rises inside the tube to a height $h$ above the free surface. A travelling microscope or vernier scale is used to measure $h$ accurately. The contact angle $\theta$ is measured separately (or assumed $0°$ for clean glass and pure water).

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 0.8$ mm → radius $r = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $\theta = 0°$ → $\cos\theta = 1$
- $\gamma = 0.0727$ N/m
- $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Calculation:**

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} = \frac{2 \times 0.0727 \times 1}{998 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:** $2 \times 0.0727 = 0.1454$ N/m

**Step 2 — Denominator:** $998 \times 9.81 = 9790.38$ ; $9790.38 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 3.916$ N/m²

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$h = \frac{0.1454}{3.916} = 0.03713 \text{ m} = 37.13 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** $h \approx 37.1$ mm

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** State and explain **Newton's law of viscosity**. Differentiate between **Newtonian** and **Non-Newtonian** fluids with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **terminal velocity of a sphere falling through a viscous fluid** (Stokes' law). A steel ball of diameter 4 mm and density 7800 kg/m³ falls through a lubricating oil of density 900 kg/m³ and viscosity 0.5 Pa·s. Calculate the terminal velocity. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

**Statement:** The shear stress $\tau$ developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) $du/dy$:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (constant for Newtonian fluids).

**Explanation:** When one layer of fluid moves relative to an adjacent layer, internal friction (viscosity) opposes this relative motion. The faster-moving layer drags the slower one, and vice versa, through molecular momentum transfer. This is the basis for fluid friction losses in pipes, bearings, and lubrication.

**Newtonian Fluids:** Viscosity is **constant** (independent of shear rate). The shear stress vs. shear rate plot is a **straight line** through the origin. Examples: water, air, gasoline, alcohol, glycerin.

**Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Viscosity **varies** with shear rate. The plot is non-linear or does not pass through origin.
- **Pseudoplastic** (shear-thinning): viscosity decreases with shear rate — e.g., blood, paint, ketchup.
- **Dilatant** (shear-thickening): viscosity increases with shear rate — e.g., starch suspension, wet sand.
- **Bingham plastic**: behaves like a solid until yield stress is exceeded — e.g., toothpaste, mud, drilling fluid.
- **Thixotropic**: viscosity decreases with time under constant shear — e.g., yogurt, honey.
- **Rheopectic**: viscosity increases with time under constant shear — e.g., printer's ink.

**(b) Derivation of Terminal Velocity & Numerical:**

[Refer to Section 3.4 above for the full derivation. Final formula:]

$$v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$$

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 4$ mm → radius $r = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\rho_s = 7800$ kg/m³
- $\rho_f = 900$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Numerator:** 
$$2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 900)$$
$$= 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 6900$$
$$= 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 67709$$
$$= 5.417 \times 10^{-1} \text{ (unit-checked)}$$

More carefully:
- $r^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- $2 r^2 g = 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 7.848 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s²
- $(\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6900$ kg/m³
- Product = $7.848 \times 10^{-5} \times 6900 = 0.5415$ kg/(m·s²) = 0.5415 N/m² (numerator value)

**Step 2 — Denominator:** $9 \times 0.5 = 4.5$ Pa

**Step 3 — Terminal velocity:**

$$v_t = \frac{0.5415}{4.5} = 0.1203 \text{ m/s}$$

**Answer:** $v_t \approx 0.120$ m/s $= 120.3$ mm/s

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversions:** Students frequently forget to convert mm to m, leading to errors by factors of $10^3$ or $10^6$. Always convert all lengths to **meters** before plugging in.
> 2. **Sign of $(\rho_s - \rho_f)$:** This must be **positive** for the sphere to fall. If the sphere is less dense than the fluid, it floats — no terminal velocity calculation is needed.
> 3. **Validity of Stokes' law:** It applies only when $Re < 1$. If $Re > 1$, a correction factor is required. Always verify by computing $Re = \rho_f v_t d / \mu$.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract buoyancy:** Some students write $W = \rho_s g V$ and then forget to subtract $F_b = \rho_f g V$. Always remember the **net driving force** is $(\rho_s - \rho_f) g V$.
> 5. **Density difference error:** Using $\rho_s$ only (without subtracting $\rho_f$) instead of $(\rho_s - \rho_f)$ — this is the **most common** mistake and costs 2–3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Density** is mass per unit volume ($\rho = m/V$); **specific gravity** is a dimensionless ratio with water at 4 °C.
- The **oscillating U-tube densitometer** is the modern electronic transducer for liquid density; frequency/period changes with $\rho$.
- **Surface tension** $\gamma$ has units of N/m and arises from imbalanced cohesive forces at the surface.
- **Capillary rise** follows Jurin's law: $h = 2\gamma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$. The sign of $\cos\theta$ decides rise or fall.
- **Du Noüy ring**: $\gamma = F/(4\pi R)$. **Wilhelmy plate**: $\gamma = F/(L\cos\theta)$. **Bubble pressure**: $\gamma = r\Delta P/2$.
- The **Young–Laplace equation** relates pressure difference across a curved interface to surface tension and radius: $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ (sphere) or $4\gamma/R$ (bubble).
- **Viscosity** is fluid friction; Newton's law is $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. SI unit of dynamic viscosity: **Pa·s**.
- **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu/\rho$ in m²/s.
- **Poiseuille's law** for laminar flow in a pipe: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8\mu L)$ — flow scales with $R^4$.
- **Stokes' terminal velocity**: $v_t = 2r^2 g(\rho_s - \rho_f)/(9\mu)$ — basis of falling-ball viscometer.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho v D/\mu$ determines flow regime (laminar / transitional / turbulent).
- **Newtonian fluids**: $\mu$ = constant; **Non-Newtonian**: $\mu$ depends on shear rate (pseudoplastic, dilatant, Bingham plastic, etc.).
- Always use **SI units** (m, kg, s, Pa, N) consistently in KTU exam calculations — convert mm to m first!

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Law of Viscosity (Newtonian Postulate):**
> *"The shear stress ($\tau$) acting between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the rate of change of velocity with respect to the perpendicular distance from the fixed surface (velocity gradient), provided the temperature and pressure remain constant."*

Mathematically, this law is expressed as:

$$
\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}
$$

Introducing the constant of proportionality **$\mu$** (mu), known as the **dynamic viscosity** (or absolute viscosity) of the fluid:

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}
$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress in the fluid layer (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (in **N·s/m²** or **Pa·s**)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (in **s⁻¹**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** A fluid that strictly obeys Newton's Law of Viscosity is called a **Newtonian Fluid** (e.g., water, air, thin oils). Fluids that do NOT obey this linear relationship are classified as **Non-Newtonian Fluids** (e.g., blood, paint, ketchup, toothpaste, starch suspensions).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a stack of **playing cards** or a **deck of paper sheets** resting on a table:

- If you push the **top card** horizontally with your finger, it slides over the second card.
- The second card slides (a little less) over the third, and so on, until the **bottom card** (touching the table) remains almost stationary.

Each card represents a **fluid layer**, and the relative sliding between layers represents the **internal friction** of the fluid. Honey exhibits a very "thick" version of this sliding (high $\mu$), whereas water exhibits a very "slippery" version (low $\mu$).

> [!TIP]
> **Concept Anchoring:** Viscosity is essentially a fluid's **internal resistance to flow** arising from molecular cohesion and momentum exchange between adjacent layers moving at different velocities.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C | **$\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s** |
| Dynamic Viscosity of Air at 20 °C | **$\mu \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s** |
| Kinematic Viscosity of Water at 20 °C | **$\nu \approx 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s** |
| Standard SI Unit of Dynamic Viscosity | **Pa·s** (also **N·s/m²** or **kg/(m·s)**) |
| Standard CGS Unit (legacy) | **Poise (P)** ; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s |
| Kinematic Viscosity Unit | **m²/s** (CGS: **Stokes (St)** ; 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Law

1. **Assumption of Laminar Flow:** Newton's law is strictly valid only for **laminar (streamline)** flow conditions where fluid layers slide smoothly over one another without intermixing. It fails in turbulent regimes.
2. **No-Slip Condition:** The fluid layer in direct contact with a solid (stationary) surface has **zero velocity**. The layer in contact with a moving surface shares the surface's velocity.
3. **Velocity Gradient Exists:** Because of the no-slip condition, a velocity difference $\Delta u$ develops across a small perpendicular distance $\Delta y$, producing a gradient $\dfrac{du}{dy}$.
4. **Molecular Momentum Transfer:** Molecules of the faster-moving (upper) layer continuously migrate into the slower-moving (lower) layer, transferring momentum. This microscopic exchange manifests macroscopically as **shear stress**.
5. **Linear Constitutive Relation:** Newton postulated that this shear stress is **linearly proportional** to the velocity gradient, with $\mu$ being the constant of proportionality (a fluid property depending on temperature and pressure).
6. **Force Formulation:** The total viscous force on a fluid area $A$ becomes:
$$
F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the KTU 2024 ESE and Continuous Evaluation assessments on this topic.

| \# | Formula | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | **Newton's Law** – the foundational constitutive relation. |
| 2 | $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Viscous force form. Used in **viscometer design** and **bearing lubrication analysis**. |
| 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ where $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\dot{\gamma}$ is the **shear rate**; this is the rheological definition of viscosity. |
| 4 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | **Kinematic Viscosity** – critical in **Reynolds Number** calculations ($\text{Re} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$). |
| 5 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{v L}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless number to classify flow regime in pipelines/channels. |
| 6 | $\mu_{\text{water, 20°C}} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s | Reference benchmark for numerical problems. |
| 7 | Conversion: 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s | Frequently tested unit conversion in KTU papers. |
| 8 | $1$ Stokes $= 10^{-4}$ m²/s | Kinematic viscosity CGS to SI conversion. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Tip for KTU Numerical Problems:** When units of $\mu$ are given in Poise and $\rho$ in g/cm³, the resulting $\nu$ will be in Stokes. Always **convert to SI** ($\mu$ in Pa·s, $\rho$ in kg/m³) **before** using $\text{Re} = vL/\nu$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Lubrication Engineering:** Multi-grade engine oils are engineered so that $\mu$ remains within optimal bounds across temperature extremes (e.g., SAE 20W-50).
- **Process & Chemical Industry:** Piping design requires accurate $\mu$ values to compute **pressure drop** using the Hagen-Poiseuille equation:
$$
\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}
$$
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** Blood viscosity anomalies (deviations from Newtonian behavior) are diagnostic markers for diseases like polycythemia and sickle cell anemia.
- **Flow Measurement Devices:** Viscosity directly influences the calibration of **capillary viscometers**, **rotational viscometers**, and **falling-ball viscometers**.
- **Hydraulic Systems & Dampers:** Viscosity governs the damping coefficient in hydraulic shock absorbers used in automotive and aerospace suspensions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Force Form from Shear Stress

Starting with Newton's Postulate in differential form:

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}
$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that shear stress is, by definition, force per unit area:

$$
\tau = \frac{F}{A}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate the two expressions for $\tau$:

$$
\frac{F}{A} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by the area $A$ to isolate the viscous force $F$:

$$
F = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}
$$

> This compact form is the **starting equation** for deriving the Hagen-Poiseuille flow, Couette flow, and Stokes' Law.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kinematic Viscosity Relationship

**Step 1:** Begin with the definition of kinematic viscosity as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density:

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the SI units on both sides to verify dimensional consistency:

$$
[\mu] = \frac{\text{N} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{m}^{2}} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}
$$

$$
[\rho] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the units of $\nu$:

$$
[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{kg}/(\text{m} \cdot \text{s})}{\text{kg}/\text{m}^{3}} = \frac{\text{m}^{2}}{\text{s}}
$$

> This confirms the SI unit of $\nu$ is **m²/s** (meter squared per second).

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style 10-Mark Solved Problem)

> **Problem:** A fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.9$ N·s/m² flows between two parallel plates separated by a distance of **10 mm**. The upper plate moves at **1.5 m/s** while the lower plate is stationary. Calculate:
> (i) The velocity gradient assuming a linear velocity profile.
> (ii) The shear stress on the lower plate.
> (iii) The viscous drag force per square meter of the plate.

**Given Data:**
- $\mu = 0.9$ N·s/m²
- $dy = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- $u_{\text{top}} = 1.5$ m/s
- $u_{\text{bottom}} = 0$ m/s

### (i) Velocity Gradient

**Step 1:** Apply the linear velocity profile relation:

$$
\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u_{\text{top}} - u_{\text{bottom}}}{dy}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$
\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.5 - 0}{0.01}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\frac{du}{dy} = 150 \ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

### (ii) Shear Stress

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity:

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
\tau = 0.9 \times 150
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\tau = 135 \ \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

### (iii) Viscous Drag Force per Square Meter

**Step 1:** The drag force per unit area is numerically equal to the shear stress:

$$
\frac{F}{A} = \tau = 135 \ \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, for a 1 m² area:

$$
F = 135 \ \text{N}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The shear stress is independent of pressure for Newtonian fluids under simple shear — it depends only on the velocity gradient and the dynamic viscosity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Behavior Type | Constitutive Equation | Graph Shape (Shear Stress vs. Shear Rate) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Straight line through origin | Water, air, gasoline, thin oils |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_{y} + \mu_{p} \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Straight line, but with a yield intercept | Toothpaste, drilling mud, sewage sludge |
| **Pseudoplastic (Shear-thinning)** | $\tau = k \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{n}, \ n < 1$ | Concave downward curve | Blood, ketchup, latex paint, polymer melts |
| **Dilatant (Shear-thickening)** | $\tau = k \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^{n}, \ n > 1$ | Concave upward curve | Cornstarch suspension, wet sand |
| **Thixotropic** | $\mu$ decreases with time of shearing | Hysteresis loop | Honey, gels, printing inks |
| **Rheopectic** | $\mu$ increases with time of shearing | Hysteresis loop (reverse) | Gypsum pastes, certain lubricants |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Non-Newtonian fluids **do not have a single constant** $\mu$. The "apparent viscosity" $\mu_{a} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ depends on the shear rate, and you must state this explicitly in answers to avoid losing marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

This schematic helps students quickly classify a fluid from experimental $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ data — a frequent KTU 2024 problem.
