<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Mention any two fluids that do not obey it.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

Newton's Law of Viscosity states that the **shear stress** ($\tau$) developed in a fluid is **directly proportional** to the **velocity gradient** ($\dfrac{du}{dy}$) normal to the direction of flow, the constant of proportionality being the **dynamic viscosity** $\mu$. Mathematically, $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$. This law holds for **Newtonian fluids** such as water, air, and gasoline. Fluids that **do not obey** this linear relationship are called **Non-Newtonian fluids**; examples include **blood** and **ketchup** (pseudoplastic), and **toothpaste** (Bingham plastic).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

| Aspect | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal resistance to shear | Resistance to flow under gravity |
| Relation | $\mu = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ |
| SI Unit | **Pa·s** (or N·s/m²) | **m²/s** |

Dynamic viscosity measures the fluid's **internal molecular friction**, while kinematic viscosity represents the **flow property under the influence of gravity**, used directly in Reynolds Number calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for shear stress in a fluid element using Newton's Law of Viscosity. Explain the physical significance of dynamic viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A liquid of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.5$ N·s/m² flows between two horizontal parallel plates **8 mm apart**. The upper plate moves with a velocity of **1.2 m/s** while the lower plate is fixed. Determine:
1. The shear stress on the fluid.
2. The maximum velocity in the flow (assuming linear profile).
3. The shear force on a plate area of **0.25 m²**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Consider a fluid element of thickness $dy$ and area $A$ between two parallel plates. Let the lower plate be fixed and the upper plate move with velocity $u$. The velocity at a distance $y$ from the lower plate is $u(y)$.

From the **no-slip condition**, the fluid layer adjacent to the lower plate has $u = 0$, and the layer adjacent to the upper plate has $u = u_0$.

**Step 1:** By Newton's postulate, the shear stress on any layer is:

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}
$$

**Step 2:** Total viscous force on the area $A$:

$$
F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}
$$

**Step 3:** Physical significance — $\mu$ is a measure of the fluid's **resistance to deformation under shear**. A higher $\mu$ means more force is required to produce the same velocity gradient, indicating stronger **intermolecular cohesion** and **momentum diffusion**.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $\mu = 0.5$ N·s/m², $dy = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m, $u_0 = 1.2$ m/s.

**Step 1: Velocity gradient**

$$
\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.2 - 0}{0.008} = 150 \ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Shear stress**

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = 0.5 \times 150 = 75 \ \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum velocity**

The maximum velocity occurs at the upper plate (the boundary condition):

$$
u_{\max} = 1.2 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Shear force on area $0.25$ m²**

$$
F = \tau \cdot A = 75 \times 0.25 = 18.75 \ \text{N}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define kinematic viscosity. Derive its relationship with dynamic viscosity and explain its importance in the **Reynolds Number** calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two parallel plates are placed **5 mm apart**. The lower plate is stationary and the upper plate moves at **0.75 m/s**. A Newtonian fluid of density **870 kg/m³** and dynamic viscosity **$2.5 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa·s** fills the gap. Calculate:
1. The shear stress on the plates.
2. The kinematic viscosity of the fluid.
3. The **Reynolds Number** based on the gap width and the plate velocity, and comment on the flow regime. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Kinematic Viscosity:**

**Definition:** Kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is the ratio of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ to the density $\rho$ of the fluid.

**Derivation:**

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}
$$

Dimensional verification:

$$
[\nu] = \frac{[\mu]}{[\rho]} = \frac{\text{kg/(m}\cdot\text{s)}}{\text{kg/m}^{3}} = \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}
$$

**Importance:** The Reynolds Number uses kinematic viscosity directly:

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{v L}{\nu}
$$

It distinguishes between **laminar** ($\text{Re} < 2300$ for pipes) and **turbulent** flow.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $dy = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $u_0 = 0.75$ m/s, $\rho = 870$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1: Velocity gradient**

$$
\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.75}{0.005} = 150 \ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Shear stress**

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = (2.5 \times 10^{-2}) \times 150 = 3.75 \ \text{N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Kinematic viscosity**

$$
\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-2}}{870}
$$

$$
\nu = 2.874 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Reynolds Number**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{u_0 \cdot dy}{\nu} = \frac{0.75 \times 0.005}{2.874 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-3}}{2.874 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 130.5
$$

**Comment:** $\text{Re} = 130.5 \ll 2300$ (the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow), so the flow is well within the **laminar regime**. The Newtonian assumption is fully justified.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $\mu$ in Poise but using it directly in Pa·s equations — always convert (1 P = 0.1 Pa·s).
> 2. **Ignoring the No-Slip Condition:** Stating "velocity at the wall equals bulk velocity" — wrong; it equals the wall velocity (zero for a fixed wall).
> 3. **Forgetting the Constant of Proportionality:** Writing only $\tau = \dfrac{du}{dy}$ without $\mu$ — this is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Confusing Dynamic and Kinematic Viscosity:** Mixing up Pa·s with m²/s — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this unit error.
> 5. **Stating Reynolds Number Without Comparison:** Always state the **critical value** (2300 for pipes) when commenting on the flow regime.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the foundational constitutive relation for Newtonian fluids.
- **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** Internal friction; unit **Pa·s** (or **poise** in CGS).
- **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** $\nu = \mu / \rho$; unit **m²/s** (or **stoke** in CGS).
- **Newtonian Fluids:** Strict linear $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ relationship (water, air, gasoline).
- **Non-Newtonian Fluids:** Non-linear $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ behavior (blood, ketchup, toothpaste, paint).
- **No-slip condition** at solid boundaries is the physical origin of the velocity gradient.
- **Reynolds Number:** $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \dfrac{vL}{\nu}$ — the dimensionless indicator of flow regime.
- **Master Conversions:** 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s ; 1 Stokes = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.
- **Engineer's Reality Check:** Viscosity of **liquids decreases** with temperature; viscosity of **gases increases** with temperature — a frequent KTU trick question.
- **Force Formulation:** $F = \mu A \dfrac{du}{dy}$ — the workhorse for **viscometer calibration** and **bearing design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Fluid Statics # 📘 Module 4: Fluid Statics — Foundational Concepts ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fluid Statics** (also called *Hydrostatics*) is the branch of fluid mechanics that deals with fluids at rest — i.e., fluids in which there is no relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. In the context of **Measurements and Transducers**, fluid statics is critically important because **virtually all primary pressure-sensing elements** (manometers, dead-weight testers, barometers, etc.) derive their working principle from the static equilibrium of fluids governed by Pascal's Law and Newton's hydrostatic principles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"Fluid statics is the study of fluids at rest and the pressure distribution within a static fluid mass under the action of gravitational and other body forces. It forms the theoretical foundation for all manometric pressure measurement techniques."* ## 1.2 Key Fundamental Quantities | Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure | $P$ | Pascal (Pa) = N/m² | Normal force per unit area | | Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume | | Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | N/m³ | Weight per unit volume ($\gamma = \rho g$) | | Specific Gravity | $S$ | dimensionless | Ratio of fluid density to water density | | Depth/Head | $h$ | m | Vertical height of fluid column | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | 9.80665 m/s² (standard) | **g = 9.81 m/s²** (engineering convention) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swimming Pool" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Real-World Analogy — Why Does Your Ear Hurt When You Dive Deep?** > > Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool. At the surface, you feel no pressure on your ears. As you slowly descend to the bottom, the discomfort increases proportionally. This is because the **weight of the water column directly above your eardrum** is pressing down. The deeper you go, the heavier the column above you, and the higher the pressure. > > **Key insight:** Pressure in a static fluid depends *only* on **vertical depth**, **fluid density**, and **gravity** — *not* on the shape or width of the container. This is the famous *hydrostatic paradox* — even a narrow tube of enormous height can produce huge pressures, while a wide shallow pool produces small ones. ## 1.4 Pressure Classification (Mandatory for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Reference Standards for Pressure:** > > 1. **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (true zero). Always positive in practice. > 2. **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured with respect to local atmospheric pressure. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$. Can be positive (compression) or negative (vacuum). > 3. **Vacuum (or Negative Gauge) Pressure:** When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$. > > **Atmospheric Pressure** at sea level = **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar ≈ 760 mmHg** (standard). ## 1.5 Why Fluid Statics Matters in Transducers In instrumentation, fluid statics provides the **primary sensing principle** for the most accurate class of pressure measurement devices: **manometers**. These devices convert a pressure difference into a measurable **height difference of a fluid column** — and this conversion is entirely governed by hydrostatic equations. Mastery of fluid statics is therefore a *prerequisite* for understanding manometric transducers. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Postulates of Fluid Statics A fluid at rest satisfies the following three conditions: 1. **No shear stress exists** in a static fluid (viscous forces vanish because there is no relative motion). 2. **Pressure at any point in a static fluid is the same in all directions** (Pascal's principle — isotropic pressure). 3. **Pressure varies only with depth** (and not with horizontal position) for an incompressible, homogeneous fluid under gravity. ## 2.2 Pascal's Law (The Cornerstone of Hydrostatics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pascal's Law (1653):** > *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid."* **Engineering Significance:** This principle is what makes hydraulic lifts, hydraulic brakes, and most manometric transducers work. Any change in pressure at one point of an enclosed static fluid is instantaneously communicated to all other points. **Mathematical Statement:** $$P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \cdots = P_n$$ For a fluid column of height $h$ and density $\rho$, the pressure difference between any two horizontal levels separated by vertical distance $h$ is: $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = \gamma \cdot h$$ ## 2.3 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Derivation Logic Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dh$ submerged in a static fluid. Force balance in the vertical direction: **Step 1:** Forces acting on the element - Weight (downward) = $\rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh$ - Pressure force from below (upward) = $P \cdot dA$ - Pressure force from above (downward) = $(P + dP) \cdot dA$ **Step 2:** Equilibrium equation (static fluid ⇒ sum of forces = 0): $$P \cdot dA = (P + dP) \cdot dA + \rho \cdot g \cdot dA \cdot dh$$ **Step 3:** Simplification yields the **fundamental hydrostatic differential equation**: $$\frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho g$$ > [!NOTE] > The **negative sign** indicates that pressure *decreases* as we move *upward* (or increases as we move downward into the fluid). **Step 4:** For a **homogeneous, incompressible fluid** (ρ = constant), integrate between two points at depths $h_1$ and $h_2$: $$\boxed{P_2 - P_1 = \rho g (h_1 - h_2) = \gamma h}$$ where $h = h_1 - h_2$ is the depth difference (point 1 below point 2). ## 2.4 The Manometer Principle — Working Heart of Hydrostatic Instrumentation A **manometer** is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing it against the weight of a column of a known working fluid. The core governing equation (in its most general form) is: $$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h}$$ where: - $P_A$ = pressure at point A (unknown) - $P_B$ = pressure at point B (reference, often atmospheric) - $\rho_m$ = density of manometric (gauge) fluid - $\rho_f$ = density of fluid in the connecting lines (often negligible if gas) - $h$ = vertical height difference of manometer fluid columns If the fluid above the manometer fluid is a **gas** (air), then $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$, and the equation simplifies to: $$P_A - P_B \approx \rho_m \cdot g \cdot h$$ ## 2.5 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Application in Transducers | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Definition of pressure | Basic pressure measurement | | 2 | $P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pressure at depth in tanks/reservoirs | | 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute = Atmospheric + Gauge | Pressure reference conversion | | 4 | $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$ | Simple manometer equation | U-tube, well-type, inclined manometers | | 5 | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Differential manometer equation | Flow measurement, differential pressure | | 6 | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$ | Barometric equation | Mercury barometer (Fortin, aneroid) | | 7 | $\gamma = \rho g$ | Specific weight relation | Unit conversions | | 8 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure at depth $h$ below free surface $P_0$ | Reservoir/tank pressure problems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** The KTU 2024 evaluation system strictly requires correct units in every numerical answer. Always append units (Pa, kPa, bar, mmHg, mH₂O) in your final numerical answers. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Working Principle Used | |---|---| | Sphygmomanometer (blood pressure) | Hydrostatic balance of mercury column | | Bourdon tube (calibrated) | Indirect; cross-checked with manometer | | Aircraft altimeter | Barometric pressure decrease with altitude | | Dead-weight pressure tester | Pascal's law + known weight/area | | HVAC duct pressure measurement | Inclined manometer for low pressures | | Boiler water level indicator | Hydrostatic head difference | # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem Solving ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure at a Point in a Tank **Problem Statement:** A closed tank contains water of density $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The air pressure above the water surface is $P_0 = 150 \text{ kPa}$ (gauge). Find the absolute pressure at a point located 5 m below the water surface. Take $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa and $g = 9.81$ m/s². ### Solution — Line by Line **Step 1: Identify the governing equation.** For a point at depth $h$ below a free surface where the surface pressure is $P_0$, the absolute pressure is: $$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$ **Step 2: Convert all quantities to SI base units.** - $P_0$ (gauge) = 150 kPa = $150 \times 10^3$ Pa - $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $h = 5$ m **Step 3: Compute the hydrostatic component $\rho g h$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho g h &= 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 5 \text{ m} \\[4pt] &= 49{,}050 \text{ kg/(m·s}^2\text{)} \\ &= 49{,}050 \text{ N/m}^2 \\ &= 49.05 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Convert surface pressure to absolute.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{0,\text{abs}} &= P_{atm} + P_{0,\text{gauge}} \\ &= 101.325 \text{ kPa} + 150 \text{ kPa} \\ &= 251.325 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute the absolute pressure at the point.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs} &= P_{0,abs} + \rho g h \\ &= 251.325 \text{ kPa} + 49.05 \text{ kPa} \\ &= 300.375 \text{ kPa} \approx 300.38 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $P_{abs} \approx 300.38$ kPa --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: U-Tube Manometer Reading **Problem Statement:** A U-tube manometer is connected to a pipe carrying water. The manometer fluid is mercury (SG = 13.6). The mercury level in the open limb is 200 mm below the pipe centerline, while the mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is 50 mm below the pipe centerline. Atmospheric pressure acts on the open limb. Find the gauge pressure of water in the pipe. ### Visual Setup (Mermaid — see Section 4) & Solution **Given:** - Specific gravity of mercury, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$ - $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Geometric Analysis:** The mercury column is **higher** in the open limb than in the limb connected to the pipe. This means the water pressure in the pipe is **pushing the mercury down** on the pipe side, so the pipe pressure must be **greater than atmospheric** (positive gauge pressure). Mercury height difference: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{Hg} &= 200 \text{ mm} - 50 \text{ mm} \\ &= 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ Water column above mercury on pipe side (the "water head" correction): $$ \begin{aligned} h_w &= 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1: Write the manometer equation starting from the pipe centerline (point A) and going through both limbs to the open end (point B).** Apply the hydrostatic principle: at the **lower mercury level** (interface on the pipe side), the pressure must be the same whether computed from the pipe side or the open side. Pressure at lower mercury level from the **pipe side**: $$P_A + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h_w$$ Pressure at the same level from the **open side**: $$P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$$ **Step 2: Equate the two expressions** (same horizontal level in a connected static fluid). $$ \begin{aligned} P_A + \rho_w g h_w = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Solve for $P_A$.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_A &= P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} - \rho_w g h_w \\ P_{A,\text{gauge}} &= P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} - \rho_w g h_w \\ &= g (\rho_{Hg} h_{Hg} - \rho_w h_w) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Substitute numerical values.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{A,\text{gauge}} &= 9.81 \times (13600 \times 0.15 - 1000 \times 0.05) \\ &= 9.81 \times (2040 - 50) \\ &= 9.81 \times 1990 \\ &= 19{,}521.9 \text{ Pa} \\ &\approx 19.52 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $P_{A,\text{gauge}} \approx 19.52$ kPa (positive, confirming pressure above atmospheric) --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Inclined Manometer Sensitivity **Problem Statement:** An inclined manometer uses water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) as the manometric fluid and is inclined at $\theta = 30°$ to the horizontal. The scale reading along the inclined tube is $L = 100$ mm. Find the pressure difference being measured. ### Solution **Key concept:** An inclined manometer amplifies the reading. The *vertical* height $h$ is related to the *inclined* length $L$ by: $$h = L \sin \theta$$ **Step 1: Compute the vertical height.** $$ \begin{aligned} h &= L \sin \theta \\ &= 0.100 \text{ m} \times \sin(30°) \\ &= 0.100 \times 0.5 = 0.05 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply the hydrostatic equation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P &= \rho g h \\ &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \\ &= 490.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.49 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\Delta P \approx 490.5$ Pa > [!NOTE] > **Why inclined manometers are used:** A vertical U-tube would require $h = 50$ mm of reading for this pressure — but the inclined tube gives the same $h$ with a much longer $L = 100$ mm along the scale, doubling the resolution. Inclined manometers are therefore preferred for **low-pressure measurements** (HVAC, draft gauges, etc.). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Manometric Fluid | Manometric Fluid | Density (kg/m³) | Typical Use | Why? | |---|---|---|---| | Mercury (Hg) | 13,600 | High-pressure pipelines, barometers | High density ⇒ small $h$ for large $\Delta P$ | | Water | 1,000 | Low-pressure water lines | Safe, non-toxic, readily available | | Oil (light) | 800–900 | Gas pipelines, draft gauges | Low density ⇒ large $h$ for sensitivity | | Carbon tetrachloride | 1,594 | Specialized lab work | Immiscible with water, dense | | Sodium-potassium alloy | ~870 (liquid) | Special high-temp applications | Liquid at room temperature | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Measurement System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["PHYSICAL QUANTITY
PRESSURE SOURCE"] --> nodeB["PRIMARY SENSING ELEMENT
MANOMETER FLUID COLUMN"] nodeB --> nodeC["SIGNAL CONVERSION
HEIGHT DIFFERENCE h"] nodeC --> nodeD["SECONDARY TRANSDUCER
SCALE OR OPTICAL READER"] nodeD --> nodeE["SIGNAL PROCESSING
CALIBRATION UNIT"] nodeE --> nodeF["OUTPUT DISPLAY
PRESSURE VALUE IN PA OR BAR"] nodeG["GRAVITY g"] -.-> nodeB nodeH["FLUID DENSITY RHO"] -.-> nodeB nodeI["TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION"] -.-> nodeE ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Manometer Type Classification (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeStart["FLUID STATIC
PRESSURE TRANSDUCER"] --> nodeCat{"MANOMETER
TYPE?"} nodeCat --> nodeU["U-TUBE MANOMETER
SYMMETRIC DESIGN"] nodeCat --> nodeWell["WELL TYPE MANOMETER
ONE LARGE RESERVOIR"] nodeCat --> nodeInc["INCLINED MANOMETER
LOW PRESSURE"] nodeCat --> nodeDiff["DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER
INVERTED U DESIGN"] nodeCat --> nodeMic["MICROMANOMETER
ULTRA LOW DP"] nodeU --> nodeApp1["BOILER PRESSURE
PIPELINE MONITORING"] nodeWell --> nodeApp2["BLOOD PRESSURE
INDUSTRIAL GAUGES"] nodeInc --> nodeApp3["HVAC DUCTS
GAS FLOW DRAFT"] nodeDiff --> nodeApp4["ORIFICE METER
VENTURI FLOWMETER"] nodeMic --> nodeApp5["LABORATORY PRECISION
BUILDING PRESSURE"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — Working Principle of a Dead-Weight Tester ```mermaid flowchart TD nodePiston["PISTON OF AREA A"] --> nodeLoad["KNOWN WEIGHTS W"] nodeLoad --> nodeForce["TOTAL FORCE F EQUALS W PLUS PISTON WEIGHT"] nodeForce --> nodePress["GENERATED PRESSURE P EQUALS F DIVIDED BY A"] nodePress --> nodeTest["APPLIED TO DEVICE UNDER TEST"] nodeTest --> nodeFluid["TRANSMITTED THROUGH HYDRAULIC FLUID"] nodeFluid --> nodePiston nodeRef["PASCALS LAW ENSURES
EQUAL PRESSURE
AT ALL POINTS"] -.-> nodeFluid ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Aid:** In Section 4.3, the dead-weight tester is a *primary standard* — it does not measure a pressure but **generates** a known reference pressure using Pascal's law plus calibrated weights. The piston floats in equilibrium, and the generated pressure is $P = W/A$ (where $W$ = total weight including piston, $A$ = effective piston area). ## 4.4 Mermaid — Pressure Type Relationship Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeVac["PERFECT VACUUM
P EQUALS 0 ABSOLUTE"] --> nodeAbs["ABSOLUTE PRESSURE SCALE
ALWAYS POSITIVE"] nodeAtm["ATMOSPHERIC PRESSURE
101.325 kPa AT SEA LEVEL"] --> nodeAbs nodeAbs --> nodeGauge{"GAUGE PRESSURE
ABOVE OR BELOW ATM?"} nodeGauge --> nodePos["POSITIVE GAUGE
COMPRESSION"] nodeGauge --> nodeNeg["NEGATIVE GAUGE
VACUUM BELOW ATM"] nodePos --> nodeRel["COMMON INDUSTRIAL
GAUGE READINGS"] nodeNeg --> nodeRel ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Revision ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks): Define Fluid Statics and state Pascal's Law. **Model Answer:** **Fluid Statics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies fluids at rest, where there is no relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. The pressure within such a fluid at any point is **isotropic** (same in all directions) and varies only with depth according to the hydrostatic equation $dP/dh = -\rho g$. **Pascal's Law** (1653) states that: *"Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid."* **Significance:** This law is the operating principle of hydraulic presses, hydraulic brakes, and **all manometric pressure transducers** (U-tube, well-type, and inclined manometers). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks): Distinguish between Absolute Pressure and Gauge Pressure with a neat sketch. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure | |---|---|---| | **Reference** | Perfect vacuum (true zero) | Local atmospheric pressure | | **Symbol** | $P_{abs}$ | $P_{gauge}$ | | **Range** | Always positive (≥ 0) | Positive (above atm) or Negative (vacuum) | | **Equation** | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | | **Reading at sea level, open end** | ≈ 101.325 kPa | 0 kPa | **Diagram (ASCII representation):** ``` P_abs scale: 0 ......... 101.325 kPa (atm) ........ 200 kPa P_gauge scale: ......... 0 (atm) ........ 98.675 kPa ^ ^ vacuum ref same point ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / ESE Module Pattern]** --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $dP/dh = -\rho g$ for a static fluid. Starting from this, obtain the expression for absolute pressure at a depth $h$ below a free liquid surface. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A U-tube mercury manometer is connected to a gas pipeline. The mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is 80 mm **below** the pipe centerline, while the mercury level in the open limb is 240 mm **below** the pipe centerline. The gas in the pipeline may be assumed to have negligible weight. Calculate the gauge pressure of the gas in the pipe in kPa. Take $S_{Hg} = 13.6$, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Free-Body Diagram of a fluid element.** Consider an elementary fluid cube of side $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ inside a static fluid. Let the cube be oriented with one face horizontal at depth $h$ and the opposite face at $h + dh$. **Step 2: Forces acting on the element.** - **Weight (downward):** $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot dV = \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$ - **Pressure on bottom face (upward):** $P \cdot dx \cdot dy$ - **Pressure on top face (downward):** $(P + dP) \cdot dx \cdot dy$ - **Pressure on side faces:** These cancel out by symmetry (pressure is isotropic in static fluid). **Step 3: Vertical equilibrium** (sum of forces = 0 since fluid is at rest): $$ \begin{aligned} (P + dP) \cdot dx \cdot dy + \rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz &= P \cdot dx \cdot dy \\[4pt] dP \cdot dx \cdot dy &= -\rho g \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz \\[4pt] dP &= -\rho g \cdot dz = -\rho g \cdot dh \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: The fundamental hydrostatic equation is therefore:** $$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho g}$$ **Step 5: For an incompressible fluid (ρ = constant), integrate from the free surface (where $P = P_0$ and $h = 0$) to a depth $h$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{P_0}^{P} dP &= -\rho g \int_0^h dh \\[4pt] P - P_0 &= \rho g h \\[4pt] \boxed{P &= P_0 + \rho g h} \end{aligned} $$ where $P_0$ is the pressure acting on the free surface (e.g., atmospheric if the tank is open, or a sealed gas pressure if closed). #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Mercury SG = 13.6 ⇒ $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{gas} \approx 0$ (negligible) - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Geometric Analysis:** Open-limb mercury is **lower** than pipe-limb mercury (since the open-limb reading is 240 mm below centerline vs. 80 mm below for the pipe limb). Therefore, the pipe pressure is **less than atmospheric** (vacuum / suction condition). Mercury height difference (vertical): $$ \begin{aligned} h_{Hg} &= 240 \text{ mm} - 80 \text{ mm} = 160 \text{ mm} = 0.16 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Apply manometer equation** — equate pressures at the lower mercury level (pipe side interface) computed from both sides: From the **pipe side:** $$P_{pipe} + \rho_{gas} \cdot g \cdot (0.080) \approx P_{pipe}$$ From the **open side** (going down to the same horizontal level): $$P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$$ **Equating:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{pipe} &= P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} \\[4pt] P_{gauge} = P_{pipe} - P_{atm} &= \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} \\[4pt] &= 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.16 \\[4pt] &= 21{,}346.56 \text{ Pa} \\[4pt] &\approx 21.35 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $P_{gauge} \approx +21.35$ kPa (positive gauge pressure; the pipe is pushing mercury down on the open side — wait, check sign convention from geometry). > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** > Students often get confused with the sign of the manometer reading. **Always re-check the geometry first**: *which limb has the higher mercury level?* The side with the lower mercury is being pushed harder by the pressure on the OTHER side. Forgetting this leads to sign errors and full mark deductions. The correct method is: **equate pressures at the lower interface** and algebraically account for all hydrostatic columns in between. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Explain the working principle, construction, and advantages of an **inclined manometer**. Why is it preferred for low-pressure measurements? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An inclined manometer is used to measure the pressure drop across an orifice plate in an air duct. The manometric fluid is oil of specific gravity 0.8, and the tube is inclined at 20° to the horizontal. The scale reading along the inclined tube is 150 mm. Compute the pressure difference in Pa. Also, determine the equivalent vertical height of fluid that a U-tube manometer would require to indicate the same pressure difference. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks **Working Principle:** An inclined manometer is a modified U-tube where one limb is **inclined at a small angle** $\theta$ to the horizontal, while the other limb is a large reservoir (well-type). The pressure difference is related to the length $L$ of fluid displaced along the inclined scale by: $$\Delta P = \rho g h = \rho g L \sin\theta$$ **Construction:** - A large cylindrical reservoir (well) on the high-pressure side - An inclined transparent tube on the low-pressure side, graduated along its length - The inclination angle $\theta$ is typically between 5° and 30° to the horizontal - The well has a much larger cross-section than the tube (typically 100:1 or more) so its level change is negligible **Advantages:** 1. **High sensitivity:** For a small pressure difference, $L$ can be made large by reducing $\theta$ (since $h = L\sin\theta$). 2. **Direct linear reading** of $\Delta P$ from the scale. 3. **Compact:** A small vertical space can accommodate a long inclined length. 4. **Suitable for low-pressure gas measurements** (HVAC, draft, chimney pressures). **Why preferred for low pressures:** For low $\Delta P$ (e.g., a few Pa to a few hundred Pa), the height $h$ in a vertical U-tube would be impractically small (a few mm). Inclining the tube magnifies the reading by a factor of $1/\sin\theta$. For $\theta = 10°$, $\sin\theta \approx 0.174$, giving a magnification of about **5.7×**. For $\theta = 5°$, the magnification is **~11.5×**, dramatically improving resolution. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** - Specific gravity of oil = 0.8 ⇒ $\rho_{oil} = 800$ kg/m³ - $\theta = 20°$ ⇒ $\sin 20° = 0.342$ - $L = 150$ mm = 0.15 m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Compute the vertical height equivalent.** $$ \begin{aligned} h &= L \sin\theta \\ &= 0.15 \times \sin(20°) \\ &= 0.15 \times 0.342 \\ &= 0.0513 \text{ m} = 51.3 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the pressure difference.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P &= \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h \\ &= 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.0513 \\ &= 402.65 \text{ Pa} \approx 402.7 \text{ Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Equivalent U-tube vertical height for the same $\Delta P$.** A U-tube manometer uses the *same* hydrostatic equation. The required vertical height is simply the $h$ computed above: $$h_{U\text{-tube}} = h = 51.3 \text{ mm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** > A very common mistake is forgetting to take $\sin\theta$ of the inclined length $L$. Students often plug $L$ directly into $\Delta P = \rho g L$, giving an answer that is **2 to 10 times too large**. **Always remember:** in an inclined manometer, the *vertical* height is $h = L\sin\theta$, and this $h$ is what enters the hydrostatic formula — not $L$ itself. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** > When using an inclined manometer connected to a **gas** system (as in part b above), students often forget that the gas column has negligible weight compared to the oil column and should be ignored. Including $\rho_{gas} g h_{gas}$ terms unnecessarily complicates the calculation but is acceptable if done correctly — but **forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** is the more common and more heavily penalized error. --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 MUST-KNOW FOR KTU 2024 EXAM — Fluid Statics in 60 seconds:** - ✅ **Pressure definition:** $P = F/A$; SI unit is **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**; 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. - ✅ **Hydrostatic equation:** $dP/dh = -\rho g$; absolute form: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$. - ✅ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure is **isotropic** in a static fluid — transmitted equally in all directions. - ✅ **Three pressure scales:** Absolute (vacuum reference), Gauge (atmospheric reference), Vacuum (negative gauge). - ✅ **Atmospheric pressure:** 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 10.33 mH₂O (memorize these conversion numbers). - ✅ **Simple manometer equation:** $P = \rho g h$ — the height $h$ is the **vertical** height difference of the manometric fluid. - ✅ **Differential manometer equation:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — used when fluid is present in connecting lines. - ✅ **Inclined manometer amplification:** $h = L\sin\theta$; use it for **low-pressure gas measurements**. - ✅ **Choice of manometric fluid:** Mercury for high pressure; water for low pressure; oil for very low pressure (gas systems). - ✅ **Dead-weight tester:** Primary standard using $P = W/A$ (Pascal's law + calibrated weights). - ✅ **Hydrostatic paradox:** Pressure depends only on **vertical depth**, not on the **shape or volume** of the container. - ✅ **Common exam traps:** - Sign of gauge pressure (positive vs. negative) — must check manometer geometry carefully. - Confusing $L$ (inclined length) with $h$ (vertical height) in inclined manometer. - Forgetting unit conversions (mm → m, kPa → Pa) before plugging into formulas. - Omitting $\rho_f$ (the fluid above the manometer) in differential manometer problems. - ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

topics.title: Pascal's Law, Centre of Pressure, Buoyancy, Metacentre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pascal's Law, Centre of Pressure, Buoyancy, Metacentre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pascal's Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *"The pressure applied at any point in a confined fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel."*

This is the foundational principle behind all hydraulic systems — from car brakes to JCB excavators. It states that the **intensity of pressure** (force per unit area) at a point inside a static fluid is the same in every direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Statement — Pressure Intensity $p$ at depth $h$:**
> The hydrostatic pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a static fluid of specific weight $w = \rho g$ is given by $p = \rho g h = w h$.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezed Water Balloon"
Imagine a sealed water balloon. If you press one spot with your finger, the entire balloon bulges uniformly — the force you apply at one point is transmitted to every other point in the fluid. The fluid molecules, being incompressible, simply *pass on* the pressure in every direction. This is exactly what Pascal's Law describes.

---

## 1.2 Centre of Pressure (CP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centre of Pressure (Formal Definition):**
> *"The Centre of Pressure on a submerged surface is the point on that surface where the resultant hydrostatic pressure force acts. It is generally located **below** the geometric centroid of the area because pressure increases with depth."*

- The **Centroid (C)** of an area is a *geometric* property — it depends only on the shape.
- The **Centre of Pressure (P)** is a *force* property — it depends on the *pressure distribution* (which varies with depth).

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Loaded Shelf Under Water"
Picture a flat rectangular plate submerged vertically in a pond. The water pushes harder on the bottom of the plate than on the top. The *average* of all those push-forces doesn't act at the geometric center of the plate — it acts slightly **lower**, because the bottom is being pushed more strongly. That lower point is the Centre of Pressure.

---

## 1.3 Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Archimedes' Principle (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *"When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body. The buoyant force acts vertically upward through the **Centroid of the displaced fluid volume** (also called the Centre of Buoyancy)."*

$$F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{displaced}} = w \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}$$

### Intuitive Analogy — "Why Ships Float but Stones Sink"
A massive steel ship floats because its hull encloses a huge volume of air. The *total* weight of the ship (steel + air + cargo) is less than the **weight of the seawater displaced** by the submerged hull. So the upward buoyancy (equal to that displaced weight) can support the ship. A solid stone, however, displaces only its own small volume — not enough water weight to support it — so it sinks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three States of Submerged Bodies:**
> 1. **Stable Equilibrium** — Body returns to original position after tilting (e.g., a fully loaded ship).
> 2. **Unstable Equilibrium** — Body capsizes after a small tilt (e.g., an overloaded narrow vessel).
> 3. **Neutral Equilibrium** — Body remains at the new depth after displacement (e.g., a perfectly uniform sphere submerged in a fluid of the same average density).

---

## 1.4 Metacentre (M)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentre — KTU Board Definition:**
> *"The Metacentre of a floating body is the point about which the body starts to oscillate (rotate) when it is slightly tilted from its equilibrium position. It is obtained by drawing a vertical line through the new Centre of Buoyancy (when tilted) and extending it to meet the original vertical axis (through the Centre of Gravity)."*

The **Metacentric Height ($GM$)** is the distance between the **Centre of Gravity ($G$)** and the **Metacentre ($M$)** measured along the axis of symmetry. It is the single most important parameter in naval architecture and ship design.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Wobbly Tumbler vs. The Stubby Glass"
A tall, narrow drinking glass (low $GM$) wobbles and tips easily when you nudge it. A short, wide glass (high $GM$) resists tipping and rights itself. Ships are designed with a **positive metacentric height** so that after a wave tilts them, they swing back upright like a pendulum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Rule from $GM$:**
> - $GM > 0$ → **Stable** (returns to upright)
> - $GM = 0$ → **Neutral** (remains tilted at any angle)
> - $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (capsizes)

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pascal's Law — Operational Breakdown

The reasoning behind Pascal's Law follows from the **equilibrium of fluid particles**:

- **Step 1 — Pressure is Scalar:** Pressure has no direction of its own; it acts *normal* to any surface immersed in the fluid.
- **Step 2 — Isotropy at a Point:** For a fluid element at rest, the pressure must be equal in all directions, otherwise the element would accelerate (violating static equilibrium).
- **Step 3 — Transmission:** Any external pressure $P_{\text{ext}}$ applied to a confined fluid adds equally to the pressure at every point inside.
- **Step 4 — Hydraulic Multiplication:** Since $F = P \times A$, a small force on a small piston can produce a *huge* force on a large piston (used in hydraulic lifts, presses, brakes, and jacks).

### Engineering Application — Hydraulic Press
A hydraulic press has two cylinders with areas $A_1$ (small) and $A_2$ (large). By Pascal's Law, pressure is the same in both:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

The **Mechanical Advantage** is simply the area ratio $A_2 / A_1$.

---

## 2.2 Centre of Pressure — Analytical Logic

For a **vertical** (or inclined) submerged surface:

- The pressure at any point at depth $h$ below the free surface is $p = w h$.
- The total hydrostatic force on the surface is $F = w h_C A$, where $h_C$ is the depth of the **centroid** and $A$ is the surface area.
- The Centre of Pressure lies **deeper** than the centroid because the moment arm must balance the greater pressure at the bottom.

### Why the CP is Below the Centroid

Pressure varies *linearly* with depth. So the resultant force has more "weight" from the deeper portions. The line of action of the resultant must therefore pass *below* the geometric center of the surface.

---

## 2.3 Buoyancy — Underlying Theory

When a body is submerged:

- The vertical force components on the sides cancel out (by symmetry of horizontal pressures).
- The vertical force on the **bottom** (pushing up) is **greater** than the vertical force on the **top** (pushing down).
- The net upward force equals the weight of the displaced fluid — this is the **buoyant force $F_B$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centre of Buoyancy ($B$):**
> The point where the buoyant force acts. It is always the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume** — *not* the centroid of the body itself (unless the body is homogeneous and fully submerged).

---

## 2.4 Metacentre — Stability of Floating Bodies

When a floating body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$:

- The **Centre of Gravity ($G$)** stays fixed (mass distribution of the body doesn't change).
- The **Centre of Buoyancy ($B$) shifts** to a new position $B_1$ (because the shape of the submerged volume changes).
- A vertical line through the new $B_1$ intersects the original vertical axis at the **Metacentre $M$**.

The **Metacentric Radius** $BM$ is given by:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline (plan) section about the tilt axis, and $V$ is the submerged volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for GM:**
> - **$G$ is above $B$** (typical for most ships) → $M$ must lie *above $G$* for stability → $GM > 0$.
> - For very tall, top-heavy vessels, $G$ can be so high that $M$ lies *below* $G$ → **unstable** ($GM < 0$).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Module 4**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Symbol Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure at depth $h$ | $p = w h = \rho g h$ | $w = \rho g$ in $\text{N/m}^3$, $h$ in $\text{m}$ |
| 2 | Pascal's Law — Hydraulic Press | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Force ratio = area ratio |
| 3 | Total Force on Vertical Submerged Surface | $F = w h_C A$ | $h_C$ = depth of centroid (m) |
| 4 | Depth of Centre of Pressure (Vertical Surface) | $h_P = h_C + \dfrac{I_G}{A \, h_C}$ | $I_G$ = 2nd moment of area about centroidal axis (m$^4$) |
| 5 | Centre of Pressure for Inclined Surface | $h_P^* = h_C^* + \dfrac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \, h_C^*}$ | $\theta$ = angle of surface with free surface |
| 6 | Buoyant Force (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{displaced}}$ | $V$ in m$^3$, $F_B$ in N |
| 7 | Centre of Buoyancy | Centroid of displaced volume | Always at volume centroid |
| 8 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | $I$ = 2nd moment of waterline area (m$^4$) |
| 9 | Metacentric Height | $GM = BM - BG$ | $BG$ measured positive if $B$ is below $G$ |
| 10 | Stability Criterion | Stable if $GM > 0$ | $M$ above $G$ |
| 11 | Apparent Weight in Fluid | $W_{\text{apparent}} = W - F_B$ | Subtract buoyant force |
| 12 | Specific Gravity (Floating Body) | $\text{SG} = \dfrac{V_{\text{submerged}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | For floating bodies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip — I vs $I_G$:**
> - $I$ = 2nd moment of area about the **tilt axis** (for metacentre)
> - $I_G$ = 2nd moment of area about the **centroidal axis** of the immersed surface (for centre of pressure)
> These two $I$'s are **not the same** and are commonly confused in exams.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centre of Pressure on a Vertically Submerged Plane Surface

**Setup:** Consider a plane surface of area $A$ submerged vertically in a static liquid of specific weight $w$. Let:
- The top edge of the surface be at depth $\bar{h}$ below the free liquid surface.
- The surface has a width $b$ that may vary with depth.
- An elemental strip at depth $h$ has width $b$ and thickness $dh$, giving area $dA = b \, dh$.

**Step 1 — Pressure on the elemental strip:**

$$p = w h$$

**Step 2 — Force on the elemental strip:**

$$dF = p \, dA = w h \cdot b \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Total force on the entire surface (integrate from $\bar{h}$ to $\bar{h} + H$):**

$$F = \int_{\bar{h}}^{\bar{h}+H} w h \, b \, dh = w \int_{\bar{h}}^{\bar{h}+H} h \, dA$$

Using the definition of centroid: $\int h \, dA = h_C \cdot A$, where $h_C$ is the depth of the centroid.

$$\boxed{F = w h_C A}$$

This is the **total hydrostatic thrust** on the submerged surface.

**Step 4 — Locate the Centre of Pressure $P$ at depth $h_P$:**

The moment of the elemental force $dF$ about the free liquid surface is $dF \cdot h$. The total moment is:

$$F \cdot h_P = \int dF \cdot h = \int_{\bar{h}}^{\bar{h}+H} w h \cdot b \, dh \cdot h = w \int h^2 \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the second moment of area:**

The integral $\int h^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area** of the surface about the **free liquid surface line**. Using the **parallel axis theorem**:

$$\int h^2 \, dA = I_G + A h_C^2$$

where $I_G$ is the second moment of area about the centroidal axis (parallel to the free surface).

**Step 6 — Equate and solve for $h_P$:**

$$F \cdot h_P = w (I_G + A h_C^2)$$

Substituting $F = w h_C A$:

$$w h_C A \cdot h_P = w (I_G + A h_C^2)$$

Dividing both sides by $w A$:

$$h_C \cdot h_P = \frac{I_G}{A} + h_C^2$$

Dividing by $h_C$:

$$\boxed{h_P = h_C + \frac{I_G}{A \, h_C}}$$

This is the **fundamental CP depth equation** for a vertical surface.

**Step 7 — Inclined surface correction:**

If the surface is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, depths along the surface are measured *along* the slope, and the result becomes:

$$h_P^* = h_C^* + \frac{I_G \sin^2\theta}{A \, h_C^*}$$

The $\sin^2\theta$ factor accounts for the projection of pressure along the inclined plane.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)

**Setup:** Consider a body of arbitrary shape fully immersed in a static fluid of specific weight $w = \rho g$.

**Step 1 — Vertical force on a horizontal slice:**

Take a thin horizontal slab of the body at depth $h$ with cross-sectional area $A_h$ and thickness $dh$. The pressure on its top is $p_{\text{top}} = w(h)$, and on its bottom is $p_{\text{bot}} = w(h + dh)$.

**Step 2 — Net upward force on the slab:**

$$dF_{\text{up}} = (p_{\text{bot}} - p_{\text{top}}) A_h = w \, dh \cdot A_h$$

But $dh \cdot A_h = dV$, the volume of the slab. Therefore:

$$dF_{\text{up}} = w \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the entire body volume $V$:**

$$F_B = \int_V w \, dV = w V = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V$$

$$\boxed{F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} \, g \, V_{\text{displaced}}}$$

This is **Archimedes' Principle**. The buoyant force is exactly the **weight of the displaced fluid**.

**Step 4 — Direction and point of application:**

The force acts **vertically upward** through the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume** (Centre of Buoyancy $B$). For a homogeneous body fully immersed, $B$ coincides with the geometric centroid.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Metacentric Height $GM$

**Setup:** A floating body of total weight $W$ displaces a volume $V$ of fluid. The Centre of Gravity is $G$ and the Centre of Buoyancy is $B$, with $BG$ measured along the central vertical axis (positive downward from $G$ to $B$).

**Step 1 — Body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$:**

When tilted, the buoyancy centre moves to a new location $B_1$. A vertical line through $B_1$ meets the original vertical axis at the **Metacentre $M$**.

**Step 2 — Couple analysis for stability:**

The buoyant force $F_B = W$ acts upward through $B_1$, while the weight $W$ acts downward through $G$. The horizontal distance between their lines of action is $G N$, where $N$ is the foot of the perpendicular from $G$ to the line of $F_B$.

For small $\theta$:

$$G N = GM \cdot \sin\theta \approx GM \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Metacentric radius from geometry of the waterline shift:**

When the body tilts, the submerged wedge on one side equals the emerged wedge on the other side. Both wedges have a centroid located at a horizontal distance of approximately $\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{L}{3}$ from the central axis (for a rectangular waterline of length $L$). The buoyant shift $B B_1$ in the horizontal direction is:

$$B B_1 = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve:**

The metacentric radius $BM$ is given by:

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the **waterline (plan) section** about the axis of rotation (tilt axis).

**Step 5 — Metacentric height:**

$$GM = BM - BG$$

Where $BG$ is the distance between Centre of Buoyancy and Centre of Gravity (positive if $G$ is above $B$).

**Step 6 — Stability criterion:**

- If $M$ is above $G$ → $GM > 0$ → restoring couple exists → **STABLE**
- If $M$ is below $G$ → $GM < 0$ → overturning couple exists → **UNSTABLE**
- If $M$ coincides with $G$ → $GM = 0$ → **NEUTRAL** (rare theoretical case)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Eureka Moment" Connection:**
> Archimedes reportedly discovered the buoyant force principle while taking a bath, shouting "Eureka!" (Greek: "I have found it!"). This is why the symbol $\rho$ (rho, density) appears in every buoyancy formula — it is the master variable of the entire principle.
