# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **State and explain Pascal's Law. Mention any two engineering applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pascal's Law:** The pressure applied at any point in a confined fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the containing vessel.

**Mathematical form:** For two connected pistons of areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ at the same elevation:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Two Engineering Applications:**

1. **Hydraulic Press / Jack** — Used in automobile repair shops and heavy machinery to lift large loads with a small input force. Mechanical advantage = $A_2 / A_1$.
2. **Hydraulic Brakes** — Used in cars and trucks. A small force on the brake pedal produces a large braking force at the wheel cylinders, enabling efficient stopping with minimal driver effort.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **Define Centre of Buoyancy. How is it different from the Centre of Gravity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Centre of Buoyancy (B):** The point through which the resultant buoyant force (upthrust) acts on a submerged body. It is located at the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**.

- **Centre of Gravity (G):** The point through which the resultant weight of the body acts. It is located at the **centroid of the body mass** distribution.

| Property | Centre of Gravity $G$ | Centre of Buoyancy $B$ |
|---|---|---|
| Acts on | Body's mass | Displaced fluid's mass |
| Position | Fixed (for rigid body) | Changes with submersion depth |
| Direction of force | Downward (Weight $W$) | Upward (Buoyant force $F_B$) |

For a *fully submerged homogeneous body*, both coincide at the geometric centroid. For a *floating body*, $B$ is **below** $G$ (typical for stable ships).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** A rectangular gate of width $2$ m and height $1.5$ m is hinged at the top and is vertical. It retains water on one side with the water surface at the level of the hinge. Determine:
> (i) The total hydrostatic thrust on the gate.
> (ii) The depth of the Centre of Pressure from the free water surface.
> (iii) The location of the Centre of Pressure from the top hinge.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solid cylinder of specific gravity $0.8$ floats in water with its axis vertical. If the diameter of the cylinder is $0.4$ m, find the length of the cylinder that is submerged. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q3(a) — Hydrostatic Thrust and Centre of Pressure (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Width of gate, $b = 2$ m
- Height of gate, $H = 1.5$ m
- Hinged at top; water surface at hinge level
- Specific weight of water, $w = 9810$ N/m$^3$

**Step 1 — Area of the gate:**

$$A = b \times H = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Depth of centroid from free surface:**

The centroid of a rectangle lies at half its height from the top:

$$h_C = \frac{H}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Total hydrostatic thrust $F$:**

$$F = w h_C A = 9810 \times 0.75 \times 3 = 22072.5 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 22.07 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Second moment of area of the rectangle about its centroidal axis:**

$$I_G = \frac{b H^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 1.5^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3.375}{12} = 0.5625 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 5 — Depth of Centre of Pressure from the free surface:**

$$h_P = h_C + \frac{I_G}{A \, h_C}$$

$$h_P = 0.75 + \frac{0.5625}{3 \times 0.75} = 0.75 + \frac{0.5625}{2.25}$$

$$h_P = 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h_P = 1.0 \text{ m below the free water surface}}$$

**Step 6 — Location of Centre of Pressure from the top hinge:**

The hinge is at the free surface (depth = 0). So the distance from the hinge to the CP is:

$$y_P = h_P = 1.0 \text{ m from the top}$$

This is **2/3 of the gate height** from the top, which is the classic result for a rectangular gate with water level at its top edge.

---

### **Solution to Q3(b) — Submerged Depth of Floating Cylinder (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Specific gravity of cylinder, $SG = 0.8$
- Diameter, $D = 0.4$ m → Radius $R = 0.2$ m
- Cross-sectional area, $A_c = \pi R^2 = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1 — Principle of floatation:**

A floating body is in equilibrium when the **weight of the body equals the weight of the fluid displaced**:

$$W_{\text{body}} = W_{\text{displaced fluid}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of submerged depth $L_s$:**

Let $L$ = total length of cylinder and $L_s$ = submerged length.

$$W_{\text{body}} = \rho_{\text{cyl}} \, g \, (A_c L) = (SG) \rho_{\text{water}} g A_c L$$

$$W_{\text{displaced}} = \rho_{\text{water}} g A_c L_s$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $L_s$:**

$$(SG) \rho_{\text{water}} g A_c L = \rho_{\text{water}} g A_c L_s$$

$$L_s = (SG) \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Find the ratio (this question implies the entire cylinder length is asked — re-read question):**

> **Note:** The question as stated says "find the length submerged" without giving the total length. The standard interpretation is to find the **fraction submerged**, OR if total length is given in original paper (say $L = 1$ m), then $L_s = 0.8 \times 1 = 0.8$ m.

**Assuming $L = 1$ m:**

$$L_s = 0.8 \times 1 = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_s = 0.8 \text{ m} \text{ (submerged portion of a 1-m cylinder)}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using specific gravity:**

For any floating body, the ratio of submerged volume to total volume equals the specific gravity of the body. So a body with $SG = 0.8$ has $80\%$ of its volume submerged — consistent with the result.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 4, 14 Marks — OR Option for Q3]**
>
> **(a)** State and prove Archimedes' Principle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A floating body has a metacentric height of $0.08$ m. The distance between the Centre of Gravity and the Centre of Buoyancy is $0.05$ m with $G$ above $B$. If the body is tilted by $5°$, find the restoring couple. The weight of the body is $2500$ N. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Q4(a) — Statement and Proof of Archimedes' Principle (7 marks)**

**Statement:** When a body is wholly or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.

**Proof:**

Consider a body of arbitrary shape fully immersed in a fluid of specific weight $w = \rho g$. Consider a vertical prism within the body of cross-sectional area $dA$ extending from the top surface (depth $h_1$) to the bottom surface (depth $h_2$).

**Step 1 — Net upward force on the prism:**

The downward force on the top face is $F_1 = w h_1 \, dA$ and the upward force on the bottom face is $F_2 = w h_2 \, dA$. The net upward force on the prism is:

$$dF_B = F_2 - F_1 = w (h_2 - h_1) \, dA = w \cdot dV$$

where $dV = (h_2 - h_1) \, dA$ is the volume of the prism.

**Step 2 — Sum over the entire body:**

The lateral forces on the sides of the prism cancel out in pairs (equal and opposite). So the net vertical upward force on the entire body is:

$$F_B = \int_V w \, dV = w \int dV = w V$$

$$\boxed{F_B = w V = \rho g V}$$

This is the **weight of the displaced fluid** — hence proved. The buoyant force acts vertically upward through the centroid of the displaced volume (Centre of Buoyancy $B$).

**Hence Archimedes' Principle is proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

### **Solution to Q4(b) — Restoring Couple on a Tilted Floating Body (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Metacentric height, $GM = 0.08$ m
- Distance $BG = 0.05$ m, with $G$ above $B$
- Angle of tilt, $\theta = 5°$
- Weight of body, $W = 2500$ N

**Step 1 — Use the restoring couple formula:**

For a floating body tilted by a small angle $\theta$ (in radians), the restoring couple is:

$$C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Convert angle to radians:**

$$\theta = 5° = 5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.08727 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the restoring couple:**

$$C = 2500 \times 0.08 \times \sin(5°)$$

$$C = 2500 \times 0.08 \times 0.08716$$

$$C = 2500 \times 0.006973 = 17.43 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 17.43 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**

Since $C > 0$, the body experiences a **restoring couple** that tends to bring it back to the upright position. This confirms the body is **stable** (consistent with $GM > 0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Angle in Degrees vs. Radians:** Many students write $\sin(5) = 5$ in the formula — this is WRONG. The small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ is only valid when $\theta$ is in **radians**. For a $5°$ tilt, $\sin(5°) = 0.0872$, not $5$.
> 2. **Sign of $GM$:** A negative $GM$ means the body is unstable and the "restoring" couple is actually an overturning couple. Always verify the sign of $GM$ first.
> 3. **$BG$ vs. $GB$:** Examiners treat $BG$ as positive when $B$ is below $G$. Use $GM = BM - BG$ (not $GM = BM + BG$) when $G$ is above $B$.
> 4. **Units of $I$ vs. $I_G$:** Many students write $\frac{I_G}{V}$ in place of $\frac{I}{V}$ for the metacentre formula. $I$ must be the second moment of the **waterline area** about the tilt axis, NOT the centroidal $I_G$ of the immersed surface.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 10 Minutes Before Exam)**

- ✅ **Pascal's Law** → Pressure transmitted equally in all directions; basis of hydraulic press, jack, brakes.
- ✅ **Pressure at depth $h$** → $p = w h = \rho g h$ — increases *linearly* with depth.
- ✅ **Total hydrostatic thrust** → $F = w h_C A$ (force = pressure at centroid × area).
- ✅ **Depth of Centre of Pressure** → $h_P = h_C + \dfrac{I_G}{A h_C}$ (always *deeper* than centroid).
- ✅ **Inclined surface** → replace $h_C$ with $h_C^* = h_C / \sin\theta$ and add $\sin^2\theta$ factor inside the $\frac{I_G}{A h_C}$ term.
- ✅ **Archimedes' Principle** → $F_B = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{displaced}}$; upward through $B$ (centroid of displaced volume).
- ✅ **Apparent weight** in fluid → $W_{\text{apparent}} = W - F_B$.
- ✅ **Floatation rule** → Body floats if $W \leq F_B$ at equilibrium; submerged fraction = $SG$ for a floating homogeneous body.
- ✅ **Metacentre $M$** → Intersection of new vertical (through $B_1$) with original vertical axis.
- ✅ **Metacentric radius** → $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{submerged}}}$ where $I$ = 2nd moment of waterline area about tilt axis.
- ✅ **Metacentric height** → $GM = BM - BG$ (with $BG$ positive if $B$ is below $G$).
- ✅ **Stability criterion** → $GM > 0$ (stable) | $GM = 0$ (neutral) | $GM < 0$ (unstable/capsizes).
- ✅ **Restoring couple** → $C = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ (θ in radians for accurate values; use $\theta$ directly only for small angles if examiner allows the approximation).
- ✅ **Common units** → $w$: N/m³; $\rho$: kg/m³; $g$: 9.81 m/s²; $h$, $H$, $L$: m; $A$: m²; $I$: m⁴; $F$, $W$: N; $F_B$: N; $C$: N·m.
- ✅ **Engineering applications** → Hydraulic press, hydraulic brakes, JCB arms, ship stabilizers, submarines (variable buoyancy tanks), Cartesian divers, hydrometers.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For any Module 4 numerical, always begin by writing the **given data**, then list the **key formula(s)**, then substitute values step-by-step with **units**. This is the KTU board examiner's formula for full marks — show your work, label your diagrams, and state the physical interpretation of the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Equations of Fluid Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Basic Equations of Fluid Flow ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition The **Basic Equations of Fluid Flow** form the mathematical backbone of fluid mechanics and constitute the governing conservation laws that every fluid (liquid or gas) must satisfy when it moves through any physical system. For a course in **Measurements and Transducers**, these equations are absolutely essential because virtually every flow-measuring device — pitot tubes, venturimeters, orifice plates, rotameters, turbine flowmeters, and ultrasonic flowmeters — is *derived* and *calibrated* using these very laws. The three fundamental equations are: 1. **The Continuity Equation** — Statement of *Conservation of Mass*. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation** — Statement of *Conservation of Energy* (for inviscid, steady flow). 3. **The Momentum Equation** — Statement of *Conservation of Linear Momentum*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT722 – Module 4):** > Students must be able to derive the continuity equation for an incompressible fluid, state and apply Bernoulli's equation across a streamline, and use the momentum equation for calculating forces on flow elements — all of which directly support the working principles of transducers covered in this module. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine water flowing through a horizontal pipe that suddenly narrows (like a garden hose pinched at the end). - **Continuity (Mass Conservation):** Water cannot appear from nowhere or vanish into thin air. When the pipe narrows, the same *amount* of water per second that enters the wide section *must* leave the narrow section. Because the cross-section is smaller, the water has to *speed up*. This is the continuity principle. - **Bernoulli (Energy Conservation):** Water possesses three forms of energy — *pressure* energy, *kinetic* energy (motion), and *potential* energy (height). When the water speeds up in the narrow section, kinetic energy increases. To keep total energy constant (assuming no friction), the pressure energy must *drop*. This is why a clogged pipe sprays farther — pressure drops, velocity rises. - **Momentum (Newton's 2nd Law for fluids):** When a jet of water strikes a turbine blade or a stationary plate, the water changes direction. This change in momentum produces a measurable force that we can use to *quantify* the flow rate — exactly how force-based flowmeters work. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants Used:** > - Density of water at **4 °C**: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - Specific weight of water: $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ > - Atmospheric (standard) pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Conservation Laws — Structured Breakdown **A. Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)** - **Why:** Mass can neither be created nor destroyed within a control volume. - **How:** The mass entering a section per second equals the mass leaving per second (for steady flow with no accumulation). - **Incompressible form:** Volume flow rate is conserved: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ - **Real-world utility:** Basis of *variable-area flowmeters* (rotameters) and *orifice meter* sizing. **B. Conservation of Energy (Bernoulli's Equation)** - **Why:** In an ideal (inviscid, steady, incompressible) flow, the total mechanical energy along a streamline remains constant. - **How:** Pressure head + Velocity head + Elevation head = Constant. - **Assumptions:** Steady flow, incompressible fluid, no friction (no viscous losses), no shaft work, flow along a streamline. - **Real-world utility:** Working principle of the *Venturi meter*, *Pitot-static tube*, and *orifice plate flowmeter*. **C. Conservation of Momentum (Momentum Equation)** - **Why:** Newton's second law applied to a fluid element — the net force on a control volume equals the rate of change of momentum. - **How:** $\sum F = \dot{m}(V_{out} - V_{in})$ - **Real-world utility:** Used to design *bends, nozzles, jets*, and to compute thrust — also underlies *target-type flowmeters*. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All variables below are universally used in KTU board questions. Master these equations thoroughly — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this module. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Applicable Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Continuity (Incompressible) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $A$: m², $V$: m/s | Steady, incompressible, 1-D flow | | 2 | Continuity (Mass form) | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$ | $\dot{m}$: kg/s, $\rho$: kg/m³ | Steady, compressible allowed | | 3 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A V$ | $Q$: m³/s | Definition | | 4 | Bernoulli's Equation | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | $P$: Pa, $z$: m | Inviscid, steady, incompressible, along streamline | | 5 | Bernoulli (Pressure form) | $P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$ | $P$: Pa | Same as above | | 6 | Momentum Equation | $\sum F_x = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x})$ | $F$: N, $Q$: m³/s | Steady flow, constant density | | 7 | Mass Flow Rate (General) | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | kg/s | Steady flow | | 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime indicator | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These three equations, collectively known as the **Eulerian triad** of fluid mechanics, are the *lingua franca* of the entire flow-metering industry. A practicing instrumentation engineer uses them daily when: - **Sizing** a venturimeter for a water-treatment plant. - **Calibrating** a pitot-static tube against a known standard. - **Designing** the nozzle of a rocket engine (momentum equation). - **Estimating** pipe friction losses using the energy equation modified with head-loss terms. - **Validating** CFD simulation results against analytical baselines. In short, no flow transducer can be properly understood without first mastering these three foundational equations. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass) **Setup:** Consider a stream-tube (a bundle of streamlines) with a fluid entering section 1 (area $A_1$, velocity $V_1$, density $\rho_1$) and leaving section 2 (area $A_2$, velocity $V_2$, density $\rho_2$). **Step 1 — Define mass flow rate at section 1:** The mass of fluid crossing $A_1$ in time $\Delta t$ occupies a length $V_1 \Delta t$. $$ m_1 = \rho_1 \, A_1 \, (V_1 \, \Delta t) $$ Therefore, the mass flow rate (mass per unit time) is: $$ \dot{m}_1 = \frac{m_1}{\Delta t} = \rho_1 \, A_1 \, V_1 $$ **Step 2 — Define mass flow rate at section 2 (similarly):** $$ \dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 \, A_2 \, V_2 $$ **Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass:** For steady flow, the rate of mass entering the control volume equals the rate of mass leaving it (no accumulation within). $$ \dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 $$ $$ \rho_1 \, A_1 \, V_1 = \rho_2 \, A_2 \, V_2 $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Incompressibility Condition:** For an incompressible fluid, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ (constant density — a valid assumption for liquids and low-speed gas flows below Mach 0.3). $$ A_1 \, V_1 = A_2 \, V_2 $$ This is the **continuity equation for incompressible steady flow**. The product $AV$ is called the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$. $$ \boxed{Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy) **Setup:** Consider a fluid particle moving from point 1 (elevation $z_1$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$) to point 2 (elevation $z_2$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$) along a streamline, under steady, inviscid, incompressible conditions. **Step 1 — Apply the Work-Energy Theorem:** The work done by all forces (pressure + gravity) on the fluid particle equals its change in kinetic energy. **Step 2 — Work done by pressure force:** The pressure force on the cross-section at point 1 is $P_1 A_1$, acting through distance $ds_1$. Similarly, pressure at point 2 does negative work as it resists motion. $$ W_{pressure} = P_1 A_1 \, ds_1 - P_2 A_2 \, ds_2 $$ Since $A_1 ds_1 = A_2 ds_2 = dV$ (mass conservation → equal volume elements), and dividing by $dV$: $$ \frac{W_{pressure}}{dV} = P_1 - P_2 $$ **Step 3 — Work done by gravity:** The fluid particle of mass $dm = \rho \, dV$ descends (or rises) by a height $(z_1 - z_2)$. The gravitational work per unit volume is: $$ W_{gravity} = \rho g (z_1 - z_2) $$ **Step 4 — Change in kinetic energy per unit volume:** Using $ds = V \, dt$ and mass conservation $dm = \rho A V dt$: $$ \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} dm (V_2^2 - V_1^2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2) $$ **Step 5 — Equate Work Done to Change in Kinetic Energy:** $$ P_1 - P_2 + \rho g (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2) $$ **Step 6 — Rearrange to the canonical form:** $$ P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2 $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant (along a streamline)}} $$ Each term has units of metres (head) and represents a specific form of energy per unit weight: - $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ → **Pressure head** - $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ → **Velocity head** (kinetic energy) - $z$ → **Elevation head** (potential energy) --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Momentum Equation **Setup:** Newton's second law for a control volume of fluid in steady flow. **Step 1 — Statement of the principle:** The net external force acting on the fluid within a control volume equals the rate at which momentum leaves the control volume minus the rate at which momentum enters it. $$ \sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}\vec{V}_{out} - \dot{m}\vec{V}_{in} $$ **Step 2 — Scalar form for x-direction:** $$ \boxed{\sum F_x = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x})} $$ Where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate and $\rho Q = \dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate. **Step 3 — Example application — Force on a reducing bend:** A pipe bend changes the flow direction. The forces on the bend (which the pipe supports must withstand) are computed using this equation. The momentum equation is the foundation of **jet-type flowmeters** and **target meters**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Stream-Tube Control Volume (for Continuity Derivation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Fluid Inlet
Section 1
Area A1
Velocity V1
Density rho1"] --> B((Control
Volume
No Accumulation)) B --> C["Fluid Outlet
Section 2
Area A2
Velocity V2
Density rho2"] D[Mass flow m_dot1
= rho1 A1 V1] --- A E[Mass flow m_dot2
= rho2 A2 V2] --- C F[Conservation Law
m_dot1 = m_dot2] --- B ``` ### 4.2 Bernoulli Energy Concept — Energy Forms Along a Streamline ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Streamline ["Along a Single Streamline — Total Energy is Constant"] P1["Pressure Energy
P / rho g"] --> Sum((Total Head
H = constant)) V1["Kinetic Energy
V squared / 2g"] --> Sum Z1["Potential Energy
Elevation z"] --> Sum end Sum --> Eq["Bernoulli Equation:
P1/rho g + V1 squared / 2g + z1
= P2/rho g + V2 squared / 2g + z2"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flow-Measuring Transducer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Fluid
Flow Phenomenon] --> B["Primary Sensing Element
Orifice, Venturi, Pitot, Nozzle"] B --> C[Differential Pressure
or Force Signal] C --> D["Secondary Transducer
Diaphragm, Strain Gauge,
Piezoelectric Sensor"] D --> E[Conditioned Electrical
Signal: mV or mA] E --> F[Display or
Controller Unit] G["Governing Equations:
1. Continuity A1V1 = A2V2
2. Bernoulli P + half rho V squared + rho g z = constant
3. Momentum F = rho Q dV"] -.-> B G -.-> D ``` ### 4.4 Force-Balance Momentum Diagram (Reducing Bend) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Inlet jet
Velocity V1
Mass flow m_dot"] --> B((Pipe Bend
Reducing
Area A2)) B --> C["Outlet jet
Velocity V2
Along different direction"] D["Net Force on Bend
F = m_dot times V2 minus V1
F = rho Q times delta V"] -.-> B ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** **State the continuity equation for a steady, incompressible fluid flow and explain its physical significance in flow measurement.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The continuity equation for a steady, incompressible fluid flow states that the volumetric flow rate remains constant along a stream tube: $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant} $$ **Physical Significance:** It expresses the *conservation of mass* — fluid cannot accumulate or vanish within the pipe. In flow measurement, this principle allows us to **infer velocity from area changes** (and vice versa). For example, in a venturimeter, the throat area is known, and measuring the velocity there (via pressure drop using Bernoulli's equation) directly gives the flow rate in the larger pipe. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the compressible form ($\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$) when the question specifies *incompressible*. For a 3-mark question, also explicitly mention that $A$ and $V$ must be at the *same* cross-section / stream-tube. Failure to do so costs 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **List the three forms of energy represented in Bernoulli's equation. State any three assumptions under which Bernoulli's equation is valid.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three forms of energy per unit weight of fluid in Bernoulli's equation are: 1. **Pressure energy / Pressure head:** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ (metres) 2. **Kinetic energy / Velocity head:** $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ (metres) 3. **Potential energy / Elevation head:** $z$ (metres) Any three of the following assumptions: - The fluid is **ideal** (inviscid — no friction / viscosity). - The flow is **steady**. - The fluid is **incompressible** (constant $\rho$). - The flow is along a **streamline** (or irrotational). - **No energy addition** by pumps or turbines and **no losses**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern: Module Internal Choice A]** **(a)** Derive the continuity equation for a one-dimensional, steady, incompressible fluid flow using the principle of conservation of mass. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Water flows through a horizontal pipe that contracts from a diameter of **20 cm** at section 1 to **10 cm** at section 2. If the velocity at section 1 is **2 m/s**, find: (i) The velocity at section 2. (ii) The volumetric flow rate. (iii) The mass flow rate. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [Present the full derivation as given in **Section 3.1** of these notes.] **Step 1:** Mass entering section 1 per unit time: $\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$ **Step 2:** Mass leaving section 2 per unit time: $\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ **Step 3:** Apply conservation of mass for steady flow: $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$ **Step 4:** Apply incompressibility: $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ **Step 5:** Final equation: $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q $$ **Assumptions:** - Steady flow. - Incompressible fluid. - One-dimensional flow along a stream-tube. - No sources or sinks of mass within the control volume. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $D_1 = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $D_2 = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Calculate areas:** $$ A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2 $$ **(i) Velocity at section 2 (using continuity):** $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 $$ $$ V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.03142 \times 2}{0.007854} $$ $$ \boxed{V_2 = 8.0 \text{ m/s}} $$ **(ii) Volumetric flow rate:** $$ Q = A_1 V_1 = 0.03142 \times 2 $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 0.0628 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 62.83 \text{ litres/s}} $$ **(iii) Mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 0.0628 $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{m} = 62.83 \text{ kg/s}} $$ --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern: Module Internal Choice B]** **(a)** Starting from the principle of conservation of energy, derive Bernoulli's equation for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A venturimeter with throat diameter **7 cm** is fitted in a horizontal pipe of diameter **21 cm**. The differential pressure recorded between the upstream tapping and throat is **0.5 bar** ($5 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$). Assuming ideal flow and using Bernoulli's equation together with the continuity equation, calculate the discharge (volumetric flow rate) through the venturimeter. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [Present the full derivation as given in **Section 3.2** of these notes — covering the work-energy balance per unit volume with pressure work, gravitational work, and kinetic energy change.] **Final boxed result:** $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 $$ **Assumptions** (must be stated for full marks): 1. Steady flow. 2. Incompressible fluid. 3. Inviscid (frictionless). 4. Flow along a streamline. 5. No pump/turbine work in the section. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - Pipe diameter $D_1 = 21 \text{ cm} = 0.21 \text{ m}$, so $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.21)^2 = 0.03464 \text{ m}^2$ - Throat diameter $D_2 = 7 \text{ cm} = 0.07 \text{ m}$, so $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.07)^2 = 0.003848 \text{ m}^2$ - $P_1 - P_2 = 0.5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$ - Horizontal pipe: $z_1 = z_2$ - $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1 — Continuity equation:** $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \implies V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 V_2 $$ $$ V_1 = \left(\frac{7}{21}\right)^2 V_2 = \frac{1}{9} V_2 $$ **Step 2 — Bernoulli's equation (horizontal pipe):** $$ \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $V_1 = V_2/9$:** $$ \frac{V_2^2 - (V_2/9)^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{81}\right)}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2 \cdot \frac{80}{81}}{2g} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$:** $$ V_2^2 = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} \cdot \frac{81}{80} = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^4}{1000} \cdot \frac{81}{80} $$ $$ V_2^2 = 100 \cdot \frac{81}{80} = 101.25 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{V_2 = 10.06 \text{ m/s}} $$ **Step 5 — Discharge (Volumetric flow rate):** $$ Q = A_2 V_2 = 0.003848 \times 10.06 $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 0.0387 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 38.7 \text{ litres/s}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Bernoulli Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting to use continuity** first to express $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$ (or vice versa) before applying Bernoulli — losing **2 marks**. > 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Pressures must be in **Pa** (not bar) when used in $P/(\rho g)$. 1 bar $= 10^5$ Pa. > 3. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — for a 7-mark derivation, the assumption list alone carries **1 to 1.5 marks**. > 4. **Sign errors in pressure differential** — always verify that the higher-pressure side is correctly identified as section 1. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three governing laws:** Mass continuity, Bernoulli (energy), Momentum. - **Continuity (incompressible):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ — *volume* conservation. - **Continuity (general):** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ — *mass* conservation. - **Bernoulli is valid only when:** steady + incompressible + inviscid + along streamline. - **Bernoulli equation form:** $P + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{constant}$ - **Head form (per unit weight):** $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ (units: metres of fluid). - **Momentum equation:** $\sum F = \rho Q (V_{out} - V_{in})$ — foundation of jet/target flowmeters. - **Pressure form is preferred** for numerical substitution; **head form is preferred** for intuitive interpretation. - **Always begin** Bernoulli-based numericals by applying **continuity first** to relate velocities. - **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. - **Practical losses** are added in real systems via a head-loss term $h_L$ on the right side of Bernoulli — but this is beyond Module 4's scope (covered in depth in later modules). - **Transducer link:** Every differential-pressure flowmeter (venturi, orifice, nozzle, pitot) *requires* these three equations for its calibration equation. ---

topics.title: Continuity, Momentum and Energy Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three Fundamental Governing Equations of Fluid Flow

In the analytical study of fluid mechanics and instrumentation, every measurement involving flow rate, force exerted by a moving fluid, or the total head available in a system ultimately derives from **three conservation laws** rooted in Newtonian physics and thermodynamics. These are the **Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass), the **Momentum Equation** (Newton's Second Law applied to a control volume), and the **Energy Equation** (First Law of Thermodynamics for a flowing fluid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Continuity, Momentum, and Energy Equations form the **analytical backbone** of all flow-measurement transducers (orifice plates, venturi meters, pitot tubes, rotameters, turbine meters, and target meters). KTU board questions frequently test the ability to apply these three equations to derive discharge, head loss, and force expressions for standard flow-metering setups.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Highway Analogy"

To grasp these three equations intuitively, imagine a multi-lane **highway** representing a fluid pipeline.

| Conservation Law | Highway Analogy | Real Fluid Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass (Continuity)** | The number of cars entering a tunnel per second MUST equal the number exiting per second (cars cannot vanish or magically appear). | Mass flowing into a control volume per unit time = Mass flowing out. |
| **Momentum** | A stream of cars hitting a road barrier exerts a force on it. More cars or faster cars → larger force. | A jet of fluid striking a stationary plate, or a fluid changing direction in a pipe bend, exerts a force. |
| **Energy** | Cars entering a hilly section with a given speed and height must possess enough kinetic + potential energy to climb the hill (minus friction losses). | Total mechanical energy (pressure + kinetic + potential) at inlet = Total mechanical energy at outlet + losses + work done. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** The Continuity Equation tells us **how much** fluid flows, the Momentum Equation tells us **what forces** the fluid exerts, and the Energy Equation tells us **how the energy is distributed** along the flow path. Together, they completely define the kinematic and dynamic state of any steady, single-phase flow.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions

**Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass):**
States that for a control volume, the rate of change of mass inside the volume equals the net mass flow rate entering minus the net mass flow rate leaving. Mathematically, for a steady flow:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Momentum Equation (Linear Momentum Balance):**
States that the net force acting on a control volume equals the rate of change of linear momentum of the fluid crossing its boundaries. For steady flow:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{external} = \sum_{out} \dot{m}\vec{V} - \sum_{in} \dot{m}\vec{V}$$

**Energy Equation (Steady Flow Energy Equation — SFEE):**
States that the total energy entering a control volume per unit weight of fluid equals the total energy leaving plus the energy losses (and minus or plus any work done by/upon the fluid). For a unit mass:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + e_{loss} - w_{pump} + w_{turbine}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for Measurement & Transducers:** Most flow measurement devices (Venturi, orifice, nozzle) operate on the principle that a pressure drop is created when flow area is reduced. The **Continuity Equation** gives the velocity–area relationship, and the **Energy Equation** (Bernoulli) gives the pressure–velocity relationship. Combining them yields the discharge coefficient and flow-rate equations used in every flow meter calibration. The **Momentum Equation** is essential for impact-type flow meters (target meters) and for calculating jet forces on plates and bends.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Flows Governed by These Equations

| Flow Type | Continuity Form | Momentum Form | Energy Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady, Incompressible, Inviscid** | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\sum F = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Bernoulli (no loss) |
| **Steady, Compressible, Inviscid** | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Same form with $\rho(V)$ variation | Modified Bernoulli |
| **Steady, Incompressible, Viscous** | Same as incompressible inviscid | Momentum with wall shear | Bernoulli + head loss |
| **Unsteady (oscillatory)** | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Includes time derivative of momentum | Transient SFEE |

For the KTU 2024 syllabus in the context of measurement transducers, we restrict ourselves almost entirely to **steady, incompressible flow** with possible viscous losses.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation — Logic Path

1. **Choose a control volume** (a fixed region in space through which fluid flows).
2. **Apply conservation of mass:** Rate of mass accumulation inside = (Net mass inflow rate).
3. For **steady flow**, the accumulation term vanishes.
4. For **incompressible flow**, density $\rho$ = constant.
5. Recognize that the volume flow rate $Q = A \cdot V$ (uniform velocity).
6. Equate inflow to outflow.

### 2.1.2 Momentum Equation — Logic Path

1. **Newton's Second Law** for a system of particles: $\sum \vec{F} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(m\vec{V})_{sys}$.
2. **Reynolds Transport Theorem** converts the system derivative to a control-volume (Eulerian) form.
3. For **steady flow**, the local time derivative vanishes, leaving only the flux term.
4. The net force is the sum of **pressure forces**, **shear (viscous) forces**, **body forces** (gravity), and **external mechanical forces** (anchors, supports).
5. In ideal measurement problems, viscous forces are neglected and only pressure + momentum flux are retained.

### 2.1.3 Energy Equation — Logic Path

1. **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a control volume: $\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt}$.
2. Apply **Reynolds Transport Theorem** to total energy $E = u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$ (internal + kinetic + potential).
3. For **steady, adiabatic, no-shaft-work** flow with only friction losses $h_L$, the mechanical energy form emerges.
4. Dividing by $\dot{m}g$ yields the form used in hydraulic circuits (head form); dividing by $\dot{m}$ yields the form used in compressor/jet problems (energy per unit mass).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Continuity Equation — Master Forms

| # | Equation | Applicable Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | Steady, compressible, 1-D | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 2 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Steady, **incompressible** | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Definition of volume flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | General (unsteady, compressible) | $\text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}$ |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \rho Q$ | Mass flow rate definition | $\text{kg/s}$ |

### 2.2.2 Momentum Equation — Master Forms

| # | Equation | Applicable Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | $\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$ | Steady, uniform inlet/outlet | $\text{N}$ |
| 7 | $F_x = \rho Q (V_{2x} - V_{1x})$ | Cartesian x-component | $\text{N}$ |
| 8 | $F_y = \rho Q (V_{2y} - V_{1y}) + W$ | With weight W of fluid in CV | $\text{N}$ |
| 9 | $F_{jet} = \rho Q V$ | Force of jet on a stationary plate | $\text{N}$ |
| 10 | $F_{bend} = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1) + p_2 A_2 - p_1 A_1$ | Force on pipe bend | $\text{N}$ |
| 11 | $F = \beta \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$ | With momentum correction factor $\beta$ | $\text{N}$ |

### 2.2.3 Energy Equation — Master Forms

| # | Equation | Applicable Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 | $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = H$ | Total head (Bernoulli sum) | $\text{m}$ |
| 13 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | **Bernoulli's equation** (no loss, no machine) | $\text{m}$ |
| 14 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | With head loss $h_L$ | $\text{m}$ |
| 15 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + e_{loss}$ | Energy per unit mass form | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 16 | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 + w_{pump} = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + e_{loss} + w_{turbine}$ | Full SFEE with pump and turbine | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 17 | $H_p = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{\Delta V^2}{2g} + \Delta z + h_L$ | Pump head definition | $\text{m}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting the consistent use of either the **head form** (divided by $g$, units in m) or the **energy form** (divided by mass, units in J/kg). Mixing them in a single equation causes dimensional inconsistency and full mark deduction. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests dimensional awareness.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Equation | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **Continuity** | Sizing of pipes, ducts, valves; calibration of rotameters and turbine flowmeters. |
| **Momentum** | Design of pipe supports and thrust blocks, jet propulsion, hydraulic turbines, impact-type flow sensors, rocket nozzle thrust calculation. |
| **Energy** | Sizing pumps and turbines, calculating head loss in pipe networks, venturi/orifice flow-meter analysis, penstock design in hydroelectric plants, HVAC duct design, aircraft pitot-static systems. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Continuity Equation (Integral Form)

**Starting Point:** Conservation of mass for a fixed control volume (CV).

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

The first term is the rate of change of mass inside the CV; the second is the net mass efflux through the control surface (CS), with $\hat{n}$ as the outward normal.

**Step 1:** Apply the **steady-flow condition.** For steady flow, the time derivative vanishes:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2:** Therefore the surface integral alone must equal zero:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3:** Split the surface integral into an inlet (1) and outlet (2). At inlet, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = -V_1$ (since velocity opposes the outward normal). At outlet, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = +V_2$ (velocity aligns with outward normal).

$$\int_{A_1} \rho (-V_1) \, dA + \int_{A_2} \rho (+V_2) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 4:** For **uniform 1-D velocity** profiles at inlet and outlet, take $\rho$ and $V$ out of the integrals:

$$-\rho_1 V_1 A_1 + \rho_2 V_2 A_2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to obtain the steady-flow continuity equation:

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2}$$

**Step 6:** For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ = constant), divide both sides by $\rho$:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q}$$

This is the canonical form used for liquid flow measurement (water, oil, etc.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Momentum Equation (Linear Momentum)

**Starting Point:** Newton's Second Law in control-volume form (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to linear momentum $m\vec{V}$):

$$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **steady-flow condition.** Time derivative of momentum inside the CV vanishes:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the net external force equals the net efflux of momentum through the control surface:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

**Step 3:** For an inlet (1) and outlet (2) with **uniform 1-D velocity** profiles:

$$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = -\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \vec{V}_1 + \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \vec{V}_2$$

**Step 4:** Using continuity ($\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m}$), simplify:

$$\boxed{\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_2 - \vec{V}_1)}$$

**Step 5:** The external forces on the CV typically include:
- **Pressure forces** on inlet and outlet: $+p_1 A_1 \hat{n}_1 + p_2 A_2 \hat{n}_2$ (forces *on* the fluid push inward).
- **Weight** of fluid: $W = \rho g V_{CV}$ acting downward.
- **Reaction force** $\vec{R}$ from solid surfaces (pipe walls, plates, supports).
- **Viscous (shear) forces** at walls — often neglected in inviscid analysis.

**Step 6 — Worked Sub-Case (Force of a Jet on a Stationary Plate):**

A jet of area $A$ and velocity $V$ strikes a flat plate held perpendicular to the jet. We want the force $F$ on the plate.

*Apply momentum in the jet direction (x):*

$$-F = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$$

The fluid enters with $V_1 = V$ and leaves the plate in the transverse direction (for a flat plate normal to the jet, $V_2 = 0$ in the x-direction because the fluid is deflected sideways).

$$-F = \rho A V (0 - V) = -\rho A V^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V}$$

**Step 7 — Worked Sub-Case (Force on a Bend):**

A horizontal pipe bend turns the flow by angle $\theta$. Pressure at inlet $= p_1$, at outlet $= p_2$, areas $A_1 = A_2 = A$, and velocity $V_1 = V_2 = V$. Continuity is trivially satisfied.

*Apply momentum in x-direction:*

$$p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2 \cos\theta - R_x = \dot{m}(V_2 \cos\theta - V_1)$$

*Apply momentum in y-direction:*

$$0 - p_2 A_2 \sin\theta - R_y = \dot{m}(V_2 \sin\theta - 0)$$

Solving these two simultaneously gives the horizontal and vertical components of the force that the fluid exerts on the bend (i.e., $-R_x, -R_y$ are the forces of the fluid on the bend). This is the basis of the **target-type flow meter** and **jet thrust** calculations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Equation (Bernoulli's Equation) for Steady, Incompressible, Inviscid Flow

**Starting Point:** First Law of Thermodynamics for a control volume, in the absence of heat transfer, shaft work, and viscous dissipation:

$$\frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} + \int_{CS} \rho \left(u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

where $u$ is the internal energy per unit mass.

**Step 1:** For **steady, adiabatic, no-shaft-work** flow, the left side and the $\dot{Q}$ and $\dot{W}$ terms all vanish. The equation reduces to:

$$\int_{CS} \rho \left(u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** For an incompressible fluid with no friction, the internal energy does not change between inlet and outlet (it is a state function of $T$ and for an incompressible fluid, $T$ is constant). Therefore, $u$ drops out:

$$\int_{CS} \rho \left(\frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3:** Split into inlet (1) and outlet (2):

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \left(\frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1\right) - \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \left(\frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2\right) = 0$$

(The sign flips because at the inlet $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = -V_1$.)

**Step 4:** Apply continuity ($\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \rho Q$) and divide by $\rho Q$:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

This is energy per unit mass conservation for inviscid flow (no pressure term yet — that comes from the work done by pressure forces in the general case).

**Step 5:** Restore the **pressure work** term. Pressure forces at inlet and outlet do work $pA \cdot V \cdot dt$ on the fluid. The work done per unit mass by the inlet pressure is $p_1/\rho$ (positive, energy added) and by the outlet pressure is $p_2/\rho$ (negative, energy removed). Adding these to both sides:

$$\boxed{\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2}$$

This is **Bernoulli's Equation** in its **energy-per-unit-mass** form (units: J/kg).

**Step 6:** Divide the entire equation by $g$ to obtain the **head form** (units: m of fluid):

$$\boxed{\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2}$$

**Step 7:** For a real (viscous) flow, add the head loss $h_L$ on the right-hand side:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 8:** If a pump adds energy $h_p$ or a turbine extracts energy $h_t$ between sections 1 and 2:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + h_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Most KTU problems specify which form (head or energy per unit mass) to use. Read the question stem carefully. The head form is preferred for hydraulic problems (water), while the energy-per-mass form is preferred for gas/compressor problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Application to Venturi / Orifice Flow Meter (Combined Use of All Three Equations)

This derivation is the most-tested application in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Setup:** A horizontal venturi meter with inlet area $A_1$, throat area $A_2$, and a differential manometer reading $h$ (in metres of manometric fluid). Let $\rho$ be the density of the flowing fluid and $\rho_m$ be the density of the manometric fluid.

**Step 1 — Continuity:**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 2 — Bernoulli (horizontal, no losses):**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute continuity into Bernoulli:**

$$\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2g(p_1 - p_2)/\rho}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2g \cdot \Delta h}{\left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}$$

where $\Delta h = (p_1 - p_2)/\rho g$ is the pressure head difference.

**Step 5 — Manometer equation (relating $h$ to $\Delta h$):**

$$p_1 - p_2 = (\rho_m - \rho) g h$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta h = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = h \left(\frac{\rho_m - \rho}{\rho}\right) = h \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 g \, h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Theoretical volume flow rate:**

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g \, h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Actual flow rate (introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$):**

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g \, h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}$$

This single equation simultaneously embodies the **Continuity Equation** (Step 1), the **Momentum Equation** (in deriving the discharge coefficient experimentally), and the **Energy Equation** (Step 2). It is the canonical flow-meter equation for KTU problems.

## 4.3 Application Flow — Venturi / Orifice Flow Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Inlet: Area A1, Pressure p1, Velocity V1] --> CON[Apply CONTINUITY]
    CON --> VREL[A1 V1 = A2 V2]
    VREL --> BER[Apply BERNOULLI]
    BER --> PBER[p1 plus 0.5 rho V1 squared = p2 plus 0.5 rho V2 squared]
    PBER --> SOLV[Solve for V2]
    SOLV --> MANO[Use MANOMETER: p1 minus p2 equals rho m minus rho times g times h]
    MANO --> FORM[Derive VELOCITY expression]
    FORM --> QTH[Theoretical Discharge Qth equals A2 V2]
    QTH --> CD[Multiply by Cd: Actual equals Cd times Qth]
    CD --> END[Final Discharge Equation for Flow Meter]
```
