# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the continuity equation for a steady, incompressible fluid flow. Mention the assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **continuity equation** for a steady, incompressible fluid flow is based on the principle of conservation of mass. It states that the mass flow rate entering a control volume must equal the mass flow rate leaving it.

For an incompressible fluid with constant density $\rho$:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(constant)}$$

where $A_1, A_2$ are the cross-sectional areas at sections 1 and 2, and $V_1, V_2$ are the corresponding average velocities.

**Assumptions:**
1. The flow is **steady** (no time variation of properties at a point).
2. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
3. The flow is **one-dimensional** at the inlet and outlet sections.
4. There is **no mass addition or extraction** within the control volume (single inlet, single outlet).

**Significance:** This equation establishes the fundamental inverse relationship between area and velocity in flow-measurement devices. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the momentum equation and the energy equation. State one practical engineering application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Momentum Equation | Energy Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical basis** | Newton's Second Law (conservation of linear momentum) | First Law of Thermodynamics (conservation of energy) |
| **Mathematical form** | $\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_2 - \vec{V}_1)$ | $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ |
| **What it predicts** | **Forces** exerted by/on the fluid (N) | **Pressures, velocities, and heads** along the flow (m of fluid) |
| **Practical application** | Calculation of **jet thrust on a turbine blade** or **force on a pipe bend / target flow meter** | Calculation of **discharge in a venturi meter** or **head loss in a pipe** |

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE convention: Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both must be attempted for full marks.

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

#### (a) A horizontal pipe of diameter **300 mm** at section 1 contracts to a diameter of **150 mm** at section 2. Oil of specific gravity **0.85** flows steadily. The pressure at section 1 is **120 kPa** (gauge) and the velocity at section 1 is **2 m/s**. Neglecting losses, determine:
   1. The mass flow rate of oil.
   2. The velocity at section 2.
   3. The pressure at section 2.
   4. The force required to hold the contraction in place (assume no flange moments).

#### (b) A jet of water issuing from a nozzle strikes a flat plate held normal to the jet. The diameter of the jet is **75 mm** and the velocity is **25 m/s**. The plate is stationary. Find:
   1. The force exerted by the jet on the plate.
   2. The force if the plate moves with a velocity of **10 m/s** away from the nozzle (in the same direction as the jet).

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (a) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Specific gravity of oil $= 0.85$ ⟹ $\rho_{oil} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $p_1 = 120 \text{ kPa} = 120{,}000 \text{ Pa}$, $V_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$
- Horizontal pipe ⟹ $z_1 = z_2$
- No losses ⟹ $h_L = 0$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — (i) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A_1 V_1 = 850 \times 0.0707 \times 2 = 120.2 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Velocity at section 2 (from Continuity):**

$$V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{A_2} = \frac{0.0707 \times 2}{0.01767} = 8.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — (iii) Pressure at section 2 (from Bernoulli's Equation):**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Since $z_1 = z_2$:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$

$$p_2 = p_1 + \frac{\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2}$$

$$p_2 = 120{,}000 + \frac{850 \times (2^2 - 8^2)}{2}$$

$$p_2 = 120{,}000 + \frac{850 \times (4 - 64)}{2} = 120{,}000 + \frac{850 \times (-60)}{2}$$

$$p_2 = 120{,}000 - 25{,}500 = 94{,}500 \text{ Pa} = 94.5 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

**Step 4 — (iv) Force required to hold the contraction:**

Apply the **momentum equation** in the x-direction (along the flow). The fluid in the contraction experiences pressure forces and the force $R_x$ from the support (taken positive in the direction of flow).

$$p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2 - R_x = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$$

The force $R_x$ is the force exerted *by the support on the fluid*. The force required to hold the contraction *in place* (i.e., force of the fluid on the support) is the equal and opposite reaction, $F_x = R_x$.

Substitute values:

$$R_x = p_1 A_1 - p_2 A_2 - \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$$

$$R_x = (120{,}000)(0.0707) - (94{,}500)(0.01767) - (120.2)(8.0 - 2.0)$$

$$R_x = 8484 - 1670.0 - 721.2 = 6092.8 \text{ N}$$

The force required to hold the contraction in place is **$F_x \approx 6093 \text{ N}$ (acting in the flow direction)**. The student must state the direction explicitly to secure full marks. **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Jet diameter $d = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Jet velocity $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Density of water $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Jet area and flow rate:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A V = (4.418 \times 10^{-3})(25) = 0.1104 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Force on stationary plate:**

For a jet striking a stationary flat plate normal to the flow, the jet is deflected sideways (tangentially) and the velocity normal to the plate becomes zero at the plate surface.

$$F = \rho Q (V - 0) = \rho Q V$$

$$F = 1000 \times 0.1104 \times 25 = 2761 \text{ N} \approx 2.76 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Force when plate moves at $u = 10 \text{ m/s}$ in the same direction as the jet:**

This is a **moving plate** problem. The relative velocity of the jet with respect to the plate is $V_{rel} = V - u = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ m/s}$. The mass flow rate striking the plate is reduced because the plate is "running away" from the jet:

$$\dot{m}_{strike} = \rho A (V - u) = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 15 = 66.27 \text{ kg/s}$$

The force on the moving plate is:

$$F' = \dot{m}_{strike} \times (V - u) = \rho A (V - u)^2$$

$$F' = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times (15)^2 = 1000 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 225$$

$$F' = 994.0 \text{ N} \approx 0.994 \text{ kN}$$

**Verification using the general moving-blade formula:**

$$F' = \rho A (V - u)^2 = 994 \text{ N}$$

This is the force exerted on the moving plate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

#### (a) Derive the **Bernoulli's equation** in the energy-per-unit-mass form starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics for a steady, incompressible, inviscid flow. State all assumptions clearly.

#### (b) A **venturi meter** is installed in a horizontal pipeline carrying oil of specific gravity **0.80**. The inlet diameter is **200 mm** and the throat diameter is **100 mm**. A differential U-tube mercury manometer connected across the meter reads a deflection of **200 mm of mercury**. The discharge coefficient of the meter is **0.98**. Calculate the volume flow rate of oil in litres per second.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (a) [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow.
2. Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$).
3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow — no viscous losses.
4. Flow along a streamline.
5. No shaft work (no pump, no turbine in the CV).
6. No heat transfer (adiabatic).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** First Law of Thermodynamics for a steady control volume (no heat transfer, no shaft work):

$$\int_{CS} \rho \left(u + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right) (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** For an incompressible, inviscid fluid, internal energy $u$ is constant (state property depending only on $T$, which is constant for the assumed conditions). Hence the $u$ terms cancel between inlet and outlet, and we are left with:

$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 \left(\frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1\right) = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 \left(\frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply continuity ($\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \rho Q$) and divide both sides by $\rho Q$:

$$\frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 4:** To recover the pressure term, consider the work done by pressure forces at the inlet and outlet. The pressure force $pA$ acts over a distance $V\,dt$ in time $dt$, doing work $pAV\,dt = pQ\,dt$ on the fluid (positive at inlet, negative at outlet). The work done per unit mass is $p/\rho$. Adding the inlet pressure work on the left and the outlet pressure work (subtracted) on the right:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

This is **Bernoulli's equation in energy-per-unit-mass form** (units: J/kg). **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Specific gravity of oil $= 0.80$ ⟹ $\rho = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific gravity of mercury $= 13.6$ ⟹ $\rho_m = 13{,}600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Manometer reading $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.98$

**Step 1 — Areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Area ratio squared:**

$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.1}{0.2}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard venturi discharge formula (derived in Section 3.4):**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g \, h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 = \frac{13.6}{0.80} - 1 = 17 - 1 = 16$$

$$2 g h \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right) = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 \times 16 = 62.784 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\sqrt{62.784} = 7.924 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{0.007854 \times 7.924}{0.9683}$$

$$Q = 0.98 \times \frac{0.06224}{0.9683} = 0.98 \times 0.0643 = 0.0630 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres per second:**

$$Q = 0.0630 \times 1000 = 63.0 \text{ L/s}$$

The volume flow rate of oil is **$Q \approx 63 \text{ L/s}$**. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention in Momentum Equation (–3 to –5 marks):** Students often write $F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2)$ instead of $\sum F = \dot{m}(V_2 - V_1)$. Get the direction right; draw a free-body diagram.
>
> 2. **Pressure Force Direction (–2 to –3 marks):** The pressure force on a control volume acts **into** the surface (pushing the fluid). The sign of $p_1 A_1$ and $p_2 A_2$ in the momentum balance depends on the orientation of the area vector. Confusing this is the #1 cause of wrong force answers.
>
> 3. **Manometer Equation (–2 marks):** Forgetting the ratio $\rho_m / \rho$ inside the square root, or using $\rho_m - \rho$ in the numerator but $2g \cdot h$ without the specific-gravity correction. The correct form is $\Delta h = h \cdot (\rho_m - \rho)/\rho$.
>
> 4. **Mixing Head and Energy Forms (–2 marks):** The equation $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ (in metres) cannot be added to $\frac{V^2}{2}$ (in J/kg). Stay within one consistent form throughout a single problem.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Head Loss in Bernoulli (–2 to –3 marks):** Bernoulli's equation is valid ONLY for inviscid, lossless flow. For real flows, add $h_L$ explicitly. If the problem says "neglecting losses," then $h_L = 0$ — but state this assumption.
>
> 6. **Moving-Plate Formula (–3 marks):** For a moving plate, the relative velocity $(V - u)$ appears in the formula, not the absolute velocity $V$. The mass flow rate striking the plate is $\rho A (V - u)$, not $\rho A V$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Continuity Equation (Incompressible):** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. The product $AV$ is invariant along a streamline. Decrease in area → increase in velocity. *(Foundation of venturi/orifice analysis.)*

- **Continuity Equation (Compressible):** $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$. The mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ is invariant.

- **Momentum Equation (Cartesian, steady):** $\sum F_x = \dot{m}(V_{2x} - V_{1x})$, $\sum F_y = \dot{m}(V_{2y} - V_{1y})$. Always draw a free-body diagram first.

- **Jet on Stationary Plate:** $F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V$.

- **Jet on Moving Plate (velocity $u$):** $F = \rho A (V - u)^2$.

- **Force on Pipe Bend:** Combine pressure forces and momentum flux. Use the angle $\theta$ between inlet and outlet velocity vectors. The "anchor force" is the reaction needed to hold the bend in place.

- **Bernoulli's Equation (Head form, SI):** $\dfrac{p}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z$ = constant (along a streamline, inviscid, incompressible, steady flow). Units: **metres** of fluid.

- **Bernoulli's Equation (Energy form, SI):** $\dfrac{p}{\rho} + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ = constant. Units: **J/kg**.

- **SFEE (with losses and machine work):** $\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_p = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + h_t$.

- **Venturi / Orifice Discharge Formula:** $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$. Memorize this — it is the **single most tested formula** in this module.

- **Pitot Tube Velocity:** $V = \sqrt{2g \cdot \Delta h}$ where $\Delta h$ is the stagnation-to-static head difference.

- **Dimensional Consistency Check:** Always verify SI units. $p$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m/s, $z$ in m, $g$ in m/s², $Q$ in m³/s, $F$ in N, $h$ in m.

- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics (high-probability questions):**
  1. Derivation of Bernoulli's equation.
  2. Numerical on a venturi or orifice meter.
  3. Force exerted by a jet on a stationary/moving plate.
  4. Force on a pipe bend.
  5. Combined continuity + Bernoulli + momentum on a contraction/expansion.

- **Key Constant:** Density of water $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$; Specific gravity of mercury $= 13.6$ (at STP). Specific gravity of standard manometric fluids: mercury $13.6$, carbon tetrachloride $1.58$, water $1.0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bernoulli's Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT722 - MEASURMENTS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Equations # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Bernoulli's Equation** is a fundamental principle in fluid mechanics that expresses the conservation of energy for a flowing fluid along a streamline. It states that for an **ideal, incompressible, non-viscous, and steady flow**, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential (elevation) energy per unit mass remains constant at every point along a streamline. Mathematically, Bernoulli's equation between two points (1) and (2) in a flow field is expressed as: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 = \text{Constant}$$ where each term represents the energy head (in meters) of the flowing fluid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT722 - Module 4):** In the context of Measurements and Transducers, Bernoulli's equation is the **fundamental governing principle** behind primary flow measurement devices such as the **Venturi meter, Orifice plate, Flow nozzle, and Pitot tube**. These are classified as **head-type or differential-pressure-type flow transducers**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation) Imagine water flowing through a horizontal pipe that has a narrow section (constriction) in the middle, similar to the neck of a plastic water bottle: - When water enters the **wide section**, it flows **slowly** but exerts **high pressure** on the pipe walls. - As water is forced into the **narrow section**, the same volume must pass through a smaller area per second, so it **speeds up**. - By the law of conservation of energy, the increase in **kinetic energy** (speed) must be balanced by a decrease in **pressure energy**. This is exactly what Bernoulli observed: **high velocity corresponds to low pressure, and low velocity corresponds to high pressure** in a horizontal flow. This principle is the reason aircraft wings generate lift (air moves faster over the curved upper surface, creating lower pressure that "sucks" the wing upward). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Example for Transducers:** When you place your thumb partially over a garden hose nozzle, the water speeds up and shoots farther. The pressure drop at the constriction is what a **Venturi meter** or **orifice plate** measures to determine the flow rate of fluid in industrial pipelines. ## 1.3 Key Assumptions (Mandatory for Validity) For Bernoulli's equation to be strictly valid, the following conditions must be satisfied: | Assumption | Description | |---|---| | **Steady Flow** | Flow parameters do not change with time at any fixed point | | **Incompressible Fluid** | Fluid density ($\rho$) remains constant (true for liquids, approximately true for low-speed gases) | | **Non-Viscous (Inviscid)** | Fluid has zero viscosity; no friction losses along the streamline | | **Along a Streamline** | Equation applies to points on the same streamline (not between different streamlines) | | **No Energy Addition/Extraction** | No pump, turbine, or heat transfer between the two points | > [!WARNING] > For real engineering transducers, a **discharge coefficient ($C_d$)** is introduced to account for viscous losses, turbulence, and non-ideal flow conditions. $C_d$ is determined experimentally and typically lies between **0.60 and 0.99** depending on the device geometry. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following constants and standard units are essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems: - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^{2}$ - Density of water (standard reference): $\rho_{water} = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ - Density of mercury (manometer fluid): $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ - Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind the Equation Bernoulli's equation is essentially a **mechanical energy balance** for an ideal fluid particle moving along a streamline. It is derived by integrating **Euler's equation of motion** along a streamline. The three energy heads have the following physical meanings: 1. **Pressure Head** $\left(\dfrac{P}{\rho g}\right)$: Represents the **flow energy** due to fluid pressure. It is the height of a fluid column that the pressure could support. 2. **Velocity Head (Kinetic Head)** $\left(\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}\right)$: Represents the **kinetic energy** per unit weight of the fluid. It is the height from which a body must fall in vacuum to acquire velocity $v$. 3. **Elevation Head (Potential Head)** $(z)$: Represents the **potential energy** per unit weight due to the fluid's position above a reference datum. > [!NOTE] > **Why does pressure drop when velocity increases?** In a horizontal pipe, mass conservation ($A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$) forces velocity to increase at a constriction. Since total energy must be conserved (Bernoulli's principle), the **kinetic energy increases** at the cost of **pressure energy**, which must decrease proportionally. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Critical) The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 4 problems: | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$ | General Bernoulli's equation (energy heads in meters) | | 2 | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2$ | Bernoulli's equation in energy per unit mass form ($\text{J/kg}$) | | 3 | $P_1 + \dfrac{\rho v_1^{2}}{2} + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \dfrac{\rho v_2^{2}}{2} + \rho g z_2$ | Bernoulli's equation in pressure form ($\text{Pa}$) | | 4 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$ | **Volumetric flow rate** through a constriction-based flow meter | | 5 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Simplified flow rate when $A_1 \gg A_2$ (tank with orifice) | | 6 | $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$ | Differential pressure head across the constriction | | 7 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Theoretical velocity at the throat/vena contracta | | 8 | $v_{actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Actual velocity accounting for losses | | 9 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Continuity equation (mass conservation for incompressible flow) | | 10 | $m = \rho A v$ | Mass flow rate ($\text{kg/s}$) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Used in Table:** $A_1$ = upstream pipe cross-sectional area; $A_2$ = throat/vena contracta area; $C_d$ = discharge coefficient; $h$ = differential head; $Q$ = volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$). Always use consistent SI units to avoid dimensional mismatches. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Bernoulli's principle is the **backbone of industrial flow measurement instrumentation**. Its applications include: - **Venturi Meter**: A converging-diverging pipe section used in water treatment plants, oil pipelines, and HVAC systems to measure large flow rates with minimal pressure loss. - **Orifice Plate**: A flat plate with a precisely machined hole, inserted into a pipe. It is the **most widely used** flow meter in chemical and petrochemical industries due to low cost and simplicity. - **Pitot Tube**: Used in aviation (aircraft airspeed indicators) and meteorological stations to measure fluid velocity at a point. - **Flow Nozzle**: A streamlined alternative to orifice plates, used in high-velocity steam and gas flow measurement. - **Carburetors and Fuel Injectors**: Automotive applications where pressure drop draws fuel into the air stream. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In a 14-mark problem, always begin by writing Bernoulli's equation between the **upstream point (1)** and the **throat/vena contracta (2)**, then apply the **continuity equation** to eliminate one unknown velocity, and finally substitute the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to get the **final flow rate expression**. This three-step structure is what examiners look for to award full marks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from First Principles We begin with **Euler's equation of motion** for an inviscid, incompressible fluid along a streamline: $$v \, dv + \frac{dP}{\rho} + g \, dz = 0$$ **Step 1: Set up the differential form of Euler's equation.** For a fluid particle moving along a streamline, the net force per unit mass in the direction of motion equals the acceleration. Euler's equation for steady, inviscid flow is: $$\frac{dP}{\rho} + v \, dv + g \, dz = 0$$ **Step 2: Apply the incompressibility condition.** Since the fluid is **incompressible**, density $\rho$ is constant and can be moved outside the differential operator: $$\frac{1}{\rho} \int dP + \int v \, dv + g \int dz = 0$$ **Step 3: Integrate each term between points (1) and (2) along the streamline.** $$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$ **Step 4: Rearrange the integrated expression to group point-1 and point-2 terms.** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2$$ This is Bernoulli's equation in **energy per unit mass** form (units: $\text{J/kg}$). **Step 5: Convert to the "head" form (energy per unit weight) by dividing throughout by $g$.** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$ This is the most commonly used form in KTU problems (units: meters of fluid). **Step 6: Convert to the "pressure" form by multiplying throughout by $\rho$.** $$P_1 + \frac{\rho v_1^{2}}{2} + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{\rho v_2^{2}}{2} + \rho g z_2$$ This form is used when working directly with pressure values (units: $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^{2}$). > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap:** Multiply the "head" form by $\rho g$ to get the "pressure" form. Multiply the "pressure" form by $1/\rho$ to get the energy-per-mass form. KTU questions may ask you to derive any one of the three forms. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Flow Rate Equation (General Constriction Device) We now derive the **volumetric flow rate** through any constriction-based flow transducer (Venturi, orifice, nozzle). **Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between upstream point (1) and throat point (2).** Assuming a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$): $$P_1 + \frac{\rho v_1^{2}}{2} = P_2 + \frac{\rho v_2^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 2: Rearrange to express the pressure difference.** $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}\right)$$ **Step 3: Apply the continuity equation to relate $v_1$ and $v_2$.** $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 4: Substitute $v_1$ into the pressure difference expression.** $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left[v_2^{2} - v_2^{2}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$$ **Step 5: Factor out $v_2^{2}$.** $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho v_2^{2}}{2}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$$ **Step 6: Solve for the theoretical (ideal) throat velocity $v_2$.** $$v_{2,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ **Step 7: Introduce the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for real-fluid losses.** $$v_{2,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot v_{2,\text{ideal}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ **Step 8: Multiply by throat area $A_2$ to obtain the volumetric flow rate.** $$Q = A_2 \cdot v_{2,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ This is the **master flow rate equation** used for Venturi meters, orifice plates, and flow nozzles. > [!TIP] > **Special Case (Large Tank with Small Orifice):** When $A_1 \gg A_2$, the term $\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} \to 0$, and the velocity at the orifice becomes $v = C_d\sqrt{2gh}$, where $h$ is the height of fluid above the orifice. This is **Torricelli's theorem**, a frequently asked KTU sub-question. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern) **Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal Venturi meter. The upstream pipe diameter is $D_1 = 0.20 \ \text{m}$ and the throat diameter is $D_2 = 0.10 \ \text{m}$. A differential U-tube manometer reads a mercury column difference of $h_m = 0.15 \ \text{m}$. Given $C_d = 0.98$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, and $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^{2}$, calculate the volumetric flow rate. **Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional areas.** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.20)^{2}}{4} = 0.03142 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^{2}}{4} = 0.007854 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Convert the manometer reading to equivalent water head $h$.** For a U-tube manometer with mercury as the manometer fluid and water as the flowing fluid: $$h = h_m \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right) = 0.15 \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right)$$ $$h = 0.15 \times 12.6 = 1.89 \ \text{m of water}$$ **Step 3: Express pressure difference in terms of $h$.** $$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.89 = 18540.9 \ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 4: Calculate the area ratio term.** $$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.007854}{0.03142}\right)^{2} = (0.25)^{2} = 0.0625$$ $$1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$ **Step 5: Apply the master flow rate equation.** $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18540.9}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$ **Step 6: Evaluate the expression inside the square root.** $$\frac{2 \times 18540.9}{1000 \times 0.9375} = \frac{37081.8}{937.5} = 39.55 \ \text{m}^{2}\text{/s}^{2}$$ **Step 7: Take the square root.** $$\sqrt{39.55} = 6.289 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 8: Calculate the final flow rate.** $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.007854 \times 6.289$$ $$Q = 0.04841 \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \approx 48.41 \ \text{L/s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $Q \approx 0.0484 \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$ or equivalently **48.4 liters per second**. This matches the expected KTU 2024 numerical answer range. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bernoulli-Based Flow Transducer System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **signal flow and functional interactions** of a complete Bernoulli-principle-based flow measurement system. This is the architecture students should draw in KTU 14-mark descriptive answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[FLUID SOURCE
Reservoir Or Pipe Network] --> B1[FLOW STRAIGHTENER
Removes Swirl And Turbulence] B1 --> C1[UPSTREAM TAP POINT 1

These four elements together typically earn full marks for the diagram. --- ## 4.3 Energy Conversion Sequence Diagram (Per-Unit-Weight Energy Heads) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1H[PRESSURE HEAD
P1 By Rho G] --> TOT1[TOTAL HEAD AT POINT 1
Energy In] V1H[VELOCITY HEAD
V1 Squared By 2G] --> TOT1 Z1H[ELEVATION HEAD
Z1] --> TOT1 TOT1 --> EQU[BERNOULLI CONSTANT
Energy Conserved] EQU --> TOT2[TOTAL HEAD AT POINT 2

Z2] --> TOT2 style TOT1 fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:3px style EQU fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style TOT2 fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:3px style P1H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style V1H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style Z1H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style P2H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style V2H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style Z2H fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px ``` **Energy Interpretation:** The total head at point 1 equals the total head at point 2. Any **increase in velocity head** at point 2 must be balanced by a **decrease in pressure head** or **elevation head**. This is the essence of Bernoulli's principle. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State Bernoulli's equation for an ideal fluid flow and mention the assumptions involved.** **Model Answer:** Bernoulli's equation states that for an ideal, incompressible, non-viscous fluid undergoing steady flow, the total energy per unit weight (sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head) remains constant along a streamline: $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Fluid is **ideal** (inviscid, no friction). 2. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant density). 3. Flow is **steady** (time-independent). 4. The equation applies **along a single streamline**. 5. **No energy addition or extraction** (no pump, turbine, or heat transfer) between the two points. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Explain the working principle of a Venturi meter as a flow transducer.** **Model Answer:** A Venturi meter is a **differential-pressure-type flow transducer** based on Bernoulli's principle. It consists of a **converging section** (inlet to throat) followed by a **diverging section** (throat to outlet) inside a pipe. **Working Principle:** - As fluid enters the **converging section**, its cross-sectional area decreases. - By the **continuity equation**, velocity increases and by **Bernoulli's equation**, pressure decreases. - The **throat** is the point of minimum area, maximum velocity, and minimum pressure. - The **pressure difference** $(P_1 - P_2)$ between the upstream tap and the throat tap is measured by a manometer or differential pressure sensor. - This pressure difference is **proportional to the square of the flow rate**, allowing calculation of $Q$ using the standard formula. > [!TIP] > The Venturi meter is preferred over orifice plates in applications requiring **low permanent pressure loss**, such as in large water-supply mains. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A horizontal Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of $300 \ \text{mm}$ and a throat diameter of $150 \ \text{mm}$. The differential pressure measured across the meter is $20 \ \text{kPa}$. If the discharge coefficient is $0.98$, calculate the volumetric flow rate of water. Take $\rho = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1: Write Euler's equation of motion for an inviscid, incompressible fluid along a streamline.** $$\frac{dP}{\rho} + v \, dv + g \, dz = 0$$ **Step 2: Since the fluid is incompressible, $\rho$ is constant. Integrate from point 1 to point 2:** $$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{\rho} + \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v \, dv + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz = 0$$ **Step 3: Perform the integration term by term.** $$\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1) = 0$$ **Step 4: Rearrange to group point-1 and point-2 terms together.** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2$$ **Step 5: State the assumptions:** - Steady flow - Incompressible fluid - Inviscid (non-viscous) fluid - Flow along a streamline - No external work or heat transfer **Step 6: Present the final equation in the standard "head" form by dividing by $g$:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$$ --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional areas.** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.300)^{2}}{4} = 0.07069 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.150)^{2}}{4} = 0.01767 \ \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the area ratio term.** $$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.01767}{0.07069}\right)^{2} = (0.25)^{2} = 0.0625$$ $$1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$ **Step 3: Substitute values into the master flow rate equation.** $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$ **Step 4: Evaluate inside the square root.** $$\frac{2 \times 20000}{1000 \times 0.9375} = \frac{40000}{937.5} = 42.667 \ \text{m}^{2}\text{/s}^{2}$$ **Step 5: Take square root and multiply.** $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times \sqrt{42.667} = 0.98 \times 0.01767 \times 6.532$$ $$\boxed{Q = 0.1131 \ \text{m}^{3}\text{/s} \approx 113.1 \ \text{L/s}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an orifice plate flow meter. Compare its performance with a Venturi meter. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of air flowing in a duct. The differential manometer reads a water column of $8 \ \text{mm}$. The air density is $1.2 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and water density is $1000 \ \text{kg/m}^{3}$. Calculate the air velocity at the measurement point. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^{2}$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Construction:** An orifice plate is a **thin, flat, circular metallic plate** with a **concentric, sharp-edged hole** (orifice) precisely machined at its center. It is installed **perpendicular to the flow direction** between two pipe flanges. Two pressure taps are provided: one **upstream** (at a distance of about one pipe diameter before the plate) and one **downstream** (at the vena contracta, about half a pipe diameter after the plate). A **differential manometer** or pressure transducer is connected across these taps. **Working:** As fluid approaches the orifice, the **streamlines converge** and the cross-section of the flow continues to contract **downstream of the plate** until it reaches a minimum area called the **vena contracta**. At the vena contracta, velocity is maximum and pressure is minimum. The **pressure difference** $(P_1 - P_2)$ between the upstream tap and the vena contracta tap is measured. Using Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation, the flow rate is: $$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$$ where $A_2$ is the orifice area and $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$ for a standard sharp-edged orifice plate. **Comparison Table: Orifice Plate vs Venturi Meter** | Parameter | Orifice Plate | Venturi Meter | |---|---|---| | Construction | Simple flat plate | Converging-diverging section | | Cost | **Low** | **High** | | Discharge Coefficient ($C_d$) | 0.60 to 0.65 | 0.95 to 0.99 | | Permanent Pressure Loss | **High** | **Very Low** | | Accuracy | Moderate | High | | Maintenance | Easy to replace | Difficult to clean | | Best Suited For | Clean, non-corrosive fluids | Large flows, low-loss applications | --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between the free stream (point 1) and the Pitot tube stagnation point (point 2).** At the stagnation point, $v_2 = 0$. Assuming negligible elevation difference: $$P_1 + \frac{\rho_{air} v_1^{2}}{2} = P_2$$ $$P_2 - P_1 = \frac{\rho_{air} v_1^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 2: Express the pressure difference in terms of the manometer water column.** The differential pressure in terms of water column height $h_w$ is: $$P_2 - P_1 = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_w$$ $$P_2 - P_1 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.008 = 78.48 \ \text{Pa}$$ **Step 3: Equate the two expressions and solve for $v_1$.** $$\frac{\rho_{air} v_1^{2}}{2} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_w$$ $$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_w}{\rho_{air}}}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.** $$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.008}{1.2}}$$ $$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{156.96}{1.2}} = \sqrt{130.8}$$ $$\boxed{v_1 = 11.44 \ \text{m/s}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_d$**: Many students write $Q = A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$ without including $C_d$. This causes an **up to 35% error** and results in **loss of 2 to 3 marks**. > 2. **Ignoring the area ratio correction term** $\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]$: This is mandatory when $A_2 / A_1 > 0.1$. Missing it costs **1 to 2 marks**. > 3. **Unit inconsistency**: Mixing $\text{cm}$ with $\text{m}$, or using $\text{bar}$ instead of $\text{Pa}$ without conversion. Always convert **all dimensions to SI (meters, kg, seconds)** before substituting. > 4. **Manometer reading conversion**: A reading of $h_m$ mm of mercury is NOT equal to $h_m$ mm of water. Use $h_{water} = h_{Hg}\left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right)$ for U-tube manometers. This is the **single most common error** in KTU numericals. > 5. **Confusing Pitot tube (stagnation point) with Venturi (throat tap)**: Pitot tube measures **stagnation** pressure minus **static** pressure; Venturi measures **upstream static** minus **throat static**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Bernoulli's Equations (Module 4):** - **Bernoulli's Equation (Head Form)**: $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$. Master all three forms (head, energy per mass, pressure form). - **Five Mandatory Assumptions**: Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external energy exchange. - **Master Flow Rate Equation**: $Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^{2}\right]}}$ — memorize this in **sleep**. - **Discharge Coefficients** (approximate, exam-acceptable): Venturi $C_d = 0.95$ to $0.99$; Orifice $C_d = 0.60$ to $0.65$; Nozzle $C_d = 0.93$ to $0.98$. - **Manometer Conversion**: $h_{water} = h_{Hg}\left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right)$. For air-water manometers, the difference in densities is approximately $\rho_{water}$ (since $\rho_{air} \ll \rho_{water}$). - **Continuity Equation**: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ — always pair this with Bernoulli's equation to eliminate one velocity. - **Torricelli's Theorem** (special case): When $A_1 \gg A_2$, $v = C_d\sqrt{2gh}$, where $h$ is the fluid head above the orifice. - **Pitot Tube Formula**: $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$, where $\Delta P$ is the difference between stagnation and static pressures. - **Energy Heads Interpretation**: Pressure head (flow energy) + Velocity head (kinetic energy) + Elevation head (potential energy) = Constant. - **Key Engineering Applications**: Water supply networks, oil & gas pipelines, chemical process plants, aircraft airspeed measurement, carburetors, HVAC systems. - **Exam Pattern Reminder**: In 14-mark KTU questions, expect a 7-mark **derivation** + 7-mark **numerical**, or a 7-mark **device explanation with diagram** + 7-mark **numerical** on the same principle. - **Always include a labeled diagram** in descriptive answers. A clear Venturi/Orifice/Pitot diagram with tap locations is worth **2 to 3 marks** on its own. - **Final unit check**: Flow rate $Q$ should always be in $\text{m}^{3}\text{/s}$ (or converted to L/s for readability). Never report $Q$ in inconsistent units.
